title: Development of an Environmental Sensor System in the Life Mechatronics Symposium and its Use in Project-Based Learning
authors: Motoharu Fujigaki
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Consumer Technology (ICCT-Pacific)
abstract: In this presentation, joint research activities in Cambodia conducted under the Life Mechatronics initiative will be introduced. An environmental sensor system has been developed and installed at two experimental sites. Because the system is expected to be deployed more widely in the Cambodia–Japan collaborative project, it requires expandability and low-cost implementation, needs that existing commercial products do not satisfy. A new system tailored to future research activities is therefore being developed. Furthermore, this environmental sensing system has been incorporated into project-based learning (PBL) activities, and the practical implementation of these activities will also be reported.

title: Intelligent Scheduling of AI Tasks in Kubernetes
authors: Michael Abdelshaheed, Mohamed Ashour
publish: 2025 35th International Conference on Computer Theory and Applications (ICCTA)
abstract: Kubernetes has become the de facto standard for orchestrating containerized workloads, including machine learning (ML). ML workloads exhibit long runtimes, variable compute and I/O demands, and high sensitivity to execution order, making them challenging to schedule efficiently. Kubernetes lacks mechanisms for fine-grained job ranking and does not control the exact order in which pods begin execution. Instead, Kubelet starts containers in parallel without coordination, causing pods to compete for resources simultaneously and degrade system performance. The work in this paper presents a custom ML-aware scheduling framework that addresses the above two limitations of the default scheduler. First, a ranking approach is introduced that scores jobs based on their ML-specific characteristics including estimated training duration, convergence behavior, and computational intensity. Second, a scheduler implementation is presented that enforces execution order according to the proposed ranking through pacing and execution checks. The framework is evaluated on a real Kubernetes cluster using simulated ML workloads, testing multiple pacing configurations and analyzing their impact on average Job Completion Time (JCT) and makespan. The best-performing configuration, balancing serialization and throughput, is then adopted for comparison against the default Kubernetes scheduler and a reversed-order variant across three workload intensities. The proposed work achieves up to 31.6 % improvement in average JCT and up 10.2 % improvement in makespan compared to the default scheduler. An ablation study with reversed execution order degraded JCT by up to 58 % relative to the intended ordering, confirming the importance of enforcing intelligent job sequencing. These findings highlight that controlling execution order, not just scheduling order, is a critical factor in ML workload orchestration, even in unconstrained, single-node environments.

title: Dagent: A Multi-Agent System for Device-Aware Assistance
authors: Ahmed Hassan, Ahmed Gamea, Omar Abdelgawad, Abdelaziz Serour, Eyad Hussien, Amr Khamees, Ahmed Abdalfatah, Ahmed Fares
publish: 2025 35th International Conference on Computer Theory and Applications (ICCTA)
abstract: The limitations of large language models (LLMs) in interacting with local computing environments have hindered their applicability in device-aware tasks. This research addresses this gap by introducing DAgent, a modular, multiagent system designed to provide personalized, context-aware assistance through integration with the user's system environment. The architecture comprises four specialized modules: the Tracer module for monitoring system activities, the RAG module for retrieval-augmented generation leveraging system logs, the Coding Agent for interacting with the environment to infer information or execute actions, and the Multi-Agent OS Assistant for orchestrating workflows. DAgent was evaluated on correctness, completeness, and clarity, scoring highly with the baseline 14 B model: 4.87,4.53, and 4.41 out of 5, respectively. Additionally, an ablation study removing the RAG module demonstrated the importance of the coding agent module, which achieved an average score of 4. 4 5 when used independently. These findings highlight DAgent's ability to excel in device-aware tasks, providing actionable, comprehensible responses and enabling secure, supervised command execution. While our evaluation was conducted on synthetic scenarios, the results indicate strong potential for deployment in real-world environments, marking a step toward intelligent, device-aware assistants that integrate language reasoning with system interaction.

title: Explainable Heterogeneous Federated Learning for Intrusion Detection in IIoT
authors: Tony Elsayed, Marwa Zamzam, Mohamed Ashour
publish: 2025 35th International Conference on Computer Theory and Applications (ICCTA)
abstract: The Industrial Internet of Things (IIoT) is increasingly exposed to advanced cyberattacks, creating an urgent need for Intrusion Detection Systems (IDS) that are accurate, interpretable, and privacy-preserving. Conventional IDS often rely on centralized data aggregation, which increases the risk of exposing sensitive information. In contrast, purely local learning avoids data sharing but struggles to generalize across heterogeneous IIoT environments. Additionally, many Machine Learning (ML)-based IDS operate as black boxes, providing limited transparency and making it difficult for operators to trust or validate alerts. These challenges highlight the need for IDS that preserve privacy, generalize effectively under nonidentical data distributions, and offer interpretable predictions. This paper introduces a Federated Learning (FL)-based IDS for IIoT that integrates Explainable AI (XAI) through SHapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP). A comparative study of centralized, local, and FL paradigms is conducted using a multiclass Edge-IIoT-set dataset. Results show that centralized learning achieves the highest accuracy (97.7%) but requires aggregation of raw client data, while local models suffer from poor generalization under heterogeneous IIoT conditions. FL achieves competitive accuracy (95%), reduces disparities across clients, preserves privacy, and provides stable, comprehensive feature attributions. Overall, FL balances accuracy, fairness, and interpretability, making it a practical and privacy-preserving solution for IIoT intrusion detection.

title: Method for Identifying Changes Between Program Versions
authors: Mariam Arutunian, Hripsime Hovhannisyan, Arthur Malajyan, Karen Avetisyan, Hayk Aslanyan
publish: 2025 35th International Conference on Computer Theory and Applications (ICCTA)
abstract: This paper presents a two-stage method for detecting changes between program versions. It combines analysis based on metrics with a code clone detection algorithm. The approach uses control and data dependencies between instructions to match functions on a many-to-many basis. Additionally, it allows detecting changed instructions with high accuracy. The developed method provides reliable comparison of executable files, even in the absence of source code. Experimental evaluation on software projects has demonstrated its effectiveness in analyzing changes and detecting modifications.

title: Tensor Methods Outperform Transformers: A Comprehensive Comparative Analysis of Knowledge Graph Link Prediction Methods
authors: Manal Helal, Hammad Khawaja
publish: 2025 35th International Conference on Computer Theory and Applications (ICCTA)
abstract: Knowledge graph link prediction has witnessed significant methodological evolution, with recent research heavily favouring transformer-based approaches. However, our comprehensive comparative analysis reveals surprising results: classical tensor factorisation methods significantly outperform modern transformer architectures across multiple domains. We systematically evaluate four major categories of link prediction methods-classical embeddings (TransE, DistMult, ComplEx), tensor factorisation (CP, Tucker Decomposition), graph neural networks, and transformer approaches-across three diverse knowledge graph datasets (UMLS, FB15K, DB15K). Contrary to prevailing trends, tensor methods achieved superior accuracy (Tensor-CP MRR: 0.2693) while transformer approaches demonstrated unexpectedly poor performance (LP-BERT MRR: <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{0. 0 3 8 9}$</tex>). Our multi-dimensional evaluation, encompassing accuracy, computational efficiency, and scalability, identifies three distinct operational tiers and provides evidence-based guidelines for method selection. These findings challenge the current research focus on transformer architectures and suggest a renewed importance for tensor-based approaches in knowledge graph completion tasks.

title: Analysis of Lightweight CNN Models for Multi-Crop Leaf Disease Classification
authors: Shivam Kumar Chauhan, Roop Pahuja
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: Quick and precise plant disease detection is a key aspect in precision agriculture, particularly in resource-sensitive and field-scale settings. Whereas deep convolutional neural networks (CNNs) have proven to be very effective in leaf disease detection, most existing models rely on computationally intensive architectures that restrict real-time deployment. In this paper, a controlled comparative benchmark of two widely used lightweight CNNs (ResNet50 and InceptionV3) is presented for multi-crop leaf disease classification. An evidence-based preprocessing pipeline, including systematic data cleaning and Contrast Limited Adaptive Histogram Equalization (CLAHE), is employed to optimize lesion visibility and feature consistency. Both models are trained on tomato and potato leaf disease classes from the PlantVillage dataset using transfer learning and evaluated under identical experimental conditions. The results indicate that ResNet50 converges stably with lower computational overhead, while InceptionV3 demonstrates stronger discrimination of complex disease patterns. Gradient-weighted Class Activation Mapping (Grad-CAM) is included as an experimental interpretability tool to qualitatively analyze model attention. Although the generated heatmaps are not always precise, they provide useful insights into disease-relevant regions influencing predictions. Overall, the findings highlight the trade-offs between accuracy, efficiency, and interpretability when deploying lightweight CNN architectures for practical multi-crop disease diagnosis.

title: Nanotube Transistor Architectures: Device Physics and Scalability Perspective
authors: Dileep Kumar Gupta, Ashish Raman, Prabhat Singh
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: When semiconductor scaling gets close to the sub-5nm range, regular MOSFETs have problems because of short-channel effects (SCEs) and the basic subthreshold swing (SS) limit of 60 mV/decade. Tunnel Field-Effect Transistors (TFETs) have become a promising alternative because they use a band-to-band tunneling (BTBT) process. This paper talks about the progress of Nanotube FET (NTFET) architectures, focusing on their hollow cylindrical shape, which gives them better electrostatic control than nanowire equivalents. We look at different types of structures, such as electrostatic doping (ED) and heterojunction integration (SiGe/Si, InGaAs/InP), and how they can improve ON-state current and make ultra-steep subthreshold slopes.

title: Comparative Evaluation of CNN Models for Maize Crop Disease Diagnosis
authors: Sainadh Singh Kshagri, C. Raju, Kuruva Raju, S. Mallikarjunaiah, Banala Saritha, G Purnachandrarao
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: To ensure the security of food in the world, agriculture plays a significant role and maize stands among the most widely grown staple crops worldwide. However, maize productivity is seriously impacted by leaf diseases that result in huge yield losses every year. Early and accurate identification of these diseases will reduce the damage to the crops and to optimally utilize the agricultural resources. Conventional techniques of detection depends on manual inspection, which is impractical for large-scale agricultural. Although recent developments in Artificial Intelligence (AI) techniques have been promising for plant disease diagnostics in an automated fashion, most of the studies have only considered one model or none compared the modern convolutional neural network (CNN) architectures systematically. This causes a research gap of finding the most appropriate DL model for reliable classification of maize diseases. The objective of this work is such that it classifies maize leaf images into 4 classes, namely, Northern Leaf Blight, Common Rust, Gray Leaf Spot and Healthy and compares the performances of CNN pre-trained models. The methodology is based on using transfer learning methods based on MobileNetV2, EfficientNetB0 and ResNet50 models pre-trained on the Plant Village dataset that is publicly available. Fast computing Image preprocessing and fine-tuning is carried out to improve the classification performance. Key findings show closures in that ResNet50 can classify samples better than MobileNetV2 and EfficientNetB0 thanks to its simple model structure with depth and residual links that help with the process of extracting strong features. The results show the effectiveness of deep learning-based approaches can be used for an accurate maize disease detection. The proposed framework has important implications for smart and precision agriculture such as early disease diagnosis, lower crop losses, more optimal use of pesticides, and better decision support to farmers.

title: A Task-Oriented Characterization of Benchmark Datasets for Predictive Maintenance of Rolling Bearings
authors: Amanpreet Kaur, Harpreet Kaur
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: Rolling-bearing predictive maintenance research relies heavily on publicly available benchmark datasets to develop and evaluate data-driven methods. However, these datasets differ fundamentally in experimental design, fault generation mechanisms, temporal structure, and labeling strategies, and are frequently applied to predictive maintenance tasks for which they violate core methodological assumptions. As a result, a significant portion of the existing literature reports performance results that are difficult to interpret, not comparable across studies, and in some cases methodologically unreliable. This paper presents a task-oriented characterization of widely used rolling-bearing benchmark datasets, including IMS, CWRU, XJTU-SY, FEMTO (PRONOSTIA), and Paderborn. The datasets are systematically analyzed and mapped to three core predictive maintenance objectives: fault diagnosis, degradation modeling, and remaining useful life (RUL) prediction based on their structural suitability. In addition, common dataset misuse patterns are identified and examined through the lens of experimental validity, highlighting threats to prognostic, construct, statistical, and ecological validity. The main contributions of this work are (i) a dataset–task compatibility framework that clarifies appropriate and inappropriate dataset usage, and (ii) a validity-driven analysis that explains why certain widely reported predictive maintenance results cannot be reliably generalized. This paper aims to serve as a corrective reference for dataset selection and experimental design in rolling-bearing predictive maintenance research.

title: SPI based Secure Communication Framework for Industrial IoT
authors: Soumyadip Motilal, Soumita Chatterjee, Ayas Kanta Swain, Kamalakanta Mahapatra
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: The rapid growth and development of IoT has led to the need for safe and effective hardware based communication protocols. As any other network system, IoT systems are also prone to security breaches. To ensure data integrity and authentication, this paper proposes a novel design of a secure FPGA-based communication protocol-SPI on Xilinx Spartan-7 XC7S50-CSGA324 FPGA for Industrial Internet of Things (IIoT) applications. The design involves design of a high-level synthesized (HLS) Intellectual Property (IP) of two security standards- Advanced Encryption Standard (AES) and Rivest–Shamir–Adleman (RSA) for encryption and decryption of the data. Following this, the serial interface is designed wherein the slave (sensor) transmits the encrypted data to the master (IoT Gateway). The decryption IP block then reveals the original message. The synthesis results show that the security protocols can achieve resource consumption as low as 3.79%. These security protocols have been also tested on Zybo, Pynq Z2 and Arty A7 boards. Additionally, these are also tested against various statistical strength measuring tests- NIST p-value and avalanche. The proposed secure design protocol is a stepping stone for next generation simple yet flexible secure IoT modules. The detailed experimental analysis also serves as a testimony to the fact that a highly secure protocol can be designed without requiring heavy resource usage or power overhead.

title: GNN-Based Malware Detection in IoT Networks: An Attack Graph Approach
authors: Irtika Khan, Anil Kumar Prajapati, Swaliya Khan
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: The rapid expansion of Internet of Things (IoT) devices is growing exponentially, significantly increasing the global attack space, making contemporary networks more susceptible to large-scale malware attacks with high sophistication. The heterogeneous and resource-constrained characteristics of IoT settings render traditional detection strategies, which rely on flow-level statistical characteristics, to be unable to reflect the relational and structural regularities of malware propagation. To address these limitations, we introduced a Graph Neural Network (GNN)-based attack graph framework that models IoT traffic as network graphs, capturing the extraction of complex behavioral dependencies among IoT devices. Traffic traces from real IoT and honeypot devices are captured in PCAP format and preprocessed to extract protocol, timing, and packet-level features. The proposed approach is evaluated using three GNN architectures: Graph Attention Network (GAT), Graph Isomorphism Network (GIN), and GraphSAGE, on the IoT-23 dataset. Experimental results demonstrate that GAT achieves highest accuracy, GraphSAGE provides consistently high detection performance, and GIN achieves competitive accuracy with precise accuracy, recall, and F1-scores, highlighting the effectiveness of GNN-based attack graphs for IoT malware detection. As a model framework, GNN provides an intelligent, robust, and scalable intrusion detection systems could be built future Internet of Things network.

title: Integrating Lean Healthcare Practices with Discrete-Event Simulation to Reduce Patient Waiting Time in Outpatient Clinics at a Sri Lankan Public Hospital
authors: Navindya Hettiarachchi, Chathumi Kavirathna, Annista Wijayanayake
publish: 2026 IEEE International Research Conference on Smart Computing and Systems Engineering (SCSE)
abstract: Excessive patient waiting time is a persistent operational challenge in outpatient departments (OPDs) of public hospitals, particularly in high-volume and resourceconstrained healthcare systems. This study aims to analyze and reduce patient waiting time in the OPD of a major Sri Lankan public hospital by integrating Lean management practices with discrete-event simulation (DES). Lean tools were applied to map the current-state process and identify non-value-added activities, capacity imbalance, and workflow bottlenecks. Primary data were collected through time studies conducted over three consecutive working days, capturing patient arrivals, service times, and waiting durations at registration, consultation, and pharmacy stages. A DES model was developed using Arena Simulation Software to evaluate multiple Leanbased improvement scenarios, including partial appointmentbased demand leveling, layout optimization, consultation capacity balancing during peak hours, and digital queue management at the pharmacy. Simulation experiments were conducted for both peak-demand and reduced-capacity periods. Results reveal that doctor consultation is the primary bottleneck driving excessive waiting, with pharmacy services acting as a secondary bottleneck. While isolated Lean interventions yielded limited improvements, integrated Lean scenarios achieved substantial reductions in waiting time and lead time, together with notable increases in patient throughput, without infrastructure expansion. The findings demonstrate the effectiveness of simulation-supported Lean integration as a practical decision-support approach for improving OPD efficiency in public healthcare settings.

title: PerfBench: Can Agents Resolve Real-World Performance Bugs?
authors: Spandan Garg, Roshanak Zilouchian Moghaddam, Neel Sundaresan
publish: Proceedings of the 2026 International Workshop on Agentic Engineering
abstract: None

title: Accurate and Efficient Equivalent Circuit Modeling for Single-Port SAW Resonators_supp1-3692761.pdf
authors: Chen Chen
abstract: An optimized equivalent circuit modeling framework for single-port surface acoustic wave (SAW) resonators is proposed in this article, addressing key limitations of conventional Butterworth–Van Dyke (BVD) and modified BVD (MBVD) models in representing spurious resonances and packaging parasitics. It incorporates: 1) simplified parasitic circuits to quantify packaging effects and 2) explicit resonance loops for spurious modes. The extraction of equivalent circuit parameters within this framework is guided by an iterative strategy that combines analytical derivation and least-squares optimization. Model validation across 15 commercial SAW resonators (303–868 MHz) shows significantly improved precision: the fit MBVD model reduces root mean squared error (RMSE) by roughly one order of magnitude, while the extended MBVD (EMBVD) further lowers RMSE by 30%–50%. The parasitic-integrated BVD (PBVD) model achieves high-accuracy packaging characterization with a relative RMSE (RRMSE) of 15.1% and root-mean-squared relative error (RMSRE) of 0.51%. In practice, EMBVD prevents oscillation mode hopping by capturing spurious resonances, while PBVD guides the optimization of devices’ packaging and PCB layout. Furthermore, the extracted equivalent circuit parameters provide clear criteria for sensor-specific resonator selection: balancing sensitivity against resolution for mass-loading sensing, or prioritizing low motional resistance (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${R}_{\text {m}}\text {)}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> and static capacitance (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${C}_{{0}}\text {)}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> to ensure oscillation stability for viscoelastic sensing. The proposed framework is also extendable to two-port SAW resonators, further broadening its applicability. This work thus facilitates the transition from precise resonator characterization to robust system design.

title: When Does Train-Time Masking Help? A Leakage-Controlled Robustness Audit of Tabular Classifiers Under Feature Missingness
authors: Sooyoung Jang, JeeSook Eun, Hyunbean Yi, Changbeom Choi
abstract: Tabular classifiers are typically benchmarked on clean train–validation–test splits drawn from the same distribution, even though deployment-time inputs routinely contain missing features that were fully observed during training, and prior robustness claims often confound train-time exposure to corrupted inputs with genuine deployment-time robustness, reuse preprocessing fitted on the full dataset, or rely on a single random split. To address this gap, we audit whether train-time stochastic masking actually improves robustness to test-time feature missingness once these confounds are controlled, using mask-augmented imputation training (MAIT)—a recipe combining stochastic feature masking, missingness indicators, and a reconstruction loss—as the audit subject. We evaluate MAIT on five OpenML benchmarks across 20 seeds with train-only preprocessing and copied MCAR, MAR, and MNAR test-set overlays, comparing against tree ensembles, an FT-Transformer-style baseline, TabPFN, and a same-backbone MLP-only control. On Covertype, MAIT degrades 0.0133 AUROC at the 30% MCAR overlay while LightGBM degrades 0.2062 and the same-backbone control isolates this gain to the masking recipe (MAIT 0.9814 nominal, 0.9682 at MCAR-30 vs. MLP-only 0.9896 and 0.9113); at the practical threshold δ = 0.005 AUROC, equivalent to a one-percentage-point Gini delta in credit-risk reporting, MAIT’s advantage is operationally meaningful on Covertype and German Credit, while tree ensembles or TabPFN lead on absolute AUROC for the remaining three datasets.We therefore recommend train-time masking when the degradation slope under missingness is itself the deployment objective, and stronger comparators when the peak nominal AUROC is the deployment objective, and present the audit protocol as a reusable template for future robustness claims under feature dropout.

title: IEEE Transactions on Nuclear Science Publication Information
authors: N/A

title: Bearing-Only Tracking of Underwater Multiple Acoustic Sources Using Underwater Vehicles Under Latency and Packet Loss
authors: Tianming Gao, Paolo Di Lillo, Gianluca Antonelli, Tianyi Guo, Alessandro Marino, Jing Yan, Xinping Guan
abstract: Bearing-only tracking (BOT) of underwater acoustic sources is crucial for numerous applications in the underwater domain. A promising approach involves reconfigurable underwater wireless sensor networks (UWSNs) composed of a group of underwater vehicles that enable target state estimation via data fusion while enhancing observability. However, the uncertainty of measurement-target association and unstable acoustic communications pose significant challenges. This article presents a selective association probabilistic multi-hypothesis tracking (SA-PMHT) solution tailored for BOT of underwater targets under latency and packet loss. Specifically, a selective association mechanism is designed to determine measurement-target associations by synthesizing candidate measurements for each target. Then, a pseudo-linear equation is constructed to establish regression matrices for targets, and a sliding-window least-squares estimator is embedded for iterative state estimation. We demonstrate that the SA-PMHT tracking framework is a pseudo-posterior variant of the expectation-maximization (EM) algorithm. Meanwhile, theoretical analyses are provided for its computational complexity and sufficient conditions for target observability. Finally, both simulation and experimental results validate the effectiveness of our solution.

title: Brewster Blind Spot: Polarization-Dependent Fisher Information Collapse in Sub-THz Penetration Sensing
authors: Qiang Wu, Weiqing Huang
abstract: Jointly estimating wall permittivity and thickness from sub-terahertz penetration loss is fundamentally limited by parameter coupling, yet the role of polarization in this ill-conditioning has not been examined. This letter reveals that the TM-polarized Fisher information matrix undergoes rank-one collapse at the Brewster angle, with the condition number exhibiting an inverse-square divergence that persists for lossy building materials with tan δ ≤ 0.1, while the TE-polarized counterpart is immune and improves monotonically. The collapse drives the Cramér–Rao lower bound on wall thickness to exceed the physical wall dimension threefold. Monte Carlo maximum-likelihood estimation reaches the dual-polarization bound. To counter this blind spot, joint dual-polarization observation at a single oblique angle achieves conditioning comparable to a two-angle design, yielding 68- to 242-fold improvement across five building materials through electronic polarization switching rather than beam steering.

title: Carrier-Based PWM Strategy of Three-Level Neutral-Point-Clamped Inverter with Optimal Extension Mode
authors: Guozheng Zhang, Yufei Liu, Xin Gu, Wenhao Huang, Wei Chen
publish: IEEE Open Journal of Power Electronics
abstract: Neutral point voltage ripple is the main technical drawback of the three-level neutral-point-clamped (3L-NPC) inverters. Conventional hybrid PWM strategies, which combine zero sequence voltage injection PWM (ZVI-PWM) with double modulation wave PWM (DMW-PWM), can be used to suppress neutral point voltage ripple for all operation conditions. However, the switching frequency of the inverter will be significantly increased in the condition of high modulation index and low power factor. To solve the above issue, a hybrid carrier-based PWM strategy with optimal extension mode is proposed in this paper. Different from conventional hybrid PWM strategies, ZVI-PWM is extended to the neutral point voltage uncontrollable intervals. The optimal extension mode and the optimal width ratio of the extended intervals are obtained to minimize the amplitude of the neutral point voltage ripple. The coordinated control of the switching frequency and the neutral point voltage ripple is realized. The feasibility and effectiveness of the proposed modulation strategy are validated by experimental results

title: Securing the Sky: A Lightweight Authentication Protocol for Emergency UAVs
authors: Saeed Ullah Jan, Fahad Algarni, Ali Delham Algarni
abstract: Utilizing drones for various disaster management tasks, such as mitigation, prevention, aid delivery, survivor search, rapid response, and recovery, establishes a communication network that integrates UAVs, ground stations (GS), and rescue personnel. However, such a network is vulnerable to security threats, including entity spoofing to gain unauthorized control, command-and-control data manipulation, and eavesdropping on sensitive information, all of which can significantly compromise the mission. Hence, a secure initial authentication between UAVs and GS is crucial in such scenarios. Authentication is one of the fundamental security measures; however, a number of schemes already proposed for this adversarial environment suffer from several shortcomings. Some rely on computationally expensive cryptography, which resource-constrained UAVs cannot afford, while others, upon analysis, are inherently vulnerable to spoofing or do not offer comprehensive key establishment. In light of these limitations, this paper proposes an efficient and verifiably secure protocol for mutual entity authentication along with session key establishment for subsequent communications. The protocol uses lightweight hash functions in its structure to ensure suitability for emergency scenarios. The security claims are formally verified using GNY logic and the ProVerif tool, demonstrating resistance against a defined threat model that includes spoofing and unauthorized access. A comparative performance analysis indicates that the proposed scheme reduces computation and communication costs by approximately 85.26% and 16%, respectively, compared to a set of contemporary schemes. Furthermore, it also performs better in terms of energy consumption and scalability.

title: Invariant Manifold Based Model-Free Predictive Control for PMSM Motor with Adaptive Time Constant
authors: Yixiao Luo, Junqiang Luo, Jiangchun Liu, Kai Yang, Shuangxia Niu, Jincheng Yu
abstract: Most existing finite control set model-free predictive control (FCS-MFPC) methods based on ultra-local model involve complex function structures and multiple hyperparameters, which inevitably increase the complexity of system structure. To overcome these limitations, this paper proposes an observer-less FCS-MFPC scheme suitable for permanent magnet synchronous motor (PMSM) based on invariant manifold constraint. The key lies in utilizing a low-pass filter to construct constraints, thereby defining a stable convergence subspace for the online computation of unknown nonlinear terms. In this way, only one parameter, namely the time constant needs to be tuned. Meanwhile, to balance the estimation accuracy and the robustness, the time constant of the low-pass filter is online optimized using the gradient descent method for different operating scenarios. The proposed method is analyzed using Lyapunov theory to ensure system convergence. The proposed method presents a simple structure with high robustness. Finally, experimental results validate the practical effectiveness of the proposed method. Specifically, it demonstrates a 50 - 70% reduction in both steady-state torque ripple and phase current THD compared to the traditional method, alongside enhanced robustness against parameter mismatch.

title: Indistinguishability Analysis of JPEG Image Encryption Schemes
authors: Yuan Yuan, Lingfeng Qu, Ji Zhang, Ningxiong Mao, Hadi Amirpour
abstract: JPEG images are widely used for communication and storage, making secure encryption essential for privacy protection. Existing JPEG encryption studies primarily rely on empirical metrics such as visual distortion, key space, or correlation, while overlooking the formal indistinguishability against chosen-plaintext attacks (IND-CPA) property. This work provides the first systematic analysis of existing JPEG encryption schemes from the IND-CPA perspective. A new metric, termed feature change rate, is introduced to quantify the preservation of residual features. Both theoretical and experimental results demonstrate that preserving residual features compromises IND-CPA. Furthermore, the relationship between feature change rate and key estimation success under CPA is established, indicating that smaller feature changes result in higher attack accuracy. Based on these findings, we propose a set of design principles for constructing secure and practical JPEG encryption schemes. Finally, we outline a feature-changing encryption strategy that enhances IND-CPA security while maintaining JPEG compatibility and compression efficiency.

title: IEEE Transactions on Ultraonsics Publication Information
authors: N/A

title: Quantification and Visualisation of Interpersonal Synchrony using Wearable Sensors: A Case Study on Autistic and Neurotypical Children
authors: Yanke Sun, Sally E. Day, Thomas J. Gilbert, Maria Bell, Antonia F. de C. Hamilton, Jamie A. Ward
publish: IEEE Transactions on Neural Systems and Rehabilitation Engineering
abstract: Interpersonal synchrony (IS), a key indicator of social interactions, is traditionally assessed through video data and manual coding methods, a process that is time-consuming and subjective. This study presents an automated sensor-based framework for quantifying and visualizing IS using time series data collected from wearable sensors, demonstrated through a case study of interactions between autistic and neurotypical children in classroom settings. We evaluated time series similarity measures, including Cross-correlation (CC), Dynamic Time Warping (DTW), and Cross-Wavelet Analysis (XWA), as features for machine learning (ML) models that classify interaction levels, where ground truth labels are derived from video-coded motor coordination as a behavioral proxy for IS. Results show that these similarity-based features outperform conventional statistical features in distinguishing high and low IS using ensemble classifiers. We further compare two approaches for identifying pseudosynchrony: a surrogate data analysis for threshold estimation and a supervised learning approach for direct prediction, providing a systematic evaluation of their methodological trade-offs that has been largely overlooked in prior synchrony research. The developed visualization tools enable dynamic tracking of interaction patterns while filtering out pseudosynchrony. The proposed workflow offers a scalable, objective, and reproducible alternative to manual coding, addressing a key gap in the current literature and supporting broader applications in social, developmental, and rehabilitation research.

title: Energy-Fair and Reliable Carrier-Sense Multiple Access for LoRaWAN
authors: Chenglong Shao
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Consumer Technology (ICCT-Pacific)
abstract: This paper presents ER-MAC, a novel medium access control protocol for LoRaWAN that enhances both energy fairness and communication reliability. Unlike existing carriersense multiple access (CSMA) schemes, ER-MAC adaptively adjusts the number and interval of Channel Activity Detection (CAD) operations according to the CAD energy consumption of different spreading factors (SFs), ensuring balanced sensing energy among end devices using distinct SFs. ER-MAC also employs a gateway-based announcement mechanism to mitigate the hidden-terminal problem and improve transmission reliability. Experimental results show that ER-MAC achieves more than <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{2 1 \%}$</tex> improvement in energy fairness and up to <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{7 0 \%}$</tex> increase in packet reception ratio over state-of-the-art CSMA approaches.

title: Zero-Shot vs. Supervised: Evaluating Multilingual LLMs for 3R Content Moderation in Malaysia
authors: Ong Yew Chuan, Sharifah Sumayyah Engku Alwi, Azwa Abdul Aziz, Azilawati Rozaimee, Shafiq Angullia Tawfeik Sulleiman, Belal Al Ifan
publish: 2026 International Conference on Data Science, Machine Learning, and Intelligence (DataSciMI)
abstract: The idea of automated moderation of Race, Religion, and Royalty (3R) content on the Malaysian social media is not easy since people use colloquial Malay, Malaysian English ("Manglish"), and mixed language when discussing a sensitive subject. We test zero-shot prompting with multilingual large language models (LLMs) on the SYN3R-TIKTOK dataset, and we compare three instruction-tuned models with supervised XLM-RoBERTa baselines across different prompts and languages. The results show that leading proprietary LLMs reach almost the same performance as supervised models, but they fall behind fine-tuned baselines by less than 3% Macro-F1, and open-weight models struggle more with informal language. The error analysis shows frequent confusion between Race and Religion, and this happens because these categories overlap in Malaysian discourse and because the task allows only one label, not because the models make random mistakes. We also find that clear and descriptive prompts reduce this confusion for smaller models. Because of these results, we propose a hybrid moderation system in which simple supervised models handle clear cases, and stronger LLMs handle content that is linguistically and socially complex.

title: Energy Efficient Mono-Crystalline Solar Cell using blended dual layer as Anti-Reflective Coating
authors: Sarabdeep Singh, Prabhat Singh, Parveen Kumar
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: Increasing the efficiency of solar cells is a key concern which is addressed in this study. It presents an approach that incorporates an Anti-Reflective Coating (ARC), to enhance the potential of mono-crystalline solar cells. The ARC layer is made of titanium dioxide (TiO2) and Silicon dioxide (SiO2). It provides a better light absorption over a wide range of wavelengths by reducing surface reflection losses. Results show that optimised p-type doping of 1e18 with a work function of 4.2 eV, yields higher performance with increased photocurrent and low thermal voltages over changing bias values. Thus, provides a clear approach for enhancing the energy conversion efficiency of solar cells in commercial applications. Proposed study paves a cost-effective and scalable answer for improving performance, in applications demanding high-efficiency power harvesting during harsh environmental conditions like solar panels, solar-powered EVs, and Space, etc..

title: A Novel Epileptic Seizure Detection Technique using Hybrid Convolutional-Spiking Neural Network
authors: Prem Kumar, Viplav Singh, Nishant, Chittotosh Ganguly
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: Automated detection of epileptic seizures from multi-channel EEG signals is critical for clinical diagnosis and patient monitoring. This paper introduces a novel hybrid deep learning architecture that synergistically combines the robust feature extraction capabilities of Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) with the temporal processing strengths of Spiking Neural Networks (SNNs). Our model first employs a deep 1D-CNN to learn hierarchical spatial-temporal features from EEG time windows. The resulting high-level feature sequence is then processed by a recurrent layer of Leaky Integrate-and-Fire (LIF) spiking neurons, which captures long-range temporal dependencies inherent in seizure dynamics. To ensure the model’s robustness and optimal performance, we implemented a systematic hyperparameter optimization strategy using the Optuna framework, automatically tuning key parameters including learning rate, architectural choices, and regularization. The proposed model was rigorously evaluated on the public CHB-MIT dataset using a dedicated 60/20/20 train/validation/test split. The final, optimized architecture achieves an accuracy of 90.97%, with a sensitivity of 90.62% and a specificity of 91.32% on the unseen test set. These results validate the efficacy of our hybrid CNNSNN approach and underscore the importance of automated hyperparameter tuning for developing state-of-the-art biomedical signal classification systems.

title: Enhancing Land Registration System Using Blockchain Technology
authors: Surendra Kumar Shukla, Vishan Kumar Gupta, Paras Jain, Pawan Kumar, Vikas Tyagi, Atul Kumar Dwivedi
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: Land registration in India is a process that is prerequisite in the affirmation of legal ownership and is faced with continued hindrances such as inefficiencies, fraud and record discrepancies. This study suggests a new solution which is founded on blockchain technology that optimizes the current structure by offering a decentralized and immutable registry that is skilled in handling transactions in a peer-to-peer network with a high level of efficiency and security. The application of blockchain technology based on cryptographic principles provides transparency, integrity, and tamper-resistant records, which makes it one of the best solutions to the modernization of land registry processes. Our proposed solution uses Ethereum smart contracts to enable the process of verifying and transferring ownership to be automated, thus, the feedback of the need to use middlemen is minimized, and the chances of human error are minimized. Furthermore, the off-chain storage of the ancillary documents is done with the help of Interplanetary File System (IPFS), which ensures scalability and efficient storage solutions. We demonstrate how the presented system can speed up the processes of land transactions, improve security, and validate the ownership authenticity using the framework of these decentralized technologies, and we will also solve the main weaknesses, including falsification of records and fraudster activities. The results of this study highlight the disruptive nature of blockchain technology to redefine land registration as a more efficient, transparent, and secure operation, therefore, authorizing property ownership a practice that conventional systems in the past found difficult to achieve.

title: An Ensemble Hybrid Machine Learning Framework for Network Security Enhancement
authors: Pavithra Danaveni, Prashanth Ragam, Ramya Bharathi Chalapaka
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: The primary motive for conducting this research work is to design and analyze the effectiveness of the machine learning approach for the objective of classifying the network traffics as normal (0) or anomaly (1). The proposed work will implement various machine learning algorithms for the purposes of designing the intrusion detection system. The trials will be conducted based on the network traffics to analyze various statistical measures. For achieving accurate results and unbiased assessment, the dataset would be split into 85% for training and remaining 15% for testing. Error- and variance-based metrics include R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>, MSE, RMSE, NRMSE, and MAD, which give insight into the prediction accuracy and stability of model performance. Besides the conventional models, a hybrid XGBoost-Random Forest approach is developed in order to enhance the detection capability by combining the strengths of Boosting with Ensemble Learning. The results clearly showed that the proposed hybrid model yields better performance in prediction compared to individual baseline algorithms. Moreover, it provides higher accuracy and much better adaptability to evolving intrusion patterns. Intrusion detection will be performed with the continuous monitoring of network activity, analyzing traffic behavior by comparing observed patterns against learned representations of normal and anomalous events. In general, these results confirm the effectiveness of machine learning techniques in IDS applications. They also point to a way in which the proposed model may provide a reliable and effective solution for network security problems using an XGBoost-RF model with a high R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> value of approximately 97%.

title: A Comprehensive Survey on Machine Learning and Deep Learning Approaches for Lung Disease Detection
authors: Mukesh Sharma, Vijay Kumar Garg
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: Recent developments in artificial intelligence (AI), specifically machine learning (ML) and deep learning (DL), have had a major impact on the pulmonary disease detection, diagnosis, and prognosis. This survey offers a suitable overview of the state-of-the-art AI-based methods used in the lung disease research, but it is mainly focused on lung cancer. The research review is a systematic review of thirty new studies, differentiating approaches, data modeling, quantification of performance, and explainable methods. Based on the comparative analysis, it is clear that deep learning models and convolutional neural networks in particular are superior in imaging-based detection problems and provide high diagnostic accuracy, whereas ensemble-based and classical ML are more useful in structured clinical and longitudinal data. The analysis based on visualization also proves that the notable portion of the studies focuses on detection and diagnosis, and the prediction of the prognosis, estimation of risks, identification of biomarkers, and modeling of treatment outcomes are under-explored. Other aspects of explainable artificial intelligence (XAI) as SHAP and Grad-CAM are also not widely adopted, yet they are vital to clinical trust and interpretability. As these observations indicate, some of the main gaps in research are outlined in that there is a lack of multimodal data integration, no universal benchmark, no external validation, and difficulty in practical clinical implementation. Lastly, the paper will provide research directions in the future focusing on multimodal learning, federated AI models and privacy-preserving AI models along with standardized evaluation regimens to develop reliable, interpretable and clinically deployable AI solutions to lung disease management and detection.

title: A Secure Affine Frequency Division Multiplexing System for Next-Generation Wireless Communications
authors: Ping Wang, Zulin Wang, Yuanhan Ni, Qu Luo, Yuanfang Ma, Xiaosi Tian, Pei Xiao
abstract: Affine frequency division multiplexing (AFDM) has garnered significant attention due to its superior performance in high-mobility scenarios, as well as multiple waveform parameters that provide greater degrees of freedom for system design. This paper proposes a novel secure affine frequency division multiplexing (SE-AFDM) system, which enhances physical-layer security by dynamically varying an AFDM pre-chirp parameter across subcarriers and over AFDM symbols. In the SE-AFDM system, the pre-chirp parameter is dynamically generated from a codebook controlled by a long-period pseudo-noise (LPPN) sequence. Instead of applying spreading in the data domain, our parameter-domain spreading approach provides additional security while maintaining reliability and high spectral efficiency. We also propose a synchronization framework to solve the problem of reliably and rapidly synchronizing the dynamic parameter in fast time-varying channels. The theoretical derivations prove that unsynchronized eavesdroppers cannot eliminate the nonlinear impact of the time-varying parameter and further provide useful guidance for codebook design. Simulation results demonstrate the security advantages of the proposed SE-AFDM system in high-mobility scenarios, and the hardware prototype validates the effectiveness of the proposed SE-AFDM system and the proposed synchronization framework.

title: Introducing Optimization Techniques into Formal Verification for Faster Convergence and Reduced Runtime
authors: Adepu Saiteja, Mamta Khosla
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: This paper presents a set of optimization techniques aimed at improving convergence speed and reducing runtime in formal verification. Although formal verification is becoming more common in industrial design flows, its adoption is often hindered by inefficient implementation practices and long proof convergence times. To address these challenges, we first introduce a structured and practical verification methodology that guides engineers through the entire verification flow—from property creation and RTL preparation to error analysis and proof closure. This approach is designed to be systematic, repeatable, and suitable for real-world designs. Building on this methodology, the main contribution of this work is a set of optimization techniques developed to accelerate proof convergence and reduce solver complexity. These techniques include targeted adding constraints, parametrizing the modules and reducing parameter size, black boxing the standard modules that adapt during the verification process. Rather than relying on conventional methods, the proposed approach focuses on limiting unnecessary state-space exploration while maintaining proof completeness. Results from representative design examples show that the proposed techniques significantly improve proof success rates and achieve meaningful reductions in overall verification time. Together, these contributions offer a practical and scalable solution that improves the efficiency, reliability, and industrial usability of formal verification.

title: Deep Learning Based Framework for Automated Gray Scale Image Colorization
authors: Shuvrajit Dey, Debajyoti Nath, Swanirbhar Majumder, Sanjib Debnath, Jayanta Pal
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: Colorization of black-and-white images, which was a tiresome process manually done earlier is completely automated today owing to the power of Convolutional Neural Networks(CNNs). This paper introduces a deep learning-based model that can breathe new colors into grayscale images with intelligent predictions about the missing color information. It performs the computation in the LAB color space and produces a colorized image with realistic colors. A GUI-friendly interface has been developed to make this technology easily accessible to everyone. A user only needs to upload grayscale images, click a button, and watch as the model works magic out, producing brilliant colorized versions. Applications for this technology are diverse, ranging from reconstructing old photographs and historical films to improving medical imaging. This approach significantly reduces the time and effort to its maximum usage in different businesses, hence making it a valuable work in general. This indicates deep learning's ability to transform the image processing activities so that the world may become a colorized version of monochrome.

title: Split-Ring Resonator-Based RF Detector for Wireless Communication Applications
authors: Ch. Raghavendra, M. Baladithya, P. Pranav, B. Bharath Reddy
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: This project deals with the design of a small RF detector based on a split-ring resonator (SRR). The idea behind using an SRR is to study a simple wireless sensing method that can be used in practical communication setups. Because of the SRR shape, the structure shows good electromagnetic coupling and a clear resonance response, which helps in detecting RF signals. The detector is designed on a microstrip line, and the SRR dimensions are slightly changed to store more electric field energy in the structure, which in turn improves the sensitivity. Simulation studies and basic analytical calculations are carried out to study and adjust key parameters such as the resonant frequency and resonance response. The simulated results show noticeable shifts in resonance for different RF input conditions. Since the proposed detector is compact, low-cost, and easy to integrate, it shows good potential for applications such as wireless monitoring and spectrum sensing.

title: Towards Comprehensive Semi-Formal Verification of AMBA AHB/APB Interconnects: Configuration Coverage, Mutation Testing, and Safety-Aware Design-for-Verification
authors: Reshav Jaswal, Mamta Khosla
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: Verification dominates effort in modern System-on-Chip (SoC) design, particularly for interconnects such as AMBA AHB and APB bridges. These interconnects are widely used but prone to subtle corner-case failures that escape simulation-only verification. This paper presents ConfigVerMet++, a semi-formal verification methodology tailored to AHB–APB interconnects. ConfigVerMet++ integrates configuration-driven coverage analysis, simulation, formal unreachability classification (UNR), counterexample-directed test generation, mutation-based robustness checks, safety-aware assertions, and design-for-verification (DFV) hooks. We apply the methodology to a representative AHB–APB subsystem implemented in SystemVerilog and show progression from baseline simulation-only functional coverage of about 75% to complete coverage closure at 100%. The paper presents detailed results: assertion proofs, UNR classification, counterexample-driven tests, mutation detection.

title: Defected Ground Structure Antenna for Radio Frequency Identification (RFID) for IoT Applications
authors: Maryam Liaqat, Matheus Henrique Barros da Silva, Maria Luysa Teles Barcelar, Antonio Celso Dantas Antonino, Maklayne Barros de Pontes Santiago, Frederico Dias Nunes
publish: 2026 International Conference on Data Science, Machine Learning, and Intelligence (DataSciMI)
abstract: A low-profile miniaturized DGS Antenna with defected ground is investigated for the high efficiency RFID based IoT applications. The FR4 substrate (ε<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">r</inf> = 4,3 and tanδ = 0.030) of thickness 1.6mm is used for fabrication with overall geometry of 22 X 21 mm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> and ground plane defects which make it suitable for RFID applications. This prototype working at 1.94 GHz with return loss of -30 dB having bandwidth of 40 MHz form 1.92 GHz to 1.96 GHz along minimum VSWR< 1.25 and ARBW<3 dB values. The proposed antenna is in the range of ultra-high frequency (UHF) with DGS design and good agreement with S-parameters, VSWR, ARBW and SAR, demonstrating its suitability for modern wireless communication applications.

title: Investigation of Linearity Performance in p-GaN Gate Enhancement-Mode High Electron Mobility Transistor
authors: Nipun Sharma, Ashish Raman, Pankaj Kumar, Sahil Hamid, Mohit Kumar
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: This work examines the linearity of a p-GaN gate HEMT using TCAD simulation. The p-GaN gate configuration is incorporated to achieve a positive threshold voltage (V<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">th</inf>) needed for enhancement mode operation. The device’s I<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">d</inf>-V<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">gs</inf> characteristics are first analysed to verify the V<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">th</inf>, drain current (I<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">d</inf>) behaviour and transconductance of the device. The device exhibits normally-off behaviour, achieving a positive V<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">th</inf> of 1.15 V and a maximum I<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">d</inf> of 4.5 × 10⁻⁴ A/µm. Linearity performance is then analysed using the fundamental linearity parameters. The impact of drain-to-source voltage (V<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">ds</inf>) on device linearity is examined by determining these characteristics under various bias conditions. Based on our analysis and results, we discovered that the device exhibited optimal linearity. The research indicates that the proposed p-GaN HEMT exhibits remarkable linearity.

title: Interpretable Emotion Detection in Social Media Using Deep Learning and Transformer-Based Models
authors: Shamyla Firdoos, Muhammad Sheraz Ul Hassan, Ali Imran Cheema, Muhammad Ahmed
publish: 2026 International Conference on Data Science, Machine Learning, and Intelligence (DataSciMI)
abstract: The detection of emotions in social media has emerged as an important task to understand opinion of the people, improve human-computer interaction, and assist in healthcare, marketing, and education. This paper explores the use of deep learning and transformer-based models to detect multi-class emotions, focusing on post-hoc interpretability. We used GPT-based data augmentation to balance the distribution of classes using a Kaggle dataset of 34,792 text samples in 8 emotions, increasing the dataset to 64,085 samples after augmentation. Classical RNN models (RNN, LSTM, GRU, BiLSTM, BiGRU) were used as well as transformer models (BERT, ALBERT, RoBERTa, DeRoBERTa, BERTweet, etc.). Findings indicate that despite the high accuracy (0.7655) achieved by BiLSTM among the RNN models, transformer models always performed better with BERTweet being the best performing model (Accuracy = 0.8367, F1 = 0.8376) following augmentation. To introduce transparency, the LIME framework was incorporated, which can be interpreted by emphasizing the words that had an impact on each prediction. Overall, this study contributes by combining performance and interpretability, positioning BERTweet with augmentation and XAI as an effective solution for emotion detection in social media contexts. Future research will be aimed at expanding the research to multilingual and multimodal emotion detection to make the research more applicable to the real world.

title: Designing a Locality-Aware Index for Prefecture-Level Recipe Recommendations
authors: Yiping Ding, Tianyu Wang, Yuanyuan Wang
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Consumer Technology (ICCT-Pacific)
abstract: Japanese food culture is deeply rooted in local regions, yet large online recipe platforms rarely provide explicit geographic information. This paper proposes a text-based Kyodo Index (KI), which uses local dishes (Kyodo) from the Ministry of Agriculture, Forestry and Fisheries (MAFF) “Uchi no Kyodo Ryori” project, railway boxed lunches (Ekiben), and user-generated recipes to construct a 47-dimensional prefectural locality profile for each recipe via Japanese sentence embeddings. First, we build a unified, 12-class dish taxonomy using Rakuten Recipe data. Then, we train a pure text classifier so that the three data sources would share the same main-category labels. Next, we treat Kyodo and Ekiben dishes as anchors, compute KI scores for Rakuten recipes based on textual similarity, and combine heuristic matches between titles and anchor names to generate, for each prefecture and each dish category, a Top-10 list containing up to ten recipes.

title: Bridging the Robustness Gap: Stress-Testing Artificial Intelligence Algorithms
authors: Sergillam B. Oliveira, Eddie de Lima Filho, Lucas C. Cordeiro
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Consumer Technology (ICCT-Pacific)
abstract: The increasing deployment of artificial intelligence (AI) elements in safety-critical systems exposes a major concern: models that prove reliable on clean data can be brittle under real-world perturbations. Besides, that may vary according to different model classes, e.g., supervised and unsupervised ones, which should guide practical applications. In that sense, numerous evaluation techniques do exist, but there is no structured scheme or results that allow for comparing and choosing between different approaches. As a response, we have conducted a study based on a methodology that applies controlled, synthetic perturbations to data, labels, and model parameters, focusing on the experimental evaluation of supervised and unsupervised algorithms. We have stress-tested 10 algorithms across 5 diverse datasets, whose outcome revealed a pronounced dichotomy: unsupervised models may suffer near-structural collapse, whereas supervised models can retain partial functionality due to the anchoring effect of labels. In conclusion, these results suggest systematic stress testing as a prerequisite for reliable and safer AI systems.

title: Exact 1-Bit Precoding for MIMO System by Deep Learning-Based Branching Variable Selection
authors: Seiryu Sugiyama, Shunta Arai, Satoshi Takabe
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Consumer Technology (ICCT-Pacific)
abstract: 1-bit precoding is a promising technique in massive MIMO systems. It addresses the challenge of high power consumption by using low-resolution digital-to-analog converters. However, precoder optimization is a non-convex combinatorial problem, implying that obtaining an optimal solution via branch-and-bound (BnB) is computationally demanding, with efficiency highly dependent on branching rules. To overcome this, we propose a machine learning-based branching variable selection method to accelerate BnB for the 1-bit precoding problem. We design a model combining a graph convolutional network and multi-layer perceptron with feature-wise linear modulation layers to score branching variables efficiently by imitating the vanilla full strong (VFS) rule. Numerical experiments show that our method outperforms VFS and other baselines, achieving fewer explored nodes and the shortest total solving time.

title: State of Power Online Estimation for Series-Parallel Connected Lithium Titanate Battery Packs Considering Cell Inconsistencies and Branch Voltage Consistency Constraints
authors: Sidi Dong, Xuexia Zhang
abstract: Accurate estimation of the state of power (SOP) for series–parallel connected battery packs is essential for ensuring safe and reliable operation. However, most existing works focus on SOP estimation at the battery cell level, while investigations on series–parallel connected battery packs remain limited due to their increased structural complexity. To address this issue, a model-based online SOP estimation method for series–parallel connected lithium titanate battery packs is proposed. First, a centralized online modeling strategy based on the ensemble Kalman filter (EnKF) is developed to explicitly account for cell inconsistencies, in which the branch currents and model parameters of all battery cells are simultaneously identified online. Subsequently, a branch-voltage-oriented systematic SOP estimation algorithm that considers both cell inconsistencies and branch voltage consistency constraints is designed. The algorithm maximizes the pack power while ensuring that all battery cells operate within their safe operating areas and that the voltage consistency among parallel branches is strictly maintained. An integrated online SOP estimation framework is then proposed by combining the EnKF-based centralized model with the designed SOP estimation algorithm. The developed online model is validated under locomotive operating conditions. Experimental results demonstrate that, compared with the unscented Kalman filter (UKF)-based centralized model, the proposed EnKF-based centralized model reduces the root mean square error (RMSE) of voltage estimation by an average of 29.24% and decreases the computational cost by 59.86%, enabling more accurate and efficient tracking of cell voltage dynamics. Furthermore, the proposed framework is applied to online SOP estimation under locomotive conditions, and the effects of cell inconsistencies and branch voltage consistency constraints on SOP are systematically analyzed.

title: High-Frequency Silicon Platelet Feedhorns for LiteBIRD
authors: Sarah A. Stevenson, Jason E. Austermann, James A. Beall, Shannon M. Duff, Nils W. Halverson, Johannes Hubmayr, Gregory C. Jaehnig
abstract: LiteBIRD is a planned space mission to observe the polarized cosmic microwave background in the frequency range 34 GHz to 448 GHz, with the goal of measuring or constraining the tensor-to-scalar ratio r. We have developed silicon platelet feedhorns for LiteBIRD, consisting of stacked metalized silicon wafers with deep-etched holes to create an array of feedhorns with profiled tapers and a coefficient of thermal contraction that is matched to the detector wafer. We present beam pattern measurements for silicon platelet feedhorn prototypes designed for 166-322 GHz, 200-388 GHz, and 356-448 GHz, corresponding to each of the planned LiteBIRD high-frequency pixel types. The measurements were carried out on a room-temperature millimeter-wave vector network analyzer and are in excellent agreement with simulation.

title: Distributed Triangulation Filter for Target Motion Estimation via Bearing-Only Measurements
authors: Yuhan Liu, Pei Chi, Jiang Zhao, Song Jiang, Luning Yang, Xuyang Gao, Yingxun Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Automation Science and Engineering
abstract: This paper introduces the novel consensus-based distributed spatial-temporal coordinated triangulation filter (CD-STCTF), a unified framework designed to overcome the fundamental challenge of achieving reliable and high-precision motion estimation of non-cooperative targets using bearing-only measurements in multi-agent systems. First, our approach centers on the spatial-temporal coordinated triangulation (STCT) model, which optimally integrates historical and spatial bearing information to transform the nonlinear problem into a robust, pseudo-linear state estimation problem. Second, this powerful geometric linearization is embedded within a consensus-on-information-matrix distributed architecture, ensuring robust operation and network consistency across multi-agent systems with sparse communication topologies. Third, a key technical feature is the incorporation of a dynamic measurement noise covariance matrix, which correctly models the distance-dependent uncertainty in angular measurements. Furthermore, we prove the global bounded stability of the CD-STCTF dynamic error. Simulation and real-world experimental results against existing distributed algorithms and the centralized Kalman filter (CKF) benchmark demonstrate that the CD-STCTF achieves superior estimation accuracy, faster convergence rates, and enhanced robustness and is comparable to the CKF, establishing its efficacy for high-performance cooperative bearing-only target motion estimation.

title: Nine-year observation of Global Precipitation Particle Size Distribution and Radar Reflectivity
authors: Qingpeng Wu, Wei Cheng, V. Chandrasekar, Tiantian Yu, Shuangshuang Bian, Hao Wang, Qiangyu Zeng
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Understanding global precipitation microphysics is essential for quantitative precipitation estimation (QPE) and numerical weather prediction (NWP). Using nine years (2015-2023) of the Global Precipitation Measurement (GPM) mission Dual-frequency Precipitation Radar (DPR) observations over the quasi-global domain (67°S-67°N), we characterize precipitation particle size distribution (PSD) properties for rainfall and snowfall. We analyse the mass-weighted mean diameter (Dm), normalized intercept parameter (Nw), radar reflectivity (Z), and dual-frequency ratio (DFR), stratified by land/ocean, convective/stratiform type, altitude, and phase. Rainfall rates are grouped into six classes and snowfall rates into five classes to quantify intensity dependence. To interpret DPR dual-frequency signatures, we develop an observation-constrained forward-scattering framework that adopts DPR-consistent PSD and particle-shape assumptions and performs T-matrix simulations for rain, dry snow, and wet snow with varying liquid-water volume fraction (Fvw), evaluated against nine-year near-surface DFR-Z statistics. Quasi-global PSD peaks (Dm∼1.0 mm; log10Nw∼3.4) closely resemble oceanic precipitation, and convective and stratiform regimes substantially overlap in Dm (0.9-1.8 mm) and log10Nw (3.0-4.0). Vertically, Dm skewness and kurtosis decrease from 2-6 km, with distinct features near ∼10 km and ∼15 km. Rainfall DSDs become more variable at high intensities (>5 mm h-1), whereas snowfall PSDs remain comparatively stable (Dm mainly 1.0-1.5 mm; log10Nw∼3.4). Forward-scattering comparisons show that dry-snow assumptions cannot reproduce the observed DFR-Z space, while increasing Fvw yields close agreement, indicating that near-surface snowfall is consistent with wetted melted particles. These results provide observation-based constraints for improving solid-hydrometeor scattering representations in DPR snowfall retrievals and microphysical parameterizations in NWP.

title: Computationally Efficient SDA-MPC With Capacitor Voltage Balancing for 3P-5L-ANPC Converters
authors: Zhixun Ma, Jiyuan Gong, Haichuan Niu, Xu Zhang, Zhongshu Shao, Xiang Wu
publish: IEEE Journal of Emerging and Selected Topics in Power Electronics
abstract: This paper proposes a computationally efficient sphere decoding algorithm model predictive control (SDA-MPC) method for the three-phase five-level active neutral-point clamped converter. The core of this approach is the dynamic incorporation of the neutral-point potential and the three-phase flying capacitor voltage balancing errors into the calculation of the hypersphere radius and the sphere centroid distance, which effectively achieves precise multi-capacitor voltage balancing. Furthermore, the strategy reconstructs the search tree, which contains redundant switching states, by imposing level transition constraints and sector partitioning constraints, thereby significantly reducing the maximum computational burden of the SDA-MPC. Experimental results fully validate the feasibility and superiority of the proposed method.

title: NOMA-Enabled Joint Sensing and Communication for MISO-Based Non-Terrestrial Networks
authors: Wali Ullah Khan, Jaehyuk Choi, Fahd N Al-Wesabi, Hadeel Alsolai, Xingwang Li
abstract: This letter proposes joint sensing and communication (JSAC) for a high-altitude platform station (HAPS) that serves downlink non-orthogonal multiple access (NOMA) users while simultaneously sensing a ground target. Unlike designs that allocate a dedicated sensing beam, we illuminate the target with the superimposed communication beams and process the echo with a single receive combiner. We formulate a joint transmit–receive design that minimizes the sensing mean-squared error (MSE) under user's minimum rate constraints. The nonconvex problem is addressed by alternating optimization, where the original joint problem is solved in two stages. First, solve the HAPS transmit beamformers problem using semidefinite programming, with the fixed combiner. Then, with the given beamforming, we update the receive combiner in closed form using the linear minimum MSE solution. The proposed NOMA-JSAC solution in HAPS guarantees a monotonic MSE decrease and converges to a stationary solution with low computational complexity. Simulation results demonstrate a clear rate–sensing trade-off versus transmit power, validating the practicality and effectiveness of the proposed NOMA-enabled HAPS JSAC design.

title: Novel Nanocrystalline Alloy Symmetrical Magnetic Focused Eddy Current Array Sensing for Microcrack Detection
authors: Xumei Yang, Qiuping Ma, Bin Gao, Rui Chen, Jiaqi Zhou, Chen Zhu, Yukuan Kang
abstract: Nondestructive detection and characterization of micronear-surface cracks is a critical and challenging task for evaluating material performance. However, existing methods are limited by low sensitivity, poor anti-interference capability, and bad adaptability to complex surfaces. This article proposes a novel eddy current array microcrack detection method based on a symmetrical magnetic focused sensing structure. In this design, a symmetrically distributed magnetic path generates a focused and uniform eddy current region of interest (ROI), significantly enhancing the field intensity for high-sensitivity sensing. This advancement improves detection sensitivity and anti-interference capability for microcracks. Theoretical derivations and simulations validate the effectiveness of the proposed method. In particular, experimental tests conducted on various samples with microcracks demonstrate the reliability and applicability of the approach. The proposed method is capable of detecting artificial crack defects with a width and depth of 0.2 mm at a liftoff of 4 mm, as well as natural weld cracks with minimum detectable dimensions of approximately 5 × 0.07 × 0.07 mm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sup>. Furthermore, the array design can cover the entire convex weld region with a width of 10 mm, enabling single scan imaging of multioriented cracks with dimensions of 10 × 0.1 × 1 mm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sup>. It is applicable to the detection of surface and near-surface cracks in both ferromagnetic and nonferromagnetic materials.

title: Local Verification of Current-State Opacity and Static-State Opacity in Distributed Discrete-Event Systems
authors: Yin Tong, Kai Cai
abstract: The integration of physical processes and cyber-networks in cyber-physical systems (CPSs) has made information security crucial, particularly in preventing unauthorized access and data leakage. Current-state opacity (CSO) in discrete-event systems (DESs) formalizes the security property that an intruder can never infer secret states based on its observation. This paper addresses CSO and its verification in distributed DESs composed of multiple local systems. Existing methods of verifying CSO in distributed DESs either rely on sufficient conditions for local verification or require costly synchronization of local systems and observers. This paper first proposes necessary and sufficient conditions for locally verifying CSO of distributed DESs, reducing the time complexity from <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mathcal {O}(2^{n^{m}})$</tex-math></inline-formula> to <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mathcal {O}(mn)$</tex-math></inline-formula>, where <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$m$</tex-math></inline-formula> is the number of local systems and <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$n$</tex-math></inline-formula> is the largest state number of local systems. We also introduce static-state opacity (SSO), a new information security notion for distributed systems characterizing intruders' incapability to deduce global secrets from local ones. We show that SSO can be verified (necessarily and sufficiently) through a matrix involving CSO of local systems with a time complexity of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mathcal {O}(m2^{n})$</tex-math></inline-formula>. Furthermore, we explore the relationship between CSO and SSO, showing scenarios where they are equivalent.
