title: AI-Driven Endangered Species Detection and Conservation Using Deep Learning
authors: Vadthyavath Nikshitha, Akali Venkata Atchutarao, Ashish Ranjan, Aviral Awasthi
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: The accelerating decline of global biodiversity requires innovative tools that not only monitor wildlife but also educate the public. However, existing identification systems are often inaccessible to non-experts or lack integrated conservation context. This paper proposes a web-based "Endangered Species Detection And Prevention Using Technology" framework that leverages the YOLO (You Only Look Once) deep learning algorithm for robust object detection. The system architecture utilizes a React.js frontend for user interaction and a Flask-based backend to handle inference pipelines. Upon processing user-uploaded images, the model identifies species and dynamically maps detections to a database of conservation metrics, including IUCN Red List categories, habitat descriptions, and population threats. Experimental results shows that the integrated model perform a detection mAp of 87.6% successfully identifying endangered species across varied environments. The system effectively combines real-time computer vision with educational retrieval, providing users with immediate, actionable biodiversity knowledge. This study confirms that integrating lightweight deep learning models with modern web technologies offers a scalable solution for environmental awareness

title: Performance Trade-Offs in Dynamic Comparator Architectures: Offset, Speed, and Energy Perspectives
authors: Akrosh Vikram Shukla, Vimla Bharti, Dhandapani Vaithiyanathan
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: Dynamic comparators play a crucial role in modern high-speed and low-power analog-to-digital converters (ADCs), where parameters such as offset voltage, propagation delay, energy consumption, and noise significantly influence the achievable resolution and sampling performance of the overall system. This work presents a comparative study of various dynamic comparator architectures in wide usage, such as the conventional dynamic comparator, double-tail comparator, enhanced StrongARM with MOS capacitors, MOSCAP-free enhanced StrongARM, and an enhanced StrongARM architecture with reduced tail capacitance. The circuits were implemented on 180nm technology scale with the clock frequency of 1GHz. Comparison is carried out based on the basis of input-referred offset voltage, decision delay, power and energy consumption, slew rate, and metastability behaviour. Architectural trade-offs among gain before regeneration, tail-node dynamics, and charge redistribution are analysed to explain performance variations across designs. Results show that conventional StrongARM comparators offer fast regeneration with minimal complexity. However, enhanced architectures significantly improve offset, noise robustness, and energy efficiency, while reduced tail-capacitance designs offer the best balance between speed and power. This comparative study gives clear design insights into choosing dynamic comparator architectures for low-voltage, high-speed ADC applications.

title: Design and Implementation of a Area Efficient Logic BIST Architecture for 4-bit ALU using Verilog and Cadence Genus
authors: Akash Kumar Prajapati, Bodile Roshan Mukindrao, Suraj Prajapati, Rohit Singh
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: This paper represents the design and the implementation of area efficient Logic Built-in Self-Test (LBIST) architecture for a 4-bit Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU) using Verilog and Cadence Genus. To minimize the hardware overhead, the proposed method uses only two small components like a Linear Feedback Shift Register (LFSR) for pseudo-random test pattern generation and a Multiple Input Signature Register (MISR) for output response compression. Testing activation and pattern generation are controlled externally through a simple mode-select signals which eliminates the need for a control FSM and reduces the additional logic block. The LBIST enable ALU was synthesized under area optimization constraints, and the results show that the inclusion of LFSR and MISR introduces only the minimal area overhead while maintaining correct functionality and reliable faulty detection. The final MISR signature effectively distinguish fault free and faulty behavior, demonstrating that an efficient and compact self-test mechanism can be achieved without a BIST controller. This approach is well-suited for small data paths unit, educational testability demonstrations, and the low-power SoCs modules where area is a primary constraints of the blocks.

title: Abstract vs. Layman Summary Classification Using Machine Learning and Transformer Models
authors: Banala Saritha, Arundhuti Sengupta, G Purnachandrarao, Sainadh Singh Kshatri, B Girirajan, Vasu Koduri
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: Scientific articles now often have several summaries aimed at different types of audience, most often expert summaries and simplified layman summaries. Automatically identifying these types of summary is crucial for advances in improving science communication, content organization and information retrieval systems. This study consists of the comparative evaluation of traditional embedding-based models of machine learning and transformers-based architectures for the task of abstract versus layman summary classification. A task of supervised learning approach is followed using balanced dataset of 1,000 scientific summaries; one-half abstract and one half of layman summaries. The dataset is separated into training, validation, and test groups so that a sound evaluation of model performance could be conducted. Traditional machine learning techniques of Word2Vec and GloVe word embedding with Logistic Regression are used as baseline models, and BERT and RoBert are fine-tuned end-to-end in order to use contextualized language representations. The models performance is measured based on traditional execution measures that are accuracy, precision, recall, and F1 -score. Experimental results show that it can correctly extract fundamental semantics differences between summary types with an accuracy rate of about 86%, which can be regarded as the effectiveness of embedding-based models. However, we can prominently see the power of transformer based models compared with the traditional ones, indicating the importance of context understanding in audience awareness text classification. BERT is accurate to 90% whereas RoBERTa gives the finest performance all around 93% accuracy as well as balanced values of precision, recall, and F1 score. The results validate the idea of using contextualized representations of the transformer to understand why they're better than static embeddings when it comes to distinguishing between expert-oriented and layman summaries. This work makes RoBERTa the most reliable model among the models under evaluation and highlights the potential of the transformer-based approaches in improving automated science communication and content classification systems.

title: Passenger Flow and Dwell Behavior Analysis in Station Plazas Using Vision-Based Deep Learning
authors: Chun-Chen Chou, Yui Onishi, Miyu Matsuoka, Masanobu Kii, Satoru Sugita
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Consumer Technology (ICCT-Pacific)
abstract: Station plazas function as key nodes for the convergence and dispersion of passengers in stations. Beyond passenger flow management, they offer significant commercial potential, especially in Japan's transit-oriented developments. While previous studies using vision-based AI have focused on bottleneck detection and crowd control, dwell behavior analyses have relied mostly on subjective methods like surveys and observations. This study introduces a framework using visionbased deep learning to analyze passenger flow and dwell behaviors. Utilizing YOLO and BoT-SORT, it maps behavioral patterns across spatial mesh units, capturing variations in movement and dwell time. The study was conducted at Osaka Umeda Station plazas, a major interchange with a symmetrical layout but distinct behavioral differences across zones. The findings reveal clear spatial and temporal patterns in user behavior, offering actionable insights for station operators. This research underscores how AI-based behavioral analytics can support intelligent space management in transit hubs and contribute to data-driven, human-centered smart city initiatives.

title: Bridging the Scale Gap: High-Resolution Mapping of Seagrass Biomass and Sediment Carbon Using UAV Imagery and Random Forest Classification
authors: Xuan Truong Trinh, Masahiko Nagai
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Consumer Technology (ICCT-Pacific)
abstract: Seagrass meadows are globally significant “blue carbon” sinks, yet they remain poorly quantified in Southeast Asia, particularly in Vietnam where nearly 50% of the national extent has been lost in recent decades. This study addresses a critical data gap by providing the first robust, spatially explicit assessment of seagrass biomass and sediment organic carbon stocks in the coastal waters of Phu Quoc Island. Adopting a multi-scale approach, we integrated ultra-high-resolution Unmanned Aerial Vehicle (UAV) imagery with standardized field sampling and laboratory analysis following IPCC wetlands guidelines. UAV orthomosaics were processed using Structure-from-Motion (SfM) photogrammetry and classified via a Random Forest algorithm, achieving an overall accuracy of 83.75% (Kappa =0.72). These high-resolution habitat maps were subsequently downscaled to integrate with broader satellite grids for carbon stock estimation. Our results reveal a strong positive gradient between seagrass density and carbon storage. The mean total organic carbon (TOC) tock across the study area was 159.1 ± 61.6 MgC ha<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">−1</sup>, with biomass pools (117.3 MgC ha<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">−1</sup>) contributing nearly three times more than sediment reserves. Notably, dense seagrass meadows were identified as significant carbon hotspots, storing up to 3 2 8. 6 MgC ha - i - m o r e than seven times the capacity of bare sediments. This research establishes a fundamental scientific baseline for blue carbon accounting in Phu Quoc, validating a scalable framework for integrating drone technology with field inventories. The findings provide essential data to support Vietnam's conservation policies and commitments to the United Nations Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs 13 and 14), facilitating the future inclusion of these ecosystems in national carbon finance mechanisms.

title: Trend-Aware Proactive Defense Using Dynamic Antibody Deployment and Updates
authors: Masato Okawa, Shingo Yamaguchi
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Consumer Technology (ICCT-Pacific)
abstract: In recent years, with the spread of IoT devices, the threat posed by botnets such as Mirai has expanded. Conventional immune-based botnet defense systems (iBDS) are reactive systems that inject antibodies after infection is detected, and the problem was that infection spread due to delays in initial response. In this research, we propose a preventive defense method that dynamically deploys and updates antibodies according to attack trends. The proposed method is characterized by dynamically placing an optimal set of antibodies calculated based on attack trends on the network even before the threat becomes apparent. Deployed antibodies have a set lifespan, and their composition is updated in line with attack trends and the passage of time. This prevents unnecessary resource consumption while ensuring that the system is always ready to respond to the latest threats. As a result of evaluation experiments using simulations, we confirmed that the proposed method can significantly reduce the maximum number of infections by bad worms and shorten the time to complete eradication by 50% compared to the conventional post-injection method. These results demonstrate that a preventive approach that involves advance deployment and dynamic updating of antibodies is extremely effective in a dynamically changing attack environment.

title: Optimizing Human-Machine Interaction in BDA-AI Systems
authors: Chia-Hung Hsieh, Yu-Chun Ma, Asif Khan
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Consumer Technology (ICCT-Pacific)
abstract: Despite growing scholarly interest in Big Data Analytics and Artificial Intelligence (BDA-AI) systems, empirical research lacks standardized measurement tools to assess their environmental impact. This research addresses the gap by developing an instrument that examines the influence of BDA-AI on eco-innovation (EIN) and ecological performance (EP). Building on the Technology-Organization-Environment (TOE) framework, this study introduces employee digital skills (EDS) and top management support (TMS) as key determinants of technology adoption. Following an extensive review of the literature from 2015 to 2025, a theoretical framework was constructed that encompasses six hypothesized relationships. The proposed measurement instrument comprises 37 items that measure BDA-AI, TOE framework, EP, EIN, TMS, and EDS. This research advances the environmental sustainability and technology management literature by offering researchers a robust tool for investigating how BDA-AI shapes organizational environmental practices.

title: Performance Fairness in Hybrid Memory Architecture
authors: Hsung-Pin Chang, Qing-Xuan Su
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Consumer Technology (ICCT-Pacific)
abstract: In the cloud environment, the memory cache are shared between users to improve resource utilization, but it also leads to unfair performance due to competition for the memory cache. To address this issue, this paper proposes a memory allocation scheme to achieve performance fairness in DRAM- and NVM-based hybrid memory architecture. To copy with the dynamic access characteristics of each application and the diverse access performance of all user applications, we dynamically adjust the size of the DRAM and NVM cache partitions regularly. This approach not only ensures performance fairness but also improves overall system performance.

title: Detecting Events and Groups at a Campus by Using WLAN Connection Log
authors: Ryoya Makida, Hinako Baba, Teruaki Kitasuka, Toru Nakanishi, Zhuotao Lian
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Consumer Technology (ICCT-Pacific)
abstract: By detecting events occurring in specific areas, benefits can be provided to people who use or stay near those areas. In this study, event detection on the Hiroshima University campus is conducted using wireless LAN access logs. From data aggregated at regular intervals, five methods Z-Score, MedAD, IQR, Prophet, and Isolation Forest are used to identify anomalies. As a result, most of these methods were able to detect both the events that were expected to occur and those that were not anticipated. In addition, the study analyzes specific connection patterns to estimate groups of users who are likely attending the same lectures that is, groups that are presumed to belong to the same faculty. While the estimation was successful for in-person lectures, it was not possible for classes that were not conducted face-to-face.

title: Designing of an AHB Protocol Using Single Master, Multiple Slaves and Power Optimization Using Clock Gating
authors: Tanya Singh, Neetu Sood
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: The interconnection of buses in a computer system has a significant impact on its implementation. The complexity of the system buses will affect the transfer of data and instructions between the processor and memory or between peripheral devices and memory. The Advanced Microcontroller Bus Architecture (AMBA) offers a wide standard for connecting and controlling multiple functional blocks in SoCs to overcome these issues. The AHB architecture ensures that the system is designed properly with the multi-processors, controllers and peripherals implemented in it.The motive of this paper is to offer a system-on-chip design summary and an RTL view of the AMBA AHB module. Since, AHB is one of the important protocol in the AMBA family. It is the backbone bus of the system because of its high performance and high-clock system modules. It allows high power peripheral devices to be directly connected to CPUs, on chip memories, and external off-chip memory interfaces. The paper emphasizes the Verilog-based AHB design and implementation, with the Xilinx simulator used for read/write operations.

title: Design of a Modulated Metasurface Reflector for Anomalous Reflection
authors: Ratna Priya, Anjali Kumari, Gaurav Sharma, Rahul Sharma, Neha Ahuja, Jagannath Malik
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: This paper introduces a modulated metasurface design to achieve anomalous reflection where unit cell consists of circular patch with alternate patch having slot to provide capacitive discontinuity and suppress surface waves resulting in a non-uniform design, on a circular substrate and ground. Horn antenna is used to incident normal waves and metasurface is working on far field region over a frequency range of 4.68 GHz to 5.32 GHz. The design is simulated, optimized in CST studio suite which validates strong directivity beam steering of 13 ° at 5 GHz and narrow beam width giving better results than conventional metasurface by tuning phase, amplitude and polarization. The proposed design can be used in beam steering metasurface and high gain reflector designs.

title: Design and Implementation of an FSM-Based DDR Memory Controller on FPGA
authors: Simran, Khushi Gupta, Kunal Gupta, Nitesh Kashyap
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: Double Data Rate (DDR) memory is widely used in modern digital systems due to its high bandwidth and efficient data transfer capability. However, reliable communication with DDR SDRAM requires strict adherence to timing constraints, refresh mechanisms, and command sequencing. This paper presents the design and FPGA implementation of an FSM-based DDR memory controller capable of handling read/write operations, refresh cycles, and DDR timing requirements. The controller translates processor-level requests into DDR commands such as ACTIVATE, READ, WRITE, PRECHARGE, and REFRESH while maintaining JEDEC-compliant timing parameters. The design was synthesized and validated on an FPGA platform using Xilinx Vivado, demonstrating correct and stable operation without timing violations.

title: A Novel Chaotic Log-Tangent Map with Bit-Level Permutation and Diffusion for Secure Image Encryption
authors: Prasun Sarkar, Mohit Dua
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: The rapid development of digital imaging applications has created an ever-increasing demand for security mechanisms that could resist attacks using modern cryptanalytic techniques. This paper proposes an image encryption approach utilizing a Log–Tangent chaotic map that is designed as a two-dimensional nonlinear system in order to introduce a high sensitivity with respect to initial states and control parameters. The whole bit-level [9] strategy adopted by the proposed cipher combines a permutation stage, aimed at rearranging bit-plane positions, with a proper diffusion process sustained by a chaos-derived keystream. The integration of both operations into a single chaotic domain ensures a high degree of pixel randomness and structural disruption. In order to investigate the dynamical properties of the Log–Tangent map, Lyapunov exponent profiles, Shannon entropy measurements, and bifurcation characteristics are conducted. These analyses show that the system has extensive chaotic intervals and produces statistically uniform sequences, which are adequate for cryptographic applications. Experimental results on images indicate that the cipher produces encrypted outputs with negligible correlation among neighboring pixels and high entropy values, and visual indistinguishability from noise. The proposed scheme is resilient to brute-force attacks because of its large key space and its sensitivity regarding the variation of the parameters. The work demonstrates that the Log–Tangent chaotic map with bit-level permutation and diffusion forms an efficient, lightweight methodology for image data protection in security-sensitive applications.

title: CLO-Driven Personalized Remediation Framework for Post-Requisite Course Success in Higher Education
authors: Wagdi Alrawagfeh, Robert Ford, Abdulhalim Dandoush
publish: 2025 35th International Conference on Computer Theory and Applications (ICCTA)
abstract: This paper introduces and implements a novel approach for a smart course enrolling system that generates remedial courses within higher education, aiming to address gaps in student learning associated with course learning outcomes (CLOs) and post-requisite courses. While most existing research on remedial education concentrates on preparing high school students for college by addressing issues such as educational inequality, student preparedness, limited attention has been given to remedial interventions through the higher education journey of students. In higer institutions, students may successfully complete courses without fully mastering all their CLOs, resulting in knowledge deficiencies that limit their performance in subsequent advanced courses. Such gaps can also foster a false sense of achievement, thereby undermining students' academic progression and the value of institutional credentials. To mitigate these challenges, this study proposes smart remedial courses that leverage technology to deliver targeted, personalized materials for specific learning deficiencies. Enrolling in advanced courses will be conditionned by passing self-evaluations (e.g., quizzes) simplifying the integration of the approach into the used ERP and avoiding a new load on the academic faculty. The approach seeks to strengthen student success, support academic progression, and enhance the overall quality of higher education. This work represents a significant step toward integrating intelligent, technology-driven solutions for personalized remedial learning at the university level.

title: IEEE Transactions on Biomedical Engineering Information for Authors
authors: N/A

title: Keynote
authors: Hironori Washizaki.
publish: 2025 35th International Conference on Computer Theory and Applications (ICCTA)
abstract: Research and Contribution: Hironori's research interests include systems and software engineering. He received his Ph.D. in information andcomputer science from Waseda University in 2003. He has published more than 120 research papers in refereed international journals and conferences, including TETC, IoT-J, EMSE, SCICO, ICSE and ASE. He has led many academia-industry joint research and large-funded projects in software design, traceability, and quality assurance. Since 2015, he has been the Convener of ISO/IEC/JTC1 SC7/WG20 to standardize bodies of knowledge and professional certifications. Since 2019, he has been Steering Committee Member of APSEC.

title: Curved Surface Strain Transfer Model for Flexible Piezoresistive Sensors Based on Arc-Length Mismatch
authors: Yuan Jin, Chenguang Guo, Tong Jia, Chao Feng, Haitao Yue, Weibing Dai, Jiayun Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: Flexible piezoresistive sensors are well suited for strain monitoring on non-planar structures, but dedicated strain transfer models for curved-surface flexible piezoresistive sensors remain scarce. In this work, a curved-surface strain transfer model for a PDMS/CB/MWCNTs composite flexible piezoresistive sensor was established based on the shear-lag framework with explicit incorporation of the curvature-induced arc-length mismatch. An effective characteristic length is further introduced to describe the saturation behavior between sensing-layer length and average strain transfer efficiency under a given curvature. Experiments show that the average strain transfer efficiency increases with sensing-layer length and gradually approaches saturation, whereas it decreases with increasing curvature. After calibration of the curvature correction term by nonlinear least-squares fitting, the modified model agrees well with the measurements, with a maximum deviation of about 3% within the investigated range. In addition, comparison with a simplified planar reference model shows that explicitly considering the curvature-induced arc-length mismatch improves prediction accuracy under curved-surface conditions. The proposed model provides a quantitative basis for strain-transfer evaluation and sensing-layer length design of flexible piezoresistive sensors bonded to curved structures.

title: IEEE Engineering in Medicine and Biology Society Publication Information
authors: N/A

title: On-Access Error Correction in Certain Types of Content-Addressable Memories
authors: Ron M. Roth, Giacomo Pedretti
abstract: summing content-addressable memory (Σ-CAM) is a device which consists of an ℓ × n array of cells, where each cell can be programmed to one of two states, 0 or 1. The input to the array is a binary ℓ-vector, and the output is an integer n-vector whose entries are the Hamming distances between the input vector and the contents of the cells along each of the n columns in the array. An ordinary binary CAM can be seen as a quantized Σ-CAM where the output at each column is just an indicator whether the Hamming distance is 0 (full match) or not. A summing ternary CAM (Σ-TCAM) is a variant of a Σ-CAM where the state/input alphabet is endowed with a third symbol (“don’t care”) whose Hamming distance from any symbol is defined to be 0. In this work, several coding schemes are presented for on-access correction of errors in both Σ-CAMs and Σ-TCAMs. All schemes entail allocating several columns in the array for redundancy such that, when an input vector is applied to the array, errors in the output vector can be corrected, provided that their number (measured by either the Hamming metric or the L1-metric) does not exceed a prescribed value. In the case of Σ-CAMs, the operation of the array resembles that of a discrete vector–matrix multiplier; therefore, the presented error-correction schemes for Σ-CAMs build upon schemes that have been proposed for such multipliers. The adaptation of such schemes to Σ-TCAMs, however, is more involved due to the don’t care symbol: the schemes in this case make use of a special type of positional binary representation of integer pairs where the representations of the two integers in a pair do not share a 1 in the same position.

title: Common-Mode Noise Analysis and Reduction in High-Gain Quadratic Boost Converter Topologies
authors: Prabhat Kumar, Mummadi Veerachary
abstract: Electromagnetic interference (EMI) associated with high frequency switching of quadratic boost DC-DC converters is the major issue that need to be addressed. This EMI generation (and common-mode (CM) noise) is essentially due to the existence of multiple high ‘dv/dt’ switching nodes and parasitic coupling paths which needs compensation/investigation. For this, three different quadratic boost converter (QBC) topologies (Type-1 to Type-3) are critically examined and identified that the QBC-Type-3 is more vulnerable from EMI point of view. Through extensive investigations, circuits structure dependent dominant parasitic capacitances (CM-noise generator) are identified and then a CM-equivalent circuit is formulated. The parasitic capacitance cancellation technique is adopted to minimize their effect. In which the coupled-inductor arranged in the tapped-inductor form where the cancellation capacitor is connected between the coupled-inductor ‘tap’ and chassis ground. On the basis of CM-equivalent circuit a detailed mathematical analysis is formulated and then noise-characterizing impedance transfer functions (NCITF) are established. With the aim to suppress the high frequency noise, an additional external compensation capacitor (ECC) is introduced in between the heat-sink and chassis ground. Its insertion modulates the NCITF enabling effective suppression of CM noise. Analytical modelling, validated through CISPR-compliant measurement setup on a 100 W prototype, demonstrates about 15 ∼ 20 dB reduction in the low-frequency band (150 kHz–1 MHz) and a substantial attenuation across the high-frequency region. The results confirm the effectiveness of CM suppression in the QBC topologies and it can easily be extendable for higher-order boost topologies composed with at least one inductive element.

title: A Gait Deviation Correction Method for Stable Walking With a Wearable Self-Balancing Exoskeleton Robot: A Technical Case Report
authors: Shisheng Zhang, Jinke Li, Wentao Li, Xihao Wang, Yongxuan Hong, Feng Li, Xinyu Wu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Neural Systems and Rehabilitation Engineering
abstract: The study reveals that neglecting the yaw-direction rotational friction moment of the support foot in a self-balancing lower-limb exoskeleton (SBLLER) causes gait trajectory deviation. However, conventional momentum compensation strategies are unsuitable for such waist-less exoskeletons designed for motor dysfunction patients. Therefore, this paper proposes a multi-level stable walking control method to reduce the yaw axis deviation and ensure the stability of the gait during the walking of the human-exoskeleton system. Firstly, a car-table based model predictive control (MPC) method is used to generate a basically stable gait trajectory; secondly, the angular momentum controllers are designed as a supplement to MPC to improve the stability of the human-exoskeleton system in the pitch and yaw directions; then, comparative experiments are conducted to verify the advantages and disadvantages of the multi-level stable walking control method in walking on flat ground and carrying users with different body feature parameters. The proposed method was tested in an exploration experiment on four healthy subjects and one stroke patient using the AutoLEE-G3 SBLLER. The results showed that the method was feasible and effective for reducing yaw axis deviations during locomotion, improving the stability of the system, and achieving robust stable walking under the specific dynamic load conditions evaluated in this technical case report.

title: Enhanced Hybrid Virtual Impedance Droop Control for Fuel Cell-Supercapacitor Hybrid Power Supply Systems Considering Line Impedances
authors: Qitong Jia, Jiawei Chen, Xinke Zhu, Wentao Wu, Qingchao Song
publish: IEEE Journal of Emerging and Selected Topics in Power Electronics
abstract: Hybrid power supply systems (HPSSs), which leverage the advantages of each source, are becoming increasingly common in transportation electrification systems. To allocate the wide-frequency variation load power among the power sources in a reliable manner, the hybrid virtual impedance droop (HVID) control strategy, which is implemented in a decentralized way, is typically adopted for HPSSs. However, due to the presence of unknown and time-varying line impedances, the HPSS with conventional HVID control may experience DC-bus voltage drops in steady states and compromised power performances in both dynamic and static conditions. To address these challenges, this paper proposes an enhanced HVID control strategy designed to adaptively eliminate the adverse effects of unpredictable line impedances without the need for complex real-time impedance measurement or observation. By integrating a DC-bus drop voltage compensation loop with a newly developed output power-sharing compensation loop, the proposed strategy can effectively capture line impedance information and adaptively offset the power allocation mismatch caused by impedance through a closed-loop mechanism. The operational principle of the proposed control strategy is detailed, followed by the construction of a 4-kW test rig comprising two fuel cells and two supercapacitor units in the lab. Experimental results verify the effectiveness of the proposed strategy, demonstrating that under heavy load conditions, the steady-state current deviation between identical fuel cells is reduced to within 3% of that in the conventional strategy, and the DC-bus voltage drop is controlled within 1%.

title: Equality cases in the Anantharam–Jog–Nair inequality
authors: Efe Aras, Thomas A. Courtade, Albert Zhang
abstract: Anantharam, Jog, and Nair recently unified the Shannon–Stam inequality and the entropic form of the Brascamp–Lieb inequalities under a common inequality. They left open the problems of extremizability and characterization of extremizers. Both questions are resolved in the present paper. Separately, we show that the Anantharam–Jog–Nair inequality may be derived as a corollary of the Brascamp–Lieb inequalities, establishing a formal equivalence between the two.

title: From Emotion to Depression: How to leverage Emotional Representations for Multimodal Depression Diagnosis?
authors: Junjie Liang, Peng Cao, Jinzhu Yang, Xiuyi Fan, Wenju Yang, Fei Wang, Osmar R. Zaiane
abstract: Existing depression diagnosis methods attempt to understand depression via general emotional representations learning and have achieved notable progress. However, these methods still remain limited in two key aspects: 1) coarse-grained emotional representation: these methods often struggle to capture fine-grained multimodal emotional cues; 2) representation gap: general emotional knowledge alone is insufficient to fully account for the complex and multifaceted depression symptom. To address these limitations, we propose a hierarchical multimodal knowledge injection framework named DEmopression, comprising multimodal emotion knowledge injection and depression knowledge injection stages that integrate fine-grained emotion representations with specific depression knowledge for multimodal depression recognition. In the multimodal emotion knowledge injection stage, we leverage a multimodal large language model to generate fine-grained emotional prior knowledge and integrate it into the multimodal representation learning process through text-bridge contrastive learning. In the depression knowledge injection stage, we construct a depression knowledge graph and employ a cross-attention injection module that enables the DEmopression to adaptively perceive relevant depressive symptoms, thereby bridging the representation gap between emotion understanding and depression cognition. Experimental evaluations on multiple public datasets demonstrate that DEmopression achieves state-of-the-art performance in depression diagnosis. In particular, compared with previous leading approaches, DEmopression reduces the MAE by 6.4% and the RMSE by 2.8% on the AVEC2014 dataset. Moreover, this study provides a comprehensive and in-depth analysis of DEmopression, examining its capability to perceive individual-level depressive symptoms and offering valuable insights for future research on depression diagnosis. Our code will be released after acceptance.

title: Cyber-Physical Coordinated Recovery of Power Grids Using UAV-Assisted Emergency Networking
authors: Yunpeng Sun, Zhichao Shi, Tao Zhang, Xucheng Huang, Kaiwen Li
publish: IEEE Transactions on Industrial Cyber-Physical Systems
abstract: Extreme disasters cause simultaneous damage to the cyber and physical layers of power grids, leading to widespread observability and controllability loss. Existing research suffers from insufficient adaptability to dynamic damage, idealized communication models that neglect backhaul link reliability, and computational inefficiencies hindering rapid response. To address these challenges, this paper proposes a Hybrid RL-Heuristic Bilevel Framework (HRHBF) for cyber-physical coordinated recovery (CPCR). A key innovation is the explicit modeling of a UAV-assisted SAGIN backhaul link with continuous coverage constraints to ensure end-to-end communication reliability. The bilevel structure employs Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) to adaptively learn UAV positioning while an inner layer solves for load restoration, enabling fast, joint optimization. Further more, an LSTM-based policy network enhances generalization across unknown disaster scenarios. Experiments on IEEE 33 bus and 118-bus systems demonstrate that HRHBF achieves a 100% communication success rate, significantly outperforms benchmarks in load recovery and QoS, and makes decisions in seconds. These results validate the framework's effectiveness in providing a reliable, efficient, and rapid solution to enhance post disaster power system resilience.

title: Design of a Visual-Tactile Sensor with Double-Layer Reflective Film and Optimized Illumination for Texture Recognition
authors: Shixin Wang, Ling Weng, Lanyang Hao, Yuheng Wang
abstract: Aiming at the problems of complex preparation process of the existing visual-tactile sensor's reflective film, uneven illumination and shortened service life, this paper presents a novel visual-tactile sensor that adopts a double-layer reflective film structure and an optimized optical illumination system. In the sensor structure design, the first reflective layer is formed by physically adsorbing 400-mesh copper powder to construct a highly sensitive reflective surface; the second reflective layer is formed by spraying gray silicone ink to create a flexible protective coating, effectively enhancing the sensor's durability and consistency. In terms of optical system optimization, uniform film and isolation ring are introduced to eliminate the spot effect and glare caused by the LED point light source, improving the uniformity of illumination. Based on the photometric stereo principle, a lookup table mapping relationship between the RGB three-channel pixel intensity and surface gradient is established, and the three-dimensional reconstruction of the contact surface is achieved by solving the Poisson equation. The experimental results show that the spatial resolution of this sensor reaches the 100 μm level, and the average absolute error of three-dimensional reconstruction is 0.0412 mm, and it demonstrates excellent durability in 8,000 cycles of testing. When this sensor is combined with the YOLOv11 algorithm and applied to pose recognition and texture recognition tasks, the recognition accuracy rates reach 98.67% and 97.4% respectively. The ablation experiments show that the double-layer reflective film, the uniform film, the isolation ring, and the LUT resolution optimization all play significant roles in improving the system performance. Among them, the uniform film has the most significant impact on the reconstruction accuracy, while the double-layer reflective film is the most crucial for enhancing the long-term stability. The comparison results of multiple networks further indicate that the performance improvement mainly stems from the hardware design of the sensor and the improvement of imaging quality, rather than relying on a specific deep learning model, verifying its application potential in robotic precision manipulation.

title: Evaluation of a Multi-Link Machine Learning Approach for Rainfall Detection Using Satellite Microwave Links
authors: Mostafa Ftouni, Margherita Spalla, Matteo Colli, Andrea Randazzo, Christian Gianoglio
publish: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Letters
abstract: The impacts of climate change have increased the need for reliable real-time rainfall monitoring systems. This study investigates rainfall detection using a network of opportunistic sensors based on satellite microwave links (SMLs) combined with machine learning (ML) classifiers. Two models, a random forest (RF) and a convolutional neural network (CNN), were trained on aggregated SML data, including information from neighboring links. The results demonstrate that leveraging information from multiple links substantially enhances the detection of both wet and dry periods compared to single-link approaches. Moreover, the RF model consistently provides a more stable and reliable trade-off than CNN, improving rainfall detection and reducing misclassifications overall, with particularly strong performance during harsh weather conditions. The study spanned up to 32 rainy days between 2021 and 2024.

title: CSR-YOLO: A deep learning approach for optical lens defect detection in microscopic vision
authors: Xiande Bao, Kiyoshi Takamasu, Meiyun Chen
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: Accurate and efficient measurement of optical lens surface defects is challenging due to their subtle appearance, irregular morphology, and scale variability. This paper presents CSR-YOLO, a lightweight detection framework designed for optical lens surface defect inspection in industrial measurement systems. The framework employs a Channel-Spatial Self-Correction Attention Fusion Block (CSSCAFusionBlock), which integrates channel attention, spatial attention, and a global context–based self-correction mechanism to adaptively recalibrate defect features and improve measurement robustness across multiple scales. A lightweight Shuffle Upsample (ShuffleUp) module is further incorporated, combining depthwise convolution, channel shuffle, and pointwise convolution to efficiently enhance feature resolution while preserving fine-grained defect details with low computational overhead. In addition, the WIoU loss function is utilized to refine bounding box regression by dynamically emphasizing hard samples, thereby improving localization accuracy for irregular defects. Experimental results demonstrate that CSR-YOLO achieves reliable inspection performance on an optical lens surface defect dataset, with an mAP50 of 0.924, an mAP50-95 of 0.677, and a processing speed of 163 FPS, satisfying the requirements of real-time industrial optical lens surface inspection and measurement.

title: Enhanced Waterline Extraction from Airborne LiDAR Data using Automated Density-Based Clustering
authors: Hieu Trung Kieu, Dawn Sok Cheng Pang, Adrian Wing-Keung Law
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Monitoring shoreline evolution over time is important for assessing sediment transport around coastal areas, predicting erosion, and designing coastal protection strategies. Airborne Light Detection and Ranging (LiDAR) provides high-resolution and elevation-rich data that can enable accurate shoreline monitoring under varying hydrodynamic conditions and with wide spatial coverage, compared to traditional ground-based shoreline monitoring methods. While recent advances in UAV technology have expanded data accessibility, the extraction of shorelines from point cloud data remains constrained by the complex discrimination of land-water interfaces and a reliance on empirical parameter tuning. Consequently, efforts to develop standardized, physically interpretable extraction methods adaptable to diverse coastal settings remain limited. In this study, we built on previous works to develop a robust, automated algorithm for extracting statistical waterlines (SWL) from airborne LiDAR point clouds. We integrate a novel Automated Density Clustering (ADC) approach that systematically identifies optimal clustering parameter based on point cloud density distribution, removing the need for site-specific manual calibration. For development, UAV-LiDAR surveys were conducted to acquire point cloud datasets for a sandy beach in Singapore. The algorithm was optimized using four datasets from localized coastal sites along the beach and then validated using other datasets obtained from the surveys. Further validation was also conducted using the optimized parameters for two point-cloud datasets of Duders Beach, New Zealand, where the algorithm achieves sub-meter SWL extraction accuracy. Finally, a multi temporal case study at Balcomie Sands, Scotland, was carried out to demonstrate the algorithm's applicability and consistency for monitoring intertidal zones under diverse coastal conditions. Long-term shoreline analysis, hazard assessment, and coastal management can now be performed by using the algorithm on high-resolution airborne LiDAR data.

title: Risk Assessment of Power System Cascading Failures Based on Sequential Monte Carlo Simulation and Fuzzy Clustering Analysis
authors: Hui Huang, Keyu Chen, An Chen, Shuang Fu, Linlin Li, Jianli Ling
abstract: Cascading failures in power systems represent a significant threat to grid reliability and can lead to widespread blackouts. Traditional deterministic methods often fall short in capturing the inherent uncertainties and dynamic progression of such events. This paper proposes a comprehensive risk assessment framework that integrates Sequential Monte Carlo Simulation (SMCS) with fuzzy clustering techniques. The approach begins by developing a multi-stage probabilistic model that accounts for the stochastic nature of fault initiation, location, recloser operation, and clearance time. SMCS is then employed to simulate the temporal evolution of system states, capturing the dynamic interactions between time-varying load profiles and failure propagation. A multi-dimensional risk index system is established to evaluate consequences including stability loss, voltage/frequency deviations, line overloads, and load/generation losses. To extract meaningful insights from the voluminous simulation output, Principal Component Analysis (PCA) is utilized for dimensionality reduction, followed by Fuzzy C-Means (FCM) clustering to classify failure modes and identify critical system vulnerabilities. The proposed methodology is validated using the IEEE 36-node test system, with results demonstrating its effectiveness in identifying high-risk components (particularly Line 54) and quantifying the risk levels of different failure sequences. This approach provides valuable insights for system planning and operational defense strategies.

title: GRAFT: Grad-CAM and FFT-based Sampling for Feedback-Efficient Reinforcement Learning
authors: Sung-Yun Park, Dae-Wook Kim, Sang-Kwang Lee
abstract: Reinforcement learning (RL) agents often struggle in sparse-reward environments where rewards are rare or delayed. Human-in-the-loop reinforcement learning (HiL-RL) alleviates this issue by training a reward model with human feedback, which provides additional learning signals when environmental rewards are insufficient. In this study, the reward model is implemented as a Feedback Neural Network (FNN) trained on labeled state–action pairs. We propose GRAFT (GRAd-CAM and FFT-based Sampling), a feedback-efficient sampling framework that enhances the learning efficiency of the FNN. GRAFT quantifies the FNN’s learning progress at the state level using Gradient-weighted Class Activation Mapping (Grad-CAM) variance to identify under-trained or overconfident states. It then ensures state-level diversity in feedback sampling through Farthest-First Traversal (FFT). A heuristic-based synthetic feedback environment provides consistent binary feedback, enabling quantitative evaluation of feedback efficiency. We evaluate GRAFT in the Atari Bowling and Skiing environments, both of which involve sparse and delayed reward characteristics. For Bowling, GRAFT achieves stronger performance than FRESH with only 360 feedback samples instead of 500. In Skiing, it achieves a more favorable balance between feedback efficiency and learning stability with 2,550 feedback samples instead of 5,000. These results show that GRAFT improves feedback efficiency under constrained feedback budgets while maintaining strong and stable task performance. Overall, GRAFT demonstrates the potential of internal representation-based sampling for self-assessing reward models in HiL-RL.

title: StigmergyRouter: A Fault-Aware Adaptive Routing Demo for Multi-Agent AI Systems
authors: Jing Du, Hang Zhao, Kenneth Huang
publish: Proceedings of the ACM Conference on AI and Agentic Systems
abstract: None

title: Reasoning-Intensive Regression
authors: Diane Tchuindjo, Omar Khattab
publish: Proceedings of the ACM Conference on AI and Agentic Systems
abstract: None

title: An Adaptive Modulation Method for UAC Based on Multi-Dimensional Channel Awareness
authors: Zhendong Jia, Zheng Peng, Qiuming Ai, Jun-Hong Cui
