title: Robust Relative Navigation for Circular Satellite Formation under Noise Uncertainty in DSL Pathfinder-1 Mission
authors: Qichang Qiang, Yonghe Zhang, Yamin Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems
abstract: Ultra-long wavelength observations are of significant scientific value for unveiling the cosmic dark ages and the reionization process. High-precision astronomical observation missions on the lunar farside rely on spacecraft formation flying to synthesize ultra-long interferometric arrays via large-scale baseline variations, imposing stringent requirements on relative navigation accuracy. However, frequent measurement interruptions, time-varying noise, and poorly characterized noise statistics in deep space severely constrain the performance of existing navigation algorithms. To address these challenges, a relative navigation method integrating the expectation-maximization algorithm and an attention-based long short-term memory network is proposed within the context of the Pathfinder-1 mission. This method employs maximum likelihood theory to rapidly compute optimal prior estimates of noise statistics and uses a temporal prediction model to dynamically predict and compensate for time-varying noise. Finally, the Kalman filtering framework integrates the estimated noise priors with dynamic compensation, achieving closed-loop adaptive fusion between noise modeling and state estimation. Simulation results from the Pathfinder-1 verification mission demonstrate that the proposed method exhibits significant advantages in navigation accuracy and robustness compared to the extended Kalman filter and its adaptive variants, particularly maintaining high-precision state estimation and rapid convergence capabilities under long-baseline configurations with intermittent angle measurements.

title: Dense Residual Dense Block Super-Resolution for Enhancing Vehicle Detection in Remote Sensing
authors: Mokhammad Mirza Etnisa Haqiqi, Ajib Setyo Arifin, Arief Suryadi Satyawan
abstract: Accurate vehicle detection in complex traffic environments remains a challenging task, particularly when dealing with low-resolution imagery and small-scale objects such as distant vehicles and pedestrians. Limitations in image quality, caused by hardware constraints, transmission noise, and real-time processing requirements, often lead to significant performance degradation in conventional object detection frameworks. To address these challenges, this paper proposes an integrated super-resolution–assisted vehicle detection framework that combines a transformer-based SR preprocessing module with a YOLOv12-based object detector. The proposed SR module is designed to exploit global contextual information and long-range dependencies, enabling more faithful reconstruction of fine-grained structural details that are critical for small-object recognition. The enhanced high-resolution representations are subsequently fed into the YOLOv12 detector, which offers a favorable trade-off between detection accuracy and computational efficiency across multiple object scales. Extensive experiments conducted on multiple benchmark datasets, including VEDAI-VISIBLE, VEDAI-IR, DOTA, and xView, demonstrate that the proposed approach consistently outperforms existing CNN- and GAN-based SR methods as well as baseline detection models. Quantitative and qualitative results confirm notable improvements in detection accuracy, particularly for small and densely distributed vehicles, while maintaining practical inference efficiency. These findings highlight the effectiveness of integrating transformer-based SR as a preprocessing stage for robust vehicle detection, contributing to more reliable intelligent transportation and traffic monitoring systems.

title: Compact and Artifact-free Perception Gaussian Splatting for Large-Scale Aerial Mapping
authors: Hao Li, Yuanxu Ma, Runxiang Wang, Xiqing Guo
publish: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Letters
abstract: Large-scale aerial imagery requires efficient 3-D reconstruction, but applying 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) to unbounded scenes often leads to excessive primitive growth, high GPU memory consumption, and geometric artifacts such as sky floaters. This letter presents CAP-GS, a compact and artifact-suppressed Gaussian Splatting framework for large-scale aerial mapping. CAP-GS integrates three lightweight components: Budget-Aware Density Adaptation, which regulates Gaussian densification under a primitive-budget schedule; Partitioned Diagnosis, which localizes high-error regions through tile-level residual analysis; and Multi-Level Pruning, which removes redundant, isolated, and oversized Gaussian primitives. These components allocate primitives to structurally informative regions while suppressing unnecessary growth in background and unstable areas. Experiments on five BungeeNeRF scenes show that CAP-GS achieves 31.04 dB PSNR, 0.953 SSIM, 0.058 LPIPS, 146.62 FPS, and 2.85 GB peak GPU memory, demonstrating a favorable balance among reconstruction fidelity, rendering efficiency, and model compactness for aerial scene reconstruction.

title: A Hybrid IEM–VRT Modeling Approach for Airborne Ground-Penetrating Radar
authors: Mohammad Ebrahimkhani, Morteza Kazerooni
abstract: A hybrid Integral Equation Method–Vector Radiative Transfer (IEM–VRT) framework is proposed for accurate and computationally efficient modeling of airborne ground-penetrating radar (GPR) backscatter from layered rough soils. The model analytically couples surface scattering via IEM with multiple scattering and attenuation described by VRT, ensuring energy and polarization continuity across rough dielectric interfaces. The formulation is validated against finite-difference time-domain (FDTD) simulations, showing agreement within 0.5 dB across 10°–65° incidence at 0.5–0.8 GHz. Compared with full-wave FDTD, the hybrid IEM–VRT achieves a 15× speedup with <0.3% energy imbalance, maintaining comparable accuracy. The framework provides a physically consistent and computationally tractable basis for UAV-borne and low-frequency SAR soil and subsurface retrievals.

title: Quantitative Comparison of Dual-Rotor High Speed Motors with Different Spoke-Type Stator PMs
authors: Zixu Dong, Mingyuan Jiang, Zhang Zhang, Zhouyang Zhang, Shuangxia Niu, K.T. Chau
abstract: Traditional rotor permanent magnet (PM) dual-rotor motors can adapt to complex operating conditions, but are susceptible to PM detachment risks. To address this issue, this paper proposes a dual-rotor dual-flux-switching permanent magnet (DR-DFS-PM) motor with spoke-type stator PMs. The key is its dual-rotor design enables independent control and multi-degree-of-freedom motion, while the flux-switching topology optimizes the magnetic circuit via air-gap harmonic modulation, hence enhancing torque density, eliminating PM detachment risks, and suppressing eddy current losses. In order to conduct a comprehensive analysis and comparison, this paper presents two different topologies of PM structures. Comparative analysis of these two topological configurations using finite element method (FEM) simulations confirms that the configuration with PMs positioned between left and right slots demonstrates superior torque output and operational stability, which is still holds true even after further algorithm optimization.

title: Evaluating Relayed and Switched Quantum Key Distribution (QKD) Network Architectures
authors: Antonis Selentis, Nikolas Makris, Alkinoos Papageorgopoulos, Persefoni Konteli, Konstantinos Christodoulopoulos, George T. Kanellos, Dimitris Syvridis
abstract: We evaluate the performance of two architectures for network-wide quantum key distribution (QKD): Relayed QKD, which relays keys over multi-link QKD paths for non-adjacent nodes, and Switched QKD, which uses optical switches to dynamically connect arbitrary QKD modules to form direct QKD links between them. An advantage of Switched QKD is that it distributes quantum keys end-to-end, whereas Relayed relies on trusted nodes. However, Switched depends on arbitrary matching of QKD modules. We first experimentally evaluate the performance of commercial DV-QKD modules; for each of three vendors we benchmark the performance in standard/matched module pairs and in unmatched pairs to emulate configurations in the Switched QKD network architecture. The analysis reveals that in some cases a notable variation in the generated secret key rate (SKR) between the matched and unmatched pairs is observed. Driven by these experimental findings, we conduct a comprehensive theoretical analysis that evaluates the network wide performance of the two architectures. Our analysis is based on uniform ring networks, where we derive optimal key management configurations and analytical formulas for the achievable consumed SKR. We compare network performance under varying ring sizes, QKD link losses, QKD receivers' sensitivity and performance penalties of unmatched modules. Our findings indicate that Switched QKD performs better in dense rings (short distances, large node counts), while Relayed QKD is more effective in longer distances and large node counts. Moreover, we confirm that unmatched QKD modules penalties significantly impact the efficiency of Switched QKD architecture.

title: Design and Analysis of a Coplanar Waveguide Grounded Double Spiral Antenna for Head and Neck Cancer Hyperthermia Treatment
authors: Nitika Sharma, Rajesh Khanna, Mayank Kumar Rai, Kiran Kumari
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: This paper presents the design and evaluation of a coplanar waveguide (CPWG)-fed grounded spiral antenna operating at 915 MHz for hyperthermia treatment of head and neck cancers. The antenna is implemented on a Rogers RO4350B substrate (ε<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">r</inf> = 3.48, tan δ = 0.004) with a dimension of 74 × 74 mm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> and 0.76 mm thickness. To evaluate its clinical applicability, electromagnetic and thermal analyses are performed using a heterogeneous and homogenous human-mimicking head and neck phantom replicating realistic tissue properties. The proposed design is optimized for efficient power coupling, considering variations in phantom size and antenna-to-phantom distance, enabling localized and focused heating within the tumor region. The electromagnetic and coupled thermal simulations using a heterogeneous head and neck phantom demonstrate excellent impedance matching (S<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">11</inf> < – 15 dB), circular polarization, and focused energy delivery to tumors located 8–17 mm beneath the skin. The Specific absorption rate analysis for 1 g and 10 g of tissue achieves values of 1.67–2.12 W/kg, with comparative evaluations confirming effective power coupling across varying tumor sizes and phantom dimensions. Furthermore, the influence of tumor size on power focusing and heat distribution is investigated through comparative studies on phantoms with and without tumors. The results demonstrate that the proposed antenna effectively adjusts its power coupling characteristics with tumor size variations, providing controlled and targeted heating for lesions. These findings indicate that the designed CPWG antenna is a promising candidate for non-invasive hyperthermia treatment of head and neck cancers.

title: Deep Learning-Based Identification of Illicit Activities in Dark Web Ecosystems
authors: Kaushik Ghosh, Amit Kumar Goyal, Akhmadaliev Khurshid, Umaraliyeva Barchinoy, Xudayberdiyev Baxtiyor, Tuwelbay Kazakbaev
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: This paper proposes a deep learning-based framework for detection of illicit activities on Dark Web based on semantic analysis of content as well as detection at ecosystem level using relational modelling. Transformer-based language representations are used to capture complex and obfuscated textual patterns, and graph-based reason based on relationships between vendors, listings, forums and extracted entities. To overcome the shortage of labelled data and class imbalance, weak supervision and active learning are included in the framework, allowing it to keep adapting to emerging illicit trends. An explainability layer will deliver to users’ case ready evidence through text highlights and relational subgraphs to support investigative transparency. Experimental evaluation shows that the proposed approach can obviously improve the detection accuracy and decrease the false positive rate as well as improve the operation efficiency compared with conventional methods. The results show the effectiveness of deep learning-driven, context-aware analysis as a scalable and ethical solution for Dark Web illicit activities detection.

title: A Comprehensive Overview of the Steane Code [[7,1,3]] CSS Quantum Error Correction Scheme
authors: Neeraj Kumar, Arpit Anand, Aijaz Mehdi Zaidi, Balwinder Raj, Dushyant Kumar
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: Since quantum states are limited in their ease of manipulation, quantum computing has great advantages when used for computation. These interactions, even if infinitesimal, are the very reason why errors arise, be it bit-flip, phase-flip, or composite, thus diminishing the accuracy in computation. Quantum error correction (QEC) intervenes systematically to guard against the loss of coherence from quantum information. Amongst the various QEC protocols developed so far, Steane code [ [7], [1], [3]] is a very famous CSS stabilizer code that was derived from a classical Hamming code. It will encode a single logical qubit into seven physical qubits, which corrects all single-qubit errors by separating X and Z syndrome measurements. The Steane code enables the implementation of transversal Clifford gates, making it an ideal playground to study fault-tolerant quantum computation. Although surface codes remain the codes of choice for scaling implementations, the Steane code will forever remain one of the cornerstone codes due to its clear structure of error correction and ease in teaching.

title: Design and Analysis of a Compact 3:8 Decoder with Reduced Transistor Count for Low Power VLSI Applications
authors: Thumala Obulesu, Kundan Kumar
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: The design and analysis of efficient 3:8 decoder applied in low-power VLSI designs is the focus of the paper. The main task here is to minimize the number of transistors and still be able to perform. Traditional decoder circuits have been designed on a large number of transistors to execute logic functions thereby adding additional dynamic power consumption, silicon area and propagation delay. To overcome these issues a hybrid design methodology by concerning the logic simplification method with the pass-transistor method is introduced. The proposed design is more efficient in terms of power consumption and area coverage by efficiently reducing unnecessary switching activity and hardware complexity. Different crucial measures are examined in the circuit like average power, propagation delay, and Power-Delay Product (PDP). The findings indicate much greater overall and energy-conscious suitability and efficiency in comparison with the traditional CMOS-based decoders.

title: AI-Powered Skin Disease Detection using ML Model
authors: Kothandaraman D, J Bhavana
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: Medical image analysis faces a significant challenge in skin disease detection because different conditions present similar symptoms which differ between patients and require precise diagnosis. This study compares six computer-based classification methods for identifying eight types of skin diseases. The methods include three traditional machine learning models—K-Nearest Neighbors, Random Forest, and Support Vector Machine—and three deep learning models—Convolutional Neural Network (CNN), VGG16 transfer learning, and EfficientNetB0 transfer learning. The Kaggle "Skin Disease Dataset," containing 1,265 preprocessed images of viral (Chickenpox, Shingles), bacterial (Cellulitis, Impetigo), fungal (Athlete Foot, Nail Fungus, Ringworm), and parasitic (Cutaneous Larva Migrans) infections, was used for experimentation. The model received better training through image size adjustments and normalization and augmentation techniques which also helped distribute data points evenly throughout the dataset. The transfer-learning models produced the best results because they demonstrated superior data generalization while making the fewest incorrect predictions. The model EfficientNetB0 produced the highest performance results with precision at 0.967 and recall at 0.968 and F1-score at 0.967 and ROC-AUC at 0.9876 which demonstrates its ability to perform accurate and efficient automated skin disease detection at 97% accuracy.

title: FedTA++: A Temporal-Aware Framework for Mitigating Spatial-Temporal Catastrophic Forgetting in Federated Continual Learning
authors: Saeed Iqbal, Imran Arshad Choudhry, Muhammad Attique Khan, Amin Ullah
publish: 2026 International Conference on Data Science, Machine Learning, and Intelligence (DataSciMI)
abstract: Federated Continual Learning has momentous challenges due to the existence of spatial-temporal data heterogeneity, which often fallouts in acute catastrophic loss of memory. To address this issue, we develop FedTA++, a new FCL architecture that enhances FedTA's capabilities by adding four crucial components: Contrastive Outlier Detection for OOD tasks, Temporal-Aware Feature Stabilization via Adaptive Tail Anchors, Trajectory-Aware Aggregation with memory of client learning histories, and Dynamic Global Prototype Selection. When combined, these components improve knowledge retention and model adaptation in highly dynamic and diverse contexts by reducing output-forgetting and parameter-forgetting. We measure FedTA++ on several benchmark datasets, such as CIFAR-100, ImageNet-R, NIH ChestX-ray14, and ODIR-100K, and compare its results with cutting-edge FCL techniques like FedAvg, FedLwF, GLFC, and FedMGP. Advanced metrics, such as Online Forgetting Rate (OFR), Drift Robustness Score (DRS), and OOD Detection Accuracy (ODA), are used in the evaluation to gauge forgetting behavior and adaptability. According to experimental data, FedTA++ regularly outperforms all baseline methods in terms of accuracy, robustness to idea drift, and out-of-distribution job identification, all while preserving significant privacy protection and minimal communication latency. Overall, the FedTA++ as a foundational method for FCL, offering a scalable and secure approach to mitigating spatial-temporal forgetting in practical continual learning applications.

title: Study and Performance Analysis of Level-Shifter Circuits for Energy-Efficient Multi-Voltage Systems
authors: Aditi Pandey, Dhandapani Vaithiyanathan, Manish Verma, Preeti Verma, Baljit Kaur
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: Level shifter (LS) circuits are crucial in both digital and analog systems for reliable communication and allying of voltage. Recent research focuses on improving level shifter performance and enlarging its applications. This review examines modern level shifter designs from conventional to advanced topologies. The current mirror-based LS with logic error detection for near-threshold operation shows superior performance with minimum delay (9.547 ns), lowest power (1.616 nW), and gives full output swing. The improved Wilson current mirror-based LS offers a balanced trade-off between speed and energy efficiency. A comprehensive evaluation of LS architectures and key performance parameters for benchmarking state-of-the-art designs is presented.

title: An Analytical Comprehension of Neuro-Fuzzy System for Deep Neural Networks; Challenges and Future
authors: Sumanta Chatterjee, Sohan Goswami, Amitava Podder, Srabanti Chakraborty, Debasish Hati, Amit Majumder
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: Neuro-Fuzzy systems have been around for the last 35 years and have been applied in various fields. For, being a hybrid intelligent system, it integrates the best of the both. It combines the learning capability of an Artificial Neural Network and the human like reasoning of Fuzzy Logic. The primary goal is to achieve maximum efficiency by making a Fuzzy System capable of learning. There are various functions like Knowledge Presentation, Learning and Adaptation, Prediction, Decision making under uncertainty etc. which are implementations of an NFS. Deep neural networks have made a lot of progress in the recent years. As a continuation, DNFS or Deep Neuro Fuzzy System has emerged as a field of research. Various fields like computing, healthcare, finance, transportation etc. have become the application domains of DNFS. This paper focuses on the current challenges faced in the research and implementation of DNFS and its future.

title: Drivers and Barriers for Blockchain Adoption in the Sri Lankan Logistics Sector: A Multi-Criteria Decision Making Approach
authors: Mishara Wickramasinghe, Chathumi Kavirathna
publish: 2026 IEEE International Research Conference on Smart Computing and Systems Engineering (SCSE)
abstract: This study examines blockchain adoption in Sri Lanka's logistics sector by identifying and prioritizing key drivers and barriers using the Analytic Hierarchy Process (AHP). Employing data collection through a quantitative survey of logistics and supply chain professionals, resulting in 15 valid expert responses. The results show that technological factors particularly traceability and transparency are the main drivers of adoption, while organizational barriers such as resistance to change and limited managerial awareness are the most significant obstacles. The findings provide practical insights for improving blockchain readiness in emerging logistics markets.

authors: Jiapeng Huang, Runxin Piao, Lianhui Liang
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: This article corrects errors in [1]. The original error is that Fig. 1 in the original article is incorrect.

title: Channel Estimation for IRS-Assisted 6G MIMO-OFDM in Smart Cities
authors: Yi-Hsien Chung, Hong-Cheng Chen, Hong-Yunn Chen
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Consumer Technology (ICCT-Pacific)
abstract: The rapid growth of sixth-generation wireless technology aims to create intelligent urban environments by integrating advanced communication systems. A key element of this development involves using intelligent reflecting surfaces to enhance Multiple-Input Multiple-Output Orthogonal Frequency Division Multiplexing (MIMO-OFDM) systems, improving spectral efficiency and coverage in smart cities. This study introduces a robust channel estimation technique designed for MIMO-OFDM systems with intelligent reflecting surfaces, particularly suited for complex urban environments. The proposed method goes beyond traditional approaches by utilizing deep learning, combining recurrent units and attention mechanisms to capture both temporal and spectral variations in wireless channels. The hybrid model demonstrates effective adaptation to variations generated by Internet of Things (IoT) sensor networks and AI-powered urban management systems while acquiring long-term channel behavior and providing suitability for intelligent infrastructure applications. Research simulations demonstrate that our GRU-Transformer approach outperforms the LSTM-Transformer one, yielding lower NMSE and residual norms and thereby indicating superior accuracy and convergence. This innovative framework is not only efficient but also resilient to environmental interference, offering high performance and optimal resource use, which are essential for the growth of intelligent urban systems and the future development of smart cities.

title: Synthetic Aperture Radar Imaging for Forest Cover Change Detection in Tesso Nilo National Park Using Cumulative Sums Method
authors: Badrul Huda Husain, Takahiro Osawa, Muhammad Rizki Nandika
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Consumer Technology (ICCT-Pacific)
abstract: Tesso Nilo National Park has experienced high deforestation rates due to land clearing for palm oil plantations, leading to significant changes in vegetation cover. This study aimed to observe forest changes in the region using Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) imagery from the Sentinel-1A satellite, employing the cumulative sums method. This method involves calculating the difference between each SAR image and the temporal mean image to generate residual images, followed by bootstrapping to enhance change detection accuracy. The dataset consists of 108 Sentinel-1A images acquired between 2022 and 2023, incorporating both VH polarizations in descending and ascending orbital passes, with a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$3 \times 3$</tex> boxcar filter. The results indicate that the cumulative sum method evaluation showed an accuracy of 82.45%, precision of 96.80%, recall of 74.36%, and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$F 1$</tex>-score of 84.11%. Although the method demonstrates ability to detect changes, some areas of change remain undetected. This study provides valuable insights into the effectiveness of Sentinel- 1A SAR for forest change monitoring and highlights the potential of incorporating multiple orbital passes to enhance the detection performance. Future research should explore additional filtering techniques and machine learning approaches to further improve the accuracy and recall rates.

title: Inter-Modality Visual Feature Fusion in Transformer-Based Video Highlight Detection
authors: Salma M. Elsayed, Noha S. Tawfik, Dalia Sobhy, Sherine Nagy Saleh
publish: 2025 35th International Conference on Computer Theory and Applications (ICCTA)
abstract: Video highlight detection has emerged as a rapidly growing research area, driven by the massive increase in video content available across online platforms. The task focuses on identifying the most important segments within a video. Achieving reliable highlight detection requires a deep understanding of both spatial and temporal aspects of video data, which makes relying solely on a single feature extractor insufficient. This paper presents a comprehensive study on various feature fusion techniques for different video representations generated by multiple feature extraction models. Comprehensive experiments conducted on the YouTube Highlights benchmark dataset demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed methodology. The methodology introduced in this paper achieves the highest performance in the “Skating” category of the benchmark dataset and notable improvements in another category, “Parkour”, surpassing the overall average mAP results reported by the adopted baseline highlight detection model. These improvements ranged between slight and average enhancements in the results.

title: Artificial Intelligence in Film Production Design: 3D Printing, Smart Materials, and the Future of Immersive Cinema
authors: Riham Hilal
publish: 2025 35th International Conference on Computer Theory and Applications (ICCTA)
abstract: Film production design faces challenges of cost, time, and sustainability while striving for immersive experiences. This paper explores how Artificial Intelligence (AI), 3D printing, and smart materials transform cinematic scenography. AI-driven generative models enhance design efficiency, 3D printing accelerates prototyping of high-fidelity elements, and smart materials introduce responsive, eco-efficient features. Case studies, including Laika's stop-motion innovations and virtual production pipelines, illustrate practical applications. Building on these insights, the paper proposes a Cognitive-Responsive Production Design (CRPD) framework that embeds AI in ideation, integrates digital fabrication for rapid realization, and leverages intelligent materials for interactivity and sustainability. The contribution lies in presenting a roadmap that merges creativity, computation, and material innovation, demonstrating how AI-enabled design can reshape aesthetics, efficiency, and ecological responsibility in future film production.

title: Hybrid Deep Learning Framework for Lung Cancer Detection Using GAN-Augmented ResNet-ViT Fusion
authors: Marwa M. Emam, Essam H. Houssein
publish: 2025 35th International Conference on Computer Theory and Applications (ICCTA)
abstract: The high global mortality rate associated with lung cancer underscores a critical demand for diagnostic methods that are both early and precise. While deep learning has shown promise in medical image analysis, conventional models are frequently hindered by insufficient data, class imbalance, and a limited capacity to represent both localized details and global contextual information. In response, we introduce FusionResViT, a hybrid architecture that synergistically combines Residual Networks (ResNet) and Vision Transformers (ViT) for the automated classification of lung cancer in CT scans. This model leverages ResNet to extract hierarchical semantic features and ViT to encapsulate long-range dependencies. A dedicated fusion layer then integrates these complementary feature sets into a unified, highly discriminative representation. To counteract dataset limitations, a Generative Adversarial Network (GAN) generates high-fidelity synthetic CT imagery, thereby augmenting the dataset and alleviating class imbalance. Evaluations on the IQ-OTH/NCCD benchmark reveal that FusionResViT sets a new state-of-the-art, attaining a classification accuracy of 99.3% and surpassing existing methodologies based solely on CNNs or Transformers.

title: Experimental Validation and Optimization of a Wheel Slip Prevention Controller for Commercial Dump Trucks under Low-Adhesion Road Conditions
authors: Tran Van Thoan, Le Anh Vu, Bui Van Cuong, To Ngoc Thien, Le Van Quynh
abstract: This paper presents an experimentally validated and particle swarm optimization (PSO)-enhanced driving wheel slip prevention (DWSP) framework for commercial dump trucks (CDTs) under low-adhesion launch conditions. A unified workflow is developed by combining full-scale vehicle testing with a control-oriented longitudinal dynamic model, thereby establishing a test-to-optimization framework that links real-vehicle validation to controller optimization. In this workflow, a baseline PID-based DWSP controller is first implemented and evaluated on a real CDT over two representative low-adhesion surfaces to verify feasibility and model consistency, and the validated model is then used for offline PSO-based PID gain optimization under more severe operating conditions. The test results show improved traction performance. Specifically, on a clay-mud surface, the duration of excessive wheel slip above 30% decreases from 6.7 s to 3.5 s. Under a road adhesion coefficient of 0.3 and 80% throttle, the PSO-tuned PID-based DWSP controller reduces excessive-slip duration by 96.88%, decreases RMS slip ratio by 65.42%, and shortens longitudinal speed build-up time by 45.24% compared with the baseline PID-based DWSP controller. The results indicate that the proposed framework improves low-adhesion launch traction and robustness while retaining practical ECU implementation feasibility and controller simplicity.

title: FedCamo: Secure and Dependable Federated Learning With Model Camouflage
authors: Rong Xie, Zhong Chen, Yubo Song
publish: IEEE Transactions on Dependable and Secure Computing
abstract: AI driven applications are entering safety critical domains and increasingly rely on federated learning (FL) to coordinate model training across many devices. Although FL limits raw data exposure, its interactive protocol reveals updated model artifacts to curious observers, which enables gradient inversion and threatens privacy. While existing encryption and perturbation methods protect content and statistical summaries, they exhibit limited compatibility and practical deployability. More importantly, they cannot conceal protocol level behavior during communication. Moreover, current concealment methods either cannot be directly extended to FL, or focus on covert message channels without supporting a hidden model. To address these limitations, we propose a novel federated model camouflage framework that covertly embeds a recoverable secret model while preserving the typical external behavior of public FL, namely, FedCamo. First, to enable content camouflage with faithful recovery, FedCamo allocates a protected parameter subspace to the secret model and records only the minimal side information required for extraction. Second, to address cross round stability under partial parameters participation, a dual track aggregation rule applies presence weighted averaging to secret parameters and a standard average to the remaining unprotected parameter subspace. Finally, to enhance robustness under heterogeneity and auditing, a gradient inversion aware personalization procedure selects each client's stego load to balance public utility, secret recoverability, and undetectability. Experiments demonstrate that FedCamo achieves reliable latent model recovery while maintaining public utility close to the encryption baseline, and it remains compatible with existing FL components, which demonstrates strong robustness.

title: An Improved-MRC in Underwater SIMO-OFDM Communication System Under Highly Spatially Correlated Channels
authors: Guanzhang Wu, Gang Qiao, Lu Ma
publish: Proceedings of the 19th International Conference on Underwater Networks and Systems
abstract: None

title: A Multi-Segment Minimum Copper Loss Fault-Tolerance Strategy for Full Torque Range Operation of Dual Star-Delta Windings PMSM Under Open-Phase Fault
authors: Jiangtao Yang, Shanshan Zhou, Jianzong Yu, Sheng Huang, Shoudao Huang
abstract: When a single open-phase fault occurs in a dual star-delta windings permanent magnet synchronous motor (DSDW-PMSM), the yaxis current in the maximum torque fault-tolerant strategy is not decoupled from the currents on the α-axis and β-axis. As a result, the conventional hybrid current fault-tolerant strategy across the full torque range cannot achieve minimum copper loss output for this type of motor. To address this issue, this paper proposes a multi-segmented hybrid current fault-tolerant strategy, which ensures that the motor can output across the full torque range while minimizing the total copper loss generated by the windings. Furthermore, finite element simulation analysis is conducted to compare the torque ripple and radial electromagnetic force density of the existing hybrid current fault-tolerant strategies. Finally, tests are conducted on a 24-slot/10-pole DSDW-PMSM with a 15° phase shift to verify the feasibility of the proposed strategy.

title: An Analytical Model for Spatiotemporal Risk of Grid Infrastructure Under Extreme Events
authors: Luo Xu, Hongtai Zeng, Ning Lin, H. Vincent Poor
abstract: Accurately quantifying the spatiotemporal risks to power grid infrastructure under extreme events is critical to ensuring system stability and resilience. The recently proposed hazard resistance-based spatiotemporal risk analysis (HRSRA) model addresses a key limitation of the widely used Sequential Monte Carlo method, which tends to overestimate infrastructure damage risk due to repeated sampling in time-series analysis. However, the HRSRA method relies on extensive simulations. This letter proposes an analytical framework and derives closed-form expressions for key statistical metrics to quantify grid infrastructure risk during extreme events. Bypassing the numerical simulations of HRSRA, the proposed analytical method agrees well with power outage observations in Puerto Rico.

title: MSGFusion: Multimodal Scene Graph-Guided Infrared and Visible Image Fusion_supp1-3695365.pdf
authors: Haiyong Zheng
abstract: Infrared and visible image fusion aims to generate high-quality images with both rich low-level details and clear high-level semantics. Yet, prevailing data-driven approaches remain largely confined to interactions at the level of low-level features, such as pixel intensities or gradients. Although some task-driven methods introduce semantic constraints via downstream labels, such as object detection or semantic segmentation, they are limited to implicit priors or local regions, struggling to provide global, context-aware structured semantic guidance. Recent text-driven methods attempt to incorporate unstructured textual priors; however, directly injecting them into low-level fusion networks often disrupts the inherent spatial and semantic coherence, ultimately leading to edge distortion and texture degradation. We therefore present MSGFusion, a novel multimodal scene graph-guided image fusion framework designed to bridge the representation gap between high-level semantics and the low-level pixel space. By deeply coupling structured scene graphs derived from both text and vision, MSGFusion explicitly represents entities, attributes, and spatial relations. It subsequently aligns the parsed structured semantics to specific local visual regions through successive modules for scene graph representation, hierarchical aggregation, and scene graph-driven fusion. This framework reformulates traditional pixel-level aggregation into spatially associated and semantically aware fine-grained fusion. Extensive experiments on multiple public benchmarks confirm that MSGFusion significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods, yielding prominent advantages in detail preservation and structural clarity. Furthermore, the proposed method not only exhibits outstanpding semantic consistency in downstream tasks like pedestrian detection and semantic segmentation, but also features high computational efficiency and strong cross-domain generalization capabilities in medical image fusion tasks. Code is available at: https://github.com/KazDong628/MSGFusion.

title: MHCL: Multi-modal Hierarchical Contrastive Learning for Physiological Signal-Based Vigilance Detection
authors: Yucheng Liu, Gaoyan Zhang
publish: IEEE Journal of Biomedical and Health Informatics
abstract: Vigilance detection using physiological signals is critical for safety-sensitive applications such as driver monitoring. However, accurately capturing vigilance-related patterns across different individuals remains a challenge due to individual variability and weak signal characteristics. This paper presents MHCL, a novel multi-modal framework that integrates electroencephalogram (EEG) and electrooculogram (EOG) signals to enhance vigilance state classification. MHCL introduces three key innovations: (1) a hierarchical contrastive learning mechanism that aligns local and global representations within and across modalities; (2) a dual-stage fusion framework that implements feature-level interaction through cross-modal attention and decision-level integration via an ensembled classifier module; (3) an adversarial domain adaptation strategy with label smoothing to mitigate inter-subject variability and an ensembled classifier to leverage both modality-specific and fused features. Extensive experiments on the SEED-VIG dataset demonstrate leading performance, achieving 96.9% accuracy in subject-dependent settings and 82.0% in cross-subject evaluations, significantly outperforming existing unimodal and multi-modal baselines. Visualization analyses confirm the model's ability to learn semantically aligned and subject-invariant representations. This work provides a robust solution for vigilance detection by addressing key challenges in multi-modal fusion and generalization. The implementation of the proposed MHCL model is publicly available at: <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/xuexiba233/MHCL</uri>.

title: Investigating the Performance of RPM JTWPAs by Optimizing LC-Resonator Elements
authors: M. A. Galí Labarias, T. Yamada, Y. Nakashima, Y. Urade, K. Inomata
abstract: Resonant phase-matched Josephson traveling-wave parametric amplifiers (RPM JTWPAs) play a key role in quantum computing and quantum information applications due to their low-noise, broadband amplification, and quadrature squeezing capabilities. This research focuses on optimizing RPM JTWPAs through numerical optimization of parametrized resonator elements to maximize gain, bandwidth and quadrature squeezing. Our results show that optimized resonators can increase the maximum gain and squeezing by more than 5 dB in the ideal noiseless case. However, introducing the effects of loss through a lumped-element model reveals that gain saturates with increasing loss, while squeezing modes degrade rapidly, regardless of resonator optimization. These results highlight the potential of resonator design to significantly improve amplifier performance, as well as the challenges posed by current fabrication technologies and inherent losses.

title: Linearized Tensor Low-rank Convolutional Network
authors: Qiangqiang Shen, Zonglin Liu, Hanzhang Wang, Yongsheng Liang, Yongyong Chen, Shiqi Wang
abstract: To balance deep learning performance and interpretability, convolutional sparse coding (CSC) has successfully incorporated differentiable optimization layers in place of the standard convolutional layers of traditional deep neural networks (DNN). However, CSC models prefer to fall into local sparsity under the ℓ1-norm, whose optimization shows limitations in preserving the geometric structure and global sparsity of tensor data. In this paper, we propose a Linearized Tensor Low-Rank Convolutional Network (LTLRCNet) to refresh the differentiable optimization layers for DNN (e.g., ResNet and UNet), which instead optimizes the tensor low-rank minimization problem for global sparsity without ruining the intrinsic structure of data. Specifically, instead of simply replacing the ℓ1-norm with a tensor low-rank regularizer, we first propose a linearized tensor lowrank representation (LTLRR) model optimized by a linearized alternating direction method to decouple the dictionary and code coefficient without introducing an auxiliary variable, whose convergence is proved theoretically. Subsequently, we extend the LTLRR model into a deep unfolding network, such as the LTLRCNet, by engineering a convolutional dictionary that functions as a differentiable optimization layer, effectively substituting the conventional convolutional layer. To match the deep unfolding framework and avoid the gradient explosion, we apply a rank-one tactic bearing the Frank-Wolfe algorithm to solve the tensor low-rank minimization problem instead of conducting the cumbersome full-rank tensor singular value decomposition. Experimental results on image classification and segmentation show the potential of our LTLRCNet with its modeling-based layers to maintain significant performance and interpretability.

title: Comprehensive Study on Health State Classification and Remaining Useful Life Prediction Using the Mamba Sequence Model
authors: Shih-Yu Li, Chin-Yu Liu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems
abstract: In this study will develop a framework based on run-to-failure data to construct health status intervals and predict the Remaining Useful Life (RUL). The study will employ the sequential model Mamba for health status classification and RUL prediction. To enhance the interpretability of the system's degradation phase, the research team will apply anomaly detection methods to identify health status intervals. The C-MAPSS dataset will be used to define these intervals. Data processing will include sensor data selection and preprocessing. First, a Random Forest classifier will be used to select the sensors most sensitive to state changes, followed by time-domain feature extraction. These features will be further refined using Fisher Linear Discriminant Analysis to ensure that the most discriminative features are used for subsequent analysis. Next, a One-Class Support Vector Machine will be employed for anomaly detection to identify abnormal segments in the system operation, establishing three health status intervals: “Healthy,” “Sub-healthy,” and “Warning.” Once the intervals are established, the research team will use the Mamba model for status classification, dividing these segments, and applying them to the test dataset that has not run to failure to validate the classification model's effectiveness. Finally, the Mamba model will be utilized for RUL prediction, and its performance will be compared with other RUL prediction models. It is expected that the developed methodology will significantly improve prediction accuracy and reliability. This series of steps will develop an excellent health status classification model and a reliable RUL prediction method, providing critical technical support for preventive maintenance and system health management in industrial applications.

title: Reduced Taxi Delays With Intelligent Departure Metering Advisory Tool
authors: Hasnain Ali, Duc-Thinh Pham, Sameer Alam, Brian Hilburn
abstract: Airport taxi delays pose significant challenges to airlines, passengers, and environmental sustainability. Addressing these delays requires synchronizing uncertain airside operations, including pushback sequencing, taxiway movements, and runway take-offs. This article presents the intelligent departure metering advisory tool (I-MATE), an artificial intelligence (AI)-based decision support system that recommends pushback timings to air traffic controllers (ATCOs) to reduce taxi delays while balancing downstream runway throughput. I-MATE's recommendation outcomes unfold over a longer time horizon compared to other AI-driven decision support systems in air traffic control, such as conflict resolution tools, posing a greater cognitive challenge for ATCOs. We conducted validation experiments to assess the efficacy and acceptability of I-MATE in assisting ATCOs to manage airside traffic. The study revealed a spectrum of compliance with I-MATE recommendations among ATCOs, highlighting the complex interplay between human behavior and AI-driven decision support systems. While ATCOs rated I-MATE highly for usefulness and reliability, concerns regarding transparency and explainability emerged. ATCO feedback also emphasized the importance of considering individual differences and human factors in the design of such systems. This research underscores the value of AI-based decision support systems in complex, dynamic environments, particularly for addressing the cognitive challenge of balancing long-term outcomes with immediate actions.

title: Coordinating Multiple Conditions for Trajectory-Controlled Human Motion Generation
authors: Deli Cai, Haoyang Ma, Changxing Ding
abstract: Trajectory-controlled human motion generation aims to synthesize realistic human motions conditioned on both textual descriptions and spatial trajectories. However, existing methods suffer from two critical limitations: first, the conflict between text and trajectory conditions disrupts the denoising process, resulting in compromised motion quality or inaccurate trajectory following; second, the use of redundant motion representations introduces inconsistencies between motion components, leading to instability during trajectory control. To address these challenges, we propose CMC, a decoupled framework that effectively coordinates text and trajectory conditions through a divide-and-conquer strategy. CMC follows a divide-and-conquer paradigm, comprising two cascaded stages: Trajectory Control and Motion Completion. In the first stage, a diffusion model generates a simplified representation of the controlled joints under trajectory guidance, based on the given trajectories, ensuring accurate and stable trajectory following. In the second stage, a text-conditioned diffusion inpainting model generates full-body motions using the simplified representation from the first stage as partial observations. To mitigate overfitting caused by limited inpainting training data, we further introduce the Selective Inpainting Mechanism (SIM), which alternates between text-to-motion generation and motion inpainting tasks during training. Experiments on HumanML3D and KIT datasets demonstrate that CMC achieves state-of-the-art performance in control accuracy and motion quality, demonstrating its effectiveness in coordinating multimodal conditions and representations. Project page: <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/CDLCHOI/cmc_release</uri>

title: Ultra-compact positioning system based on fiber optic extrinsic Fabry-Perot interferometer array
authors: Qing-Hua Yang, Quan-Xu Cui, Dian-Heng Huo, Jing Wang, Bo Yin, Zhong-Ze Zhao, Shan-Shan Wang
abstract: An ultra-compact positioning system based on fiber optic extrinsic Fabry-Perot interferometer (EFPI) array is proposed and demonstrated. The sensitivity of EFPI fiber sensor is investigated theoretically, by which parameters of the EFPI fiber sensor are optimized and sensitivity (re 1 V/μPa) of -198 dB at 5 kHz is reached. In addition, to realize the accurate positioning within a relatively large dynamic range, a demodulation method based on F-P laser is proposed. Based on the design, fabrication and demodulation of five EFPI fiber sensors, a five-element cross array for underwater positioning is built and tested in both anechoic sink and lake environments. Results show that positioning errors of 0.0247 m and 0.0296 m are obtained, respectively, which demonstrates the good performance and environmental applicability of the positioning array. The positioning array demonstrated here shows advantages of compact structure, fast employment, easy fabrication, good applicability and large dynamic range, which shows potential applications in marine resource exploration, unmanned collaborative equipment underwater and marine environment monitoring.

title: Guest EditorialComments by the Senior Editor

title: Multi-Stage Series Connection Methodology of High-Voltage Ultra-Fast Solid-State Discharge Switches
authors: Xiaotong Shen, Jiapeng Liu, Chunpin Ren, Zaixuan Shang, Hongfan Zhu, Xiaoyu Xu, Fei Tong, Lingyao Meng, Lihui Xu, Chunying Zhang, Jinpeng Wu, Rong Zeng
abstract: Ultra-fast turn-on solid-state device, owning the intrinsic merits of long life-span and low randomness, is regarded as the potential candidates for next-generation ultra-fast discharge switches. However, when constructing discrete device chips into a real switch, there stands two long-standing bottlenecks, which are the depression of parasitic inductance guaranteeing a fast turn-on, and the high operation consistency among devices in prevention of device destruction. To address the issues, in this work, a systematic mechanical and electrical connection design methodology is proposed, leveraging vertical mechanical architecture, together with ultra-fast voltage-balancing electrical design to realize ultra-low total inductance and high consistency respectively. Based on that, prototype switches utilizing super gate turn-off thyristor (SGTO) chips across single-stage, four-stage, and twelve-stage configurations are fabricated and tested. The correlation between total inductance and the number of stages is investigated. Experimental results verify ultra-low inductances of 254 nH for the twelve-stage configuration, indicating an 34% reduction in comparison with conventional architecture; In term of operation consistency, the experimental results verify a decrease of voltage spike from 435 V to 165 V during turn-on dynamic.

title: NH 3 Plasma Surface Treatment in β -Ga 2 O 3 : Interface Trap Density Reduction and MOSFET Performance Gains
authors: Xiaole Jia, Shuqi Huang, Yibo Wang, Bochang Li, Cizhe Fang, Haodong Hu, Xiaoxi Li, Zhengdong Luo, Yan Liu, Yue Hao, Genquan Han
abstract: In this work, an NH<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sub> plasma treatment (NPT) is introduced to engineer the Al<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>O<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sub>/<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\beta $</tex-math> </inline-formula>-phase gallium oxide (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\beta $</tex-math> </inline-formula>-Ga<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>O<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${}_{{3}}\text {)}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> interface for high-performance MOS devices. The NPT effectively suppresses Ga suboxide-related surface states, leading to substantial reduction of interface trap density (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${D}_{\text {it}}\text {)}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>. High–low frequency capacitance analysis of Al<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>O<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sub>/<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\beta $</tex-math> </inline-formula>-Ga<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>O<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sub> MOS capacitors (MOSCAPs) reveals a record-low shallow-level <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${D}_{\text {it}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$2\times 10^{{10}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> cm<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${}^{-{2}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>eV<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${}^{-{1}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) confirms a 31% reduction in Ga<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">+</sup> suboxide components after NPT, indicating improved surface stoichiometry. The improved interface quality directly translates into enhanced transistor performance. <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\beta $</tex-math> </inline-formula>-Ga<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>O<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sub> metal–oxide–semiconductor field-effect transistors (MOSFETs) with NPT exhibit reduced subthreshold swing (SS), suppressed hysteresis, ~10% enhancement in effective mobility, lower <sc xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">on</small>-resistance (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$R_{\mathrm {ON}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>), and increased breakdown voltage (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$V_{\mathrm {br}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>). These improvements are attributed to reduced Coulomb scattering and suppressed charge trapping at the dielectric/semiconductor interface. The demonstrated NPT process is fully CMOS-compatible and integration-friendly, offering a practical and scalable interface engineering strategy for realizing high-performance and reliable <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\beta $</tex-math> </inline-formula>-Ga<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>O<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sub> power transistors.

title: Table of Contents
authors: N/A

title: FDTD Study of Ionospheric Reflections Associated With Consecutive Strokes of the Same Lightning Flash
authors: Shuta Furutani, Yoshihiro Baba, Si Chen, Vladimir A. Rakov
abstract: Ground-level electric fields generated by four consecutive strokes in a negative cloud-to-ground lightning flash at a horizontal distance of 176 km from the field observation point, including both the ground wave and the first ionospheric reflection (skywave), are computed using the finite-difference time-domain (FDTD) method in the 2D cylindrical coordinate system. The lightning return-stroke is represented using the modified transmission-line model with linear current decay with height (MTLL) and the modified transmission-line model with exponential current decay with height (MTLE). Parameters of the exponential atmospheric conductivity profile providing the best match to the corresponding measured daytime electric fields are found. The best-match parameters are not much influenced by the choice of return-stroke model (MTLL or MTLE), the ground conductivity (σgr = ∞, 10 mS/m or 1 mS/m), the propagation speed of current wave along the lightning channel (v = 2c/3, c/2 or c/3 where c is the speed of light), or the height of the channel in the MTLL model (H = 7 km or 5 km).

                    O
                    <sub>3</sub>
                    Solar-Blind Photodetectors Using Plasma-Enhanced Atomic Layer Deposition
authors: Siyi Zhu, Yujie Yang, Sizhe Li, Qiren Luo, Hao Wu, Qianqian Lin, Chang Liu
abstract: Ga<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>O<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sub> with a wide bandgap of 4.8–5.3 eV has received much attention in the field of solar-blind detection. Due to the low carrier concentration and weak ultraviolet (UV) absorption, the as-deposited Ga<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>O<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sub> solar-blind photodetectors (SBPDs) usually exhibit poor photoresponsivity. In this work, we propose the incorporation of indium into Ga<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>O<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sub> (IGO) thin films since the concentration of indium dopants influences the density of oxygen vacancies (V<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${}_{\text {O}}\text {)}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> so that the optical bandgap and carrier concentration can be modulated. In this regard, plasma-enhanced atomic layer deposition (PEALD) with a supercycle technique was developed to precisely control the chemical composition of IGO thin films. Photoconductive detectors were then fabricated using the metal–semiconductor–metal (MSM) structure. With an appropriate indium cycle ratio of 0.20 using PEALD, the IGO thin films have a low density of V<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">O</sub>, which effectively suppresses the persistent photoconductivity (PPC) effect. And the corresponding SBPD shows the best performance, i.e., a low dark current of 0.31 pA, a high photo-to-dark current ratio (PDCR) of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$2.32\times 10^{{5}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>, a high responsivity of 0.45 A/W, and a fast response time of 0.83/0.16 s. Thus, In-doped Ga<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>O<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sub> thin films represent a practical approach toward the next generation of SBPDs.
