title: Multi-Modal Character Localization and Extraction for Chinese Text Recognition
authors: Qilong Li, Chongsheng Zhang
abstract: Scene text recognition (STR) methods have demonstrated their excellent capability in English text images. However, due to the complex inner structures of Chinese and the extensive character categories, it poses challenges for recognizing Chinese text in images. Recently, studies have shown that the methods designed for English text recognition encounter an accuracy bottleneck when recognizing Chinese text images. This raises the question: Is it appropriate to apply the model developed for English to the Chinese STR task? To explore this issue, we propose a novel method named LER, which explicitly decouples each character and independently recognizes characters while taking into account the complex inner structures of Chinese. LER consists of three modules: Localization, Extraction, and Recognition. Firstly, the localization module utilizes multimodal information to determine the character's position precisely. Then, the extraction module dissociates all characters in parallel. Finally, the recognition module considers the unique inner structures of Chinese to provide the text prediction results. Extensive experiments conducted on large-scale Chinese benchmarks indicate that our method significantly outperforms existing methods. Furthermore, extensive experiments conducted on six English benchmarks and the Union14M benchmark show impressive results in English text recognition by LER.

title: Harmonic Estimation and Suppression for PMSM Drives via a Hybrid RBF-ADALINE Framework
authors: Xiang Cheng, Shaocheng Qu, Jing He, Changfan Zhang, Haonan Yang
publish: IEEE Journal of Emerging and Selected Topics in Power Electronics
abstract: Observer-based deadbeat predictive current control (DPCC) effectively handles lumped disturbances in permanent magnet synchronous motor (PMSM) drives, but suffers from periodic disturbances caused by inverter nonlinearities. While conventional harmonic rejection methods, such as resonant controllers and ADALINE-based methods, often exhibit instability or significant performance degradation at high speeds, due to discretization-induced phase lag and the absence of rotor electrical angle compensation. To overcome these limitations, this paper proposes a novel framework for harmonic estimation and suppression. First, by analyzing the failure mechanism of conventional radial basis function neural network observer (RBFO) in periodic disturbance rejection, a modified PMSM dynamic model with comprehensive disturbance characterization is obtained. Then, a radial basis function neural network disturbance observer (RBFDO) is proposed to simultaneously handle both low-frequency disturbances caused by parameter mismatch and a portion of high-frequency harmonic disturbances. Third, an adaptive linear neural network compensator (ALNC) is developed to achieve dq-axis harmonic decoupling with rigorously proven stability, further improving harmonic compensation accuracy. Fourth, a parallel hybrid RBF-ADALINE framework (HRADF) is developed. Theoretical analysis shows that it reduces the observer’s relative order to suppress high-frequency phase lag, thus enhancing harmonic estimation and stability at high speeds. Finally, various experiments are conducted on a 64W PMSM experimental platform to validate the effectiveness of the proposed method.

title: A Physics and Data Co-driven Approach for Heterogeneous Vehicle Platoon Control with Incomplete Dynamics
authors: Jianhua Yin, Bingrong Xu, Yi He, Chaozhong Wu, Lingxi Li
publish: IEEE Transactions on Automation Science and Engineering
abstract: Autonomous vehicles platooning on highways can save energy costs, improving traffic safety and efficiency. Vehicles take corresponding actions in response to various transportation environments with an embedded dynamics model. However, it is unrealistic to expect that we have all accurate dynamics models due to the heterogeneity of vehicles. This work proposes a physics and data co-driven framework for heterogeneous vehicle platoon control by integrating physics-informed machine learning (PIML) with model predictive control (MPC). The proposed method targets scenarios where only limited observations (data) of vehicle dynamics and partially known physics are available for use. The partially known physics serves as the prior knowledge to penalize the training of the neural network resulting in the physics informed neural networks (PINN), with which we can not only reduce the variance of the neural network but also restore the missing physics during the training of the neural network. We demonstrate the proposed method with two scenarios in the case study. The results show that: 1) the neural network penalized by the partially known physics can accurately forecast the vehicle states in certain time ranges, 2) the missing vehicle dynamics parameters are recovered, and 3) the proposed PINN-MPC outperforms conventional data-driven MPC (NN-MPC) without physics constraints. The demonstration manifests that PINN-MPC has great potential to be used for heterogeneous platoon control with reduced data acquisition cost and only partially known physics.

title: A Two-Stage Reactive Auction Framework for the Multi-Depot Rural Postman Problem with Dynamic Vehicle Failures
authors: Eashwar Sathyamurthy, Jeffrey W. Herrmann, Shapour Azarm
abstract: Although unmanned vehicle fleets offer efficiency in transportation, logistics and inspection, their susceptibility to failures poses a significant challenge to mission continuity. We study the Multi-Depot Rural Postman Problem with Rechargeable and Reusable Vehicles (MD-RPP-RRV) with vehicle failures, where unmanned rechargeable vehicles placed at multiple depots with capacity constraints may fail while serving arc-based demands. To address unexpected vehicle breakdowns during operation, we propose a two-stage real-time rescheduling framework. First, a centralized auction quickly generates a feasible rescheduling solution; for this stage, we derive a theoretical additive bound that establishes an analytical guarantee on the worst-case rescheduling penalty. Second, a peer auction refines this baseline through a problem-specific magnetic field router for local schedule repair, utilizing parameters calibrated via sensitivity analysis to ensure controlled computational growth. We benchmark this approach against a simulated annealing metaheuristic to evaluate solution quality and execution speed. Experimental results on 257 diverse failure scenarios demonstrate that the framework achieves an average runtime reduction of over 95% relative to the metaheuristic baseline, cutting rescheduling times from hours to seconds while maintaining high solution quality. The two-stage framework excels on large-scale instances, surpassing the centralized auction in nearly 80% of scenarios with an average solution improvement exceeding 12%. Moreover, it outperforms the simulated annealing mean and best solutions in 59% and 28% of scenarios, respectively, offering the robust speed-quality trade-off required for real-time mission continuity.

title: Trajectory Prediction Considering Asymmetric Interactions Among Heterogeneous Traffic Participants
authors: Dongming Han, Sizhe Cheng, Jinxiang Wang, Yongjun Yan, Jiangfeng Nan, Duanfeng Chu, Guodong Yin
abstract: Trajectory prediction is a critical component of autonomous driving control systems, as its accuracy and adaptability directly determine the safety and decision reliability of vehicles operating in mixed traffic environments. However, heterogeneous traffic participants in mixed traffic environments display pronounced behavioral differences. Existing models often have limited capability to accurately capture their true interaction dynamics, thereby restricting long-term prediction performance. This paper proposes a trajectory prediction method that accounts for the asymmetric interaction behaviors among heterogeneous traffic participants. Specifically, the interaction intensity between heterogeneous agents is modeled as the combination of interaction effort and interaction cost during the conflict resolution process, thereby reflecting the asymmetric social attributes among traffic participants. Then, a self-attention mechanism is employed to capture the temporal interaction features between the target vehicle and surrounding agents, while a graph Transformer is utilized to extract spatial interaction features among all participants. Furthermore, an auxiliary interaction intensity prediction task is innovatively incorporated to enhance the feature extraction process. Experimental results on the InD and SinD datasets demonstrate that the proposed asymmetric interaction-aware trajectory prediction (AIA-TP) model achieves superior long-term prediction performance compared to baselines and exhibits better adaptability in interactive scenarios.

title: A Topology-Preserving Coreset for Kernel Regression in Scientific Visualization
authors: Weiran Lyu, Nathaniel Gorski, Jeff M. Phillips, Bei Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Visualization and Computer Graphics
abstract: Modern simulations and observations generate vast amounts of data, fueling a growing interest in replacing discrete data with continuous surrogate models, such as functional models and implicit neural networks, to enhance data storage, transfer, analysis, and visualization. To that end, kernel regression is a well-known class of non-parametric techniques useful for surrogate modeling. In this paper, we propose a new framework to use coresets for kernel regression-a small dataset that is used as a proxy for the original data-in scientific visualization. Using kernel regression as a surrogate for scientific data, we construct an optimized coreset that is both compact and highly accurate, reducing errors from randomly-sampled and grid-based coresets by orders of magnitude. We evaluate our framework on large spatial datasets and demonstrate that it incurs negligible error while preserving the underlying topological features.

title: Kernel Recursive Ridge Regression With Varying Hyperparameter and Favorable Sample Incorporation for Slow Time-Varying Process Monitoring
authors: Tongze Hou, Xin Ma, Mingliang Cui, Hui Wang, Youqing Wang
abstract: Industrial process data typical exhibit nonlinear and time-varying characteristics, which limit the effectiveness of traditional process monitoring methods. Kernel-based techniques can address nonlinearity by projecting data into high-dimensional feature spaces, but their application to time-varying systems remains limited due to two key challenges: the near-singularity of the kernel matrix during updates and the high computational cost of updating implementation. This study proposes a kernel-based time-varying vector autoregressive model to characterize system dynamics. An adaptive regularization strategy is introduced to balance model bias and numerical stability by dynamically tuning the regularization parameter. To reduce computational burden in updating implementation, the eigenvalue decomposition of the kernel matrix is reformulated as that of diagonal plus rank-one matrix and arrow matrix, thereby reducing at least one-third of the computational cost. To facilitate a practical model, a favorable sample incorporation strategy is designed to suppress the influence of faulty samples and enhance model robustness. The experimental results show that the proposed method achieves superior average fault detection rates and false alarm rates on five industrial datasets compared with existing advanced time-varying process monitoring methods, demonstrating good generalization performance.

title: PMLSF: A Privacy-Preserving Machine Learning Service Framework for Efficient Task Allocation in Mobile Crowdsensing_supp1-3684881.docx
authors: Naixue Xiong
abstract: The proliferation of machine learning services in mobile crowd sensing (MCS) brings both new opportunities and severe privacy challenges. Location leakage, identity linkage, and centralized trust bottlenecks remain critical risks, while conventional differential privacy (DP) techniques often degrade service responsiveness due to noise generation latency and increased training overhead. To address these issues, this paper proposes PMLSF, a Privacy-Preserving Machine Learning Service Framework that integrates differential privacy, deep reinforcement learning (DRL), and blockchain-based orchestration to enable secure, efficient, and scalable task allocation in MCS environments. Specifically, we design Optimized Geo-indistinguishability (OptGI), a lightweight DP mechanism that reduces noise generation latency while maintaining rigorous privacy guarantees. We further introduce TopK Double Deep Q-Network (TopK-DDQN), a task allocation service that prunes infeasible actions and supports latency-aware decision-making over privatized inputs. To eliminate single points of failure, PMLSF employs a permissioned blockchain orchestration layer, ensuring anonymity, accountability, and distributed trust without imposing prohibitive overhead. Extensive experiments demonstrate that PMLSF achieves low latency privacy protection and significantly improves allocation utility and profit compared with state-of-the-art methods, positioning it as a scalable foundation for integrating DP methodologies into machine learning-driven MCS services.

title: SmokeNeXt: Dual-Stream CNN-Transformer Hybrid Architecture With Multiscale Adaptation for Satellite Remote Sensing Smoke Image Classification
authors: Jinhong Wu, Haihui Wang, Zhenhai Qin, Jinyang Dai, Qixing Zhang
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Smoke detection by remote sensing plays a vital role in forest fire monitoring due to its wide coverage and fast update frequency. At present, two main challenges remain: 1) the spatio-temporal variability of smoke's shape and color, and 2) the drastic scale differences of smoke instances across images, both of which hinder the generalization of existing approaches. Traditional CNNs are effective in capturing local details but struggle with large-scale context modeling. Transformers offer global self-attention and long-range dependency modeling but often miss fine-grained information, especially for small targets. To address these issues, we propose SmokeNeXt, a dual-stream, multiscale adaptive model tailored for smoke classification. By fusing the Conv-branch's local detail extraction with the Trans-branch's global context modeling, a semantic merge module with a self-designed multi-axis convolutional cross-attention enables cross-stream feature complementation. In addition, A gated redundancy suppression module is introduced to dynamically filter out irrelevant background noise, enhancing robustness to appearance variability. Experiments on the FASDD_RS and USTC_SmokeRS datasets demonstrate that SmokeNeXt effectively mitigates both challenges. On FASDD_RS, it achieves 97.8% accuracy and 99.1% AR@<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\bf {\phi _{0.04\%\rm{--}0.8\%}}$</tex-math></inline-formula>, with high recall even under extreme small-scale smoke conditions, and consistently focusing on smoke regions across diverse appearances and backgrounds. On USTC_SmokeRS, SmokeNeXt achieves an accuracy of 96.2%. Compared with ResNet-50, ConvNeXt-B, and MobileNetV3, SmokeNeXt improves AR by 8.0%, 1.5%, and 6.6%, respectively, indicating strong generalization under extreme scale variation and morphological diversity.

title: A Statistical Analysis-Based PINN Framework for Joint SOC/SOE Estimation Across Multi-conditions
authors: Yuntian Liu, Aili Fan, Yuhua Du, Yigeng Huangfu
abstract: Accurate joint estimation of the state of charge (SOC) and the state of energy (SOE) is essential for reliable management of lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) under diverse operating conditions. However, robust estimation remains challenging because polarization induces pronounced variations in voltage response and internal dynamics, especially when only limited multi-condition data are available. In this work, a unified SPMe-based physics-informed neural network (PINN) framework is proposed for joint SOC/SOE estimation under small-sample, multi-condition settings. SPMe-derived physical constraints are embedded into the learning process, while a statistical representativeness analysis is conducted to justify the use of limited multi-condition data for cross-condition learning. An SOC fixed-point supervision (SOCFPS) strategy is further introduced to suppress cumulative SOC drift and improve the stability of joint estimation. Experimental results show that the proposed framework achieves an SOC RMSE below 1.2% in nearly all cases and an SOE RMSE consistently within 2% across 16 operating conditions. Compared with the SPMe-based particle filter (PF) method and representative data-driven baselines, including a gated recurrent unit (GRU) model, the proposed framework provides more accurate joint SOC/SOE estimation and stronger robustness. Moreover, under an unseen Dynamic Stress Test (DST) profile, the method still maintains clear advantages in terminal-voltage, SOC, and SOE estimation, indicating promising generalization beyond the constant-current training regime.

title: MIPAMS JUMBF: A framework for efficient development of extended JPEG image and metadata applications
authors: Nikolaos Fotos, Jaime Delgado
abstract: JPEG Universal Metadata Box Format (JUMBF) provides a carrier format for embedding arbitrary data in image file formats and beyond. Through its extensibility and native referencing scheme, it enables the construction of rich data models to accommodate emerging use cases and applications. The proposed implementation framework enables JUMBF adoption in end applications as well as the extension of the standardized data model to cover complex requirements. Through standardized applications built on top of JUMBF, this work demonstrates the modularity and extensibility of the JUMBF data model. It further highlights the practical impact of the developed software by showcasing real-world applications and introducing a comprehensive conformance suite for JUMBF implementers. Endorsed by the JPEG Standardization Committee as a reference software, this library represents the first complete reference implementation of the JPEG Systems suite of standards.

title: A Comprehensive Characterization of Glass Fiber Wire: Thermal Life, Electrical Life, and Partial Discharge
authors: Muhammad Raza Khowja, Hadi Naderiallaf, Kuldeep Singh, Sarang Satheesh, Gaurang Vakil, Chris Gerada
abstract: This paper presents a comprehensive investigation of glass-fiber wire insulation, evaluating its thermal life, electrical life, partial discharge (PD) behavior and the impact of combined thermal and electrical ageing on insulation lifetime. Thermal endurance tests were conducted to establish thermal life versus temperature characteristics. The impact of thermal ageing was assessed by monitoring dissipation factor and insulation capacitance, confirming progressive degradation within the insulation. The time-to-failure of each sample was estimated and statistically analyzed using Weibull probability distribution to develop life models. The model, derived at the 50% failure percentile, was compared with manufacturer data, showing close agreement with a percentage error of only 0.44%. Subsequently, electrical ageing tests were conducted to determine electrical life versus voltage excitations. PD measurements under PWM excitation were then carried out to evaluate the impact of pulse frequency, rise time, and duty cycle on the PDIV of glass-fiber insulation. Finally, the impact of combined thermal and electrical ageing on the life of glass-fiber insulation is evaluated using Miner’s law. A combined representative thermo-electrical stress profile is suggested under which the insulation has consumed approximately 96.3% of its lifetime, corresponding to 7,704 hours out of the targeted 8,000 hours lifetime.

title: Real Time Dynamic AES-256 Key Generation Using DWT Coefficients and Lightweight SHA-Inspired KDF for Secure Embedded Systems
authors: Vetcha Venkat Bhargav, R.K. Sharma
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: Real time encryption systems need to be able to handle a lot of data and have a lot of different keys. This is especially true for growing edge security, IoT, and audio applications. But traditional AES implementations use keys that don’t change very often or at all, and they have performance problems when dynamic key creation interrupts the encryption pipeline. To address this limitation, this study proposes a hardware design that employs a lightweight SHA based Key Derivation Function (KDF) to perform key expansion after acquiring per frame AES 256 keys from Discrete Wavelet Transform (DWT) coefficients and a device specific secret. This paper’s main contribution is a double buffered, fully pipelined KDF AES integration that combines encryption and key generation. This lets one block per cycle of continuous throughput after initialization without stopping the datapath. The suggested design has an AES 256 core with a separate key load interface, an asynchronous FIFO for clock domain isolation, a DWT feature extractor, an interleaving coefficient packer, and a KDF that is based on SHA. Experimental simulations show that small changes in the input audio lead to very different 256 bit keys, with an average inter key Hamming distance close to the best value of 128 bits. The pipelined method keeps real time operation at high clock speeds with less hardware overhead than standalone AES. These results show that the proposed solution is suitable for secure, context aware, audio dependent encryption in embedded systems with limited resources.

title: A Comparative Study of Transformer-Based Models for Multi-Class SMS Phishing Detection
authors: Farwa Khalid, Mahib Hussain, Iram Noreen, Zeshan Karim
publish: 2026 International Conference on Data Science, Machine Learning, and Intelligence (DataSciMI)
abstract: One of the emerging cybersecurity risks is SMS phishing, comprising of the spam, and smishing attacks, which use the popularity of mobile communication to obtain unauthorized access. Some of the common methods of detecting frauds like rule-based filters and classical machine learning paradigms do not apply suitably to adapt with the changing phishing trend and language differences. This paper includes a comparative analysis of five text classification models based on transformers BERT-base, RoBERTa, DistilBERT, DeBERTa-v3, and ELECTRA to detect multi-class SMS phishing cases. The experiments have been performed on the Balanced SMS Spam and Smishing Dataset of Mendeley Data (Bakshi & Patil, 2023) including 10,191 labeled SMS messages that are put in ham, spam and smishing categories. Each model was trained with the same preprocessing and hyperparameter environment and every model was tested. The measures of performance were based on accuracy, precision, recall and F1-score. The results show that BERT-base attained the accuracy of 97.79 and 0.9779 F1-score, ELECTRA reached t accuracy of 97.55 and 0.9755 F1-score, and DistilBERT reached accuracy of 97.35 and 0.9736 F1-score, which suggests that transformer-based models can be effective to detect SMS phishing. Although the lightweight models like DistilBERT can have lower computing needs that may be good in a real-time or resource-limited setup, comparisons with conventional baseline machine learning and issues of deployment are still in the future research.

title: Toward Intelligent Breast Cancer Screening Harnessing Capsule Networks for Robust Detection
authors: Samra Nawazish, Muhammad Zulkifl Hasan, Muhammad Amir Shahid, Noor Ul Hira, Nadeem Sarwar
publish: 2026 International Conference on Data Science, Machine Learning, and Intelligence (DataSciMI)
abstract: This paper examines the possibility of Capsule Networks (CapsNets) as a way of increasing the accuracy and reliability of breast cancer detection. According to the experimental work on the Wisconsin Diagnostic Breast Cancer data, the proposed CapsNet-based classifier is better than the selected baseline models (a CNN baseline and machine learning classical algorithms) in terms of discriminative performance of benign and malignant cases. This study aims at showing the best diagnostic features of CapsNets. Experiment measurement on accuracy, precision, recall and F1-score measurements prove that CapsNets is always better than the baseline models when distinguishing between benign and malignant cases. Moreover, graphical representation such as ROC, precision-recall curves underlines the versatility of CapsNets in the range of different thresholds, which highlights their ability to enhance the rates of early detection and low re-diagnostic rates. Even with such positive results, there still exist challenges. The lack of interpretability of the models, high levels of computational complexity, and generalization to different populations and imaging modalities remain a weakness to the widespread clinical use. The future studies must aim at improving the explainability, improving the computation efficiency, and utilizing the domain adaptation and transfer learning methods to improve the cross-domain resilience. Lastly, the work places Capsule Networks as a possible avenue of further research in feature-based intelligent diagnosis and challenges research on raw modalities of medical imaging. To help in transforming these models into clinical practice, explainability, computational efficiency and cross-domain evaluation are highlighted in future research.

title: Survey on Explainable and Ethical Use of Large Language Models in Telemedicine
authors: Rishi Raj, Yogesh Singh, Harshpreet Kaur
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: Introduction of Large Language Models (LLMs) into the process of telemedicine has reaffirmed the significance of Explainable Artificial Intelligence (XAI) and powerful ethical systems. The use of LLM is increasingly invested in learning to assist clinical reasoning, diagnosing, in managing the patients and making decisions, which requires transparency, interpretability, fairness, and accountability in their use. This survey provides the systematic review of recent research on explainable and ethical application of LLMs in telemedicine and captures the trend in model development, evaluation, and ethical compliance with standards, including GDPR, HIPAA, and the EU AI Act. It also outlines the main technical and ethical issues, and provides the ideas of the further trends in the development of reliable, patient-focused AI system in the medical field.

title: Platform Insensitive, Compact Flippable Near Field UHF RFID Tag, Compliant with ETSI / FCC bands
authors: Abhishek Choudhary, Deepak Sood
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: A compact flippable tag antenna of size (30 × 13 × 1.6) mm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sup> has been designed and characterized to work for UHF RFID near field communication in ETSI/FCC bands. The design incorporates meandering to achieve a compact tag and ensure proper impedance matching between antenna and chip. The designed antenna features a distinctive two-layered structure with meandered dipoles designed on both top and bottom layers. The RFID chip connected at one edge of the structure in between the two layers. The tag is designed for dual band operation (ETSI and FCC) through flipping technique. The tag works in the ETSI band with the top layer acting as the radiator and the bottom layer attached to the metal plate. When flipped, the opposite layer (bottom) functions as the radiator for the FCC band. The proposed tag is compared with previously reported works through its size, working band, read range, platform tolerance. With its dual band functionality and, platform insensitivity, the proposed tag eliminates the need for multiple tags or change in electronic product code (EPC) across intercontinental routes, making it highly suitable for item level tagging in global NF UHF applications such as Item-level tracking, Orthopedic implants, libraries and media.

title: Design of Doherty Power Amplifier For 6G Communication
authors: Tejendra Kumar Singh, Sukwinder Singh
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: This paper describes the simulation and design of a 7 GHz Doherty Power Amplifier (DPA) with the target output power of 15 W using the Advanced Design System (ADS) software. The MACOM CG2H80015D transistor is used in the design. To ensure unconditional stability the stability factor of the amplifier was calculated and verified. load pull analysis : Using load pull Analysis we were able to figure out the output impedance of the active device. Then matching networks were created and built to make the source and load impedances match the device. the DPA has a Power Added Efficiency (PAE) of 70.801 percent at the design frequency and an output power (Pdel) of 41.267 dBm. The gain recorded across the operational band is more than 11.267 dB. When the output power is turned down by 6 dB, the amplifier keeps a PAE of 51.17% and puts out 37.95 dBm of power. In this backed off state the gain is improved and is in excess of 13.95 dB over the operational bandwidth.

title: The Impact of Global Geopolitical Conflicts on Maritime Logistics Activities and Performance in Sri Lankan Apparel Exports Sector
authors: Uthpala G.A. J, Kavirathna C. A
publish: 2026 IEEE International Research Conference on Smart Computing and Systems Engineering (SCSE)
abstract: Global geopolitical conflicts increasingly disrupt maritime trade, creating volatility in freight markets, shipping schedules, and regulatory conditions that threaten exportdependent industries. This study examines how global geopolitical conflicts affect maritime logistics activities in Sri Lanka's apparel export sector and how these effects translate into firm performance outcomes. Data were collected through a structured questionnaire administered to maritime logistics-related professionals in apparel exporting firms, yielding 125 valid responses. The research model captures geopolitical conflict impacts through four dimensions, conflict-induced disruptions, supply chain and trade flow distortions, financial disruptions, and policy and regulatory uncertainties and analyzes their effects on operational and financial performance of Sri Lankan apparel companies, with maritime logistics performance (MLP) as a mediating variable. Using Partial Least Squares Structural Equation Modeling (PLSSEM), the results show that geopolitical conflict dimensions do not have significant direct effects on operational performance and have limited direct effects on financial performance, except for policy and regulatory uncertainties. However, MLP plays a critical mediating role, fully mediating the effects of all geopolitical conflict dimensions on operational performance and most effects on financial performance, while partially mediating the impact of policy and regulatory uncertainties. In addition, ordinal data analysis was conducted to evaluate the perceived effectiveness of resilience strategies adopted during geopolitical disruptions, providing insights into strategic priorities for future resilience planning. Overall, the findings emphasize maritime logistics performance as the key channel through which geopolitical shocks influence apparel export outcomes, highlighting the importance of logistics-focused resilience strategies.

title: GlucoMu: A Mobile Non-Invasive Blood Glucose Estimation System Using Low-Cost Dual-Channel PPG and a Lightweight Deep Spatio-Temporal Inference Network for Hyperglycemia Screening
authors: Karisma Trinanda Putra, Mahendro Prasetyo Kusumo, Sunardi, Shazana Dhiya Ayuni, Mustapha Barrow, Hsing-Chung Chen
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Consumer Technology (ICCT-Pacific)
abstract: To control hyperglycemic conditions, blood glucose concentration must be monitored frequently and continuously. However, conventional glucose monitoring is predominantly invasive, causing discomfort and raising infection risks, which limits its suitability for long-term, homebased use. In parallel, the rapid adoption of consumer electronics, including smartphones, smartwatches, and lowpower wearable sensors, has increased demand for unobtrusive, real-time health monitoring that is affordable, portable, and easy to use. In response, this study proposes a non-invasive blood glucose monitoring system based on photoplethysmography (PPG), designed for consumer-grade mobile and low-cost platforms and enabled by Internet of Things (IoT) connectivity to estimate blood glucose concentration. The proposed approach comprises: (1) the development of a non-invasive prototype for real-time blood glucose estimation; (2) the design of a novel algorithm for dualchannel PPG acquisition and feature extraction that can be effectively executed on an IoT-enabled microprocessor; (3) spatio-temporal feature learning from PPG signals by training an AI model across diverse blood glucose concentrations; and (4) a Deep Spatio-Temporal Neural Network (DSTNN)-based multi-head inference architecture (classifier and regressor) that maps PPG signals into six fine-grained glucose levels while simultaneously predicting glucose concentration. The dataset includes 685 subjects with a total of 3,425 samples to evaluate the proposed DSTNN. Experimental results indicate that the system reliably acquires and processes PPG signals, achieving a mean absolute error of 3.84% and a classification accuracy of 93%. These findings highlight the feasibility of deploying noninvasive biometric-driven glucose monitoring as a consumer-electronics-ready solution, enabling scalable, non-invasive, and home-based hyperglycemia screening while providing a practical reference for integrating IoT and AI into sustainable digital healthcare.

title: Bimodal Handover Disruption in Local 5G: Preliminary Measurement Study on a Campus Testbed
authors: Ryutaro Higa, Hiromu Shimoji, Kouji Nishimura
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Consumer Technology (ICCT-Pacific)
abstract: Local 5G is expected to underpin digital transformation in factories, campuses, and public services, yet mobility across small cells makes handover-induced service disruption a practical concern. This paper presents preliminary findings from an ongoing investigation into handover-induced service disruption in local 5G. We conduct controlled measurements along a repeatable mobility route, vary downlink load conditions (e.g., offered bandwidth and background traffic), and evaluate disruption as the pause in packet reception at the user equipment. We further correlate terminal-side observations with user-plane traces on Central Unit (CU) interfaces toward the source and target Distributed Units (DUs) to localize where time accumulates in the handover pipeline. The results show a two-regime (bimodal) disruption behavior whose longer regime occurs more frequently under higher load, and they reveal a timing gap between the CU's resumption of forwarding and the end-to-end recovery at the terminal, pointing to effects downstream of the CU and/or within the UE stack. This paper aims to clarify the condition-dependent nature of mobility interruption in local 5 G. At this stage, we report initial observations and propose directions for future research, including bandwidth-aware handover control and queue/buffer management.

title: Context-Aware Pipeline for Automated Code Review Using Large Language Models
authors: Fatma Abdalgalil, Gehan Sherif, Marwan Mohamed, Nasrah Maragaa, Doaa Shawky
publish: 2025 35th International Conference on Computer Theory and Applications (ICCTA)
abstract: Modern software projects are increasingly complex, placing significant demands on human reviewers during code review. While review is essential for ensuring quality, security, and maintainability, the rapid growth of contributions in large repositories often overwhelms reviewer capacity, leading to delays and inconsistent feedback. Existing automated tools, such as linters and static analyzers, provide only surface-level checks and fail to capture the nuanced, context-dependent aspects of review. This study introduces an AI-based code review agent designed to generate high-quality, actionable feedback from static analysis of real pull requests. The proposed system employs a context-aware pipeline with two specialized agents: a summarization agent that condenses source code into structured representations and a review generation agent that produces expert-level comments. To further enhance review quality, the system incorporates prompt-based experimentation, including chain-of-thought reasoning, role-based prompting, and multi-agent collaboration. Experiments across Python, C++, Go, and Java demonstrate that the context-aware agent consistently outperforms existing baselines in semantic alignment with human reviews and issue detection, effectively capturing logic, performance, security, and maintainability concerns. These results highlight the potential of combining structured reasoning, prompt engineering, and large language models to advance scalable and reliable automated code review.

title: Physics-Informed Neural Networks for Anisotropic Cardiac Activation Mapping
authors: Mohamed A. M. Gad, Sherif M. M. El-Gendy, Mazen A. A. Atlam, Ahmed S.A. Mohamed
publish: 2025 35th International Conference on Computer Theory and Applications (ICCTA)
abstract: Cardiac activation mapping is critical for guiding catheter ablation of arrhythmias but remains challenging due to the difficulty of reconstructing wavefront propagation from sparse intracardiac catheter scans. Existing methods often neglect the physical laws governing electrical signal propagation in cardiac tissues. Approaches that do incorporate physics rely on isotropic models, assuming constant conduction velocity in all directions, and thus fail to account for the influence of fiber orientation, leading to physiologically inconsistent activation maps. In contrast to previous physics-informed frameworks that assume isotropic conduction, this article introduces a Physics-Informed Neural Network (PINN) framework that integrates the anisotropic Eikonal equation to embed the underlying physics of cardiac conduction and explicitly model the effect of myocardial fiber orientation on wavefront propagation, enabling physiologically consistent reconstruction of cardiac activation maps that reflect these directional effects. The framework employs a dual-network architecture: one network estimates activation time <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\phi(\mathrm{x})$</tex>, and the other reconstructs the direction-dependent conduction velocity tensor <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$D(x)$</tex> from sparse intracardiac catheter data. Evaluated on a dataset with sharp conductivity transitions, the framework accurately reconstructed activation times (NRMSE: 1.67%) and captured key features of conduction anisotropy. This physics-consistent approach represents a significant step toward clinically reliable cardiac activation maps for ablation planning.

title: Cloud-Integrated Battery Health Monitoring and Predictive Analysis using AWS SageMaker
authors: Abijith D, Vasantha Kumar, C Harsha Vardhan Kumar, Gnana Swathika O.V
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: The increasing use of Electric Vehicles (EVs) and renewable power sources has raised the stakes for the development of efficient battery health monitoring systems. The traditional Battery Management System faces challenges due to the limitations of available storage and the processing capabilities. This report describes the development of a Battery Health Monitoring System coupled with the cloud processing capabilities based on the Amazon Web Services (AWS) infrastructure. The system uses AWS S3 for data storage and AWS SageMaker for analysis purposes. Based on the publicly available battery data from NASA, the system mimics real-life scenarios where the battery details of the EVs are sent continuously for instant analysis. Although the system is currently applicable for analysis purposes with the help of static data inputs, its architecture will easily integrate with the live inputs of future system applications. The results show strong mapping with the voltage-temperature factors and the feasibility of using the capabilities of the cloud for efficient battery health monitoring.

title: Physics-Guided Multi-Stage Enhancement for Underwater Vision: A Sequential Divide and Conquer Strategy
authors: Karan Agrawal, Vaibhav Krishna, Nagendra Pratap Singh
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: Collecting high-quality visual data is very important for making autonomous underwater navigation and subsea exploration possible. The aquatic medium, on the other hand, makes optical imaging very difficult. This is largely because it shows up as color casts that depend on wavelength, contrast collapse caused by back-scattering, and structural blurring caused by forward scattering. To solve these problems, this work proposes a modular, three-stage sequential enhancement pipeline that avoids the problems of traditional restoration priors and the difficulty of deep learning. The methodology begins with Robust Color Channel Stretching, which uses percentile-based statistics to get rid of strong blue-green tints while being immune to outlier noise like marine snow. It is based on the "Divide and Conquer" concept. Next is a Non-Linear Gamma Correction stage that brings back small details in areas of compressed shadow and keeps the overall brightness stable. The third step is to use Detail Sharpening via Unsharp Masking to make the high-frequency structural edges stronger. This is done by dispersing them.We used the UIEB dataset to fully test the suggested framework with a set of multi-dimensional measures, such as UCIQE, PCQI, and UIQM. The results reveal that our "parameter-free" method works really well at restoring chromatic balance and structural clarity, which makes it the best at patch-based contrast (PCQI) and perceptual quality (UIQM). The proposed method remains exceptionally computationally efficient for real-time implementation on Autonomous Underwater Vehicles (AUVs), significantly surpassing physics-based and CNN-based models in these critical domains while necessitating no trainable parameters.

title: RTL Design of Multi-Sensor Outlier Detection Engine using EMA-Based Statistics
authors: Ravi Kumar, Kundan Kumar, Shreya Swaraj
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: Embedded sensor systems operate in real time and require a continuous stream of accurate data from multiple sensors. In practical environments, sensor readings are often affected by noise, aging, interference, or sudden environmental changes. These effects introduce abnormal values, commonly known as outliers, which can degrade system performance or lead to incorrect decisions if they are not detected promptly. Software based outlier detection techniques often fail to meet strict real-time requirements due to variable execution time and increased memory usage. To address these limitations, this paper presents a fully hardware-based outlier detection system implemented at the register-transfer level (RTL). Many FPGA-based anomaly-detection systems in earlier work rely on neural-network or learning-based models. These approaches are powerful, but they usually need more hardware, more power, and larger memory, which makes them difficult to use in small or real-time embedded devices. In this work, a simpler hardware-focused method is adopted. The system is designed as a lightweight RTL-based outlier detection engine that uses exponential-moving-average statistics to estimate the mean and variance directly from the incoming sensor data stream. The proposed design uses exponential moving average (EMA) based mean and variance estimation to analyse sensor data in real time without storing historical samples. An adaptive threshold is generated dynamically using the estimated variance, allowing the system to adjust automatically to changing signal conditions. Multiple sensor channels are processed in parallel within a single clock domain, ensuring deterministic timing behavior. The entire system is implemented using synthesizable Verilog and verified through functional simulation, synthesis, timing analysis, and power estimation. Experimental results demonstrate correct statistical behavior, reliable outlier detection, and very low hardware resource utilization, making the proposed RTL-based EMA outlier detection system well suited for real-time embedded and multi-sensor applications.

title: Diabetes Prognosis using Machine Learning Algorithms
authors: Prashanth Ragam, Bhanu Chander Balusa
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: Food is the main essential part of the human life to survive. Currently people are preferring sugarless food to sugar content food. These precautions are taken to prevent diabetes in their blood. The count of diabetes patients are increasing in the recent period. Machine learning algorithms are used for predicting the diabetes positive patient. Past data of the patients such as Pregnancies, Insulin, BMI, Age etc. are used for predicting the patient is positive or not. The use of ML models for forecasting the outputs in the medical fields will create ease of resource development. Hospitals are developing according to generations. The change in the hospital fields with the help of artificial intelligence will create the best resourceful development in the medical fields, everything around us is having its development in artificial intelligence. The first objective of this work is to develop predictive models for diagnosing diabetes in patients using various machine learning algorithms. Specifically, we employed Multiple Logistic Regression (MLR), Artificial Neural Network (ANN), Naïve Bayes, Random Forest (RF), and Support Vector Machine (SVM) models. Among these, the MLR model achieved the highest accuracy, with a test score of 0.8334 and a mean squared error (MSE) of 0.0279.

title: Advances in Tunnel Field-Effect Transistor Architectures: A Comprehensive Review
authors: Sahil Hamid, Soumya Sen, Ashish Raman, Nitesh Kashyap, Angshuman Khan, Subarna Mandal, Nipun Sharma, Pankaj Kumar, Imon Shahriar, Tribid Roy Mazumder
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: The continuous scaling of MOSFETs presents numerous challenges, including Short Channel Effects (SCEs). When the length of the channel reduces, undesired current is generated in the OFF-state, leading to elevated leakage current and power dissipation. This condition also limits the Subthreshold Swing (SS), which is about 60 mV/decade.. To mitigate these limitations and sustain MOSFET scaling, the development of new MOS devices becomes imperative. Of them, the Tunnel Field Effect Transistor (TFET) shows promise as a substitute for conventional MOSFETs. Numerous TFET structures, such as dual gate TFET, p-i-n structure TFET, heterojunction TFET, surface tunnel transistors, junction-less TFET, nanowire TFET, and nanosheet TFET, are reviewed in this study along with physical parameters and their impact on device performance.

title: MMSE Estimation Enabled Full-Duplex Ambient Backscatter Systems
authors: B. Srikar Ranganath, Ribhu Chopra, Kumar Appaiah
publish: IEEE Transactions on Green Communications and Networking
abstract: Ambient Backscatter Communication (AmBC) is a wireless communication technology that allows devices to communicate without the need for any active radio frequency (RF) components by modulating ambient RF signals from other active sources. However, like regular wireless communication systems, the performance of AmBC systems is contingent on the availability of accurate channel state information (CSI) at the receiver. In this paper, we present a novel iterative MMSE based channel estimation technique for full duplex AmBC systems with an efficient pilot utilization scheme. We then use these MMSE estimates to derive expressions for the rates achievable over all the links in the considered AmBC system. Finally, using extensive numerical simulations, validate our derived results and show that the proposed algorithm outperforms the state of the art machine learning based channel estimation algorithms considered in the literature.

title: Hybrid Flying Capacitor Inverter with Simplified Self-Balancing Control and Reduced Power Losses
authors: Margarita Norambuena, Guillermo Huerta, Pablo Lezana
publish: IEEE Journal of Emerging and Selected Topics in Power Electronics
abstract: This paper proposes a novel hybrid converter that integrates a Flying Capacitor Converter with a two-level Voltage Source Inverter and employs a low-complexity modulation scheme. The modulation strategy inherently balances the voltage of the flying capacitors and applies a modified Space Vector Modulation (SVM) technique to optimize the output waveforms of the two-level inverter. The hybrid converter and its modulation strategy are experimentally validated using a 4kW prototype, which demonstrates fast and stable operation, improved harmonic performance, and reduced overall losses, achieving up to 20% efficiency improvement at low modulation indices and a significant reduction in current THD compared to a conventional 2L-VSI.

title: A Lightweight PUF-Based Authentication and Key-Establishment Protocol With SoC Implementation for Industrial IoT
authors: Chi Trung Ngo, Sang Tran, Van Khanh Pham, Jong-Phil Hong
abstract: This article presents a novel lightweight authentication and secure communication protocol and its energy-efficient system-on-chip (SoC) implementation for resource-constrained industrial Internet of Things devices operating in unattended and physically exposed environments. The proposed protocol supports device-to-server verification, device-to-device mutual authentication, and session key establishment without storing long-term shared secret keys in nonvolatile memory (NVM), thereby mitigating physical key-extraction and cloning attacks common in exposed industrial deployments. Device-specific keying material is regenerated on demand from an on-chip challenge-response pairs (CRPs)-based physical unclonable function (PUF). Formal security analysis using Mao–Boyd logic and Scyther-based verification demonstrates resistance to replay, man in the middle, impersonation, side-channel, and physical cloning attacks, while protocol-level evaluation confirms low computational cost achieved through exclusive use of symmetric cryptography and a single PUF evaluation per session. Building on the proposed protocol, an energy-efficient SoC architecture is implemented and evaluated. The fabricated design in a 28-nm CMOS process occupies 191 kGE and operates at 25 MHz from a 0.9-V supply. Measurement results show that the complete authentication and key-establishment procedure consumes 11.48 <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mathrm{\mu }$</tex-math></inline-formula>J. The implemented protocol achieves a 1.4× increase in total energy efficiency compared to state-of-the-art cryptographic architecture implementing DTLS and the double ratchet protocol. The security and robustness of the hardware implementation are further assessed through circuit-level analysis of the CRPs-PUF. These analysis results demonstrate the suitability of the proposed protocol and hardware architecture for scalable and long-lifetime industrial IoT deployments.

title: Unveiling Time-Dependent Dynamics in MFMIS FeFETs
authors: Simin Chen, Taewon Jin, Xinkai Sun, Gijun Ju, Zewei Wang, Jaehoon Han, Younghyun Kim
publish: IEEE Journal of the Electron Devices Society
abstract: Metal–ferroelectric–metal–insulator–semiconductor (MFMIS) ferroelectric field-effect transistors (FeFETs) using an amorphous indium–tin–zinc-oxide (a-ITZO) channel and Hf<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">0.5</sub>Zr<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">0.5</sub>O<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub> (HZO) ferroelectric layer are fabricated. By engineering the area ratio (AR = A<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">I</sub>/A<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">F</sub>) between the MIS transistor region and the MFM ferroelectric-capacitor region, the memory window (MW) can be continuously tuned while maintaining robust erase operation. To probe time-dependent behavior, single-sweep readouts are performed after program/erase pulses with a controlled delay time (T<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">delay</sub>). We found that, as T<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">delay</sub> increases, the threshold voltage (V<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">th</sub>) shift of the program becomes larger than that of the erase under pulsed conditions, producing a progressive reduction—and at low AR, a possible inversion—of the MW. This asymmetric delay dependence has not been reported previously for oxide-semiconductor MFMIS FeFETs. Guided by the experimental trends, we propose a band-diagram-based mechanism that explicitly incorporates oxygen-vacancy-related traps at the HZO/floating-gate interfaces. The model attributes the observed evolution to the interplay among ferroelectric polarization relaxation, charge trapping/de-trapping in the floating gate. These findings establish AR engineering and delay-aware operation as effective levers to optimize MW, retention, and erase behavior in AOS-based MFMIS FeFETs, paving the way for compensation-free display drivers and embedded non-volatile memory.

title: Expressing Emotion Through Heartbeat: A Soft Robotic Heart Integrated With a Soft Sensor for Arts in Robotics
authors: Fei Xiao, Ziyin Liu, Jinlong Xu, Zhuoheng Wei, Zhiyuan Sun, Xuanyang Xu, Jisen Li, Suxuan Jiang, Ning Ding, Shaomin Shen, Jian Zhu
abstract: The heartbeat is both a physiological signal and a universal expression of emotion. This work presents a pneumatically actuated soft robotic heart for interactive art. A fully 3D-printed, shielded soft capacitive bending sensor is integrated onto the heart’s surface, providing stable, real-time displacement feedback without affecting its motion. A lightweight multilayer perceptron (MLP) model estimates deformation from the sensor signal and enables closed loop control. Emotional states are selected via a graphical interface or inferred from ambient light to modulate target heart rates (0.5–3 Hz) for displacement control. Physiologically inspired trajectories derived from left ventricular volume data (Wiggers diagram) are reproduced at 0.5 Hz and 1 Hz. A state-switched proportional–derivative (PD) controller drives the heart to track these targets with good accuracy and robustness. In public exhibitions, the heart was paired with the poetic narrative “To My Love. ” The work shows how soft actuation, soft sensing, machine learning, and feedback control can be combined with physiological modeling and artistic expression in a single soft robotic platform.

title: Hybrid-Driven Nonlinear Model for Enhanced Current Prediction in Giant Magnetostrictive Transducers
authors: Xinyu Wang, Peng Guo, Qianming Xu, Chunwang Lv, Wei Jiang, Cheng Tang, Bing Gao, An Luo
abstract: Accurate current prediction is essential for system analysis, control, and health monitoring of giant magnetostrictive transducers (GMTs). However, the existing GMT models have insufficient accuracy and poor adaptability, due to limitations in measurable information, variability in external conditions, and the inherent nonlinearity of GMTs. To address these issues, this article proposes a hybrid-driven nonlinear model for enhanced current prediction in GMTs. First, an equivalent circuit model with controlled nonlinear components is constructed based on the physical mechanisms of GMTs. Second, a two-stage parameter identification strategy is proposed to improve the convergence speed and stability of the identification process. The zero-input response characteristics are utilized to assist impedance fitting for identifying linear model parameters under small-signal conditions, while the shape similarity features of the nonlinear response current are further introduced to assist traditional objectives for identifying nonlinear parameters under large-signal conditions. The proposed modeling method has been verified on a GMT operating within the 200–800 Hz frequency range. The results demonstrate that the proposed hybrid-driven nonlinear model can accurately predict the current of the GMT under excitation with a wide amplitude range and wide bandwidth.

title: Equivalence-Based Tuning of ADRC- and SRF-PLLs for Grid-Connected Systems
authors: Hafiz Ahmed, Adel Rahoui, Saif Ahmad
abstract: AC-DC power conversion is fundamental to grid-connected consumer technology, where phase-locked loops (PLLs) are typically used for real-time parameter estimation. This paper demonstrates that a linear active disturbance rejection control (ADRC)-based PLL is dynamically equivalent to a conventional synchronous reference frame (SRF)-PLL equipped with an inloop low-pass filter (LPF). This equivalence is established analytically through small-signal modeling and parameter mapping. Using bandwidth-parameterization-based tuning for the ADRC-PLL, equivalent proportional-integral (PI) control gains and LPF cut-off frequency for the SRF-PLL are derived analytically, while the inverse mapping–including cases based on the symmetric optimum method–is performed numerically by solving a third-order polynomial equation. Theoretical findings are validated through experimental results using a grid-connected AC-DC PWM rectifier. When tuned under equivalent conditions, both PLLs exhibit nearly identical dynamic performance. Experimental tests under non-ideal grid conditions reveal a performance trade-off: the ADRC-PLL offers greater robustness to high-order harmonics, whereas the SRF-PLL demonstrates superior performance under low-order distortion and voltage unbalance. These results provide a unified framework linking ADRC and SRF-PLL formulations, supporting the design of advanced PLL architectures that combine the strengths of both approaches.

title: Visual-Tactile Fusion Semantic Communication for Embodied Interactive Perception Systems
authors: Mingkai Chen, Qinyan Liu, Zhuorui Wang, Zhe Zhang, Lei Wang, Shahid Mumtaz, Shi Jin
abstract: Nowadays, with the advancement of intelligent systems, the integration of vision and tactile modalities is crucial to understanding the physical world. However, technical challenges exist, including multimodal feature fusion complexity, uncertainty in the wireless environment, and tactile data fidelity requirements. This paper introduces a novel visual-tactile fusion semantic communication framework for the embodied interactive perception systems. First, our multimodal feature fusion enhancement network strengthens tactile recognition and deepens semantic understanding through profound-level feature extraction. Then we present a semantic communication framework using a tactile knowledge base and the Contrastive Language Image Pretraining (CLIP) method to refine textual descriptions and enhance the precision of transmission of tactile semantic information. In addition, we train an end-to-end tactile semantic communication model with joint source channel coding optimized for perceptual communication. Moreover, we propose tactile signal reconstruction based on ImageBind, a multimodal large language model (MLLM), to achieve high-fidelity tactile signal reconstruction. Finally, our experiments confirm high similarity between the reconstructed and original tactile data in visual features and pressure distribution, validating the model's proficiency in semantic tactile reconstruction. Our multilevel optimization framework ensures low bit rate, high semantic fidelity, and realistic sense generation, providing a comprehensive solution for visual-tactile fusion in embodied interactive perception systems.

title: Hybrid Control Bumps Attenuation of Networked Switched Systems with Multi-Source Disturbances: A Bump-Related Adaptive Event-Triggered Method
authors: Siyuan Zhang, Yading Xie, Guang-Xin Zhong, Jian Li, Qingyu Su
publish: IEEE Transactions on Automation Science and Engineering
abstract: In this paper, the hybrid control bumps attenuation problem is investigated for networked switched systems subject to multi-source disturbances and event-triggering. First of all, a new hybrid bumpless transfer performance characterization is established to attenuate abrupt large hybrid control bumps jointly caused by triggering and switching. Secondly, by introducing the bump-dependent function with switched adjustable parameters, a novel bump-related adaptive event-triggered strategy is creatively established to improve hybrid bumpless transfer performance. Thirdly, the sampled-state-based switching strategy is designed to simultaneously eliminate the Zeno phenomenon of switching and remove the dependency on real-time information of system state, which is a necessary prerequisite for the classical state-dependent switching law to work. Then, sufficient conditions are derived to guarantee the asymptotic stability of the augmented system while achieving both H∞ performance and hybrid bumpless transfer performance. Moreover, we propose the hybrid bumpless transfer control scheme through the co-design of the switching rule, bump-related adaptive event-triggered strategy, and disturbance observer-based event-triggered controllers. Finally, the turbofan engine model is employed to verify the effectiveness and applicability of the presented method.

title: A Novel IoT-based Spatio-temporal Prediction and Resilience Optimization Method for Tunnel-Induced Ground Settlement
authors: Xinghui Dong, Jichao Li, Huanqi Zhang, Renjie Xu, Kewei Yang, Hongyan Dui
abstract: With the acceleration of urbanisation, the problem of ground settlement in tunnel construction has become a serious challenge. Aiming at the existing ground settlement monitoring and resilience management problems such as limited monitoring range, neglected spatio-temporal characteristics, and poor combination of prediction results and resilience management, this paper proposes a two-stage spatio-temporal prediction and resilience optimization for tunnel-induced ground settlement. Specifically, firstly, this paper constructs a comprehensive monitoring architecture integrating SBAS-InSAR technology and Internet of Things (IoT) technology, which realises accurate and comprehensive monitoring of ground settlement. Secondly, a two-stage spatio-temporal prediction method of ground settlement is proposed: based on the wide-area spatio-temporal data acquired by SBAS-InSAR technology, the spatio-temporal transformer model is used to make the preliminary prediction. Then, with the small-area variables collected by IoT, the parameter-seeking optimisation algorithm based on the Grid Search-Particle Swarm is used in conjunction with the Time Convolutional-Bidirectional Long and Short-Term Memory Network (GR-PSO-TCN-BiLSTM) model to correct the prediction error. In terms of resilience optimisation, this paper proposes a multi-stage resilience enhancement strategy based on ground settlement prediction, which combines prevention importance, degradation importance and recovery importance, aiming to maximise the resilience of tunnel-induced ground settlement area. Finally, an empirical analysis using the traffic along the Zhengzhou Metro as an example verifies the effectiveness of the proposed method. These results indicate that coupling wide-area remote sensing with local IoT correction can substantially improve settlement prediction accuracy and provide actionable guidance for maintenance prioritization, thereby enhancing the robustness and recovery capability of metro systems.

title: From MTJ to Access Path: System-Level Radiation Behavior of a 40-nm STT-MRAM
authors: Yantong Di, Zeying Ding, Haoran Du, Huanghui Wang, Hao Cai
abstract: Spin-transfer torque magnetic random access memory (STT-MRAM) has been widely recognized as a viable nonvolatile memory for space electronics, benefiting from the intrinsic radiation tolerance of its magnetic tunnel junctions (MTJs). However, the radiation response of the peripheral CMOS circuitry remains insufficiently characterized, and it may dominate the system-level reliability. In this study, a 40-nm 4-Mbit STT-MRAM macro was evaluated under heavy-ion and total ionizing dose (TID) irradiation. Under heavy-ion irradiation, no single-event latch-up (SEL) was observed up to 37.3 MeV<sc xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">•</small>cm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>/mg. Radiation-induced disturbances occurred mainly at the access level, including SEFI-like burst events, without persistent MTJ data corruption, indicating vulnerabilities along shared access paths and system-level access failures rather than independent cell-level bit flips. TID irradiation up to 100 krad(Si) induces only minor electrical-parameter variations without functional degradation. These results demonstrate that the radiation resilience of the evaluated STT-MRAM device is primarily constrained by the timing-critical peripheral access circuitry, highlighting the shared access paths as the key hardening targets for system-level robustness.

title: Topology and Hybrid-frequency Modulation of a Novel MMC-SST with Soft-switching Capability
authors: Yinyu Yan, Yichao Sun, Carlos A. Teixeira, Brendan P. McGrath, Donald Grahame Holmes, Xiong Yang, Zheng Li
abstract: The modular multilevel converter-based solid-state transformer (MMC-SST), containing a dual active bridge (DAB) converter connected to each individual submodule, presents high cost because of its large number of switching devices. A novel integrated switch pairs-based MMC-SST (ISP-MMC-SST) is proposed in this work, where a switch pair (i.e., half-bridge) in each submodule serves as both the conventional MMC submodule and half of the primary-stage of the DAB converter. This approach reduces the switching devices count by 47% compared to the traditional DAB-type MMC-SST and achieves a significant reduction of the SST cost. A hybrid-frequency modulation strategy that enables soft switching of the primary- and secondary-side switching devices using the superposition of carrier-frequency square-wave modulation signals to the fundamental-frequency modulation commands is also proposed. The superimposed square-wave modulation signals are arranged as differential-mode terms across the primary-side switching pairs to deliver power to the secondary side. The proposed topology, hybrid-frequency modulation, and mapping of soft-switching regions for various operating scenarios are validated using industry-scale converter simulations and experiments on a laboratory-scale converter prototype.
