title: Coordinated Formation and Resilience in Mobile Multi-Robot Systems: Consensus-Based Control in Dynamic Environments
authors: Esraa Hussien, Sameh Saad, Moutaz Hegaze
publish: 2025 35th International Conference on Computer Theory and Applications (ICCTA)
abstract: Since the late 1980s, research on Multi-Robot Systems (MRS) has aimed to improve efficiency, flexibility, and resilience over single-robot systems. These systems have become essential for tasks such as search-and-rescue, environmental monitoring, and industrial automation. However, many existing MRS solutions still struggle with fault tolerance and dynamic reconfiguration, leaving them vulnerable to failures and environmental disruptions. This work presents a multi-robot system composed of three identical differential-drive mobile robots and introduces a consensus-based control approach. The proposed method enhances formation stability and system adaptability in dynamic environments through three core innovations: (1) dynamic leader reassignment during failures, (2) integration of a standby leader to improve resilience, and (3) real-time obstacle avoidance combined with formation reconfiguration. Unlike conventional methods, the system can reassign leadership roles on the fly, allowing continuous operation during unexpected disruptions. The approach was implemented using the Robot Operating System (ROS) and tested in a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 5} \boldsymbol{\times} \mathbf{2 0 ~ m}$</tex> simulation environment in Gazebo, with RViz used for visualization. The robots reliably formed and maintained triangular formations under various starting conditions. During leader failure tests, the system preserved formation stability through automatic role switching and standby activation. Additional navigation experiments further confirmed its ability to avoid obstacles, adapt to changing scenarios, and maintain mission continuity. These results suggest strong potential for deploying the system in real-world applications such as emergency response, exploration, and automated industrial tasks.

title: Evaluating the Effect of Feature Binarization on 1D-CNN Based Intrusion Detection Systems
authors: Mohamed El-Sherif, Ahmed Khattab, Magdy El-Soudani
publish: 2025 35th International Conference on Computer Theory and Applications (ICCTA)
abstract: This paper investigates the impact of feature binarization on the performance of one-dimensional convolutional neural networks (1D-CNNs) for intrusion detection. Using publicly available security datasets, An optimized 1D-CNN model was trained, and the effect of applying binary encoding to non-binary features was evaluated. The results indicate that 8-bit encoding and higher provide a slight improvement in training and validation classification accuracy compared to the original floating-point features. In contrast, using fewer than 8 bits reduces classification accuracy but yields faster inference times, highlighting a trade-off between performance and efficiency. Furthermore, binarization was found to improve the detection of low-density classes, which are often underrepresented in security datasets. The proposed approach was validated on the NSL-KDD dataset for both binary and multiclass classification tasks. The best achieved accuracies in 16-bit encoding case were 99.46 % (binary) and 99.52% (multiclass).

title: Review of Tunnel Field Effect Transistors
authors: Abhishek Chauhan, Navneet Kumar, Hemaunt Kumar
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: MOSFETs are widely used devices in IC fabrications Due to their various advantages and its scaling abilities. But as the channel length reduces into nano-scale range, its performance degrades because of various factors such as SCE, Vth effects, tunneling effects etc. TFET is promising device in replacing the MOSFET as it has compatible technology, structure and improved short channel effects. TFET has BTBT conduction mechanism in comparison to thermionic emission of conventional MOSFET. In this paper we will study about the various TFET structures like DG TFET, Vertical TFET, Dopingless TFET, Nanowire TFET along with their advantages and physical significance in the development of next generation devices.

title: Content-Based Image Retrieval for Urban Scene Understanding: A Distance Metric Evaluation Using Deep Semantic Features
authors: Rajani Misra, Shilpa Sharma
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: Content Based Image Retrieval (CBIR) is increasingly important in computer vision-driven applications such as urban scene analysis and intelligent transportation systems, where large volumes of visual data must be searched efficiently. While deep convolutional neural networks (CNNs) have enhanced the discriminative power of image representations, the effect of similarity distance measures on retrieval performance has received limited attention, particularly in complex urban environments. This work investigates the role of commonly used distance metrics, Euclidean, Cosine, Minkowski, Manhattan, and Mahalanobis within a deep semantic CBIR framework. Semantic features are extracted using a DeepLabV3+ based network with a ResNet-50 backbone, designed to capture contextual and structural information from road-scene images. The proposed approach is evaluated on the CamVid dataset using Precision @K, mean Average Precision (mAP), and retrieval time as performance indicators. Experimental results show that Euclidean distance provides the most reliable balance between accuracy and computational efficiency, achieving a Precision@5 of 0.89 and mAP of 0.72. These findings emphasize the importance of metric selection when designing practical deep feature based CBIR systems.

title: Fluid Antenna Systems: A Geometric Approach to Error Probability and Fundamental Limits
authors: Xusheng Zhu, Kai-Kit Wong, Hao Xu, Han Xiao, Hanjiang Hong, Hyundong Shin, Yangyang Zhang
abstract: Fluid antenna systems (FAS) utilize position reconfigurability to improve spatial diversity in wireless communications. However, a rigorous framework for error probability analysis under spatially correlated channels remains absent. This paper fills this gap by deriving a closed-form asymptotic expression for the symbol error rate (SER). This mathematical expression establishes the fundamental scaling law between the error performance and the spatial correlation matrix. A key insight from our analysis is that the achievable diversity gain depends entirely on the effective rank of the spatial channel, rather than the total number of antenna ports. To quantify this effective rank, we propose a dual approach: a theoretical derivation and a geometry-based algorithm. Both methods rigorously prove that the effective rank converges to a fundamental limit of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$2W+1$ </tex-math></inline-formula>, where <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$W$ </tex-math></inline-formula> denotes the normalized aperture width. Specifically, the geometry-based algorithm extracts distinct performance thresholds from the eigenvalue spectrum of the channel. These thresholds perfectly match the derived theoretical limit. Furthermore, the proposed effective rank model demonstrates higher accuracy than existing approaches in the literature. Based on this robust framework, we offer a complete characterization of diversity gains and coding gains. The analytical results reveal a definitive design principle: enlarging the physical aperture increases the effective rank and drives performance improvements, whereas simply increasing port density within a fixed aperture yields diminishing returns.

title: Dynamic Spectrum and Power Allocation in C-V2X: A Multi-Agent A3QN Approach
authors: Hui-Tang Lin, Po-Chuan Lin
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Consumer Technology (ICCT-Pacific)
abstract: With the rapid development of Intelligent Transportation Systems (ITS), Cellular Vehicle-to-Everything (CV2X) plays a vital role in providing reliable communication for vehicles and infrastructure. However, dynamic environments and interference between Vehicle-to-Infrastructure (V2I) and Vehicle-to-Vehicle (V2V) links make resource allocation challenging. To enhance both spectrum efficiency and link reliability, this paper proposes an Attention-based Dueling Double Deep Q-Network (AD3QN) for joint spectrum and power allocation. The selfattention mechanism helps agents focus on critical states, while Hysteretic Q-Learning alleviates non-stationarity in multi-agent settings. A reward design combining V2I throughput and V2V transmission success ratio further improves reliability. Simulation results demonstrate that AD3QN outperforms existing D3QN- and DDQN-based methods in both V2I capacity and V2V reliability under various payload and velocity conditions.

title: Evaluating AI Mentoring in Cybersecurity Education with Multi-Turn Dialogues and LLM-as-a-Judge
authors: Moe Yamanaka, Keishiro Watanabe, Haruhisa Hasegawa
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Consumer Technology (ICCT-Pacific)
abstract: We propose an evaluation framework for AI-based mentoring systems in cybersecurity education that emphasize non-directive guidance through extended multi-turn dialogues. Conventional accuracy-based metrics cannot assess how effectively mentors guide thinking without providing direct answers. Our framework introduces a five-criteria rubric implemented through LLM-as-a-Judge to evaluate instructional quality by focusing on the learning process. Evaluation of 90 dialogues across diverse mentee types and dialogue turns demonstrates the framework's effectiveness in identifying system strengths and weaknesses. Analysis reveals that while RAG ensures reliable knowledge retrieval, the system requires enhanced adaptive hint delivery for mentees facing learning challenges. This work establishes a foundation for systematic assessment of process-oriented mentoring systems.

title: PigView: A Minimum Bounding-Box Pig Weight Estimation System Using Artificial Intelligence Deep Learning
authors: Wan-Jung Chang, Wei-Yuan Shih, Bo-Yun Tsai, Jian-Ping Su
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Consumer Technology (ICCT-Pacific)
abstract: This paper proposes a minimum bounding-box pig weight estimation system based on artificial intelligence deep learning, called PigView, which is used to evaluate and manage the growth status of pigs in pig houses. Using AI cameras equipped with onboard model inference capability, PigView can be easily and cost-effectively deployed in piggeries and enabling automatic pig weight estimation through image processing. Experimental results demonstrate that PigView effectively and accurately track pig weight variations and growth trends, enabling farmers to better manage feeding and production efficiency, thereby reducing costs and increasing profits.

title: Systematic Evaluation of Unimodal and Multimodal Fusion for Emotion Recognition
authors: Aabhash Mukherjee, Arpita Kumari, Nagesh Ch, Prabhat Singh
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: This Human emotion is by its very nature a multimodal phenomenon due to the complex relationship between visual and auditory data. Despite the increasing popularity of deep learning techniques in current research, fair evaluation across fusion algorithms and thoroughly evaluated, reproducible baselines are still inadequate. Despite their advantages, unimodal systems frequently fall short in practical situations where just one modality may be unreliable or noisy. The complete Ryerson Audio-Visual Database of Emotional Speech and Song (RAVDESS) dataset was analyzed in a systematic comparison analysis for the audio-visual fusion for emotion recognition in order to analyze this. Four different models were developed and evaluated: an audio-only baseline using Mel-Frequency Cepstral Coefficients (MFCCs) with a Random Forest classifier (52.53% accuracy), a video-only baseline using deep features from a pre-trained ResNet-18 with Random Forest (74.87% accuracy), a decision-level Late Fusion model (80.00% accuracy), and an Early Fusion model integrating normalized low-level features prior to classification. Our central hypothesis-that Early Fusion would yield superior performance was empirically validated, with the proposed model achieving 92.67% accuracy, a statistically significant improvement over all others. The results obtained show that developing inter-modal connections through early feature integration is a more accurate methodology for affective computing and empathetic interaction between humans and computers, and they offer a robust quantitative standard.

title: MyCustomBot: A Next Gen Search Engine for Customized Hyper Meta search of Public Domain Data in Conjunction with Secured User Private Data
authors: Kumud Majumder, Sardor Hazratov
publish: 2026 International Conference on Data Science, Machine Learning, and Intelligence (DataSciMI)
abstract: Information retrieval using web search engines (SE), and AI tools have become practically an essential part of our daily life. To improve the search quality, over the years various SE, meta SE, and meta-meta search engines have been developed. Yet certain obvious functionalities are clearly lacking in all publicly available search engines. A case in point is that no publicly available internet search tools allow user uploading of private or custom data thereby preventing value added personalized search from a combination of private and public domain data sets. Such search capabilities are highly valuable for medical patient data and several other areas. AI tools do allow uploading of private data, but all major AI systems have moderate to high data leakage and privacy risks. To overcome these lacunae, we have developed MyCustomBot, (https://www.mycustombot.com), a specialized search engine that performs cumulative meta search of public domain data and/or private domain data. A key feature of our tool is the very high level of data security and privacy afforded to the uploaded private data. This is achievable as private data is accessible only to the user that uploaded the data. Furthermore, unlike AI systems, private data is neither retained nor maintained on the system beyond the minimum duration needed for probing the data by user. MyCustomBot is not AI and has no model training steps. This makes data security of MyCustomBot superior to current AI tools. To highlight the unique functionalities of MyCustomBot that are not currently available in any public SE, we distinguish MyCustomBot as a "Hyper Meta Search Engine" or HMSE, essentially a next generation SE of higher order. Hyper meta search refers to the ability to perform not only meta search, but also be able to optionally search through previous search data and most importantly through secured private data. Since not all user searches require higher order search, MyCustomBot has options for simple quick search using SE of users’ choice without having to leave the portal. In this paper, we demonstrate the design and functionalities of MyCustomBot.

title: Deep Learning Method for Identifying Unhealthy Tomato Leaves
authors: Ashish Kumar, Shashank Gupta, Ranjeet Kumar Dubey, Alok Kumar, Vikramaditya Rajdeep, Vipin Kumar Chaudhary
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: Food is produced by agriculture, which also supplies resources for other industries. Hence, agriculture is essential for humanity. Consequently, it is our responsibility to ensure that the quality of the yield is up-to the mark. Therefore, the primary focus should be on protecting crops, particularly the leaves. Numerous traditional methods exist to address this issue; but they are tedious, time-consuming and inefficient. There comes a necessity to introduce new and efficient methods to address this problem with the emerging technology trends. This paper aims to study the existing system and provide a more accurate result by applying the deep learning model. The proposed VGG16+GAP architecture mentioned in this paper classifies the leaves as healthy or unhealthy based on their attributes of the leaf. The model has an accuracy of 94.65%, indicating its effectiveness in the early detection of diseased leaves. This approach offers a scalable and high-performance solution for identifying unhealthy tomato leaves.

title: Design, Implementation, and Analysis of 64-bit Hybrid Adders Using Carry Look-Ahead and Carry Select Techniques on FPGA
authors: Gouthami Valigonda, R.K.Sharma
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: This project focuses on the design, implementation, and analysis of various 64-bit adder architectures using Verilog, targeting the Xilinx Artix-7 FPGA. Three distinct types of adders were developed: a conventional 64-bit Ripple Carry Adder (RCA), a 64-bit Carry Look ahead Ripple carry adder composed of sixteen 4-bit Carry Look-Ahead Adder (CLA) blocks connected in a ripple fashion, and a 64-bit Carry Select Adder (CSA) made of sixteen blocks, each containing two 4-bit CLA units to handle carry-in values of 0 and 1. The primary goal was to explore architectural differences and validate the functional correctness of each design. All adders were implemented on the FPGA and interfaced with VIO (Virtual Input/Output) to apply inputs dynamically. The Integrated Logic Analyzer (ILA) was used to observe the output of individual 4-bit segments. The analysis phase focused on verifying that the final 64-bit sum output was accurate for all test inputs. It was observed that all three adder architectures produced the same correct output values for every test case, demonstrating functional consistency across designs. This project highlights the modular design of arithmetic circuits and the effective use of FPGA debugging tools for functional verification.

title: Design Optimization of N-channel Graphene MOSFET
authors: Sk. Fareed Basha, J. Sravanthi, G. Raviteja, U. Bhagyasri, A. Bhargav
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: The design and performance optimization of a single-gated N-channel Graphene MOSFET for low-power and high-speed nanoelectronic applications using COMSOL Multi-physics is presented in this paper. Graphene is a promising substitute for traditional silicon channel materials due to its remarkable carrier mobility and high thermal conductivity; however, its ON/OFF current modulation is limited by the lack of an intrinsic bandgap. Key device parameters, such as gate oxide thickness and channel geometry, are methodically optimized to improve electrostatic control in order to overcome this difficulty. The optimized device achieves a threshold voltage of 0.356 V and an ON/OFF current ratio of 1.2×10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">4</sup>, according to self-consistent numerical simulations based on the drift–diffusion and Poisson equations. When compared to unoptimized single-gate structures, the results show better switching behavior, lower power consumption, and improved current drivability. These findings confirm the potential of optimized single-gate graphene MOSFETs for future energy-efficient nanoelectronic and RF circuit applications.

title: Design and Analysis of High Performance MGDI-FS based Brent Kung Adder on the 45nm Technology for Low-Power VLSI Applications
authors: Abhishek Kumar, Bodile Roshan Mukindrao, Rohit Singh
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: This paper is all about the Brent Kung adder circuit based on the usage of Modified Gate Diffusion Input (MGDI) full swing for the low power, less PDP and high-speed operation. The MGDI circuit offers the benefits of reduced transistor count and switching activity without sacrificing the logic swing. This circuit can be applied at the prefix tree stage of the Brent Kung architecture to optimize the propagation and generate functions. The simulation results show that the MGDI-based Brent Kung adder circuit offers less area usage, less power dissipation and less PDP (power delay product) when compared with traditional CMOS and transmission gate adders.

title: Enhancing Deepfake Image Detection with Swin Transformer and Multi-Form Data Augmentation
authors: Mounisha Tripathi, Shubham Chaurasia, Priyanka Chaurasia, Mohd Amaan Asim, Shikhar Pandey
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: The rapid advancement of deepfake generation techniques poses significant and escalating threats to digital security, privacy, and the integrity of information. This paper presents a robust deepfake image detection framework that leverages the hierarchical representation capabilities of Swin Transformer V2, enhanced with a comprehensive, targeted data augmentation strategy. Our method is designed to address the critical challenge of generalizing across diverse deepfake variations, including face-swapping, attribute editing, and full-face synthesis. We employ a multi-stage training approach incorporating extensive data augmentation techniques—such as random facial region cutout, local cropping, cartoonization, and sketch transformation—to simulate a wide range of artifacts and improve model robustness. Experimental results on the challenging MultiFF dataset demonstrate that our approach achieves state-of-the-art performance with a detection score of 0.969±0.003, significantly outperforming EfficientNet baselines by 31.5% and other recent methods. The proposed framework shows strong potential for real-world deployment in digital forensics and content authentication applications, while maintaining a practical inference speed of 39.2 FPS on modern hardware.

title: An Intelligent Federated Learning-Based Task Scheduling Framework for Efficient Cloud Computing
authors: Upma Bansal, Puneet Banga
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: Nowadays, Cloud computing environments face important limitations in task scheduling due to heterogeneous workloads, non-independent and identically distributed (non-IID) data, and vigorously varying resource demands. These parameters often result in ineffective virtual machine (VM) resource utilization (RU) and improved energy consumption (EC). Conservative centralized scheduling and heuristic-based methods fight to scale efficiently in huge and decentralized cloud infrastructures. To identify these challenges, this research article introduces a federated learning (FL) enabled task scheduling framework for cloud VM environments, planned to improve RU and optimize EC. The research method introduces a hybrid particle swarm optimization-whale optimization algorithm (PSO-WOA) with a convolutional neural network (CNN) method. This research framework, each VM locally trains an optimized CNN utilizing task execution parameters, like CPU utilization, memory usage, task completion, and EC. The hybrid PSO-WOA method(Proposed) efficiently tunes the CNN hyperparameters, leading to rapid convergence, enhanced scheduling accuracy rate, and managed VM workload distribution. The performance of the research framework is calculated using the cloud task scheduling database and compared with other methods. The simulation outcomes determine that the researched PSO-WOA federated CNN framework attains the maximum accuracy, precision, etc., while importantly enhancing virtual machine RU and optimizing over EC.

title: Dynamic Graph-enhanced Event Refinement for Temporal Sentence Grounding of Micro-moments
authors: Mingjin Kuai, You Qin, Xiang Fang, Yiming Wu, Wei Ji, Roger Zimmermann
abstract: Temporal Sentence Grounding in Videos (TSGV) aims to localize video moments that are semantically consistent with a given query. Despite the proliferation of approaches to TSGV in recent years, existing methods still face limitations in localizing micro-moments in videos and struggle to capture meaningful fine-grained event information within them. To address these challenges, we propose a Dynamic Graph-enhanced Event Refinement (DGER) framework specifically designed for the Temporal Sentence Grounding of Micro-moments (Micro-TSG) task. This framework efficiently explores event-aware information among text queries, long videos, and micro-moments. Our approach begins with a Query-specific Event-aware module that includes a modality-specific encoding module, a multi-modal fusion module, and an event- and query-refinement processing module. These components comprehensively leverage fine-grained event information both within and across modal contexts. Additionally, we have designed a Dynamic Information Propagation module that automatically refines the structure of attentional regions based on the Graph Information Bottleneck (GIB) principle, enhancing the attentional mechanism's ability to efficiently extract key features from significantly short-depth signals. Extensive experiments demonstrate that the proposed method outperforms all state-of-the-art techniques on the Charades-STA and TACoS datasets, achieving comparable results on the ActivityNet Captions dataset.

title: Data-Driven Variable Polyhedral Uncertainty Set for Renewable Planning
authors: Wenda Chen, Hongxing Ye, Shuyi Zhang
abstract: With the high penetration of renewables, the uncertainty of renewable generation becomes an important factor in power system operation and planning. In the planning model, renewable installed capacity is a decision variable, which makes the uncertainty set variable. This letter presents a novel tractable approach with a variable polyhedral uncertainty set (VPUS) to capture the temporal-spatial correlations of renewable generation. A modified surrogate affine policy (SAP) is introduced to solve the problem. Numerical tests are conducted on the planning problem of the simplified transmission system. The results indicate that the proposed approach can reduce investment and operational costs for flexibility.

title: Likelihood-Based Modulation Classification for Full-Duplex SC-FDMA Relaying Communications
authors: Hala Mostafa
abstract: This study investigates the problem of automatic modulation classification (AMC) for uplink single-carrier frequency-division multiple-access (SC-FDMA) transmissions. The analysis is conducted in the context of full-duplex decode-and-forward (FD-DF) relaying systems. The interaction among modulation formats, multiple-access interference (MAI), multipath channels, and relative delays yields a tightly coupled maximum-likelihood (ML) problem that is computationally prohibitive for practical implementation. To overcome this difficulty, we propose a novel low-complexity space-alternating generalized expectation–maximization (SAGE) solution that enables joint parameter estimation. By exploiting the inherent characteristics of FD-DF signals, the proposed approach achieves a substantial reduction in computational burden while preserving high modulation classification accuracy. Simulation findings demonstrate that the proposed algorithm delivers robust and reliable performance compared to existing techniques under realistic operating conditions.

title: Active Compensation Using Physics-Informed MPC for Real-Time Path Accuracy Improvement in Robotic Milling
authors: Qingyu Peng, Wei Xu, Wenlong Li, Hanyu Zhang, Ruixi Zou, Han Ding
abstract: Robotic milling suffers from low contour accuracy because limited structural stiffness induces end-effector deflection. Existing methods mainly rely on passive strategies such as posture optimization, which reduce errors by improving robot stiffness through configuration adjustment. However, these methods reduce deflection through stiffness improvement rather than direct error compensation. Some studies attempt to predict deflection by simulated milling force and robot stiffness model, but time-varying milling forces make the deflection difficult to estimate in advance, limiting the effectiveness of compensation. To address this limitation, this article proposes a novel control method to enhance machining contour accuracy. The proposed method integrates a physics-informed model to predict stiffness-induced deflection during machining. Specifically, physical priors-stiffness distribution similarity and Hooke’s law are embedded into a dedicated kernel within a multioutput framework, enabling high-fidelity deflection prediction. Building on this model, an event-triggered scheme with confidence-ellipsoid-based probabilistic constraints is introduced to ensure robustness against uncertainty. Furthermore, this event-triggered mechanism adaptively updates the controller’s Jacobian matrix, achieving a balance between computational efficiency and control accuracy. Extensive simulations and physical experiments validate the proposed method, demonstrating contour accuracy improvements of 57.35% and 53.13% for two distinct aircraft skin parts, respectively.

title: Efficiently Computable Limits on EPR Pair Generation in Quantum Broadcast Channels
authors: Patrick Hayden, Debbie Leung, Hjalmar Rall, Farzin Salek
abstract: We investigate the generation of EPR pairs between three observers in a general causally structured setting, where communication occurs via a noisy quantum broadcast channel. The most general quantum codes for this setup take the form of tripartite quantum channels. Since the receivers are constrained by causal ordering, additional temporal relationships naturally emerge between the parties. These causal constraints enforce intrinsic no-signalling conditions on any tripartite operation, ensuring that it constitutes a physically realizable quantum code for a quantum broadcast channel. We analyze these constraints and, more broadly, characterize the most general quantum codes for communication over such channels. We examine the capabilities of codes that are fully no-signalling among the three parties, positive partial transpose (PPT)-preserving, or both, and derive simple semidefinite programs to compute the achievable entanglement fidelity.We then establish a hierarchy of semidefinite programming converse bounds—both weak and strong—for the capacity of quantum broadcast channels for EPR pair generation, in both one-shot and asymptotic regimes. Notably, in the special case of a point-to-point channel, our strong converse bound recovers and strengthens existing results. Finally, we demonstrate how the PPT-preserving codes we develop can be leveraged to construct PPT-preserving entanglement combing schemes, and vice versa.

title: A Resonant Period Tracking Control Scheme for Flyback Converter to Achieve ZVS With Reduced Switching Frequency Variations
authors: Kuang Wang, Xinbo Ruan, Lingxuan Xiao, Fei Liu
abstract: The conventional zero-voltage-switching (ZVS) flyback converters suffer from wide switching frequency variations or require auxiliary switch. This article proposes a resonant period tracking control scheme for flyback converter, with which the number of resonant periods is selected according to the load to reduce the switching frequency variations, while the synchronous rectifier (SR) tracks the resonant period and generates an appropriate negative magnetizing current to realize ZVS for the primary switch over the full input and load range. The operating principle is analyzed, the ZVS conditions for the power switches are discussed, and the implementation of the proposed control scheme is given. Finally, a 65-W GaN-based prototype is built and tested in the lab. The experimental results are provided to verify the effectiveness of the proposed control scheme.

title: DeCoral: A Fast Low-to-High-Resolution Denoising Diffusion Feature Learning Architecture for Coral Reef Classification in Underwater IoT Systems
authors: Liang-Bi Chen, Xiang-Rui Huang
abstract: Underwater image classification (UIC) within Internet of Things (IoT) systems faces significant challenges, such as color casting, turbidity, and blurring, which reduce image quality and semantic accuracy. To address these issues, DeCoral, a fast low-to-high-resolution denoising diffusion feature learning architecture that is designed specifically for detailed coral reef classification in underwater IoT (UIoT) environments, is presented. DeCoral includes a deterministic denoising diffusion process that is conditioned based on underwater degradation priors, where the noise schedule is based on the combined effects of color casting, turbidity, scattering, and blur. Instead of using random Gaussian initialization, DeCoral employs a Dirac-delta deterministic startup scheme based on real underwater conditions, which accelerates convergence and prevents instability in the reverse diffusion process. Architecturally, DeCoral features a DeCoral-core encoder–decoder network that captures and reconstructs high-resolution features using a hybrid strategy involving convolution, multihead self-attention (MHSA) mechanisms, residual MLP attention, and FPN bottlenecks. The encoder estimates underwater noise and degradation cues, whereas the decoder recovers fine structural details for accurate coral identification. The results of extensive experiments conducted with public underwater coral datasets reveal that DeCoral achieves 95.23% accuracy, 95.46% precision, and 96.13% recall and outperforms existing convolutional neural network (CNN), transformer, and diffusion-based models, with a lower computational complexity of 63.09 GFLOPs. The architecture provides a robust and efficient solution for diffusion-based feature learning in underwater imaging, thus improving intelligent marine monitoring and precision coral reef conservation in artificial intelligence of things (AIoT) systems.

title: Deterministic Modeling of Indoor Location-Compliance States Using Binary Matrices
authors: Artur Arciuch
abstract: This paper presents a deterministic, matrix-based framework for evaluating indoor location-compliance states and detecting unauthorized object placements. Allowed object–location relationships and observed presence data are encoded using binary matrices, and compliance is assessed through logical operations that identify violations without relying on probabilistic inference or machine-learning techniques. Time-stamped observations are processed incrementally, enabling the system to record only meaningful state changes and maintain linear time and space complexity with respect to the number of objects, monitored zones, and observation intervals. Experimental results demonstrate that lightweight sensing mechanisms—such as BLE-based RSSI and Angle-of-Arrival measurements—provide sufficiently stable presence information to support deterministic compliance-monitoring workflows. Although the experiments do not quantify localization accuracy, they confirm that simple and widely available technologies can reliably supply the binary presence data required by the proposed method. Owing to its transparency, reproducibility, and low computational overhead, the approach is well suited for real-time indoor monitoring, audit support, and rule-based detection of unauthorized presence in smart-building environments.

title: Stair-Climbing Strategy in a Novel Wheel-legged Hybrid Robot
authors: Wei-Ting Chen, En-Chieh Tsui, Wei-Shun Yu, Shao-Yu Chien, Bo-Rui Chen, Pei-Chun Lin
abstract: Stair climbing remains one of the most challenging tasks for autonomous mobile robots, especially in environments with varying step dimensions and limited foothold stability. Existing wheeled robots exhibit high energy efficiency but lack adaptability to discontinuous stairs, while legged robots offer superior stability at the cost of high mechanical and control complexity. To tackle this problem, this paper reports on an adaptive stair-climbing strategy based on a circular finite-state machine (FSM) concept for a novel Wheel-legged hybrid robot, Kilin. The proposed method decomposes the stair-climbing process into three steps — Enter-stair, Mid-stair, and Leave-stair — and divides each step into four states — Fall prevention, Leg rotation, Body raising, and Riser detection — to ensure stability during transitions. The strategy was validated through extensive experiments on various indoor and outdoor stairs of different dimensions. The results demonstrate a high climbing success rate and, in some cases, up to 30% reduction in pitch angle, significantly improving comfort and ease of control compared to conventional legged robots. The proposed approach enables reliable stair traversal and shows strong potential for multi-terrain mobile robot applications.

title: Calibration-Free Online Detection in Wearable Motor Imagery Brain-Computer Interfaces
authors: Zuguang Rao, Zilin Lu, Jing Xiao, Kendi Li, Mengnan Zhu, Zijin Guan, Yantao Chen, Hui Cao, Yuanqing Li
publish: IEEE Transactions on Neural Systems and Rehabilitation Engineering
abstract: Motor imagery brain-computer interfaces (MI-BCIs) remain challenging for practical use due to their reliance on multi-channel EEG devices and long calibration. To address these limitations, we proposed a wearable system for calibration-free online decoding using a lightweight, few-channel EEG headband, enabling portability, ease of use, and rapid setup. Specifically, we first built a large-scale wearable MI-EEG dataset from 100 healthy subjects to train a subject-independent model. We then developed a CNN-based temporal convolutional network (CTCNet) for online MI detection, which reduced computational complexity while maintaining high decoding performance. Furthermore, we introduced a supervised self-training (SST) strategy that leverages labeled online data and progressively fine-tunes a pre-trained subject-independent model, enabling calibration-free BCI operation without offline calibration. Four online experiments were conducted, involving 25 healthy subjects (Experiments II-IV) and 10 stroke patients (Experiment V). With the SST strategy, the accuracy of the subject-independent model improved from 69 % initially to 81 % after the first update and further increased to 86% after the second update, surpassing the subject-specific model (80%). Stroke patients exhibited a similar improvement trend. Moreover, simulated experiments confirmed the superiority of the subject-independent model compared to training from scratch. These findings demonstrate the effectiveness of the wearable MI-BCI system based on SST and CTCNet for online MI detection and highlight its substantial potential for motor recovery in stroke patients.

title: A dual-port, smartphone-linked, pocket-size fluorimeter for rapid molecular diagnostic assays at point-of-care
authors: Han Keun Lee, Jongwon Lim, Skye Shepherd, My Thi Tra Nguyen, Lucas Akin, Amanda Bacon, Yasmine Sidavi, Max Shurgalin, Alban Cobi, Andriy Nikolayenko, Vladimir Fuflyigin, Rashid Bashir, Xing Wang, Brian T. Cunningham
abstract: We present the design, testing, and demonstration of a portable, pocket-size, smartphone-linked fluorimeter called the "VPodDuo." The instrument is capable of reading the output of several fluorescence-generating biomolecular detection assays with sensitivity that is similar to larger and more expensive laboratory-based instruments. In this work, we focus upon demonstrating the capability for readout of assays used to detect target nucleic acid sequences associated with infectious pathogens and cancer with incubation times of approximately 10 min. The VPodDuo features a dual-port configuration that allows simultaneous measurements of a negative experimental control in parallel with the test sample. We demonstrated compatibility with several assay protocols, including reverse transcription loop-mediated isothermal amplification (RT-LAMP), recombinase polymerase amplification (RPA), clustered regularly interspaced short palindromic repeats (CRISPR)/Cas, and the target recycling amplification process (TRAP). Benchmarking against three commercially available fluorimeters showed comparable detection limits for clinically relevant nucleic acid sequences, including Zika virus (ZIKV, 104 copies/μL ), Staphylococcus aureus (MSSA, 102 copies /μL ), human immunodeficiency virus (HIV, 6.83 pM ), a lung cancer-associated circulating tumor DNA sequence (L858R point-mutated EGFR gene, 29.2 pM ) and a microRNA biomarker associated with lung cancer (miR-375-3p, 500 pM ). We further validated the VPodDuo’s performance under varying ambient temperatures through in-lab simulations and real-world outdoor testing using the TRAP assay for miR-375-3p detection, demonstrating cancer associated biomarker detection at POC settings. With its compact form-factor, low cost, portability, and real-time data transmission and analytical capabilities, the VPodDuo represents a promising solution for expanding access to rapid, on-site molecular diagnostics in diverse clinical and field settings.

title: A Review on the Role of Generative AI and Large Language Models in Telemedicine Diagnosis and Clinical Triage
authors: Asad Ali, Kundan Kumar, Harshpreet Kaur
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: In recent years, telemedicine has expanded at a very high pace, and the emergence of Generative has added to this. Large Language Models (LLMs) and Artificial Intelligence (AI) is incorporating a. new dimension to the remote care delivery. This review looks at how the technologies are being utilized in telemedicine especially in. workflow, diagnostic support, and clinical triage. Drawing In the literature of late, we discover that systems based on LLM are highly promising indeed. in enhancing the accuracy of diagnostics and administration workload on. clinicians. With that said, actual real-life adoption is still constrained by a number of things. unremitting anxieties, hallucinated outputs, biased training data, patient privacy is at risk, and the challenge of trusting that clinicians do. suggestions of non-transparent models. Both the opportunities and the disadvantages are weighed in this paper. flaws in implementing LLams in clinical telemedical practices and proposes. the argument of domain specific validation, a more explicable model and prudent governance systems can be put in place in advance to massive deployment. justified.

title: A Branch Line Coupler for 5G Applications
authors: Sadhana Singh, Aijaz M. Zaidi
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: This research proposes a branch line coupler for 5G applications. The branch line coupler employ a two-section coupler topology and design on the Roger4350B of thickness 20 mill. The coupler has been designed on Advanced Design System of Keysight Technologies for band n78 band of 5G (3.3 – 3.8 GHz) that is allotted to India. The simulation results of the coupler shows that it achieves a return loss better than 19 dB and maintains an insertion loss of 3.0±0.5 dB over the band.

title: State-of-Health Prediction of Lithium-Ion Batteries Using Physics-Informed Inverted Attention Transformer
authors: Sonti Thirupathamma, Sudha Jyothi Veerapaneni, Vani Chillabanda, Vishnu Vardhan Reddy Pothireddy, Swarnalatha Kuna, Sudheer Babu Devarapalli, Anuj Pradeep Deshpande, Sibendu Samanta
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: Accurate and stable prediction of the State-of-Health (SOH) of lithium-ion batteries is essential for extending battery lifetime, preventing catastrophic failures, and enabling predictive maintenance in electric mobility and energy storage applications. Conventional machine-learning approaches rely heavily on historical feature engineering, whereas deep recurrent models struggle to represent long-range temporal dependencies and produce smooth degradation trajectories. This paper proposes a unified transformer-based approach for cycle-resolved SOH estimation using physics-informed deep learning. Three transformer variants are evaluated on two widely used public datasets: the Oxford Battery Degradation Dataset and the NASA battery Aging Dataset. A baseline iTransformer is introduced for direct sequence modeling of voltage, current, temperature, and cycle time. A time-informed iTransformer integrates positional encodings to explicitly model temporal patterns in capacity fade. Finally, a physics-informed variant incorporates a smoothness regularization term based on second-order discrete derivatives to enforce physically meaningful degradation behavior. Experimental results demonstrate that the physics-informed iTransformer achieves superior accuracy, smoother prediction curves, and improved cross-dataset generalization. Compared with the baseline, The physics-informed model reached a MAE of 0.309, RMSE of 0.188, and R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> of 0.95 on the Oxford dataset. Cross-dataset experiments on the NASA benchmark further confirm that physics guidance significantly reduces overfitting and improves real-world robustness. The proposed approach provides a scalable foundation for coming generation physics-consistent deep learning strategies for electrochemical modeling and battery management systems.

title: Hybrid Error Correction Encoding for DNA Data Storage
authors: Rajlakshmi Gurumayum, Aditya Krishna Jaiswal, Rakesh Biswas
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: The ability of DNA to shrink into a very compact and robust format for the storage of a massive amount of data. Nevertheless, the utility of DNA is further hampered due to a very high error rate, especially substitution, insertion, and deletion (Indels), coupled with the constraints of biochemicals, such as GC balance and homopolymer size constraints. The classical method of correcting the error deals with the error caused by substitution and deletion independently. In this manuscript, the Hybrid Error Correction (HEC) pipeline is proposed, which incorporates Reed-Solomon (RS) coding and the HEDGES coding scheme to counter the two main sources of errors in the DNA sequences: indels and sequence constraints, as well as the third source: errors in the DNA symbols, which are taken care of by RS coding.We propose the first FPGA implementation of the HEDGES algorithm, in Verilog as part of a pipelined hardware encoder. The system includes strand-specific encoding, dynamic salt value production, and an overall code rate of 1.45 bits per nucleotide. Error correction up to 10% mixed errors is possible without altering the GC content or the homopolymer constraints suitable for DNA production and sequencing.

title: Simulation Study on PID and LQR Control Strategies for Quadcopter Trajectory Tracking
authors: Gopika S N, Devadath S, Anshu Abhishek Raj, Anoop Sasidharan
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: Quadcopter unmanned aerial vehicles exhibit non-linear, coupled, and underactuated dynamics, which make accurate trajectory tracking a challenging control problem. This paper presents a simulation-based comparative analysis of Proportional–Integral–Derivative (PID) and Linear Quadratic Regulator (LQR) controllers for quadcopter trajectory tracking. Both controllers are implemented under identical conditions and evaluated using multiple reference trajectories, in MATLAB/Simulink. Simulation results indicate that the PID controller achieves lower cumulative tracking error for continuously varying and curved trajectories, while the LQR controller exhibits steady-state tracking errors that accumulate over time due to the absence of integral action. However, despite this limitation, the LQR controller demonstrates significantly faster transient response and settling behavior compared to the PID controller. For piecewise-linear trajectories, the performance difference between the two controllers is reduced. The results highlight the influence of controller structure on trajectory tracking performance and emphasize the limitations of standard LQR control for tracking time-varying references without integral augmentation.

title: Artificial Intelligence and Fuzzy based Simulations and Virtual Reality for Educating Contemporary Social Movements and Decision-Making Process
authors: Dhenesh Raj, Pankaj Kumar, Surayyo Eshboyeva, Abduganiyev Nazirjon, Dinara Elchieva, Nazarova Nigina
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: To enhance the process of teaching and decision-making concerning the contemporary social movements, the present research proposes a hybrid architecture, integrating virtual reality, fuzzy-based simulations, and artificial intelligence. The volatile nature of the current trends created by the speedy development of the modern movements with the help of digital communications, the changing general view, and the complex socio-political relations makes the usual tools of analysis unable to cope with the matter. The proposed hybrid system leverages VR to engage with the learning through immersion and a situation-based learning, fuzzy logic to deal with the uncertainty in human behavior and AI to make predictions and forecasts. All these factors together create an exciting environment where the user can evaluate the policy reaction, determine the nature of protests, and see the instant impact of calculated actions. Experimental results have shown that hybrid models are more realistic, accurate and stable than single methods. This framework can serve as an innovative tool that can help researchers, legislators, activists, and law enforcement to better understand movement behavior and assess interventions as well as improve situational awareness. Within the framework of everything that has been mentioned, the research demonstrates how the combination of immersive technology and computational intelligence can significantly enhance the educational process of social movement and departmental decision-making.

title: Disaster-Responsive and Emergency-Aware Traffic Management for Autonomous Vehicle Networks
authors: Wania Tahir, Kou Yamada
publish: 2026 International Conference on Data Science, Machine Learning, and Intelligence (DataSciMI)
abstract: Through traffic simulation and controlled system majorly for the development of Autonomous vehicles, which is operating at the time of high priority of emergency vehicle and the natural disasters. The recommended framework extends standard Intelligent Driver Model for vehicle preemption of emergency transportation and actions of law enforcement, which enable realistic modeling of the diverse and vast behavior of vehicles. Furthermore, it integrates disaster awareness, management of the traffic functional module that modifies the traffic lane provisions, timing of the traffic signals, and directing according to the existing danger circumstances. The implemented architecture aims to implement the system which composes self-directed agents i.e. emergency vehicles and other emergency responses at time of traffic for best possible flow and protection. The result of Simulation aims to demonstrate background knowledge of operating substantial interferences such as traffic congestion, communication failure and evacuation circumstances. The tentative results suggest substantial developments in other areas, such as disaster response time, optimization of route, and congestion control as assessed with predictable urban transportation management systems. The adaptive control logic also enables scalable deployment across regional and metropolitan transportation networks enabling resilient mobility during a crisis. The adaptive control logic also enables scalable deployment across regional and metropolitan transportation networks enabling resilient mobility during a crisis. By incorporating self-control, emergency driven, and disaster resistance into a single framework, the conducted research improves smart transportation systems.

title: FraudSight: A Comprehensive Analysis of Machine Learning and Deep Learning Models for Credit Card Fraud Detection
authors: Ayesha Jaffar, Abdul Rehman Bin Waseem, Muhammad Amir Shahid, Junaid Nasir Qureshi, Noor Ul Hira, Alina Akhtar
publish: 2026 International Conference on Data Science, Machine Learning, and Intelligence (DataSciMI)
abstract: The rapid growth of e-commerce has led to an increasing reliance on plastic money, resulting in a surge in credit card fraud worldwide. As the use of credit cards rises, so does the number of fraudulent transactions, which are often interwoven with legitimate ones, making detection challenging through traditional methods like pattern matching. Consequently, financial institutions prioritize the development of accurate and efficient fraud detection systems. To address this issue, various machine learning (ML) and deep learning (DL) techniques have been applied to the latest dataset of European cardholders' credit card transactions from 2023. A comparative analysis of ML and DL models was conducted, revealing that the Random Forest classifier, K-Nearest Neighbours (KNN), Gaussian Naïve Bayes (GNB), and Artificial Neural Network (ANN) models demonstrated superior performance in detecting credit card fraud.

title: Practical Distributed Inter-Media Synchronization in Digital TV Environments
authors: Marcos A. A. de Oliveira, Anderson O. Dantas, Mateus Said, Aguinaldo Silva, Rômulo Fabrício, Eddie de Lima Filho
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Consumer Technology (ICCT-Pacific)
abstract: Digital TV (DTV) media content is encoded according to the Moving Picture Experts Group's standards, and inter-media synchronization is achieved using a unique clock reference together with a set of presentation timestamps distributed throughout different media streams. Consequently, if everything occurs within the same device, i.e., a DTV receiver, global synchronization is achieved. However, if a specific media stream that is part of a DTV program is handled by another device, such as a smartphone, synchronization with other media components is no longer possible, which hinders its use as a companion processor. This work addresses such a problem and proposes a practical framework that keeps inter-media synchronization for video decoded in DTV receivers and audio decoded in peripheral devices. Its implementation demonstrated its feasibility, paving the way for enhancing the DTV user experience without requiring hardware modifications or updates in current commercial devices.

title: A Handwriting Assessment Algorithm for a Tablet-Based Handwriting Tool
authors: Zharlaw Calimpon, Jan Francesca Logroño, Josephine E. Petralba, Eva Divinagracia
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Consumer Technology (ICCT-Pacific)
abstract: Educators face increasing challenges in providing consistent and objective handwriting assessments, particularly in inclusive classrooms with diverse learning needs. Traditional methods often rely on subjective evaluation, leading to inconsistencies and delayed interventions. This paper presents the design and evaluation of a handwriting assessment algorithm integrated into a tablet-based handwriting tool. The algorithm aims to quantify handwriting accuracy by analyzing stroke data collected from a stylus-enabled device. Handwriting accuracy was evaluated by comparing user-drawn strokes with predefined templates using Euclidean distance, with normalization and inversion applied to generate accuracy scores between 0 and 1. Final accuracy metrics incorporated stroke path, start point, and end point alignment to provide a comprehensive performance assessment. Preliminary results shows system's effectiveness in capturing fine-grained variations in handwriting. It demonstrates the algorithm's potential for real-time feedback and performance assessment in handwriting learning and rehabilitation applications.

title: Evaluation of Multi-Valued Chirp Sequences on Software-Defined Radio
authors: Manato Adachi, Kanta Arase, Yuta Ida, Yasuaki Ohira, Sho Kuroda, Takahiro Matsumoto
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Consumer Technology (ICCT-Pacific)
abstract: In recent years, Internet of Things (IoT) technologies are rapidly grown. IoT devices do not require large-capacity and/or high-speed transmission because they typically operate on small batteries. To meet these requirements, chirp-based methods are effective. A chirp signal varies its frequency over time and can be applied to sequence design. Previously, we have designed a simple sequence based on chirp characteristics. However, this method is not evaluated with hardware. Therefore, in this paper, we evaluate the multi-valued chirp sequence on software-defined radio (SDR).

title: Ensemble Driven Insight Benchmarking Classical and Ensemble Machine Learning Models for Multi Aspect Misinformation Classification
authors: Usman Mustafa, Muhammad Abdullah Butt, Syed Hazik Abbas Jaffri, Abdullah Hassan, Ghulam Mustafa
publish: 2026 International Conference on Data Science, Machine Learning, and Intelligence (DataSciMI)
abstract: The rapid spread of COVID-19 vaccine misinformation on Twitter and other social media platforms makes it essential to develop effective systems for detecting false information. This research evaluates the performance of traditional machine learning models together with ensemble models for identifying COVID-19 vaccine misinformation through text-based analysis across vaccine effectiveness and adverse reaction and conspiracy theory domains. The research evaluates six machine learning models including Logistic Regression and Support Vector Machines (SVM) and Naivve Bayes and Random Forest and Gradient Boosting and XGBoost through accuracy and F1-score and additional performance metrics. Ensemble models demonstrate superior performance than classical models according to the research findings because XGBoost and Random Forest achieve the best results in real-world misinformation detection systems. The research conducts spatial and temporal analysis of misinformation spread to show how misinformation changes throughout time and across different geographical areas. The complete framework presents a new method to improve public health responses against vaccine misinformation.

title: Task-oriented framework for panchromatic–multispectral fusion of OSE-GF01 satellite images
authors: Rongfan Dai, Mi Wang, Ru Chen, Haiming Zhang, Tingjian Yang, Yizhuo Wang
publish: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Letters
abstract: Traditional remote sensing satellite systems are susceptible to sluggish system responses and high data processing delays, which severely limit their application in emergency monitoring and real-time decision-making. To overcome these limitations, the Oriental Smart Eye (OSE)-GF01 satellite is equipped with a high-performance onboard processing unit, promoting a shift in remote sensing data service models toward intelligent real-time onboard processing. However, the conflict between limited onboard computational resources and the massive volume of remote sensing data poses a significant challenge to achieving high-precision, low-latency onboard processing. We propose a position-driven onboard processing method based on satellite-ground collaboration,integrating a lightweight onboard pipeline and adaptive smoothing filtering-based spectral decomposition to achieve real-time ROI positioning, sensing correction, sub-pixel registration, andpanchromatic-multispectral image fusion. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method maintains superior geometric performance across different terrain scenarios, with positioning accuracy within 10 m and registration accuracy better than 0.5 pixels. The no-reference comprehensive evaluation index (QNR) of the fusion product exceeds 0.86, with low spectral and spatial distortions, and ROI fusion products are rapidly generated within 2.5 seconds, outperforming the other typical methods in both accuracy and efficiency. These results confirm the feasibility of the proposed method for high-precision real-time fusion of high-resolution remote sensing images under resource-constrained onboard conditions.

title: Missing-Kernel-Based OCSVM for Anomaly Detection on Missing Data
authors: Jianan Liu, Jingxuan Pang, Chunguang Li
abstract: Anomaly detection is challenging with the presence of missing data. The common two-stage strategy first imputes missing values and then performs anomaly detection, making its performance highly dependent on imputation quality. When the data distribution is complex, the imputation quality may be poor, which degrades the performance of anomaly detectors. To address this issue, we propose a Missing-Kernel-based One-Class Support Vector Machine (MK-OCSVM), in which a novel missing kernel capable of handling missing data is constructed. MK-OCSVM avoids explicit imputation so that its anomaly detection performance is not affected by the imputation quality. Besides, we use the partially observed data in the observation space to model the data distribution in the latent original space, thereby enabling anomaly detection for the latent complete data. MK-OCSVM incorporates the data distribution modeling and OCSVM construction in a joint optimization framework, which alleviates the singularity issue and enhances the suitability of the missing kernel for anomaly detection. To solve the joint optimization problem of MK-OCSVM, a gradient-based alternating optimization algorithm is designed. Experimental results demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method.

title: Reinforcement Learning with Partial Online State Information in POMDPs: Regret Bounds and Limits
authors: Ming Shi, Yingbin Liang, Ness B. Shroff
abstract: Partially observable Markov decision processes (POMDPs) are a general framework for sequential decision-making under latent state uncertainty, yet learning in POMDPs is intractable in the worst case. Motivated by sensing and probing constraints in practice, we study how much online state information (OSI) is sufficient to enable efficient learning guarantees. We formalize a model in which the learner can query only partial OSI (POSI) during interaction. We first prove an information-theoretic hardness result showing that, for general POMDPs, achieving an ϵ-optimal policy can require sample complexity that is exponential unless full OSI is available. We then identify two structured subclasses that remain learnable under POSI and propose corresponding algorithms with provably efficient performance guarantees. In particular, we establish regret upper bounds with Õ (√K) dependence on the number of episodes K, together with complementary lower bounds, thereby delineating when POSI suffices for efficient reinforcement learning. Our results highlight a principled separation between intractable and tractable regimes under incomplete online state access and provide new tools for jointly optimizing POSI queries and learning control actions.
