title: Data-Driven Internal Model Control for Output Regulation
authors: Wenjie Liu, Yifei Li, Jian Sun, Gang Wang, Keyou You, Lihua Xie, Jie Chen
abstract: Output regulation is a fundamental problem in control theory, extensively studied since the 1970s. Traditionally, research has primarily addressed scenarios where the system model is explicitly known, leaving the problem in the absence of a system model less explored. Leveraging recent advances in Willems et al.’s fundamental lemma, data-driven control has emerged as a powerful tool for stabilizing unknown systems. This article tackles the output regulation problem for unknown single and multiagent systems (MASs) using noisy data. Many existing data-driven approaches rely on solving data-based output regulator equations (OREs), which become inadequate for achieving zero tracking error in the presence of noisy data. To overcome this limitation, we advocate the use of a classical tool from robust output regulation, namely, the internal model principle. We first apply this idea to linear time-invariant (LTI) systems and show that exact output regulation, that is, zero tracking error, can be achieved by solving a simple data-based linear matrix inequality (LMI). The framework is then extended to the <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$k$</tex-math> </inline-formula>th-order output regulation problem for nonlinear systems, followed by applications to both linear and nonlinear MASs. Finally, numerical tests validate the effectiveness of the proposed data-driven controllers.

title: Improving Reliability of 3D Parcel Dimension Estimation Software via Dual-Camera Stereo Vision and Efficient Deep Neural Networks
authors: William Cheng-Chung Chu, Tse-Chuan Hsu, Yu-Chia Cheng, Wenqing Qu
abstract: In the rapidly developing e-commerce and innovative logistics sectors, the accuracy and efficiency of package dimension measurement technology are crucial for accurate shipping cost calculation. This study proposes a novel, low-cost 3D dimension measurement system based on deep learning and a dual-camera stereo vision compensation model to improve the reliability of measurement software in computer vision computing. This system uses standard camera equipment and integrates the YOLO algorithm to establish image-based object size estimation. The design concept mimics the human eye's binocular vision mechanism. It utilizes the parallax between the center points of two cameras to estimate distance, thereby optimizing computational performance and recognition speed. A mathematical parallax model is applied to improve depth accuracy. To mitigate computational errors and enhance reliability, a two stage linear regression method combined with a weighted error compensation model is used to correct for systematic deviations between depth estimates and ground truth measurements. Experimental results show that this framework achieves an average error of less than 1 cm and an overall accuracy of 89% for 3D dimension estimation of various logistics packages under uncontrolled conditions, meeting the accuracy requirements for practical logistics billing. These results demonstrate that leveraging flexible software-based stereo vision computing can significantly improve the feasibility and reliability of automated measurement solutions in real-world logistics applications.

title: ICCTA 2025 Committees
authors: N/A
publish: 2025 35th International Conference on Computer Theory and Applications (ICCTA)

title: Impact Path of Government Excessive Behavior on Investors' Rational Conformity in PPP Projects: Demand and Government Control Rights_supp1-3694969.docx
authors: Jicai Liu
abstract: When governments make excessive behavior, investor conformity may hinder effective risk management. Elucidating the reasons behind investors' conformity to government's excessive behavior is crucial for enhancing decision-making efficacy in high-stakes scenarios. Drawing on field dynamics theory, hypothetical paths are proposed to elucidate the impact of government excessive behavior on investor conformity, with a focus on investor demand and government control rights. The study employed rigorous questionnaire design, valid respondent screening, reliability and validity tests, as well as robustness testing including competitive model analysis and multi-group analysis to ensure methodological rigour. Through hypothesis testing and path analysis of 244 data, the study found that under excessive government behavior, investor demand response contains the inherent logic of technological capabilities and long-term development driving decision-making, reflecting a proactive response pattern within apparent passivity. Government control rights exerts a dual effect on investors' conformity: economic benefits and market access act as “hard levers”, while trust and reputation serve as “soft bonds”. Moreover, industry capability need dominates compliance decisions, revealing that implicitly conveyed “capability threshold” signals carry more weight than explicit rules. These findings show that investor conformity is a rational choice based on balancing dual government control rights and multidimensional interests, reshaping the traditional cognitive framework that regards conformity as irrational. This insight offers valuable guidance for mitigating the risks associated with investor conformity and significantly enhances the theoretical framework of project risk management.

title: Integrating multi-sensor data to assess long-term grassland degradation in China's Ili region_supp1-3695177.docx
authors: Fei Zhang

title: Neuromuscular Control of CT and LCA Muscles in Human Vocal Pitch and Register Regulation: Insights from sEMG Analysis
authors: Xinyi Yang, Shubo Xing, Jinbo Zhang, Jing Yan, Liang Wu
abstract: Objective: Precise regulation of vocal pitch and register relies on the activity of intrinsic laryngeal muscles. This study aims to elucidate the neuromuscular mechanisms of the cricothyroid (CT) and lateral cricoarytenoid (LCA) muscles during pitch modulation and register transitions, and to further investigate their associations with voice quality. Methods: Sixteen healthy participants were recruited to produce five sustained vowels while performing pitch glides spanning the Chest and Falsetto registers. Surface electromyography (sEMG) signals of the CT and LCA muscles were measured using a validated approach, along with synchronized speech signals. Subsequently, sEMG features were extracted and analyzed for correlations with pitch and voice quality metrics. Results: During pitch regulation, the CT and LCA muscles exhibited directionally symmetric activation patterns (“rise-fall-rise” during pitch ascent and “fall rise-fall” during pitch descent) regardless of vowel type. Their control over pitch was stable in the Chest/Falsetto register but less stable in the Transition region. Additionally, muscle activation improved voice quality in the Chest register, yet had constrained effects in the Transition region and Falsetto register. Conclusion: The CT and LCA muscles show activation patterns of different intensities, which contribute to precise pitch control and register transitions. The core function of these two muscles lies in pitch regulation, while voice quality modulation is not solely determined by them. Significance: This study provides non-invasive empirical evidence revealing muscular control strategies in human phonation, thereby advancing the understanding of the physiological mechanisms underlying voice production and offering implications for vocal training and clinical assessment.

title: Quantitative Metrics for Evaluating Grid Strength Across Power System Stability Domains: A Review
authors: Yu Liu, Wen Wang, Xinlan Jia, Muhammad Umar Afzaal, Jian Zhang, Yilu Liu
abstract: The increasing integration of inverter-based resources (IBRs) is reducing grid strength, making accurate assessment essential for reliable and stable power system operation. Quantitative metrics offer systematic and practical tools for evaluating grid strength, supporting both planning and operational decisions. This paper provides a comprehensive review of quantitative metrics for grid strength assessment across static, small-signal, and transient stability domains. Metrics are categorized and discussed in terms of their definitions, derivation methods, advantages, and limitations, with particular attention to the effects of IBR interactions, network topology, and control strategies. Relevant stability criteria, practical applicability, and recommended threshold values are also summarized. This review aims to support system planners and operators in selecting and applying appropriate grid strength metrics in power systems with high IBR penetration.

title: A Gain-Improvement Method using Mixed Metasurface Superstrate with Channel Capacity Enhancement
authors: Jiayue Jiang, Luyu Zhao, Weijian Wang, Jiafeng Ge, Wei Lin, Mengguan Pan, Feng Liu, Jiayin Guo, Zhixiang Huang, J. C. Vardaxoglou
publish: IEEE Transactions on Antennas and Propagation
abstract: A gain-improvement(GI) method using mixed metasurface superstrate for the compact ±45° orthogonal dual-polarized base station antenna array is proposed in this article. The method consists of two metasurface-superstrates, the high permittivity ceramic lens (HPCL) and the high refractive-index surface (HRIS) for ±45° polarizations. The HPCL adjusts the phase distribution of radiated waves of every antenna in the array to form a quasi-plane wavefront and the HRIS refracts the waves to further form a nearly planar wavefront to achieve GI. Moreover, the GI method doesn’t affect impedance match of the antenna array and the realized gain of the array improves. Two GI examples, including the GI of a 1 x 3 subarray and the GI of a 4 x 3 array, are presented. The measured peak realized gain of the 4 x 3 array is enhanced by up to 2dBi and the array achieves a measured realized aperture efficiency(RAE) up to 90% within 1.71-2.17GHz band. The GI method brings the increase up to 22% of the channel capacity of a 4 x 4 MIMO system. The above improvement allows a 4 x 3 array to be used, instead of a 4 x 4, which results in a 25% aperture reduction. The attracted features of this method make it a key candidate technology for future mobile communications for a massive MIMO application.

title: PneumoX-CL: Continual Learning with Similarity-Aware Stratified Replay for Domain-Shifted Chest Radiographs
authors: Danu Kim
abstract: Deep learning models can accurately detect pneumonia from chest X-rays; however, most are trained under static assumptions that do not reflect real clinical environments. In reality, imaging domains shift owing to changes in devices, patient demographics, or inter-hospital variability, often causing significant performance degradation. Continual learning can address this challenge; however, many methods encounter class imbalance and redundant memory storage. We introduce PneumoX-CL, a replay-based continual-learning framework that integrates a stratified class-balanced buffer to preserve balanced memory, a similarity-aware buffer update that maintains diverse samples using cosine-similarity selection, and a dynamic class-weighted loss to mitigate batch-level imbalance. PneumoX-CL achieved 87.69% accuracy with 2.23% forgetting on a synthetic five-domain PneumoniaMNIST benchmark, exceeding baseline models (experience replay and class-balancing reservoir sampling) in average accuracy while maintaining low variance across runs. These results suggest that diversity-aware, class-balanced replay can improve the robustness of diagnostic AI systems under sequential imaging-domain shifts.

title: Forge: Closing the Agentic Reliability Gap Between Self-Hosted and Frontier Language Models
authors: Antoine Emil Zambelli
publish: Proceedings of the ACM Conference on AI and Agentic Systems
abstract: None

title: LLM-in-the-Loop Descriptor Prompt Refinement for Dialogue-Based Big Five Personality Recognition
authors: Ryo Takahashi, Naoki Saito, Keisuke Maeda, Takahiro Yoshioka, Takeshi Konno, Takahiro Ogawa, Miki Haseyama
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Consumer Technology (ICCT-Pacific)
abstract: Personalizing dialogue systems requires adapting responses to individual personality traits. To achieve more consistent personalization, this study focuses on personality recognition from dialogue using Large Language Models (LLMs). Existing LLM-based zero-shot approaches using fixed descriptor prompts have not examined how prompt phrasing affects model alignment with personality traits. We propose an LLM-in-the-loop prompt refinement method that iteratively revises prompts based on the model's misinterpretations of user personality, thereby improving alignment without altering the model parameters.

title: Investigating Dual-Gate Junctionless Tunnel FET for High-Sensitivity Biosensing Devices
authors: Aabhash Mukherjee, Sandeep Singh, Dharmendra Singh Yadav, Aabha, Prabhat Singh
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: Biosensors are a common phenomenon now a days and used for various medical applications. So, they are required to be more sensitive and reliable. Tunnel Field Effect Transistor are now a days used for these applications and have replaced MOS- FETs because they offer high ratio of OFF to ON current (IOFF, ION), faster switching times, improved sub-threshold swing (SS), thereby low leakage current and low power consumption. A proposed DG-JL-TFET (Dual-Gate Junctionless TFET) structure is presented for biosensing application. A nanocavity is made in the gates where the biomolecules with different permittivity can be filed. These different permittivity’s represent biomolecules with different dielectric constants. Where the tunnelling is visible in the ON state, the energy band diagrams for the OFF and ON states are displayed. Tunnelling facilitates current flow even at lower voltages, and it yields drain current at varying gate voltage values. The graphs of ratio of ION to IOFF current are plotted. The values of threshold sensitivity, drain cur-rent sensitivity are calculated for various biomolecules.

title: Comprehensive Evaluation of Machine Learning for Type 2 Diabetes Risk Prediction: Large-Scale External Validation and Fairness Analysis
authors: Rajveer Singh Pall, Sameer Yadav, Siddharth Bhalerao, Sourabh Sahu, Ritu Ahluwalia, Bhaskar Awadhiya
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: Machine learning-based Type 2 diabetes risk prediction models mostly obtain good internal validation results but lose their effectiveness in real-world applications due to deficient external testing and fairness assessment. We developed a comprehensive, multi-dimensional framework for evaluating discrimination, calibration, interpretability, and algorithmic fairness when applied to nationally representative populations. An XGBoost model and baseline models were trained on NHANES 2015-2020 (n = 15,685) using eight non-laboratory predictors: age, sex, race/ethnicity, BMI, smoking status, physical activity, history of heart attack, and history of stroke. External validation was performed with BRFSS 2020–2022 (n=1,285,783), a phone-based surveillance system that has realistic deployment conditions and a large distribution shift. Internal validation showed good discrimination (AUC 0.794, 95% CI 0.788 to 0.800), but there was some loss of performance on external validation (AUC=0.717, relative decrease: −9.7%, p < 0.001). Fairness analysis revealed severe bias: predictions for elderly adults (≥60) were much worse than for young adults (AUC 0.607 vs 0.742, difference 0.135, p < 0.001), the obese underperformed compared to normal weight individuals (AUC 0.698 vs 0.735, difference of 0.037). Gender demonstrated comparability (male 0.723 versus female 0.712, p =0.142). Calibration showed that there was an overestimation of the risk (Brier score 0.123). The SHAP analysis showed that age, BMI, and physical activity were the main risk drivers with a clinically consistent direction. Internal validation severely overestimates real-life performance. Massive external validation revealed systematic errors in the most vulnerable: those who are old and obese, who have the highest risk of diabetes, get a worse AI-based score. Such differences call for the development of equity consideration-based and age-specific deployment approaches before any clinical use.

title: Empowerment of Social-Media Apps Using RSQM Approach and Multi-Layered Model
authors: Ahmad Bacha, Muhammad Mukhtar, Khalid Hamid, Muhammad Akram Mujahid, Muhammad Waseem Iqbal
publish: 2026 International Conference on Data Science, Machine Learning, and Intelligence (DataSciMI)
abstract: A personal relationship between a brand and its followers can be developed using social media. Having more than 400 million users and 80 million posts every day, Instagram is the social media marketing tool that all businesses cannot do without. It boasts a large user base, more so than any other similar site. Nevertheless, some individuals do not know whether Instagram is simple to use and enjoy staying on the platform. They experience difficulty in locating what they desire or performing what they desire on Instagram. Some of the things Instagram does, which include altering the appearance, repositioning, or commanding them on what to do, also lose their interest or make them angry. This study gives a usability test of Instagram based on the Response Surface Quadratic Methodology (RSQM) that experiments with various activities and observes the response of the users. The paper identified four activities that people perform on Instagram: create a profile, share with friends, join groups and control privacy, and then applied the RSQM to determine how those aspects could be made easier. We employed real individuals to use Instagram with various settings and RSQM assisted us in observing which settings make Instagram the best. The 6-attribute (efficiency. Effectiveness, satisfaction, operability, attractiveness, and awareness) usability test allows us to provide Instagram with recommendations and come up with a formula on how to enhance the product, so that everyone can enjoy the time they spend on it.

title: Analyzing Adversarial Attacks on Federated Learning based Medical Image Classification
authors: Soomaiya Hamid, Narmeen Zakaria Bawany
publish: 2026 International Conference on Data Science, Machine Learning, and Intelligence (DataSciMI)
abstract: The recent breakthrough in deep learning techniques, such as distributed learning (Federated Learning - FL) enables collaboration in model building across different organisations without data exchange. This is very advantageous in the medical field, where privacy is of the highest importance. In the case of detecting brain tumors, FL enables hospitals and research centers to construct strong diagnostic models by using different datasets without compromising the confidentiality of patients. However, FL systems are vulnerable to security threats as data is managed by many sources, where any node can act maliciously and generate adversarial attacks. This can significantly harm the performance of the model and possibly affect the diagnoses for patients. To address these limitations, this study proposes a strong FL-based framework for brain tumor detection, which takes adversarial training into consideration to counter attacks. It uses a FL-based architecture that has been trained on a distributed dataset of brain MRI scans from multiple clients. To test the robustness of this framework, we have induced malicious adversarial attacks, the Fast Gradient Sign Method (FGSM) and Projected Gradient Descent (PGD). Our experimental results show that the accuracy of the proposed framework in detecting brain tumors is 92% while being robust to adversarial attacks. The adversarially trained model is more robust, the detection tool can effectively detect and eliminate malicious clients. This work opens the possibility of FL as a privacy and robust approach for medical image processing.

title: Secure and Efficient IoT Software Updates Using Blockchain with CPABE, ECDSA, and IPFS
authors: S.Nagendrudu, Masapogu Nagesh, K Vanitha
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: It is crucial to update the software on resource-constrained Internet of Things (IoT) devices often to ensure their security, since these devices are becoming common targets for malicious attacks. Current software payment and delivery systems, on the other hand, have a client-server architecture with several services, which increases the attack surface due to the number of data transits between the client and server. Our proposed blockchain-powered end-to-end secure software upgrade delivery structure for IoT devices transfers cryptographic computation away from devices with limited resources and onto a decentralized blockchain system, with the goals of ensuring the security, reliability, availability, efficiency, as well as auditability of verified software delivery. To reduce the computational cost for creating keys and key delivery on the device's side and to ensure that software upgrades can only be installed and decrypted on authorized IoT devices, we utilize cipher Text Policy Attribution-Based Encryption (CP-ABE) and create a customized authorization policy. Plus, smart contracts guarantee the manufacturer-IoT device payment process and the safe, atomic transfer of software. Offloading computation-based verification validation to intelligent contracts ensures the given software's legitimacy. Smart contracts effectively ensure the delivery of improvements to software in return for payment, and immutable data stored on the blockchain’s public record helps satisfy compliance audits. To verify the efficacy of the proposed framework, we conduct experiments and security analyses to compare it to state-of-the-art research.

title: Design and Simulation of a Divide-by-4 Clock Divider Using Transmission-Gate D Flip-Flops in 180-nm CMOS
authors: Mohit Kumar, Prakash Chandra Joshi, Mansi Jain, Ashish Raman, Amit Kumar Rana, Rahul Sharma
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: This paper describes the design and verification of a transistor-level divide-by-4 frequency divider in 180-nm CMOS using Cadence Virtuoso. Rather than pursuing architectural novelty, this work emphasizes correct functionality, appropriate power behavior, and layout integrity. A divider based on cascaded D flip-flops is realized and experimentally validated through transient simulations across a wide input frequency range extending from 33.3 MHz up to 1 GHz. Simulation results reveal an accurate frequency scaling for a constant division ratio of four, with a stable output duty cycle close to 50% for all operating points. The measured power consumption appears clearly dependent on the input frequency, ranging from approximately 33 µW at 1 GHz to about 1 µW at 33.3 MHz, and reflects the reduced switching activity within the CMOS circuitry. A compact layout with a size of approximately 32µm × 28µm is designed and validated using DRC, LVS, and parasitic extraction against Mentor Graphics Calibre. Simulations carried out below 100 MHz show a marginal difference from the schematic result and thus form a verification of the controlled parasitic extraction effect. The proposed divider thus offers a reliable and power-saving solution for clock scaling that can be verified on a layout level for applications involving clock management on digital and mixed-signal integrated circuits.

title: Switching-Activity Reduction in BIST Through a Hybrid Test Pattern Generator by Using Gray-Code
authors: Prachi Sawarn, Vrinda Gupta
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: Built-In Self-Test (BIST) remains a crucial strategy for evaluating the correctness and reliability of digital systems, but traditional LFSR-based pattern generators often produce high transition densities, leading to excessive dynamic power consumption, IR-drop, and potential reliability degradation during test. To address this limitation, this work presents a Gray Code Assisted Low Power BIST architecture that integrates a low transition Gray code generator with a conventional pseudo-random LFSR. The hybrid structure allows seamless mode selection without requiring any changes to the Circuit Under Test (CUT) or the Multiple Input Signature Register (MISR). Switching-activity data obtained through SAIF (Switching Activity Interchange Format) analysis demonstrates a substantial reduction in toggles at both the Test Pattern Generator (TPG) and CUT inputs when the Gray-code mode is activated. This establishes that the suggested design is compatible with conventional BIST streams, in addition to providing much better power conduct. The technique forms a convenient basis of low-power test integration, and could be extended in the future to adapting test sequences and massively scaling SoC benchmarking.

title: MATLAB-oriented Smart Blood Glucose Monitoring and Regulation Using a Model Predictive Control
authors: Imon Shahriar, Soumya Sen, Sahil Hamid, Tribid Mazumder, Ashish Raman, Abheek Gupta, Angshuman Khan, Atanu Chowdhury
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: This study presents a MATLAB-oriented Smart Blood Glucose Monitoring and Regulation System that integrates non-invasive IoT-based sensing and Model Predictive Control (MPC) to enhance diabetes management. The proposed system utilizes near-infrared (NIR) sensors to measure blood glucose concentration (BGC) through fingertip scanning. A Light Emitting Diode (LED) emits signals through the fingertip, and a phototransistor detects reflected signals, which are analyzed using regression models implemented in Arduino. The collected data is stored in ThingSpeak for remote access and continuous monitoring. In vitro and in vivo testing of the system offers evidence of its accuracy, revealing an error range from 1.14% to 17.39%.To regulate BGC within the physiological range of 65-130 mg/dL, a Model Predictive Controller (MPC) based on MATLAB-SIMULINK is employed for insulin regulation in Type 1 diabetes. The controller changes the amount of insulin injected in real-time according to glucose readings, obtaining optimal glycemic control. The combination of IoT-driven monitoring and advanced predictive control provides a smart diabetes framework with integrated personalised management.

title: Hybrid Transfer Learning-Based Crop Disease Prediction (HTCDP) Model for Multi-Crop and Multi-Stage Disease Identification
authors: Ruchita Kale, Pranita Deshmukh, Yogita Dhumale, Rupali Meshram, Anand Chaudhari
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: Detecting crop diseases quickly and accurately is vital for maintaining agricultural productivity and reducing economic losses. Traditional manual inspections are often inefficient, labor-intensive, and susceptible to human error. This issue is especially true when symptoms appear similar across various crops and stages of disease progression. To address this, the research introduces a Hybrid Transfer Learning-Based Crop Disease Prediction model. This model aims to identify and classify crop diseases on different parts of plants and at various stages of progression. It uses MobileNet and InceptionV3 as feature extractors, combined with a Random Forest Classifier (RFC) to improve classifier performance. The team performed extensive data preprocessing, including augmentation, normalization, and managing data imbalance, to improve data quality and model reliability. They conducted experiments using a modified multi-crop dataset that included 13 disease classes, extracting over 101 million features in the process. The proposed HTCDP model achieved a maximum accuracy of 95.73%, surpassing both deep learning and machine learning methods in precision and generalization. In addition to detecting diseases, the system also suggests treatments for the identified issues, supporting proactive management. These findings confirm that the HTCDP model is a suitable and scalable solution for intelligently identifying crop diseases in precision agriculture.

title: Image Encryption Based on Bloch Sphere Representation for Satellite Communications
authors: Wan-Ni Wu, Pei-Jun Lee, Chia-Ray Chen, Yu-Tang You
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Consumer Technology (ICCT-Pacific)
abstract: Ensuring data security is a critical requirement in satellite-to-ground station communication systems. This paper proposes a novel quantum-inspired image encryption framework wherein images are represented via the Bloch sphere model to facilitate quantum operations. Furthermore, two distinct chaotic maps are integrated to perform pixel-level confusion and permutation, thereby significantly enhancing algorithmic complexity and security. Experimental results show that the proposed method requires only 0.6981 seconds to encrypt large-scale images while generating highly random cipher images. In the differential attack analysis, the algorithm achieves an NPCR (Number of Pixels Change Rate) of 0.9903 and a UACI (Unified Average Changing Intensity) of 0.3269, demonstrating excellent resistance against differential attacks. Furthermore, the decryption process enables lossless reconstruction of the original image, confirming the strong potential of the proposed encryption framework for secure satellite image transmission.

title: Decentralized Clustering for Customer Segmentation: Evaluating Federated Learning with RFM Features
authors: Loai Khairalla, Mohamed Soliman Halawa, Essam Elfakharany
publish: 2025 35th International Conference on Computer Theory and Applications (ICCTA)
abstract: In today's competitive business environment, customer segmentation plays an important role in understanding customers, especially in the e-commerce industry. Data centralization is one of the major issues facing these businesses analyzing customers' data, forcing them to collect a large amount of data into a central server, risking customers' data privacy. This study proposes a Federated Learning (FL) model for customer segmentation using Recency, frequency, Monetary (RFM) Features. The study implemented and evaluated three federated clustering algorithms-K-means, Gaussian Mixture Models (GMM), and Hierarchical Clustering with multiple levels of data fragmentation, and compared their results with those of their centralized counterparts. The comparison results showed the viability of using federated kmeans, as it performed nearly identically to its centralized model. At the same time, federated hierarchical clustering outperformed its centralized model in capturing high value customer segments. Results proved that FL can be a strong solution for organizations aiming for clear customer segments without violating data privacy standards and with low to no performance trade-offs.

title: Underwater Acoustic Noise Generation Based on WGAN-GP
authors: Shuoshuo Xu
publish: Proceedings of the 19th International Conference on Underwater Networks and Systems
abstract: None

title: 2D-VMD-Based Wave Spectrum Inversion in Directional Bimodal Sea States using Shipboard Coherent Microwave Radar
authors: Sitao Wu, Fan Ding, Zezong Chen, Kaixuan Xu, Rui Liu, Xinyu Fu, Chen Zhao
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Shipboard coherent microwave radar is a promising approach for wave measurement during vessel navigation. However, in complex multi-directional sea states, the radial velocities measured by the radar result from the superposition of waves propagating both toward and away from the antenna. This superposition causes overlapping wave spectra and degrades the accuracy of wave parameter inversion. To address this issue, a novel method based on Two-Dimensional Variational Mode Decomposition (2D-VMD) is proposed for separating and reconstructing the radial velocities corresponding to waves propagating toward and away from the radar. First, spatiotemporal radial velocities are extracted from raw radar sea echoes and decomposed into multiple quasi-orthogonal submodes via 2D-VMD. Based on the spectral distributions of these sub-modes, “non-wave velocities” are eliminated, while the toward and away wave velocity components are further separated and reconstructed. Finally, non-overlapping directional wave spectra are derived from the reconstructed radial velocities, from which wave parameters are estimated. The proposed method is validated using two shipboard experiments conducted in the South China Sea: one in 2020 under directional bimodal sea states and another in 2019 under unimodal sea states. The wave spectra and parameters estimated by the radar show excellent consistency with buoy measurements in both scenarios, thus validating the effectiveness and robustness of the proposed method in separating velocity superposition and inverting directional wave spectra precisely.

title: Dissipativity-Based Output Feedback Control of Networked Sampled-Data Systems Under Actuator Failures and Consecutive DoS Attacks
authors: Min Xue, James Lam, Huaicheng Yan, Ka-Wai Kwok
abstract: This article investigates the problem of dissipativity-based output feedback control for networked sampled-data systems under actuator failures and consecutive denial-of-service (DoS) attacks. Two distinct sampling periods are considered, each governed by constant occurrence probabilities following a Bernoulli distribution. The communication channel from the sampler to the controller is vulnerable to malicious DoS attacks, with both the maximum number of consecutive occurrences and the attacking rate taken into account. On this basis, a mathematical model is built to characterize the interval between two sequential update instants at the controller side, whose randomness is captured by the probabilistic distributions of the stochastic sampling and consecutive attacks. By developing a sampled-data output feedback controller, an equivalent discrete-time closed-loop system is constructed. Then, two alternative control synthesis conditions are suggested for solving the controller, which can ensure the stochastic stability and strict dissipativity of the system. Finally, simulations are performed on two examples to illustrate the effectiveness of the proposed method.

title: A Probabilistic Generative Model for Spectral Speech Enhancement
authors: Marco Hidalgo-Araya, Raphael Tresor, Bart Van Erp, Wouter W.L. Nuijten, Thijs Van De Laar, Bert De Vries
publish: IEEE Open Journal of Signal Processing
abstract: Speech enhancement in hearing aids remains difficult in nonstationary acoustic environments. This paper introduces AIDA-2, a unified modular framework that formulates signal processing, learning, and personalization as Bayesian inference with explicit uncertainty tracking. The proposed framework replaces ad hoc algorithm design with a single probabilistic generative model that continuously adapts to changing acoustic conditions and user preferences. It extends spectral subtraction with principled mechanisms for in situ personalization and adaptation to acoustic context. The system is implemented as an interconnected probabilistic state-space model, and inference is performed via variational message passing in the RxInfer.jl probabilistic programming environment, enabling real-time Bayesian processing under hearing-aid constraints. We focus the evaluation on the warped-frequency filter bank (WFB) and speech enhancement model (SEM), evaluated on the public VoiceBank+DEMAND corpus. Additionally, an acoustic context model (ACM) and end-user model (EUM) are described architecturally. The evaluated system requires 85 parameters, two orders of magnitude fewer than the tens of thousands to millions of trainable parameters used by contemporary deep-learning baselines. This proof of concept establishes an interpretable, data-efficient foundation for uncertainty-aware hearing-aid processing, pointing toward devices that learn continuously through probabilistic inference.

title: KPViT: Kronecker-Patch Vision Transformer for Musculoskeletal Abnormality Detection
authors: Xu Wang, Zhaoshui He, Yonglu Chen, Deyi Wang, Shengli Xie
publish: IEEE Transactions on Emerging Topics in Computational Intelligence
abstract: Musculoskeletal abnormality detection is of great significance to diagnosing musculoskeletal system diseases. However, it is challenging to detect musculoskeletal abnormality from X-rays because of these problems: 1) Lesion imaging findings are often indistinguishable in musculoskeletal radiographs; 2) The imaging findings of abnormal bones are similar to those of normal ones. To solve such issues, a Kronecker-Patch Vision Transformer (KPViT) is proposed for musculoskeletal abnormality detection from radiographs, where the Local Selective Region Aware (LSRA) module is developed to locate small lesions from musculoskeletal radiographs by focusing on local lesion areas in the image, and the Global Fine-Grained Feature Aware (GFFA) module is devised to distinguish similar lesion imaging findings from normal ones by mining fine-grained differences in global view feature maps. Experiments conducted on the MURA dataset show that the proposed KPViT outperforms the state-of-the-art methods, achieving the Cohen’s Kappa of 84.75% and an accuracy of 85.66%, further assisting radiologists in musculoskeletal abnormality diagnosis.

title: CSAP: Coarse-to-Fine Structured Angle Prediction for Oriented Object Detection
authors: Beihang Song, Hongquan Sun, Tong Liu, Kai Zhu, Jun Wan
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology
abstract: Arbitrary-oriented object detection plays a crucial role in numerous vision applications. A key challenge lies in accurate orientation estimation, which suffers from angular periodicity and boundary discontinuity, leading to unstable learning and reduced accuracy. To address this issue, we propose a Coarse-to-Fine Structured Angle Prediction (CSAP) approach that reformulates angle estimation as a sequence of structured sub-tasks. Specifically, CSAP first discretizes continuous angles into coarse orientation intervals to ensure robust global prediction. Then, fine-grained angular components are modeled in a two-dimensional structured space, where elliptical Gaussian heatmaps capture local variations. An axis-rescaling strategy is further introduced to alleviate peak-shift errors and enhance localization precision. In addition, an optional multimodal contrastive alignment module is incorporated to provide semantic regularization by embedding category and orientation information into a shared representation space. This design provides a lightweight semantic regularization signal that can improve feature consistency and robustness to rotation variations. Extensive experiments across multiple benchmarks demonstrate that CSAP consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods in both accuracy and robustness.

title: Self-Error Estimation and Dual Refinement Plug-in for High-Quality Instance Segmentation
authors: Sangbeom Lee, Seunghyeok Back, Kangmin Kim, Sungho Shin, Kyoobin Lee
abstract: Instance segmentation is a crucial task in computer vision with numerous applications. Recent approaches incorporating refinement modules have shown promise. However, their reliance on indirect guidance often results in suboptimal performance and high computational costs.We introduce the self-error estimation and dual refinement (SEED), a simple yet powerful plug-in module that significantly enhances instance segmentation quality through explicit fine-grained guidance. SEED identifies proposed self-error, defined as pixel-wise discrepancies between initial predictions and ground truth. It then leverages this information to guide the dual refinement of bounding boxes and masks. SEED seamlessly integrates into various existing methods by being added as a plug-in, and enables targeted and effective improvements in both detection and segmentation quality. Extensive experiments demonstrate that SEED significantly improves the performance of state-of-the-art models across diverse datasets, including natural, driving, and robotic scenes while maintaining computational efficiency, outperforming existing refinement-based high-quality instance segmentation methods. The code is available at https://github.com/gist-ailab/seed.

title: ADAD: Multivariate Time Series Anomaly Detection via Adaptive Series Decomposition
authors: Yueyue Yao, Zhi-Xin Yang
abstract: Time series anomaly detection aims to identify observations deviating from normal statues. As time series deliver complicated temporal patterns, many studies decompose time series into trend and seasonal components and then mine pattens in each component to simplify pattern learning process. However, mainstream methods often adopt fixed decomposition strategy such as moving average on the whole training set, ignoring the fact that time series evolve dynamically along time and each variate owns its unique evolutionary patterns. Some researchers try to conduct dynamic decomposition but treat pattern learning in each component as independent process, overlooking the guiding effect of learned features in one component on mining component-specific patterns in the other. To address the above issues, we propose an <underline xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">a</u>daptive <underline xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">d</u>ecomposition-based multivariate time series <underline xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">a</u>nomaly <underline xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">d</u>etection algorithm, dubbed as ADAD. Specifically, we conduct time series decomposition in the frequency domain and formulate it as the spectrum splitting problem, with variate-specific splitting points dynamically learned for each sequence. Thus, decomposed trend and seasonal components could well reflect both variate-specific and time-specific features, enabling precise pattern mining in the following stages. Moreover, a cross-component connection is introduced to the pattern mining process, regarding patterns learned by one component as guidance for the pattern learning process in the other component. By excluding patterns that should not be learned, our design enables the model to learn distinct patterns in each component. Extensive experiments demonstrate that ADAD achieves superior performance over current state-of-the-art methods on public benchmarks.

title: Marketing Mix Modeling: Conceptual Foundations, Methodological Advancement and Strategic Applications
authors: Vaibhav Maldhure, M. A. Pund
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: The Marketing Mix Modeling is the fast growing and widely used concept in industries to help business to grow by understanding the spend flow among different channels. This conceptual paper shows the theoretical concepts, ways to do, methodological evolution, practical details for MMM framework. Data driven decision making can be achieved by using the fundamentals of MMM and main beneficiaries of this MMM is the business managers. We found MMM's intellectual foundations in econometric modeling and demand analysis by examining how foundational concepts such as ad stock effects, diminishing returns, elasticity measurement, and incremental attribution make possible rigorous causal inference from observational marketing data. The paper synthesizes contemporary methodological approaches, contrasting traditional regression-based models with advanced Bayesian hierarchical frameworks, machine learning extensions, and hybrid architectures. We particularly focused on spend optimization, channel level contribution, scenario planner that help business plans for strategic decisions. We apply these issues in real world scenarios among retail, financial services, and consumer packaged goods sectors, documenting quantified business impact and ROI improvements. We address important contemporary challenges such as managing multicollinearity, privacy first measurement in post cookie environments, validation of causal inference using experimental calibration, and ethics associated with algorithmic recommendations. We present recent innovations that include Google Meridian's Bayesian probabilistic framework, Meta's Robyn automation capability, and privacy preserving data clean room technologies. Finally, the paper discusses some future research directions that involve hierarchical heterogeneous treatment effects, federated learning approaches, real time streaming analytics, and multiobjective optimization balancing revenue, brand equity, and sustainability objectives.

title: Analysis and Comparison of XNOR Gate Implementation using CMOS Logic and Domino Logic with Keeper Technique
authors: Akriti Dewangan, Pawan Kumar Verma, Ashish Raman, Prabhat Singh
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: As technology scales into deep submicron regimes, dynamic power,leakage power, and noise susceptibility have become critical challenges in digital circuit design. The XNOR gate is a fundamental building block in arithmetic, error detection, and cryptographic circuits. This work presents detailed analysis and comparison of XNOR gate implementations using Static CMOS logic, Domino logic, and Domino logic with a keeper transistor in 90 nm technology using Cadence Virtuoso. The impact of keeper transistor sizing on propagation delay, power consumption, charge sharing is systematically analyzed. The proposed analysis provides practical design guidelines for high-speed and low-power XNOR circuits.

title: A Hybrid EfficientNet-B0 and Tiny Transformer Framework for Knee Osteoarthritis Severity Classification
authors: G. L. Madhumati, Badam Vanaja, Kurakula Surya Kiran, Devarapalli Apuroop
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: Knee osteoarthritis (KOA) can keep a person from performing their daily activities and can have a significant negative effect on a person’s quality of life. Diagnosing KOA in its early stages, and developing an appropriate treatment plan to minimize the loss of mobility as a result of KOA, requires that the severity of KOA be classified as accurately as possible based on a knee X-ray image. This paper presents a hybrid deep learning framework for automatically classifying KOA severity, combining local feature extraction from knee X-ray images via EfficientNet-B0 with global feature modeling via a lightweight version of Transformer called Tiny Transformer, with the ability to recognize and account for long-range contextual relationships between the bones of the knee. The model classifies KOA severity based on three clinical classifications: "normal", "osteopenia", or "osteoporosis". The model was tested using a dataset of publicly available KOA knee X-ray images on Kaggle, with augmentation of the data, and special consideration of the benefits of transfer learning. The proposed model indicates a test accuracy of 92.22% and a macro F1 score of 0.92 which indicates that the model’s classification across all classes is robust and well balanced. These findings point toward the value of the proposed model as a reliable support tool for clinical decision making related to KOA severity assessment.

title: MycoSim: An Agent-Based Simulator for Bio-Inspired IoT Botnet Defense
authors: Vaishali Ravi, Mohd Anuaruddin Ahmadon, Nazleeni Samiha Haron, Shakirah Mohd Taib, Izzatdin Abdul Aziz, Shingo Yamaguchi
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Consumer Technology (ICCT-Pacific)
abstract: The proliferation of IoT botnets poses catastrophic risks to both individuals and organizations. While numerous studies propose detection and mitigation approaches, the operational effectiveness of these defenses is often not sufficiently demonstrated. This paper introduces MycoSim, an agent-based simulator designed to evaluate botnet propagation and the systemic effects of the Myco-Barrier defense architecture. This novel architecture adapts two bio-inspired concepts: the decentralized signaling of Fungal Mycorrhizal Networks (FMN) and the protective isolation of the Blood-Brain Barrier (BBB). The simulator is developed in Python using the Mesa agent-based modeling framework. It enables sophisticated modeling of node-level behaviors, supporting both Command-and-Control (C2) and Peer-to-Peer (P2P) botnet architectures. MycoSim allows researchers to study botnet propagation dynamics by systematically varying defense parameters and network topologies. In the future, this simulator will be expanded to incorporate and evaluate other proposed defense mechanisms in a simulated environment.

title: Deep Learning Frameworks Solve Traffic Signal Congestion in Smart Cities
authors: Hong-Cheng Chen, Yi-Hsien Chung, Hong-Yunn Chen
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Consumer Technology (ICCT-Pacific)
abstract: The traffic congestion from inefficient traffic light control remains a major urban transport problem. We propose a real-time adaptive signal control method that combines wavelet preprocessing and transformer-based object detection within the YOLO framework to improve vehicle detection and classification. Wavelets denoise and enhance multi-scale features from camera feeds, improving small-object and occluded-vehicle recognition. A lightweight transformer module refines spatial and temporal conditions, boosting YOLO's accuracy without prohibitive latency. Detected vehicle counts, classes, and queue lengths feed a decision module that dynamically adjusts green-phase durations and phase sequences to match current demand. In simulations results, our approach reduced average vehicle waiting times and queue lengths compared with fixed-time control, and increased intersection throughput during peak periods.

title: Optimization Based Inverse Kinematics Solution of 6 DOF Robot
authors: Mustafa Osman Bayoum, Mostafa Abdelgeliel, El- sayed Saber, Hassan El-Gamal
publish: 2025 35th International Conference on Computer Theory and Applications (ICCTA)
abstract: The present research focuses on two optimization approaches as an attempt to solve the inverse kinematics (IK) of the Viper650 robotic arm used in surgical applications. The discrete method uses Branch and Bound (B&B) used to find the best solution IK and it has not yet been applied to 6DOF IK. On the other hand, the continuous method uses Sequential Quadratic Programming (SQP), which deals with forward kinematics (FW) and IK as a numerical optimization problem and it is applied for the first time to this robot using MATLAB Simulink and Automation Control Environment (ACE) software. Through different trajectories, both optimization methods were tested on their execution time and path accuracy. The results demonstrated that the continuous method was quicker and more precise, whereas the discrete method gave more reliable solutions, causing the increase in the time to attain the necessary accuracy. The study demonstrates that the trade-off between accuracy and robustness, as well as the consequences of the findings, is a significant consideration in the selection of IK techniques in surgical robots.

title: Design of a Compact Multiband PIFA Antenna for IoT Systems
authors: K. Mohan Krishna, U. V. Ratna Kumari
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: This paper proposes a compact multi-band planar inverted-f antenna (PIFA) for modern internet of things (IoT) devices that need to be reliably connected wirelessly to multiple sub-6 GHz communication standards. The multi-band PIFA can work in all the most common IoT frequency bands (i.e., 0.9 GHz, 1.7 GHz, 2.4 GHz, 4.3 GHz, 5 GHz, and 5.6 GHz). It has an extremely compact form factor of 80 × 50 × 6 mm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sup> so it can easily be mounted on generic IoT hardware. This design uses a very simple multi-band PIFA configuration without the use of any fractals or slots to create additional resonances. The simplicity of this design will allow for easy fabrication and will make the antenna much less susceptible to detuning effects caused by the environment. To achieve multiresonance in the PIFA, optimized carefully selected the length of the radiating element and positioned the shorting strip to resonate at each band. The position of the feedpoint was also carefully selected to provide a match between the PIFA and the RF circuitry. Simulations using full-wave simulation software show excellent impedance matches (i.e., |S<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">11</inf>| <−10 dB) for all six bands, as well as good radiation patterns and high efficiencies for low-power IoT transmissions. The peak gains varies approximately 6 dBi to 10 dBi based on frequency bands, which should provide reliable omnidirectional coverage even when densely deployed. Due to its compact size, simple structure, and robust multi-band performance, the new PIFA should be applicable to a wide variety of IoT applications, such as smart sensing, wearable electronics, industrial monitoring, and home automation systems.

title: Scalable Network Intrusion Detection via Advanced Feature Engineering and Correlation Analysis on the CICIDS2017 Dataset
authors: Ali Mohsin, Muhammad Zunurain Hussain, Kabeer Ahmad, Muhammad Zulkifl Hasan, Fatima Ahmad, Iqra Azam
publish: 2026 International Conference on Data Science, Machine Learning, and Intelligence (DataSciMI)
abstract: In light of the current trends of massive applications of interconnected networks (IoT, cloud, etc.), this paper presents a Network Intrusion Detection System (NIDS) that is scalable and employs the latest feature engineering methods and correlation analysis. This paper presents a feature logistics technique to address the shortcomings of traditional NIDS models of large-scale heterogeneous data, creating new features and selecting important features among originals based on the latent mapping and correlation study outcomes. Denoising autoencoder (DAE) is employed to extract strength features and correlation-based dimensionality reduction is employed to get useful representations. The findings reveal that engineered features have high detection rates of standard attacks at lower computation costs than traditional methods on the CICIDS2017 dataset. The accuracy and recall of the Random Forest Classifier and Decision Tree Classifier is much higher with the engineered features than the raw ones. The suggested framework succeeds in minimizing occurrence of false alarms and computation costs and offers a viable NIDS model that can overcome key deployment issues in complicated network settings.

title: Design and Analysis of a CPW-Fed Koch Fractal Patch Antenna for Microwave and mm-Wave Applications
authors: Anuradha S, Anil Kumar Nayak, Aijaz Mehdi Zaidi
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: The proposed design utilizes a Koch fractal antenna for its multi-band behavior and a CPW feed method for enhanced radiation performance. The overall structure is designed and simulated using the CST Microwave Studio. This structure is analyzed for various performances of antenna parameters, including gain, directivity, radiation efficiency, and bandwidth. The proposed antenna obtained a gain of 8.4 dBi, a directivity of 8.6 dBi, and an efficiency of 95.44%. The CPW feed helped to observe a better impedance match at four frequencies.

title: Three-Port DC–DC Converter With Enhanced Operational Capability, Optimized Power Management, and Superior Structural Characteristics
authors: Sina Ahmadian, Vahid Abbasi, Mohammad Mehdi Kashani
abstract: This article presents a new three-port DC–DC converter (TPC) with notable features, including optimized power management and enhanced performance compared to previous topologies. The proposed TPC contains two bidirectional ports to enable power exchange between a battery, a renewable energy source (RES), and a grid. As a result, the proposed converter can operate in seven different modes to maximize the use of the generated power through flexible power management, enhanced functionality, and operational flexibility. Also, the method used for power-sharing and combining power from multiple ports addresses several common problems in existing designs. These include control difficulties, sudden voltage changes during mode transitions, and the inability to operate under unbalanced conditions with different input powers and voltage levels. In addition, several structural features were considered in the design process of the converter. These include a common ground for the ports and continuous input currents, especially for battery charging and discharging. Finally, a 200 W laboratory prototype was implemented to evaluate the converter’s performance, which was then compared with similar TPCs.

title: An Arc Model for Extracting the Breakdown Thresholds Under Various Conditions
authors: Xiaotian Tang, Zhenyuan Zhang, Jie Ren, Peng Wang, Qi Huang, Shi Jing, Wei-Jen Lee
abstract: The development of clean energy has introduced numerous power electronic devices, which generate voltage spikes that can exceed the voltage of the DC bus. Furthermore, to enhance efficiency, the voltage levels of DC photovoltaic (PV) systems gradually increase, and the PV station contains numerous connection points. These factors significantly increase the occurrence of DC arc. To prevent arc-related electrical accidents, the breakdown voltage of the air gap under various conditions should be further investigated. Nevertheless, the breakdown voltage varies significantly with factors such as conductor spacing, ambient temperature, and humidity, making it challenging to cover all scenarios through experimental methods. Thus, establishing an arc model to restore the arc behaviors is significant. In this article, typical DC arc models are established based on physical mechanisms and MHD, accurately calculating the variation in fault current before and after arc formation under different conditions, as well as the pattern of breakdown voltage under different air parameters. In addition, the model provides an instrument for arc characteristic analysis, enabling flexible adjustment of electrical topology and parameters, as well as variables related to air electrical strength, according to actual requirements.

title: Performance Analysis of Single-Antenna Fluid Antenna Systems via Extreme Value Theory
authors: Rui Xu, Yinghui Ye, Xiaoli Chu, Guangyue Lu, Kai-Kit Wong, Chan-Byoung Chae
abstract: In a single-antenna fluid antenna system (FAS), the transceiver dynamically selects the antenna port with the strongest instantaneous channel to enhance link reliability. However, deriving accurate yet tractable performance expressions under the fully correlated fading channel remains challenging, primarily due to the absence of a closed-form distribution for the FAS channel. To address this gap, this paper develops a novel performance evaluation framework for FAS under fully correlated Rayleigh fading by modeling the FAS channel using extreme value distributions (EVDs). We first justify the suitability of EVDs and model the FAS channel as a Gumbel distribution, whose parameters, estimated via the maximum likelihood (ML) criterion, are expressed as functions of the number of ports and antenna aperture size. Closed-form approximate expressions for the outage probability (OP) and the ergodic capacity (EC) are then derived. Simulation results show that the Gumbel distribution provides simple yet sufficiently accurate expressions for EC, although slight deviations in OP accur in the low-probability region of practical interest. To further improve accuracy, the FAS channel is modeled using the generalized extreme value (GEV) distribution, yielding closed-form expressions for OP and EC based on ML-estimated parameters. Simulation results confirm that the GEV distribution provides superior accuracy compared to the Gumbel, particularly for OP, while both EVD-based approaches offer computationally efficient and analytically tractable tools for evaluating FAS performance under the fully correlated fading channel.

title: AI/ML based Joint Source and Channel Coding for HARQ-ACK Payload
authors: Akash Doshi, Pinar Sen, Kirill Ivanov, Wei Yang, June Namgoong, Runxin Wang, Rachel Wang, Taesang Yoo, Jing Jiang, Tingfang Ji
publish: IEEE Journal on Selected Areas in Information Theory
abstract: Channel coding from 2G to 5G has assumed the inputs bits at the physical layer to be uniformly distributed. However, hybrid automatic repeat request acknowledgement (HARQ-ACK) bits transmitted in the uplink are inherently non-uniformly distributed. For such sources, significant performance gains could be obtained by employing joint source channel coding, aided by deep learning-based techniques. In this paper, we learn a transformer-based encoder using a novel “free-lunch” training algorithm and propose per-codeword power shaping to exploit the source prior at the encoder whilst being robust to small changes in the HARQ-ACK distribution. Furthermore, any HARQ-ACK decoder has to achieve a low negative acknowledgement (NACK) error rate to avoid radio link failures resulting from multiple NACK errors. We develop an extension of the Neyman-Pearson test to a coded bit system with multiple information bits to achieve Unequal Error Protection of NACK over ACK bits at the decoder. Finally, we apply the proposed encoder and decoder designs to a 5G New Radio (NR) compliant uplink setup under a fading channel, describing the optimal receiver design and a low complexity coherent approximation to it. Our results demonstrate 3 – 6 dB reduction in the average transmit power required to achieve the target error rates compared to the NR baseline, while also achieving a 2 – 3 dB reduction in the maximum transmit power, thus providing for significant coverage gains and power savings.

title: Addressing Covariate Shift in Industrial Soft Sensing Using Conditional Density Ratio-Based Reweighting
authors: Qingtong Gao, Cheng Lu, Shoujun Huang, Jiusun Zeng
abstract: Data-driven soft sensors in industrial processes often suffer from performance degradation due to covariate shift, where process variable distributions drift over time while the input-output relationship remains unchanged. Existing adaptation methods either struggle to capture complex distribution changes or require additional quality measurements that is unavailable in real time. To address these challenges, this paper proposes a covariate shift adaptation framework for industrial soft sensing using a conditional energy-based model (CEBM). The CEBM models the energies of training and testing data using shared neural network parameters, thereby implicitly defining their probability densities. Since the ratio between two densities depends only on the difference of their energies, the conditional density ratio between the training and testing domains can be obtained directly from the learned energy functions. The estimated density ratio is used to reweight training samples, improving the generalization capability of the soft sensor. To efficiently learn the energy function in CEBM, a Conditional Denoising Score Matching (CDSM) strategy is developed. Furthermore, an online learning mechanism with a replay buffer continuously updates the CEBM using streaming test data and historical samples, enabling real-time density ratio estimation and stable predictive performance under evolving covariate shifts. Experiments on simulated and industrial datasets demonstrate that the proposed framework substantially enhances prediction accuracy.

title: Progressive First-hit RWA for Multi-fiber Optical Networks with Blocking OXCs
authors: Dannan Hong, Tong Ye
abstract: Low-cost large-scale blocking optical cross-connects (OXCs) are promising candidate solutions for optical nodes in multi-fiber optical networks. However, existing routing and wavelength assignment (RWA) algorithms for networks with blocking OXCs suffer from excessively high time complexity, as they consider both link state information and OXC switching states when performing RWA for each lightpath (LP) request. To address this issue, we propose a progressive first-hit RWA (p-RWA) algorithm, which is motivated by our observation that RWA for most requests can be determined using only link state information. At low traffic loads, the blocking probability (BP) of OXCs is low, and considering only link state information is sufficient for RWA. At high traffic loads, path search failures caused by insufficient link resources become prominent, and thus the proportion of requests blocked by OXC internal blocking is also not significant. Thereby, the p-RWA algorithm first attempts to perform RWA using almost exclusively link state information, and uses both link state information and OXC switching states only if LP setup fails due to OXC internal blocking. We prove that the p-RWA algorithm incurs no BP performance degradation. Extensive simulations on various networks each equipped with a different type of blocking OXC show that the p-RWA algorithm can (a) remarkably reduce the average computation time, and (b) achieve identical or even lower BP, when compared with state-of-the-art RWA algorithms.

title: A Novel Outlier Detection and Reconstruction Method for State of Health Prediction of Lithium-Ion Batteries
authors: Ziwang Wang, Jie Meng, Yajun Zhao, Ding Li, Zhize Wu
abstract: Accurate prediction of lithium-ion battery SOH is critical for the safety and lifespan extension of a battery system. Feature noise and insufficient model accuracy remain major obstacles. To address these challenges, this study proposes a correlation-driven outlier detection and reconstruction method grounded in multidimensional feature engineering to enhance SOH prediction. First, from five dimensions—constant current charging, constant voltage charging, incremental capacity, temperature, and unit time voltage change rate—this work systematically defines 45 degradation features, with 33 applicable to CALCE and 38 to NASA datasets due to dataset-specific signal availability. Second, for each target feature, it screens highly correlated feature subsets using the Pearson correlation coefficient. A linear mapping is then established through regression analysis to model the expected values, enabling identification and reconstruction of outliers. Finally, based on the reconstructed feature dataset, this study conducts comparative experiments to systematically evaluate the effects of different noise-filtering strategies on model prediction accuracy and robustness. Experimental results showed that the proposed method significantly improved both the accuracy and robustness of SOH prediction. On the CALCE dataset, relative to raw data, the average RMSE, MAE, and MAPE decreased by 9.40%, 4.30%, and 6.30%, respectively. Compared with the traditional 3σ filtering method, the improvements reached 10.60%, 3.78%, and 5.63%, respectively. On the NASA dataset, the standalone gain was smaller; however, when combined with 3σ filtering, the average improvements in RMSE, MAE, and MAPE reached about 8.5%, exceeding the 3σ filtering alone by approximately 7%. Comprehensive SHAP analysis further revealed that, compared with the 3σ filtering scheme, the features processed by CorODR exhibited more stable and interpretable importance distributions.
