title: A 4.24–4.99 GHz Complementary LC VCO with Low Kvco Variation in 180-nm CMOS
authors: Yen-Chung Chiang, Tzu-Hsuan Tseng, Chun-Tsung Chen
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Consumer Technology (ICCT-Pacific)
abstract: This paper presents a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$4.24-4.99 \text{GHz}$</tex> voltage-controlled oscillator (VCO) implemented in a TSMC <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 8 0 - n m}$</tex> CMOS process with small Kvco variation. A 2-bit switched capacitor/varactor array and the complementary cross-coupled pair topology were employed. Measurement results show a frequency tuning range of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{4. 2 4}-\mathbf{4. 9 9 ~ G H z}(\mathbf{1 6. 2 5 \%})$</tex>, with phase noise of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{- 1 1 3. 9 ~ d B c} / \mathbf{H z}$</tex> at <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 ~ M H z}$</tex> offset. The variation for Kvco is smaller than <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$40 \text{MHz} / \mathrm{V}$</tex> over the entire control range, and the overall variation of Kvco is within <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\pm \mathbf{1 0 \%}$</tex>. The VCO core consumes 6.3 mW power from a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$1.8-\mathrm{V}$</tex> supply, and the chip area is <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{0. 7 6 8 ~ m m} \times \mathbf{1. 1 2 ~ m m}$</tex>.

title: Energy-Efficient Motor Imagery Brain-Machine Interface on Edge TPU
authors: Nobuaki Kobayashi, Atsushi Miyawaki
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Consumer Technology (ICCT-Pacific)
abstract: Brain-machine interface (BMI) technology facilitates the control of machines and devices via measured brain activity signals. This study focused on BMI using motor imagery (MI-BMI), which involves electroencephalography (EEG) during recall of body movements, rather than actual body movements. In terms of processing speed and privacy, BMI is unsuitable for offloading to the cloud. Although it is preferable for use in case of edge owing to convenience, there are concerns regarding insufficient memory and computational requirements in achieving high classification accuracy within the limited computational resource environment of edge. In this study, MI, which is originally represented as a 32-bit single-precision floating-point (fp32), was reduced to a low-precision 8-bit integer (int8). This was then introduced into EEGNet, which is a standard deep learning model recently used for MI classification, to examine classification accuracy and processing speed. We also quantized the weights and activations to int8 to reduce the amount of memory and computation required, while investigating whether the classification accuracy could be maintained. The BCI Competition IV-2a Dataset, a global standard benchmark for MI, was used as the dataset. The results showed that the classification accuracies were approximately 75% and 74% for fp32 and int8, respectively. Further, a sufficiently high classification accuracy could be achieved even when the numerical representation was limited to low precision. No degradation was observed even when the output data of the A/D converter was converted to integer values. We used the Edge tensor processing unit (TPU) by Google, which comprises cystic arrays, to run the quantization model. Consequently, the energy efficiency and processing speed of the edge for high-speed neural networks was verified. EEGNet has few convolutional layers and thus cannot be used continuously. Therefore, we examined whether this TPU structure was effective for EEGNet with MI. The results showed that the average processing speed per inference was approximately 0.2 ms faster. Furthermore, the power consumption was improved by approximately 0.1 mW compared to the CPU (Arm Cortex-A78AE) in the NVIDIA Jetson ORIN Nano and thus demonstrated high usefulness.

title: A Verified Pipeline Framework for Certifiable AI Robustness
authors: Sergillam B. Oliveira, Eddie de Lima Filho, Lucas C. Cordeiro
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Consumer Technology (ICCT-Pacific)
abstract: Artificial intelligence models often perform well on curated datasets but may fail under real-world perturbations, an aspect that has the potential to hinder practical applications. In that sense, conventional robustness evaluations help expose such weaknesses and then minimize field problems. However, they offer no guarantee of testing correctness. As a response, we propose the smart inference pipeline with verification (SIP-V), which addresses such limitations by formally verifying robustness evaluation pipelines rather than models themselves. It encodes perturbation rules as logical constraints and employs multiple satisfiability modulo theories solvers, at runtime, to verify preconditions and postconditions on data, label, and parameter transformations. By experimenting on benchmark datasets and representative models, we demonstrated consistent detection of injected violations and full validation of compliant cases, with average execution times of 0.24 s, using cooperating validity checker number 5, and 9.9 s, using Z3. Indeed, by integrating empirical testing with formal reasoning, SIP-V establishes a practical foundation for certifiable and auditable AI robustness.

title: Trusted Hyper-Local Urban Heat Island Spatial Estimation System for Urban Resilience
authors: Taewook Kim, Geonho Jin, Yongeun Ryu, Jaehyeok Shim, Kyeonghyeon Min, Soochang Park, Yongun Ban
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Consumer Technology (ICCT-Pacific)
abstract: The urban heat island (UHI) phenomenon poses serious risks to public health and energy management, while conventional satellite monitoring suffers from limited temporal resolution and low-cost IoT sensors face data reliability issues. This paper proposes a trusted hyper-local urban heat island spatial estimation system integrating edge computing-based IOTA blockchain and Gaussian Process Regression (GPR). Data integrity is ensured using IOTA's protocol, and high-resolution spatial temperature distributions are estimated from sparse sensor measurements using GPR. A campus-scale living lab deployment demonstrates reliable reconstruction of fine-grained temperature distributions and effective visualization of localized heat-risk areas, highlighting the feasibility of trustworthy urban microclimate monitoring.

title: Machine Learning Data-Driven Fouling Diagnostics for Marine Engine Cooling Systems
authors: Anwar El-Gafrawy, Ahmed Hebala, Mohab Gaber, Mostafa S. Hamad
publish: 2025 35th International Conference on Computer Theory and Applications (ICCTA)
abstract: Marine diesel engines operate under continuous thermal and mechanical loads, making efficient heat dissipation essential for engine reliability and longevity. This study presents a simulation-based approach to analyze and predict the effects of fouling on a marine engine cooling system using a Scania DI16 (221 kW) engine model developed in MATLAB Simscape. The freshwater and seawater pumps are powered by DC Motors controlled through H-bridge drivers using Pulse Width Modulation (PWM), enabling dynamic response to thermal demands and fouling-induced restrictions. The model also integrates into an AI-based predictive maintenance (PdM) framework, where signals such as temperature, pressure, and flow rate are used to detect early signs of performance degradation due to fouling. This fault classification is achieved using an Artificial Neural Network (ANN). Which is trained using labelled simulation data, with input features including radiator inlet flow rate, radiator outlet temperature, engine outlet temperature, and freshwater pump speed. The results demonstrate that even moderate fouling significantly impacts temperature profiles and flow behaviour, offering key indicators for early fault detection. These findings support the development of hybrid model-AI strategies for proactive system monitoring and optimised maintenance scheduling.

title: Early Sepsis Detection from ICU Data Using Multimodal Deep Learning
authors: Abhishek S. Shamkuwar, Parikshit Bhardwaj, Naveen Kumar, Manjeet Singh
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: The early prediction of sepsis detection is very important in intensive care units (ICUs) for timely intervention to save the patient’s life. But it is very challenging to detect the sepsis early due to its irregular, high-dimensional, and temporally evolving nature of the electronic health record (EHR) data. To address this problem we have evaluated the classical machine learning technique, the deep learning technique, and the multimodal learning approaches to detect early sepsis using the dataset PhysioNet Computing in Cardiology Challenge 2019 dataset which is labelled under the Sepsis-3 criteria. The traditional machine learning model and the deep learning model are used as a interpretable baseline where the machine learning model are tested on custom temporal features while the deep learning technique is used to capture the physiological time-series dynamics. To make the better clinical decision-making and to detect the early sepsis a multimodal framework is proposed that integrates physiological signals, laboratory measurements, and static patient attributes through modality-specific encoders and the attention-based fusion. The unified preprocessing pipeline is constructed where missing-value imputation is used and the mitigation of the class-imbalance is done throughout the model. The experimental findings have also proved that the multimodal learning has always performed better compared to the unimodal and recurrent baselines in regards to early sepsis detection which highlight its application to real time clinical decision support systems.

title: Intelligent Traffic Signal Optimization Using Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL)
authors: Sharique Jamal, Farheen Siddiqui, Sherin Zafar
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: In recent years, the main problems faced by smart cities were urban traffic management. To maximize traffic flow and reduce congestion, advanced computational techniques are needed. This study presents anovel computational integration model for intelligent traffic signal control in urban environments with Deep Reinforcement Learning. We combine real-time traffic flow data with machine learning to provide a dynamic optimization adaptive mechanism with a component of machine learning. Our comprehensive simulation testing using the SUMO traffic simulators suggest our proposed DRL-based architecture outperforms standard fixed-time control systems with 27% reduction in CO2 emissions and a 48% improvement in average vehicle waiting times. Our method uses adaptive incentive functions to improve policy with the metric of sustainability, with fuel use, emissions and congestion indicators as sustainability indicators. Traffic flows were predicting 96% accuracy, and traffic conditions could be changed in real time. The results of experimental validation on multi-intersection networks reveal a greater performance for real time flexibility on different traffic scenarios. The proposed approach uses scalable machine learning techniques to address problems of smart city mobility while ensures efficient computation for practical deployment.

title: Impact of Technostress on Academic Performance and Student Learning Burnout and Decision Making using Fuzzy concept and Machine Learning Techniques
authors: Neeraj Kumar Pandey, Prinkle Vohra, Dinara Tolubaevna Elchieva, Yuldasheva Dilafruz, Allanazarov Shokhrukhbek Farkhodovich, Hikmatilla Abdunabiyev
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: The speed at which digital technologies have integrated within the educational field has caused a notable change in the learning environment while at the same time introducing technostress amongst students. It is a study to investigate the effect of technostress on academic performance, learning burnout, and decision-making efficiency using a hybrid framework using fuzzy logic concepts and machine learning techniques. The key dimensions of technostress such as techno-overload, techno-invasion, techno-complexity, techno-insecurity, techno-uncertainty as well as the indicators of learning burnout, academic attributes are analyzed. Fuzzy logic is used for the management of the uncertainty and subjectivity in understanding the psychological data using linguistic modeling and Rule-based inference, whereas Machine learning algorithms such as Support Vector Machines, Random Forests Machines, Decision Trees, Neural networks, etc., capture the non-linear relationship between the data. Experimental results show that the combination of fuzzy outputs to boost the prediction results provides accuracy and the interpretability of the results compared to the standalone models. The results show an extremely negative impact of important levels of technostress influence on performance at work (academic) and decision-making efficiency mediated by an increased state of learning burnout. The proposed approach offers an effective decision support mechanism for the early identification of technostress affected students and data driven academic interventions.

title: Movable IRS-Aided ISAC Systems: Joint Beamforming and Position Optimization
authors: Yue Geng, Tee Hiang Cheng, Kai Zhong, Kah Chan Teh, Qingqing Wu
abstract: Driven by intelligent reflecting surface (IRS) and movable antenna (MA) technologies, movable IRS (MIRS) has been proposed to improve the adaptability and performance of conventional IRS, enabling flexible adjustment of the IRS reflecting element positions. This paper investigates MIRS-aided integrated sensing and communication (ISAC) systems. The objective is to minimize the power required for satisfying the quality-of-service (QoS) of sensing and communication by jointly optimizing the MIRS element positions, IRS reflection coefficients, transmit beamforming, and receive filters. To balance the performance-cost trade-off, we proposed two MIRS schemes: element-wise control and array-wise control, where the positions of individual reflecting elements and arrays consisting of multiple elements are controllable, respectively. To address the joint beamforming and position optimization, a product Riemannian manifold optimization (PRMO) method is proposed, where the variables are updated over a constructed product Riemannian manifold space (PRMS) in parallel via penalty-based transformation and Riemannian Broyden–Fletcher–Goldfarb–Shanno (RBFGS) algorithm. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed MIRS outperforms conventional IRS in power minimization with both element-wise control and array-wise control. Specifically, with different system parameters, the minimum power is achieved by the MIRS with the element-wise control scheme, while suboptimal solution and higher computational efficiency are achieved by the MIRS with array-wise control scheme.

title: A Unified Framework With Capped Tensor Norm Minimization for Multiview Subspace Learning_supp1-3694749.pdf
authors: Zhi Wang
abstract: In recent years, multi-view subspace clustering (MVSC) has emerged as a powerful tool for high-dimensional data analysis, due to its ability to exploit intrinsic structures across multiple feature spaces. However, existing MVSC methods still suffer from two shortcomings: 1) the prevalent use of convex low-rank approximations inadequately exploits complementary subspace information, and 2) the neglect of intrinsic geometric relationships and the execution of spectral clustering as a separate step often lead to suboptimal solutions. To alleviate these problems, in this paper we propose a robust and effective model for the MVSC task by jointly incorporating the capped norm, which not only effectively captures the intrinsic global low-rank structure and suppresses the influence of feature outliers by utilizing capped tensor nuclear norm (CTNN), but also integrates the hyper- Laplacian regularization and the spectral embedding through joint optimization to organically benefit from each other. Then, a high computational efficiency algorithm is developed under the alternating direction method of multipliers (ADMM) framework to solve the proposed model. Meanwhile, rigorous mathematical analysis shows that the global optimum of the CTNN regularized least squares subproblem can be analytically determined in closed-form. More importantly, the convergence of the proposed algorithm is theoretically established by proving that the sequence it generates converges to a desirable Karush-Kuhn-Tucker (KKT) point. Finally, extensive experiments on various datasets demonstrate the superiority of the proposed model. MATLAB code is available at <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/wangzhi-swu/UFCTNM</uri>.

title: ULTER: Unified Latch-Based Hardware Solver for Versatile Combinatorial Optimization Problems
authors: Yimin Wang, Xuanyao Fong
publish: IEEE Journal on Emerging and Selected Topics in Circuits and Systems
abstract: Electronic solvers for combinatorial optimization problems (COPs) have attracted significant research interest as a poor man’s replica of the costly quantum computers. Though existing works have shown their great potential in speed and energy/area efficiency, two primary challenges remain: i) efficient optimization of the circuit-level parameter to improve the system robustness; and ii) extensibility to solving the full portfolio of COPs, beyond binary variable and second-order coupling. They are important for system scaling and solving general COPs, and rely on a comprehensive hardware-software co-optimization framework. This work focuses on a framework unifying the representations of various COPs on bistable latch-based circuit building blocks, named Unified Latch-based COP Solver (ULTER) and using a corresponding co-optimization approach to perform system-level optimizations. The ULTER system is comprehensively evaluated on the MaxCut, XORSAT, NAE-SAT, and graph coloring problems, greatly extending the robustness and generality of the existing analog COP solvers.

title: A Trajectory Prediction Method for USV Considering Marine Disturbance Environments
authors: Pengfei Zhao, Xiaohui Liu
abstract: This paper studies the trajectory prediction of low-speed Unmanned Surface Vehicles (USVs), particularly the influence of the marine environment. The trajectory-prediction technique predicts the future position, speed, and heading of the USV by analyzing influencing factors such as its historical trajectory, motion state, and current environmental conditions. Firstly, the kinematic and dynamic models of the USV are discussed. Then, three main aspects of the marine environmental disturbance, including wind, waves and current, are taken into account. Through the analysis of wind, wave and hydrodynamic forces, the corresponding mathematical models are built to predict the navigation trajectory of the USV. Finally, the effectiveness of the prediction method is verified by comparing it with experimental data. The results show that the proposed USV trajectory prediction method has good accuracy, with the overall error being only 4.5%, and it can be applied to the practical USV track-following.

title: Traffic-Aware Design for Multi-dimensional Lookup and Forwarding: From IP Routing to Packet Classification
authors: Xinyi Zhang, Qianrui Qiu, Zhiyuan Xu, Peng He, Guangxing Zhang, Luyiyun Li, Jianer Zhou, Kavé Salamatian, Gaogang Xie
abstract: Packet processing in modern routers and switches relies on rule matching, primarily performed by two core modules: IP prefix lookup for next-hop determination and packet classification for multi-field policy enforcement. However, most existing algorithms are rule-centric and assume uniform rule access, overlooking the highly skewed nature of real-world network traffic. Such mismatch between static rule organization and dynamic traffic behavior leads to inefficiency in both lookup and classification. To address this limitation, we propose a Traffic-aware Lookup and Forwarding (TLF) framework that leverages traffic measurement with lookup operations, enabling online adaptation to dynamic traffic patterns and frequent rule updates. Experimental results demonstrate that TLF provides 1.04×–3.37× speedups for lookup and forwarding over state-of-the-art algorithms, while substantially reducing both memory overhead and construction time. Furthermore, integrating TLF into Vector Packet Processor (VPP) and Open vSwitch (OVS) results in throughput improvements of 2.61× and 4.88×, respectively.

title:
Machine Learning Prediction of Threshold Switching Voltages Indicates a Filamentary-to-Homogeneous Transition in TiO
                    <sub>2</sub>
                    /TiO
                    <sub>2-x</sub>
                    /TiO
                    <sub>2</sub>
                    Memristors
authors: Fatih Gül
abstract: We report a machine-learning (ML) analysis of 763 bipolar switching cycles measured on 62 nanoscale TiO2-based memristors of two architectures: single-layer TiO2 (Device A) and TiO2/TiO2-x/TiO2 (Device B), the latter incorporating an engineered oxygen-deficient interlayer. Under leave-one-device-out (LODO) cross-validation, XGBoost predicts VSET with R2 =0.875, MAE = 6mV and VRESET with R2 = 0.834, MAE = 14mV on entirely unseen devices. SHapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP) identify the sweep upper limit and compliance current as dominant predictors, while the device-type indicator carries a statistically meaningful secondary weight and captures a ∼ 6× offset in mean threshold voltage (Device B: 311mV vs. Device A: 51mV for VSET). This offset is consistent with an interlayer-induced transition from filamentary to homogeneous switching, providing a data-driven indication of oxygen-vacancy engineering in metal-oxide memristors and is corroborated by independent X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) evidence of an oxygen-vacancy-rich TiO2-x stoichiometry on the same fabrication platform.

title: Leveraging Fourier Token Mixing for Efficient Position-Aided Beam Selection
authors: Mohamed Mohsen, Ahmad A. Aziz El-Banna, Octavia A. Dobre, Hyundong Shin
abstract: The next-generation wireless networks demand ultra-reliable and low-latency communication with seamless mobility support. A key enabler is intelligent beam selection in millimeter-wave systems (mmWave), where traditional beam training methods suffer from excessive overhead, especially in high-mobility scenarios. To address this limitation, we propose a novel Fourier network (FNet)-based beam prediction approach that efficiently captures temporal dependencies in user location data to enable low-latency and high-accuracy beam selection. FNet, a transformer encoder-inspired architecture, replaces the multi-head self-attention sublayer in the bidirectional encoder representations from transformers (BERT) network with the fast Fourier transforms, reducing the computational complexity while retaining the ability to capture spatial-temporal dependencies. Simulation results show that FNet achieves a 96% reduction in power loss and up to a 47% accuracy improvement over the baselines, including deep-learning-based approaches such as deep neural networks and autoencoders, while also outperforming conventional non-learning baselines such as k-nearest neighbors and lookup-table methods. In addition, by leveraging the parameter-free nature of the Fourier transform, the model significantly optimizes computational overhead, yielding training and inference time reductions of up to 78.5% and 58.5%, respectively, compared to the standard BERT architecture. These results validate FNet as a computationally lean, high-precision solution for real-time spatial context-aware beamforming in highly dynamic mmWave systems.

title: Efficient Slice-Parallel Distributed Probing in SRv6 Networks
authors: Kaiyang Zhao, Han Zhang, Yao Tong, Yahui Li, Xingang Shi, Zhiliang Wang, Xia Yin, Jianping Wu
abstract: Segment Routing over IPv6 (SRv6) is widely deployed, where operators construct numerous parallel SR-based network slices. An accurate diagnosis of latency bottlenecks in SRv6 tunnels is essential to maintain service-level objectives. However, building a distributed, at-scale probing system is non-trivial: SRv6 priority policies, SR-aware multipath forwarding, and slice isolation collectively invalidate assumptions made by existing diagnostic methods. In this paper, we present SRmesh, a distributed system for diagnosing latency bottleneck links in SRv6 networks. First, we adopt distributed SR-based probing agents to control routing paths and ensure that probe packets emulate per-slice production traffic. Second, we employ a latency-based multipath inference that runs in parallel across slices to resolve SR-induced routing ambiguities. Third, we introduce a sliceparallel progressive diagnosis that incrementally reuses probe results to reduce redundant measurements, optimizing diagnostic overhead for large-scale SRv6 overlay networks. We implement a prototype of SRmesh and conduct extensive evaluations on 247 real network topologies. The results indicate that SRmesh achieves high diagnostic accuracy with a 93.4% reduction in probe overhead, demonstrating its practicality and scalability in large-scale SRv6 environments.

title: Automated Bacteriuria Detection and Grading using Deep Learning
authors: Vidyashree R. Kanabur, Deepu Vijayasenan, S. Sumam David, Sreejith Govindan
abstract: Urine culture is a standard method for diagnosing Urinary Tract Infections (UTIs) by identifying bacteria and pus cells, but it is often time-consuming and may produce negative results. To expedite diagnosis, clinicians frequently examine urine smears under a microscope to detect bacteria before conducting the culture test. This manual approach is labor-intensive, time-consuming, and requires specialized expertise. Additionally, bacteriuria grading is essential to assess the severity of infection. Our study aims to address these challenges by automating the segmentation and grading of bacteria from gram-stained microscopic urine smear images using deep learning approaches. Although deep learning has been explored for urine smear analysis, it’s potential for accurate bacterial segmentation and grading remains under explored. Deep learning models require large annotated datasets, but acquiring comprehensive data covering all grades of bacteriuria is challenging.We address these limitations in two stages. First, we propose a morphology-guided, bacteriuria-grade-aware label synthesis framework that covers all morphological classes of bacteria. Second, we propose a novel conditional GAN architecture that uses these synthesized labels to generate clinically realistic urine smear images.We used this synthetic dataset to train the proposed system. Our system demonstrated a 2% higher Fscore compared to Baseline, a 14% improvement in bacteriuria grading accuracy, and a 30% reduction in Root Mean Square Error (RMSE) for bacterial counting compared to state-of-the-art (SOTA) deep learning methods. These findings are statistically significant, indicating that our proposed approach could significantly enhance the accuracy and efficiency of UTI diagnosis, leading to improved patient outcomes.

title: Dual-Stream Autoencoder Architecture for Audio-Based Anomaly Detection in Industrial Quality Control
authors: Milad Ashourpour, Ghazaleh Azizpour
abstract: Quality control and assurance in manufacturing have evolved from traditional visual and manual inspection methods to complex automated systems. Modern industrial environments increasingly rely on advanced sensing technologies, moving away from basic threshold-based monitoring to machine learning approaches, and ultimately to deep-learning frameworks for comprehensive defect detection. Among these emerging technologies, sound-based anomaly detection has gained attention due to its non-invasive nature and ability to capture deviations that could indicate product defects or equipment malfunctions. However, current acoustic-based anomaly detection systems face challenges in effective integration of diverse audio features for industrial quality control. This study investigates the use of acoustic data for anomaly detection through the combined analysis of statistical and temporal data streams. A framework is developed to explore the integration of acoustic features for industrial quality control, addressing the need for robust systems capable of distinguishing between normal and anomalous acoustic signatures in real manufacturing environments. The methodology presents Modular Statistical Temporal Anomaly Network by employing separate processing streams for statistical and temporal features that are subsequently integrated through adaptive weighted combination strategies. The approach implements a CNN-based temporal autoencoder with multi-head reconstruction capabilities, coupled with dense neural networks for statistical feature processing. Evaluation on real industrial shopfloor data with severe class imbalance (13:1) demonstrates effective anomaly detection capabilities. A five-seed ablation study shows the statistical stream achieves AUC 1.0000±0.0000 and the temporal stream AUC 0.9644±0.0118, with the combined model maintaining perfectAUC and pAUC across all seeds. At the recommended operating point, precision = recall = F1 = 1.000 on a 129-sample held-out test set with zero false positives, and a processing time of 0.246 seconds per clip supports near real-time industrial deployment. External validation on the MIMII public benchmark across four mechanically distinct machine types (valve, fan, slide rail, pump) at 6 dB SNR shows the MSTAN statistical stream exceeding the published Hitachi DAE baseline on all four machine types, with the fused model surpassing the baseline on three of four and the dual-stream architecture producing non-degrading fusion over both individual streams on the slide rail machine.

title: To Trust or Not to Trust: Optimal Trust-Aware Task Offloading in Vehicular Fog Computing
authors: Zahra Seyedi, Jiejun Hu-Bolz, Shahzad Ahmad
abstract: Given a Vehicular Fog Computing (VFC) scenario, computing tasks with varying sensitivity levels must be offloaded while jointly balancing latency, energy, and trust requirements. Existing trust-based schemes often neglect some of these trade-offs or fail to adapt to task sensitivity and node reliability in dynamic vehicular settings. This paper proposes a trust-based task offloading (TTO) framework that employs a multi-dimensional trust model, combining Reputation, Experience, and Knowledge (REK) to compute a trust-aware overhead function that adapts to task requirements.We also incorporate computable encryption options, enabling task owners (TOs) to protect highly sensitive tasks. The interaction among TOs is modeled as a potential game, which implies the finite-improvement property and the existence of a Nash equilibrium, enabling distributed, self-adaptive decision-making that aligns individual incentives with overall system efficiency. This design allows vehicles to achieve an optimal balance between trust assurance and performance without centralized coordination. The efficiency of the framework against centralized approaches is validated by analyzing the price of anarchy. The simulation results confirm that TTO converges in 60 iterations for 100 nodes, achieving a 30-35% lower average delay and up to 25% lower total overhead compared to the considered baselines. These results indicate that TTO effectively balances trust, latency, and energy requirements.

title: Design and Implementation of a 16-Bit RISC Processor Using Verilog HDL with FPGA
authors: Aditya, Surati Vishnuvardhan, Uppari Sathwik, Nitesh Kashyap, Vineet Kumar
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: The paper represents layout, implementation, simulate, and synthesis of a 16-bit RISC (Reduced Instruction Set Computer) processor using Verilog HDL (Hardware Description Language). The processor is designed with a simplified instruction set architecture to demonstrate core concepts of CPU design such as datapath organization, control signal generation, and instruction execution flow. The architecture consists of essential modules including the IM (Instruction Memory), DM (Data Memory) ALU (Arithmetic Logic Unit), CU (Control Unit), Reg (Register) File and PC (Program Counter). The processor follows a structured execution flow comprising fetch instruction, decode instruction, execute instruction, memory access instruction, and write-back stages. Functional verifications carried out using simulation waveforms, and synthesis is performed using Xilinx Vivado. Simulation results confirm correct instruction execution and data memory updates for all supported operations. The proposed design serves as an educational platform for understanding processor architecture and VLSI design methodologies.

title: Microwave Passive Circuits for 5G Applications
authors: Sadhana Singh, Gopala Krishna Rapaka, Aijaz M. Zaidi
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: This research presents the design of a wideband Wilkinson power divider (WPD) and a matching circuit 5G applications. Both the circuits have been designed on Rogers4350B of thickness 20 mill for n78 Band of 5G ranging from 3.3 GHz to 3.8 GHz frequency. Both the circuits have been designed on Advanced Design System of Keysight Technologies. It has been observed from simulation results that WPD achieves insertion loss of -3.1 dB and return loss of -20 dB. However, a return loss of less than 22 dB and a transmission of better than 0.2 dB have been is achieved for matching circuity over the band n78.

title: Prediction and Decision-Making Process of Cultural Trends using Big Data: Enhanced Social Analysis Through an Artificial Intelligence-Based Fuzzy System
authors: Varsha Mittal, Bobbinpreet Kaur, Husniddin Tojiyev, Umarov Ismoiljon Olimjonovich, Darmenov Janjayxan Abuovich, Normatov Sanjar
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: Cultural trends are growing more popular in the tourist industry. The objective of this research is to enhance the prediction and decision-making process for cultural tourist attractions using deep learning (DL) methods and Internet of Things (IoT) technologies. The primary focus is on detecting and extracting features from Mongolian decoration trends, which will provide novel perspectives into the broad utilization of cultural tradition and visual architecture. This research proposes that fuzzy decision-making enables more precise assessments in a setting of unpredictability. The investigation creates a multiple impact through the use of a smart decision-making platform. This multiplier impact is apparent in the framework's ability to handle big databases and provide timely, accurate decision assistance, making it critical for tourist asset administration and scheduling. The outcomes of this research show that improving smart growth technologies for local tourism using IoT can improve the performance of smart approaches. In regard to pattern identification performance, VGGNet, AlexNet, and ResNet reach accuracy scores of 94.6%, 90.9%, and 97.0%, respectively, whereas the suggested fusion-based fuzzy approach achieves 98.9%. These findings provide practical data for remote tourist image development and highlight the value of using fuzzy decision algorithms in city visual and tourism development. Furthermore, the study's findings have important practical consequences for the growth of Mongolian cultural tourist attractions, as well as critical insights for investigating new approaches in image evaluation and pattern identification. This investigation provides valuable information for future study projects in relevant fields.

title: Towards a Generalized Machine Learning Framework for Diabetes Prediction Across Public Datasets
authors: Muhammad Shamraiz Iqbal, Maliaka, Adven Masih, Asma Irshad, Tanzil Rabbani
publish: 2026 International Conference on Data Science, Machine Learning, and Intelligence (DataSciMI)
abstract: Diabetes is a major global health challenge that requires early and accurate diagnosis to reduce complications and alleviate the burden on health care systems. This research aims to present a machine learning approach to predict diabetes, comparing four supervised machine learning algorithms [Logistic Regression (LR), Support Vector Machine (SVM), Random Forest (RF) and XGBoost (XGB)] on three diverse datasets: the Pima Indians Diabetes Dataset (768 records), the Diabetes Health Indicators Dataset (100000 samples) and the Pabna Diabetes Dataset (465 records) from Bangladesh. A uniform pipeline was implemented, including data preprocessing, under-sampling for class imbalance, feature scaling, and standardized evaluation methods. Performance was measured via accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and paired t-tests for statistical significance. Findings indicate that XGBoost excelled consistently, attaining 77.27% accuracy on the Pima dataset, 89.50% accuracy with 93.80% recall on the Diabetes Health Indicators dataset, and 100% accuracy on the Pabna dataset. Random Forest similarly achieved perfect scores on the Pabna data. However, the perfect results on the Pabna dataset (test size of 93 samples) require careful interpretation and likely indicate overfitting or dataset-specific artifacts rather than true model perfection; external validation is essential before clinical deployment. This result highlights the efficacy of ensemble methodologies in different datasets with varying sample size, features, and population. This study contributes to the development of robust, population-independent models for predicting diabetes, and calls for cross-dataset generalization testing and external validation.

title: Ontology Development Of Literary Epics
authors: Shikha Mishra, Sneha Kumari, Maria Jamal, Rashmi Sharma
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: Ontologies provide a way of knowledge representation in a structured and machine-readable form, supporting reasoning, semantic search, and question answering [1]. While they have been widely applied in scientific and technical domains, their use in literature, especially in large epics presents unique challenges [2]. Epics such as the Mahabharata, the Ramayana, or the Quran contain hundreds of characters, layered events, and culturally rich relationships that are difficult to capture with traditional ontology-building methods [3]. The GENOME methodology was an important step forward, offering the first systematic approach for epic ontology construction from the earlier used ad-hoc approach [4]. However, its workflow is almost entirely manual, making it time-taking, with a limited accuracy of competency question (CQ) answering to approximately 89% [4].In this paper, we propose an extension of the already existing GENOME ontology model that introduces semi-automation through the incorporation of Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Learning (ML). The use of Natural Language Processing (NLP) helps the system automatically identify entities, relationships between entities and the events in the text [5], [6]. Along with this, AI-based reasoning helps in suggesting schema refinements and check consistency [7]. This approach links epic concepts with external knowledge bases thus enriching the ontology while a human-in-the-loop review ensures that the literary nuance and cultural accuracy is not lost [8].Similarly to the GENOME model, we applied this semi-automated workflow to the Mahabharata, improving CQ answering accuracy to 92–93% while significantly reducing manual effort. These results demonstrate that combining AI/ML with expert review can make epic ontology development more accurate, scalable, and efficient, and open the door for further automation in digital humanities.

title: An Intelligent Hybrid Quantum Machine Learning Approach for Early Anomaly Detection
authors: Bijay Singh, Dilip Kumar, Pawan Kumar, Vikas Tyagi
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: The integration of Quantum Computing and Machine Learning (QML) holds transformative potential for intelligent data analysis, especially in processing high-dimensional and complex datasets. This paper proposes a hybrid quantum-classical framework for proactive anomaly detection, combining Quantum Auto-Encoders (QAEs) for feature compression with Quantum k-Nearest Neighbors (QkNN) for classification. Tailored for high-stakes domains like cybersecurity and IoT analytics, the approach leverages quantum entanglement and exponential state-space properties to enhance anomaly identification accuracy and efficiency. QAEs capture latent representations of typical behavior, while QkNN applies quantum parallelism to classify deviations against known patterns. Evaluations on standard benchmark datasets demonstrate the proposed QAE+QkNN framework’s superiority in precision, scalability, and resilience, particularly under noisy and sparse data conditions. While current limitations in quantum hardware pose constraints, the results underscore the method’s practical viability and promise. This study contributes to the growing body of QML research and highlights the potential of quantum-augmented detection systems in enabling the next generation of real-time, intelligent monitoring solutions.

title: Comparative Study of Phase-Frequency Detector Architectures for CMOS PLLs in 180-nm CMOS
authors: Prakash Chandra Joshi, Mohit Kumar, Ashish Raman
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: Phase-frequency detectors (PFDs) are an important building block in phase-locked loops (PLLs) and strongly influence locking behavior, jitter, and overall loop performance. In practical PLL designs, different PFD architectures show noticeable differences in dead-zone behavior, power consumption, and operating speed. This paper presents a comparative study of commonly used CMOS PFD architectures, namely the XOR phase detector, the conventional tri-state PFD, and a modified tri-state PFD. All PFDs are implemented using the same CMOS technology and simulated under identical supply voltage and input conditions to allow a fair comparison. We have evaluated the various parameters such as dead-zone width, reset delay, maximum operating frequency, power consumption, and output pulse behavior of the PFD in SCL 180nm PDK using Cadence Virtuoso. In the simulation results we have seen the strengths as well as the limitations of each architecture and show how the choice of PFD affects PLL performance. The observations from this study can assist designers in selecting a suitable PFD architecture for low-power and low-jitter CMOS PLL applications.

title: Ultra-Low Latency Real-Time Annotation System for 8K Laparoscopic Surgery Telementoring
authors: Hiroshi Shimamoto, Yukihide Kanemitsu, Shunsuke Tsukamoto, Katsuhito Kameda, Yasuaki Kanatsugu, Jun'ichi Tachino, Jun'ichi Yamazaki, Takayuki Ito
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Consumer Technology (ICCT-Pacific)
abstract: We have previously developed an 8K laparoscopic surgery system and a real-time telementoring system that transmits live 8K laparoscopic video over a communication network, enabling experienced surgeons at a remote site to provide guidance to surgeons in the operating room. In telementoring systems, it is essential that instruction information from the remote expert, particularly visual annotation information, be displayed with minimal latency. In this study, we present a detailed description of an ultra-low-latency annotation system incorporated into the previously reported 8K real-time telementoring system. The annotation system employs a high-speed UDP/RTP transmission protocol and a local image compositing device that overlays annotation information onto 8K video with only one video frame of latency. This paper describes the system design, performance characteristics, and clinical validation of the proposed annotation system.

title: Single-Camera Gaze Parallax Correction for Video Conferencing Systems
authors: Wen-Chung Kao, Chun-Chia Lee, Chun-Yu Kao
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Consumer Technology (ICCT-Pacific)
abstract: Gaze parallax caused by the offset between the camera position and the fixation point of the viewer remains a key limitation of video conferencing systems. It disrupts natural eye contact and reduces the sense of presence. This paper proposes a real-time gaze-correction framework that adopts only a single visible-light camera and requires no additional hardware. The method estimates the viewing direction using an eyeball-rotation model and predicts the ideal iris position for a camera-facing gaze. It then applies geometric deformation and image inpainting to reconstruct the corrected eye region. Experimental results show that the system generates visually consistent gaze-corrected images in real time. The proposed approach enhances perceived eye contact and improves the naturalness of remote interactions.

title: Read Voltage Optimization in Nonlinear Ion Drift Memristor Model with Reduced SET/RESET Voltages
authors: Vinay Parab, Dharmendra Singh Yadav
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: Memristor is a promising data storage device using resistive switching behavior. Conventional memories faced with low-power, high-density features. The read and write operation advancement plays crucial role in the system performance. In this paper, using Nonlinear Ion Drift memristor model, the I-V characteristics of memristor is shown with reduced SET/RESET voltages. Further, using the non-destructive read/write memory circuit, the write operation is done and delay of both write ‘0’ and write ‘1’ is calculated. For stable read ‘0’ and read ‘1’ output, read voltage is finalized by applying read voltage sweep from 1.0 V to 0.4 V.

title: Explainable Multimodal Plant Disease Classification with Prompt-Guided Transformers
authors: Muhammad Mohsin, Imran Ashraf, Muhammad Junaid, Anam Ahsan, Saira Moin U Din, Sana Fatima
publish: 2026 International Conference on Data Science, Machine Learning, and Intelligence (DataSciMI)
abstract: Plant pathogens are serious challenges to food security in the world, and thus automated diagnosis systems are required to ensure timely response. Although the current deep learning models can classify with high accuracy, they are black boxes that are not interpretable and based on visual features only, which makes them not as reliable when applied in real-life agricultural scenarios. The existing methods do not incorporate semantic data with visual cues, and do not offer any quantified values of model explainability. The current piece suggests PCViT-DRS, a Prompt-Guided Cross-Modal Vision Transformer with Disease Region Supervision, which incorporates textual symptom description in the form of handcrafted descriptions on the visual embeddings using cross-attention, and the lesion-centered attention through pseudo-ground-truth masks. Stratified splits, data augmentation, and weighted cross-entropy loss were applied to the PlantVillage dataset (54,306 images, 38 classes) to train and evaluate the model to address imbalanced classes. PCViT-DRS was able to reach 98.87% with standard deviation of 0.11% and it is a 2.75% absolute accuracy improvement (68% reduction of error) over the ViT base and significantly better than EfficientNetV2B3 + ViT ( 85.06% ) with statistical significance ( p < 0.01 ). Measures of quantitative interpretability showed significant positive changes: the IoU improved by 25.6% points to 67.1%, and the accuracy of the Pointing Game by 68.2% to 88.6%, which confirmed faithful disease localization. The multimodal scheme offers an understandable and strong basis of credible diagnosis of plant diseases in precision agriculture practices.

title: Design and Simulation of a 3.5 GHz RF Power Amplifier Using GaN HEMT Technology
authors: Komal Chhaya, Neetu Sood, Sukwinder Singh
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: This paper covers the design and simulation of a RF Power Amplifier, operating within the 3.4–4.2 GHz frequency spectrum, and thus applicable to sub-6GHz 5G technologies. The proposed amplifier uses a Cree CGH40010F GaN HEMT transistor, chosen for its ability to handle high power and its efficiency at microwave frequencies. The design procedure involved examining the DC bias condition, checking amplifier stability, performing load-pull simulations, and developing the input and output matching networks using microstrip lines. The simulation outcomes demonstrate that the amplifier exhibits effective impedance matching and consistent performance throughout the 3.4–4.2 GHz frequency range. At the centre frequency of 3.5 GHz, the amplifier achieves a small-signal gain of approximately 12 dB, delivers an output power of about approximately 39 dBm and exhibits a peak power-added efficiency of nearly 52% with a drain efficiency of approximately 57.5%. These results confirm that the proposed power amplifier is suitable for mid-band 5G transmitter applications [1], [6].

title: RDLF-Net: A Robust Hybrid Radiomics-Deep Learning Framework for Non-Invasive Caries Classification and its External Validation
authors: Hanuman Maurya, Radhey Shyam, Nishi Singh, Nagendra Pratap Singh
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: The correct diagnosis of caries extension and depth plays a central role in planning the dental treatment. Although recent developments have shown the potential of AI, current approaches, be it deep learning (black-box) or radiomics (limited feature set), are associated with the lack of accuracy, interpretability, and generalizability. RDLF-Net is a new hybrid framework presented in this paper that provides a synergistic fusion of hand-engineered radiomic features and data-driven deep features obtained in a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) to classify caries in intraoral photos. The model provides a feature selection mechanism in order to maximize performance and explainability through SHAP. On an internal test of 286 images, RDLF-Net attained accuracy of 97.2% with ICDAS extension and 96.8% with E-D depth. These results were statistically evaluated using McNemar as well as on DeLong test, and they are significantly better than the radiomics-only and deep learning only baselines. More importantly, the SMARTSmile dataset was externally validated, and the generalizability was better with a single caries detection accuracy of 94.7, which is much higher than the corresponding approaches. This paper confirms that the proposed RDLF-Net is a strong, precise, and clinically transparent method of non-invasive caries evaluation. The next steps in work are the inclusion of automated segmentation and multi-center trials, which will be carried out to make AI-based dentistry accessible in the future.

title: AI-Driven Legal Decision Support Systems for Strengthening the Rule of Law in Digital Societies
authors: Ayushi Jain, Jaspreet Singh, Shohrat Dovletov, Akhmedov Abdulatif Mashrapovich, Najimov Miratdiyin Shamshetdinovich, Sadirdin Khudoykulov
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: The blistering digitalization of the legal systems has escalated the issues with case overload, discrepancy in cases, lack of transparency, and an unequal access to justice. At this, Artificial Intelligence (AI)-based Legal Decision Support Systems have become a promising option that will allow to increase the efficiency of the legal domain and maintain basic rule-of-law principles. In this paper, we suggest an AI-Driven Legal Decision Support System (AI-LDSS) which will enhance the rule of law within the digital societies by providing consistent, transparent, and ethically oriented decision support. The suggested system unites legal information processing, natural language processing, legal reasoning based on a knowledge graph, predictive analytics, explainable AI, and human-in-the-loop monitoring into a consistent workflow system. In contrast to the fully automated decision system, the AI-LDSS is an aid system that offloads legal professionals and preserves the power and responsibility of humans. Several performance metrics were used to evaluate the system and they included; predictive accuracy, prediction alignment, processing efficiency, fairness and explainability. As it was proven in experimental research, the proposed AI-LDSS is more effective than traditional rule-based and machine learning algorithms because it has a high predictive accuracy, high conformity to judicial precedents, shorter decision time, low-bias scores, and a higher level of organism transparency. These results show that AI when structured and controlled conscientiously can thus secure the integrity of the law as opposed to eroding it. The paper concludes that legal decision support systems defined by AI can be crucial in the current digital ecosystem and legal justice by enhancing efficiency, fairness and confidence among citizens, thus empowering the rule of law in the digital society.

title: Machine Learning and Optimization for Future Healthcare Devices
authors: Meenakshi Munjal, Manjusha Magdum, Harpreet Singh Saghra, Nayana U Shinde, Nilashri Sakhalkar, Mohit Garg
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: Medical machines in the near future are more dependent on machine learning (ML) to provide continuous sensory and decision support to wearables and intelligent diagnostic systems. Nonetheless, the current ML-enabled healthcare hardware is characterized by low deployability because of expensive computational power, energy consumption, and poor performance in real-life operating environments. The paper focuses on these challenges and presents a resource and reliability-conscious machine learning framework model that is specifically developed to address next-generation healthcare devices. The suggested system combines featherweight ML models and multi-objective optimization to achieve a combined balance of predictive performance, energy consumption, and inference latency on embedded healthcare systems. The framework is assessed with the help of multimodal physiological information that was obtained with the help of wearable monitoring devices, such as time-series biosignals and contextual sensor data. The optimization goals are set in such a way that energy consumption and response time are kept at a minimum without compromising on the clinically acceptable prediction accuracy. It is experimentally tested on a realistic edge-device simulation and benchmarked with the traditional ML pipelines, not optimized in terms of its coupling. Findings indicate that there are uniform decreases in energy consumption and inference latency and predictive performance is stable under different operating conditions. The paper points out trade offs between modeling complexity and device-level constraints, and speaks on limitations associated with heterogeneity of datasets and devices. The results justify the possibility of using optimization to design new healthcare devices in the future and do not emphasize the readiness to implement the new device in clinical settings.

title: A CMOS-Based Voltage Reference with Cascaded Current Mirror
authors: Krishan Kumar, Manoj Kumar, Mansi Jhamb
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: A CMOS-based voltage reference with a cascaded current mirror circuit is proposed in this work. A voltage reference eliminates the need for passive resistors or amplifiers is reported. Line sensitivity is enhanced through the use of a cascaded current mirror at output stage of a two-transistor voltage reference circuit. Voltage reference circuit simulation is done using standard 90nm CMOS Process. Power dissipation in two-transistor CMOS voltage reference circuit is 12.80 nW at 1V, and power dissipation in the Cascaded current mirror is 12.88 nW at 1V. Line sensitivity for a two-transistor CMOS circuit is 0.47727 %/V, and Line sensitivity for CMOS voltage reference with a cascaded current mirror is 0.47195 %/V. The output reference voltage of two-transistor-based voltage reference circuits is 2.38858 mV, and the Output voltage of the voltage reference with cascaded current mirror is 2.27034 mV.

title: Lung Disease Classification through Explainable AI and Transfer Learning Approach
authors: Anurag Kumar Srivastava, Garima Verma, Km Ikra, Salman Khursheed Ahmad, Basu Dev Shivahare, Shiva Prakash
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: The objective of this study is to examine an innovative framework for automated lung disease classification by employing a combination of transfer learning methods and explainable artificial intelligence (XAI). In order to improve diagnostic accuracy and interpretability, we carefully evaluate four deep architectures (effectiveNet-XAI, DenseNet-CAM, ResNet-SHAP, and ViT-GradExplainer) to improve diagnostic accuracy and interpretability is being compared. Each model utilizes domain-specific fine-tuning and visual explanations (Grad-CAM++, SHAP, LIME-GradCAM) to demonstrate important pathological regions in chest radiographs. Experiments conducted on the CheXpert and NIH ChestX-ray14 datasets indicate that EfficientNet-XAI achieves the best classification accuracy of 95.3%, AUC of 0.986, and IoU of.080, compared with the other examined architectures. The empirical evidence suggests that combining deep transfer learning with an explanation mechanism offers advantages in classification reliability while also providing explanations that can be understood by clinicians. This work highlights the potential of interpretable deep methods to be used reliably as computer-aided diagnosis and clinical imaging interpretation tools in pulmonary disease diagnostics.

title: Electromagnetic Design of Hybrid Stepper Motors Accounting for Magnetic Saturation Toward Servo-Level Torque Density
authors: Min-Fu Hsieh, Anh Thanh Huynh, Minh-Hoc Duong Le, Yu-Xian Lee
abstract: This paper aims to close the performance gap between stepper and servo motors by enhancing the electromagnetic capabilities of a hybrid stepper motor to approach servo-level performance, while preserving its advantage of low permanent-magnet usage. Due to the complex three-dimensional (3-D) magnetic circuits and highly saturated operation of hybrid stepper motors, traditional analytical models often fail to accurately predict torque and assist in structural design. Therefore, this study develops a saturation-aware electromagnetic analysis to clarify the effects of tooth alignment, local saturation, flux linkage, and back electromotive force (back EMF) on torque density. By analyzing the relationship among rotor–stator tooth alignment, magnetic saturation, flux linkage, and back EMF through decomposition of the rotor into multiple sub-regions over one electrical cycle, the study finds that torque improves when rotor and stator teeth approach magnetic saturation in both fully aligned and half-pitch misaligned states. Conversely, saturation in the fully misaligned state is minimized to reduce negative torque. To improve torque, key structural parameters such as rotor tooth-width ratio, split ratio, and multi-stack rotor configuration are systematically refined via 3-D finite element analysis. Results show that the proposed topology achieves a 3.5% increase in torque density under saturation conditions and extends the torque–speed range, positioning its performance between conventional stepper and servo motors while conserving magnet volume. An existing benchmark motor is utilized, and experimental tests are performed to validate the analysis.

title: Compact 3-D Printed Hybrid-Integrated Waveguide Bandpass Filter Based on Multiple-Mode Resonator
authors: Zhihong Xu, Zeng-Pei Zhong, Tao Yuan
publish: IEEE Transactions on Microwave Theory and Techniques
abstract: This article presents a novel hybrid-integrated waveguide bandpass filter based on multiple-mode resonator (MMR). The filter employs a rectangular waveguide (RWG) cavity, in which four independent side-wall septum metal structures introduce a folded waveguide structure to realize an MMR without altering the RWG cavity dimensions. Mode coupling is achieved via coupled inductive/capacitive metal posts, and a cascaded triplet (CT) topology is adopted to generate rich filtering responses. The proposed design enables a fifth-order response with two controllable transmission zeros (TZs), which can be positioned above/below the passband and on both sides of the passband. The cavity is fabricated using PolyJet 3-D printing and electroplating, while a PCB serves as the top cover and provides L-shaped probes for energy coupling, forming a hybrid-integrated structure. Measured results show excellent agreement with full-wave simulations, validating the design feasibility and superior performance. The filter achieves a center frequency of 10.037 GHz, return loss greater than 19 dB, and insertion loss below 1.05 dB, with the first spurious passband separated from the main band by a ratio greater than <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$2.0f_{0}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>.

title: Introducing IEEE Collabratec
authors: N/A

title: Similarity-Based Control: Bettering Operation of Iterative Learning Under Noisy I/O Data
authors: Chenchao Wang, Deyuan Meng
abstract: This paper targets at establishing a similarity-based control framework between heterogeneous systems to better the operation of iterative learning control (ILC) by proposing a data-based design approach in the presence of noisy input-output (I/O) data. Owing to the absence of model information, appropriate I/O tests are designed to guarantee the data sufficiency, based on which the admissible behavior of the controlled system can be estimated. Moreover, the I/O data-based similarity and similarity indexes are presented to measure how close the admissible behaviors of heterogeneous systems are. Thanks to the similarity indexes and by employing an experience projection mechanism, a similarity-based control approach enabling the controlled system to learn from a heterogeneous ILC system is developed using only the I/O data. It is shown that the controlled system can accomplish the tracking tasks by learning from the successful control experience from a similar heterogeneous ILC system, thereby eliminating the necessity of trial-and-error processes. A rigorous performance analysis of the resulting learning error is performed. Simulations are also provided to verify the effectiveness of the similarity-based control.

title: Guest Editorial Special Issue From the ICRA 2024 Workshop on Field Robotics
authors: François Pomerleau, Philippe Giguère, Tim Barfoot

title: Online Edge Caching for 360-Degree Videos
authors: Zhenghao Sha, Zhongyuan Liu, Kechao Cai, Jinbei Zhang
abstract: 360-degree videos have gained considerable popularity by offering immersive experiences to viewers. However, they consume a significant amount of bandwidth and demand specialized caching schemes at the edge network. Previous caching schemes have certain limitations due to either complete historical data or neglect the viewer-varying characteristics in 360-degree videos. In this paper, we address these limitations by proposing online caching schemes for two scenarios: (1) single-video and (2) multi-video. In the single-video scenario, we propose the online Single-Video caching scheme (SV-caching scheme). The SV-caching scheme predicts tile popularity using the PopPred algorithm, and optimizes caching decisions to enhance viewers’ Quality of Experience (QoE) using the CacheOpt algorithm. In the multi-video scenario, we propose the online Multi-Video caching scheme (MV-caching scheme). The MV-caching scheme dynamically allocates cache space for each video using the MVCacheAlloc algorithm according to the video popularity, and optimizes caching decisions using the MVCacheOpt algorithm. We prove that all algorithms achieve sublinear regret, i.e., O(√K), where K is the number of viewers. This guarantees that our schemes’ performance approaches the optimal as more viewers are served. Experiments on real-world data demonstrate that our caching schemes outperform existing algorithms in regret, QoE, and hit ratio for both scenarios.

title: Edge Microservice Deployment Based on Genetic Algorithm and Hierarchical Fault Tolerance
authors: Min Yuan, Jiaxin Mu, Hongbing Wang
abstract: Efficient and stable microservice deployment in edge environments remains challenging due to stringent resource constraints and high node failure rates. Existing works typically address isolated optimization factors, while this singular focus results in suboptimal deployment performance. To overcome these limitations, we propose a new deployment approach combining genetic algorithm (GA) with a hierarchical fault-tolerance mechanism. Our approach first formulates microservice deployment problem as a hierarchical optimization problem, sequentially optimizing response time, cost, deployment success rate under constraints of budget and load. Next, we design a genetic algorithm with an elite chromosome generation strategy to reduce search complexity, incorporating a fault-aware mechanism to avoid unreliable nodes. Furthermore, we introduce a three-layer fault-tolerance mechanism based on cloud-edge-end architecture that performs fault prevention at the cloud layer, real-time remediation at the edge layer, and quality evaluation at the end layer. Extensive experiments on real-world data demonstrate that our approach outperforms baseline methods, improving Quality of Service (QoS) by 13.2% and increasing the deployment success rate (DSR) by 22.9%.
