title: Deepfake Detection via Exploring Degradation Inconsistency_supp1-3695433.pdf
authors: Weiming Bai
abstract: The detection of face forgery has become increasingly vital due to the severe security concerns posed by face manipulation techniques. While recent studies on forgery detection have demonstrated promising results when the training and testing samples come from the same domains, the problem remains challenging when attempting to extend the detector to unseen methods. In this work, we propose an innovative approach to enhance the generalization capability of forgery detection methods by exploring degradation inconsistency clues interspersed between the background and the manipulated face regions. Our motivation stems from the observation that digital photos undergo different degradation during acquisition and transmission, resulting in backgrounds and faces from different sources containing distinct degradation patterns in the forged faces. The proposed framework, termed the Degradation Consistency Learning Framework, integrates two core components: a data generation network that modulates degradation transformations to obtain tampered facial images, and a detection network that mines degradation inconsistency clues from both spatial and frequency domains. These two components are tightly coupled through adversarial training, forming a dynamic architecture akin to a Generative Adversarial Network (GAN). Experimental results on different benchmark and evaluation protocols (i.e., indataset and cross-dataset) have demonstrated the effectiveness of our method.

title: Vicarious Calibration for MISR’s 36 Channels Using In-Situ Measurements at Railroad Valley Playa
authors: Graziela R. Keller, Carol J. Bruegge, Marcin L. Witek
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: The Multi-angle Imaging SpectroRadiometer (MISR), launched in 1999 onboard NASA’s Terra satellite, employs nine pushbroom cameras fixed at different angles and observing in four spectral bands. MISR’s scientific objectives put strict constraints on absolute and camera-to-camera calibration (3% and 1%, respectively). MISR’s absolute radiometry was established early in the mission, through the vicarious calibration of the nadir camera, using in-situ measurements of desert sites. Off-nadir camera calibrations were subsequently determined using lunar and Earth-view studies. In this work, we perform vicarious calibration of all 36 MISR’s channels using reflectance measurements of the Railroad Valley (RRV) Playa taken between 2012 and 2022, during campaigns led by NASA’s Jet Propulsion Laboratory. In total, 78 in-situ measurements collocated with 22 MISR overpasses of the Playa were used. For 34 of MISR’s 36 channels, the results confirmed MISR’s absolute scaling within the 5% uncertainty of this type of analysis. This verifies the usefulness of this technique for off-nadir cameras, though future work will explore further refinements. In particular, the nadir camera scaling deviates from our results by 0.2%, -0.1%, -2.5%, and -1.4% for the Blue, Green, Red, and Near-infrared bands, respectively. In the Blue band, for the 60.0 and 70.5 degrees forward-viewing cameras, the results diverged the most from MISR’s absolute scaling (7% and 11%, respectively), possibly due to unresolved multiple-scattering effects and uncertainties in surface bidirectional reflectance factors. Our results for the nadir camera are used in the latest reprocessing of the MISR Level 1B2 Georectified Radiance Product, designated Version 2025.

title: Fractional-Order Sliding Mode Control of Active Power Filter Using Petri Probabilistic Fuzzy Neural Network
authors: Limin Yang, Juntao Fei
abstract: This paper proposes a fractional-order sliding mode control method based on Petri probabilistic fuzzy recurrent neural network (PPFRNN) to address the issues of unknown nonlinear dynamic characteristics and external disturbances in an active power filter (APF), which is used for current control and harmonic suppression. By integrating the characteristics of Fuzzy Neural Network (FNN), Probabilistic Neural Network (PNN), Recurrent Neural Network (RNN), and Petri Net (PN), the proposed PPFRNN achieves superior generalization and approximation capabilities. The combination of PN and PNN enhances the network’s adaptability and noise immunity, while an error compensator is introduced to offset the minimum approximation error, thereby further improving its approximation performance without assuming the minimum approximation error is bounded. Moreover, the introduction of fractional-order differential operator enhances the flexibility in selecting the sliding surface and reduces chattering. The effectiveness and feasibility of the proposed control algorithm are validated through simulations and hardware experiments of APF. Experimental results show that the proposed control method can effectively suppress the harmonic pollution of the power grid, with strong robustness and small tra cking error.

title: Self-Triggering Resilient Control of Cyber-Physical Systems Against System Integrity Attacks
authors: Bo Tu, Zhihua Qu, Marwan A. Simaan
publish: IEEE Transactions on Control of Network Systems
abstract: Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS) and their net worked controls are vulnerable to system integrity attacks. Thesetypes of attacks can besevere andstealthy in nature, as the attacker may hijack both input and output channels of the system and synthesize attack signals to completely blind its control center. To prevent such attacks, resilient network control structures have been designed by incorpo rating a chaotic signal and dynamically encoding/decoding the control signal. Although this approach is very effective, the current version of the control structure requires continuous synchronization between the master and slave chaotic circuits. To relax this restriction and make the synchronization robust to intermittency and delay in communication, a new self-triggering resilient control structure is proposed in this paper. This new structure contains three novel components. First, a digital twin of the slave chaotic circuit is introduced on the side ofthemasterchaoticcircuit to develop time-dependent and state-dependent synchronization criteria. Second, the self-triggering mechanism is designed to incorporate the two criteria into the resilient control structure so that the synchronization is renewed only when necessary. Third, the communication delay in the synchronization scheme is detected and compensated for in real-time by using Einstein's formula. The robustness of this new control structure is analyzed using the Lya punov direct method. In addition to analytical analysis, the proposed self-triggering resilient control system and the application of the digital twin of the slave chaotic circuit are shown to be effective by employing an example of a fourth order control system and Chua's chaotic circuits.

title: Disturbance Observer Based Decentralized Controller for Disturbance Rejection and Cross-Regulation Mitigation in Single-Inductor Multi-Output Buck Converters
authors: Mohammed Waris Senan, Md. Nishat Anwar, Mantu Kumar Ram
abstract: In this work, a controller augmented with an inverse plant model-based disturbance observer (DOB) is proposed for the control of single-inductor multi-output buck converters (SIMOBCs). The suggested control scheme effectively handles the plant modelling mismatch as well as internal uncertainties in addition to the cross-regulation issues. The controller has been designed using direct synthesis approach in frequency domain and its parameters have been optimized using Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO). The suggested method is generic and can be applied to SIMOBCs of any size & order. This is validated experimentally for SIMOBCs of two as well as three outputs in different test conditions. Superior performance has been observed in terms of overshoot, settling time for disturbance rejection and cross-regulation suppression when compared with other reported control schemes.

title: 2 Parallel Heart: Parallel Experiments of a Highly Accurate Dual Independent Neural Network for Predicting Myocardial Infarction in Service of Clinical Practice
authors: Zijun Zhang, Zhewei Chen, Xingyu Zhou, Siyang Zhong, Xinbo Qu, Siming Li, Yutong Xin, Huijiao Deng, Yirong Zhang, Lei Wang, Lin Yang
abstract: Objective: To enhance acute myocardial infarction (AMI) prediction accuracy and interpretability by developing a dual-model parallel framework integrating electrocardiography (ECG) signals and blood biochemical markers. Methods: A convolutional neural network (CNN) extracted ECG features, while a backpropagation (BP) network analyzed 13 biochemical markers (troponin I/T, CK-MB, NT-proBNP, glucose, and the standard chemistry panel). Models were trained and validated on the PTB-XL ECG dataset (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$n=21{,}799$</tex-math></inline-formula>), the MIMIC-III biochemical database (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$n=4{,}760$</tex-math></inline-formula>), and a Chinese ECG dataset (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$n=503$</tex-math></inline-formula>) for regional calibration. Results: The CNN-based ECG model achieved an AUC of 0.933, with P–R interval and QRS duration as significant predictors. The BP-based biochemical model achieved an AUC of 0.960. The integrated parallel framework achieved a total accuracy of 96.6%, significantly reducing missed diagnoses for non-ST-segment elevation myocardial infarction (NSTEMI) patients with inconspicuous ECG changes. Conclusion: This dual-model framework provides an efficient automated screening tool for AMI, bridging ECG and biochemical diagnostics and highlighting the importance of ethnic-specific calibration. Significance: By recovering NSTEMI cases that ECG-only models systematically miss and by showing that a regional cohort can recalibrate a European-trained classifier, the framework extends automated AMI screening to the presentations and populations where it currently fails most often.

title: Quantifying Darkness: Developing a Dual-Channel Real-Time Light Sensor Integrated into a Blindfold for Visual Deprivation in Perception Research_supp1-3693473.pdf
authors: D. del Rosario-Gilabert

title: Efficient 3D Liver Segmentation via Dynamic Slice Windowing for Consumer Medical Video
authors: Wenjing Xiao, Yijiang Long, Chunyu Liang, Zhe Zhang, Giancarlo Fortino, Min Chen
abstract: Accurate automatic liver segmentation is a fundamental step in medical image analysis and is critical for clinical diagnosis and preoperative planning. However, automatic liver segmentation in consumer medical video remains challenging due to blurred boundaries, low contrast, sparse foreground distribution, and structural discontinuity across slices. These challenges hinder conventional 2D segmentation methods from fully capturing the liver’s global spatial structure, leading to suboptimal continuity and stability in the results. To address these issues, this paper proposes SliceNet, a novel 3D liver segmentation framework. By leveraging the robust semantic representation of a pretrained Segment Anything Model 2 encoder, SliceNet elegantly pairs large-model capabilities with a classic U-Net decoder architecture. Unlike methods that directly perform fully convolutional modeling on 3D volumes, the proposed model is structurally adapted to the characteristics of volumetric video data. It maintains a lightweight and efficient design while preserving cross-slice coherence and spatial consistency, thereby effectively capturing 3D context. Specifically, the Dynamic Slice Windowing module selectively retains contiguous informative slices through a foreground-aware gating mechanism, significantly reducing irrelevant background interference from empty slices. In addition, the Center-Neighbor Cross-Attention module introduces cross-attention interactions between the center slice and multiple neighboring slices to model spatial consistency along the axial direction, thereby enhancing the model’s contextual awareness. With these designs, the model demonstrates excellent adaptability to 3D video data and strong capability for medical image modeling. Experiments on three public datasets, SLIVER07, 3DIRCADb-1, and CHAOS, show that the proposed method achieves Dice scores of 97.91%, 96.77%, and 97.92%, respectively, significantly outperforming existing approaches and validating its effectiveness for efficient 3D liver segmentation in consumer medical video.

title: A Time-Domain Analysis of Normal-to-Antenna Mode Conversion in Coaxial Lines Using Generalized Transmission-Line theory
authors: Shuji Kitora, Souma Jinno, Hiroshi Toki, Masayuki Abe
abstract: With the increasing operating frequencies of electronic and electrical systems, undesired electromagnetic radiations have grown, posing significant challenges in meeting electromagnetic compatibility (EMC) standards. A significant source of such radiations is the antenna-mode current generated unintentionally due to system asymmetry. However, conventional transmission-line theory presents challenges when handling these antenna-mode currents and associated mode coupling phenomena. In this article, we introduce a generalized transmission-line (GTL) theory and clarify the mechanism of radiated noise caused by mode conversion. The GTL theory enabled comprehensive handling of antenna-mode currents and their mode coupling. By employing numerical methods using exact retarded potentials in the time domain, we precisely identify when and where mode coupling occurs, quantifying its contribution to electromagnetic radiation. Numerical results are validated by comparison with cylindrical-coordinate finite-difference time-domain analysis. The proposed approach provides new insights for effectively resolving EMC issues in modern high-frequency systems.

title: On Quantum Teleportations of EPR States by Three-Qubit Entanglements
authors: Ami Ukai, Syunsuke Fujiwara, Hideaki Okazaki
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Consumer Technology (ICCT-Pacific)
abstract: In quantum computing, although quantum teleportation via quantum entanglement has been explored, general implementation methods using primitive quantum circuit operations remain unclear. This paper proposes a quantum circuit for the teleportation of EPR states using GHZ-state triplets. Quantum circuits for two-qubit and three-qubit entanglement are first presented, followed by teleportation circuits. Simulation results obtained using Yao, an open-source Julia package, are then provided to validate the proposed circuits. Finally, the main results and future research directions are discussed.

title: Solar Smart Tracker: Comparative Efficiency Evaluation of an ESP32-Controlled PV System
authors: Pawandeep Kaur, Parth Tripathi, Abhinav Krishna, Rohit Kumar Singh
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: The central goal of the research is to enhance the performance of the solar panels. Household solar panels are usually mounted facing a single direction, and this reduces their output since they are cannot move with the movement of the sun during the day. In order to overcome this, a dynamic sun-tracking system has been proposed. This system is also meant to be easy to use and affordable, and one that can help increase energy production by ensuring that the panel is at all times aligned with the sun to ensure maximum exposure. The setup proposed applies the ESP32 microcontroller to control the position of the solar panel with the help of servo motors so that the sun can be tracked automatically. Its efficiency would be determined by comparing the output voltage of a standard fixed panel and that of the sun-tracking model at varying angles. Light Dependent Resistors (LDRs) are used to sense sunlight intensity in real-time, which causes the servo motors to control the panel positioning. LED bulbs are attached as a load to simulate real-life energy consumption under real-world conditions, and the voltage across the bulbs is measured to compare the static and dynamic panel performance. The project is based on a problem statement from a hackathon (Smart India Hackathon) and shows the possibility of improving the efficiency of solar panels with an intelligent tracking system.

title: Jigsaw Puzzle Mitigates Overfitting of Position Embedding in Vision Transformer
authors: Toshikazu Samura, Thanawit Gerdprasert
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Consumer Technology (ICCT-Pacific)
abstract: Vision Transformer (ViT) is known to exhibit high class prediction performance in image recognition tasks. However, realizing the full potential of ViT requires large-scale datasets. In the remote sensing field, which utilizes nonstandard image types, constructing large-scale datasets may be challenging. In such cases, it is necessary to achieve the full potential of ViTs using small-scale datasets. We observed that when a ViT is trained on a small-scale dataset, the Position Embedding (PE) tends to overfit to the dataset. To address this, we propose a ViT architecture that simultaneously solves the jigsaw puzzle task and the standard class prediction task. We demonstrate that this architecture not only mitigates PE overfitting but also improves class prediction performance. These results suggest that the proposed architecture contributes to achieving the full potential of ViTs even on small-scale datasets, thereby expanding the application scope of ViTs in the remote sensing field.

title: Phase Optimization Scheme of RIS-Assisted MC-OTFS Wireless Communication Systems
authors: Kelvin Kuang-Chi Lee, Ruofan Wu, Chiao-En Chen
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Consumer Technology (ICCT-Pacific)
abstract: Multicarrier orthogonal time frequency space (MCOTFS) modulation effectively mitigates Doppler shifts and intersymbol interference in high-mobility environments. Reconfigurable Intelligent Surface (RIS) technology further enhances coverage and improves channel quality by controlling the phase of reflective meta-elements. Integrating these two techniques offers improved signal integrity and channel adaptability. However, practical systems must account for fractional delay and Doppler shifts, which often degrade performance and increase computational complexity. This paper develops a RIS-assisted MC-OTFS framework that incorporates fractional channel parameters and applies a semidefinite relaxation (SDR) method for RIS phase design. Simulation results show that the optimized phase configuration substantially reduces the bit error rate (BER).

title: Enhancing STEM Education with Interactive Robotics: Narrative-Based Cyber-Physical Learning Approaches
authors: Chih-Chien Hu, Imam Yuadi
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Consumer Technology (ICCT-Pacific)
abstract: This study explores the integration of interactive robotics and cyber-physical systems (CPS) in STEM education, emphasizing the development of narrative-based robotic-assisted learning systems (RALS). Advances in human-robot interaction (HRI) enable adaptive and engaging learning environments that make complex STEM concepts more accessible. By embedding robotics into narrative-driven activities, the approach fosters multimodal learning, deeper engagement, and stronger skill development. Beyond classroom contexts, the model extends to professional training, enhancing staff participation and supporting scalable, high-quality development. Findings suggest that narrative-based cyber-physical learning not only enriches educational experiences but also democratizes access to advanced STEM training and professional growth.

title: Federated Learning Application to Classification of Hypertension Using Photoplethysmography
authors: Vytaras Juraska, Wei-Che Chien
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Consumer Technology (ICCT-Pacific)
abstract: Internet of Things (IoT) devices in medical applications are becoming more popular, especially with the Healthcare Industry 5.0 seeing significant development. One of the features, that is often implemented in such devices, but not fully utilized is Photoplethysmography (PPG) sensors, which detect heart rate variability and oxygen saturation. With this information, various cardiovascular diseases, such as Hypertension, could be monitored or even detected using Artificial Intelligence (AI). However, one of the concerns when training AI models is data privacy, especially when it comes to medical records. There have been various of approaches applying AI models to PPG data, however methods often rely on singular system processing all of the data, meaning one party requires access to all medical data. This paper considers Federated Learning (FL) approach, which allows for data privacy and relies on distributed learning methods. Throughout this paper an FL approach is finalized and the results compared to centralized approaches are similar, however with the added benefit of data privacy.

title: AIoT Base Smart Consumables Drying Cabinet
authors: Jun-Wei Liu, Zi-Yu Hong, Yu-Zan Pan, Wei-Hung Lu, Wen-Ray Chen
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Consumer Technology (ICCT-Pacific)
abstract: In this laboratory, seven 3D printers are operated concurrently, requiring frequent access to multiple filament spools. The ambient humidity typically remains around 70%, posing significant challenges to material preservation even under continuous air conditioning. Elevated humidity accelerates filament moisture absorption and degradation, leading to print defects, increased failure rates, and workflow disruptions. Commercial filament dryers available on the market generally accommodate only one or two spools at a time. Scaling up to meet multi-printer demands necessitates deploying several units simultaneously, which substantially raises energy consumption, operational complexity, and maintenance burden. Moreover, most conventional dryers employ fixed-temperature control without real-time humidity feedback or adaptive adjustment mechanisms, resulting in inefficient energy utilization and inconsistent drying outcomes, including over-drying or under-drying of materials. In addition, the majority of these dryers lack remote monitoring, data logging, and alert functionalities, restricting users to local interfaces with rudimentary status displays. This limitation hampers the ability to monitor drying progress, respond rapidly to anomalies, and integrate filament conditioning into broader smart laboratory automation systems.

title: Land Appraisal Support System Using Vision Transformer and Local Large Language Model
authors: Yunhao Tu, Aoi Shoji, Sota Sasaki, Mayu Urata, Mamoru Endo, Hirokazu Shimazaki, Yuto Nakamura, Tomoyuki Kimura
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Consumer Technology (ICCT-Pacific)
abstract: In the assessment of fixed asset tax, accurately identifying land use types and building conditions is essential; however, the declining number of municipal staff and growing reliance on individual expert knowledge have made traditional field-based surveys increasingly difficult to conduct efficiently. To address these challenges, this study proposes a land appraisal support system that leverages aerial photographs and parcel boundary data owned by local governments, developed through industry-government-academia collaboration. Specifically, a transformer-based deep learning model is used to classify land use from aerial imagery. Furthermore, when the confidence score of the classifier falls below a predefined threshold, a locally deployed large language model (LLM) automatically generates supplemental explanatory text describing potential decision factors and possible misclassification patterns. Experiments using aerial imagery from Handa City, Aichi Prefecture, demonstrate that the LLM is capable of suggesting plausible alternative land use categories in misclassified cases. These findings indicate that the proposed system can support both the efficiency of field surveys and the accuracy of tax assessments.

authors: Mohamed Dwedar, Alexander Jesser
publish: 2025 35th International Conference on Computer Theory and Applications (ICCTA)
abstract: In the era of intelligent automation, autonomous decision-making and trajectory optimization in Multi-Robot Systems (MRS) remain pivotal for mission success, particularly in unstructured and dynamic environments. This paper presents a novel hybrid path planning architecture that integrates classical A* algorithms with a trainable neural heuristic, forming a differentiable A* Neural Network (A* NN). Designed for IoTconnected MRS, the system enables decentralized task execution, where object poses, human positions, and obstacle data are shared across a hierarchical communication layer utilizing MQTT broker based messaging. This structure supports scalable data exchange, coordination, and real-time updates between heterogeneous agents, including drones and humanoid robots. The proposed framework leverages sensor fusion from different sensors, visual cameras, and onboard sensors to construct a unified environmental map for navigation and obstacle avoidance. A decentralized data structure is introduced to manage robot states, task conditions, battery levels, and anomaly signals. These parameters drive a learned guidance function that generates heuristic maps dynamically, allowing the <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{A}^{*} \text{NN}$</tex> to prioritize efficient, collision-free paths. The network is trained through end-to-end gradient descent to continuously refine decision quality based on real-world feedback. By combining classical path planning with deep learning and decentralized IoT-based communication, the system delivers robust, energy aware, and adaptive navigation performance for mission-critical operations. This work demonstrates a scalable and intelligent solution for real-time decision-making in IoTconnected robotic fleets.

title: High speed and low Power MAC unit using enable controlled approximate Multiplier
authors: Hassan M. Raza, Y. Varthamanan
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: The demand for energy-efficiency in computing field had triggered the use of approximation in applications that are error pliable and can tolerate with calculated amount of error. Arithmetic units of computing devices are crucial in deciding the power requirement of the device. Eventually introducing approximation in arithmetic unit can dictate the energy efficiency requirement of the application. The paper presents a multiply and accumulate (MAC) unit populated with approximate array multiplier. The deployment of approximation in the multiplier leads to energy efficient design with tolerable error limits. The proposed MAC unit is design using Verilog and implemented on FPGA device. Preliminary result shows that the proposed MAC unit with approximate multiplier achieves the least delay of 14.29 ns with a tolerable power consumption of 22.51 W compare to existing designs. Thus makes the design more appropriate for energy constrained DSP applications

title: Ensemble Learning based Intrusion Detection System for IoT Networks
authors: Abhishek Kumar Singh, Ritu Garg
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: Day by day, the use of Internet of Things (IoT) devices is increasing in daily life, powering various types of practical services. In an IoT network, many heterogeneous devices are connected over the internet, which makes IoT devices more vulnerable to different kinds of cyber-attacks, with DoS/DDoS attacks being a major concern. Therefore, it is essential to keep these networks secure from such threats. To protect these networks, an Intrusion Detection System can play an important role. In the proposed method, a novel NGBoost-based IDS is used to detect DoS/DDoS attacks, which separates traffic in multiple stages. This two-stage model uses an NGBoost-based binary classifier at both stages. At the first stage, attacked traffic is separated from benign traffic, and the traffic that is identified as attacked at the first stage is later separated as DoS/DDoS and others. Training of the model is carried out on the CICIoT2023 dataset. The model achieves over 98% accuracy at the first stage and more than 99% at the second stage.

title: SwachhaSetu: AI-based Smart Waste Collection and Civic Monitoring
authors: Vedant Nandkishor Mali, Dinesh Vinod Gavai, Atharva Vinod Abruk, Smit Sanjay Kukade, Roshan Rajendrakumar Karwa
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: Urban waste management still faces major challenges such as irregular collection schedules, lack of public transparency, slow complaint resolution, limited administrative control, and poor waste segregation. Fixed-route garbage collection leads to overflowing bins in busy areas and unnecessary fuel usage in others. Citizens often have no information about bin locations or vehicle timings, making participation difficult. Complaint systems remain slow and untraceable, while administrators struggle to monitor vehicles, bins, and field workers effectively. Moreover, confusion about waste segregation reduces recycling efficiency, and the absence of proper data prevents planning and timely decisions. SwachhaSetu: AI-based Smart Waste Collection and Civic Monitoring addresses these issues through a set of connected and easy-to-use digital tools. Smart bins with sensors send live fill-level data to create flexible collection routes based on actual needs. A mobile application shows live bin and vehicle locations, and allows quick, geotagged complaint submissions. An AI-powered waste scanner helps users identify and segregate waste correctly. A web-based control panel gives administrators real-time visibility and control over all operations, supported by predictive analytics for route optimization and trend identification. Together, these solutions make waste management more efficient, transparent, and responsive for both citizens and authorities.

title: Attention-Guided Explainable Deep Learning Framework for Diabetic Retinopathy Grading
authors: Samia Akhtar, Shabib Aftab, Munir Ahmad, Usama Ahmed
publish: 2026 International Conference on Data Science, Machine Learning, and Intelligence (DataSciMI)
abstract: Diabetic Retinopathy (DR) is ranked among the top causes of poor vision and blindness. Advanced stages of this disease are hard to identify because of minor changes present in the retinal features. Existing deep learning methods often have lower accuracy when they are used for detecting these severe cases. Moreover, the black-box nature of these methods reduces trustworthiness in clinical settings. All these challenges call for automated DR grading systems which can provide accuracy as well as interpretability. In this research we designed a deep learning system. A custom Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) called DR-CBANet is developed which incorporates Convolutional Block Attention Module (CBAM) and Explainable AI (XAI) techniques. The goal is to emphasize on lesion relevant regions and improve classification across severity levels. We combined IDRiD and the APTOS 2019 datasets to enhance generalization. After fusion, the images were preprocessed to enhance their quality and then augmentation was used to tackle class imbalance. The CBAM block refined both spatial and channel features. As a result, the model could detect faint pathological patterns more accurately. For explainability, Gradient-weighted Class Activation Mapping (Grad-CAM) was utilized to highlight the areas which had the highest impact on the model’s decision making. It also highlighted lesions like exudates, hemorrhages and microaneurysms. The experiments show that our attention-guided CNN achieved 97.5% accuracy in multi-class grading and outperformed previous deep learning methods on comparison. The framework demonstrates good predictive performance which supports its deployment in clinical diagnostic workflows.

title: Med-Convo: A LoRA-Optimized LLaMA-2 Framework for Efficient Medical Conversations
authors: Muhammad Omar Sajid, Areesha Ramzan, Muhammad Umar Khan, Alishba Mahnoor, Rana Fayyaz Ahmad, Faizan Hamayat
publish: 2026 International Conference on Data Science, Machine Learning, and Intelligence (DataSciMI)
abstract: Large language models are increasingly used in industrial applications. Models such as GPT and LLaMA provide strong general conversational abilities. However, they lack reliability in medical question answering due to limited domain specialization. To address this issue, we propose Med-Convo, a medical chatbot fine-tuned on LLaMA-2-7B for healthcare conversations. The model is first trained on the Alpaca dataset to learn general dialogue behavior. It is then further fine-tuned on 100,000 patient–doctor conversation records. Data preprocessing is applied to improve quality, remove private information, and correct grammatical errors. Low-Rank Adaptation is used to reduce computational cost during fine-tuning. Med-Convo is deployed using Flutter and FastAPI to support real-world interaction. Experimental results show that Med-Convo achieves a precision of 82%, outperforming GPT-2 and Bio-GPT. These results demonstrate that domain-specific fine-tuning significantly improves the reliability of medical conversational systems.

title: RMS-Based Feature Extraction Using RBFNN for Fault Detection and Classification
authors: Syamprakash V L, Sarang Harihara Kumar, Suraj S Menon, Angel T S, Sreelekshmi R S
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: Effective fault diagnosis and classification are necessary to guarantee the safe and stable operation of modern power transmission networks. This paper presents an RMS-based feature extraction method combined with a Radial Basis Function Neural Network (RBFNN) for dependable fault detection and classification. RMS values are calculated to capture the fault characteristics after voltage and current signals are divided into fixed windows. An RBFNN is given the extracted features to classify LG, LL, LLG, LLL faults, and normal conditions. The suggested method is appropriate for real-time protective relay applications due to its high classification accuracy, resilience to noise, and low computational complexity.

title: EPISPY: Integrating User-Zone Analytics and Officer Proximity Surveillance for Smart Pandemic Response
authors: Sakinah Ali Pitchay, Hazeeqah Amny Kamarul Aryffin, Murtadha Arif Bin Sahbudin, Azni Haslizan Ab Halim
publish: 2026 International Conference on Data Science, Machine Learning, and Intelligence (DataSciMI)
abstract: The present paper proposes a new smart response framework to the pandemic which integrates user-zone analytics and officer proximity surveillance to enhance quarantine compliance and enforcement. The four synchronized algorithms that power the system include: (1) computation of Euclidean distance between the users and the assigned quarantine location, (2) multi-layer geofencing and temporal monitoring to classify safe, medium-, and high-risk violation, (3) officer-to-user distance estimation to apply proactive intervention, and (4) automated officer surveillance activation triggered by a high-risk violation or time violation. The key innovation is to organize automated alerts, geospatial intelligence, and human enforcement to the 1 km surveillance space, which enables making real-time decisions and minimizes delayed responses. The proposed hybrid human-machine contact-tracing approach will be tapped through the incorporation of three concepts: enforceability, accountability, and contextual intelligence on the monitoring of the public health. The scalable architecture itself has been fairly adapted to the deployment of smart cities, and, by extension, facilitates adaptive governance, which goes a long way in increasing preventive strategics in the event of an outbreak during future health crises, which is now the order of the day in this age of pandemics.

title: Research on a Novel Group Index Direct Sequence Spread Spectrum System in Long-time-delay Multipath Channels for UWA Communication
authors: Xiaoyu Yang, Yuehai Zhou, Junhui Yao, Feng Tong, Pengyu Du

title: Mainlobe Deceptive Jamming Suppression with FDNA-MIMO Radar
authors: Zhengxi Wang, Ximin Li, Shengqi Zhu, Shixing Yang, Congfeng Liu, Guisheng Liao
abstract: This work prioritizes the suppression of mainlobe deceptive jamming within coarray Frequency Diverse Nested Array (FDNA) Multiple-Input Multiple-Output (MIMO) radar architectures. To overcome the range resolution limitation in the conventional FDA, a novel FDNA structure is proposed. Leveraging differential processing for virtual aperture extension, the design enables precise discrimination of mainlobe deceptive jamming and target. Subsequently, a Spatial Smoothing-based Minimum Variance Distortionless Response (SS-MVDR) beamformer is introduced to eliminate the contamination of training samples by target data during jamming suppression. Furthermore, a frequency offset selection strategy is developed to simultaneously suppress both rapid and delayed repeated jamming. The efficacy of the proposed scheme in suppressing mainlobe deceptive jamming is confirmed by simulation results.

title: Causality-Aware Pinning Control for Fixed-Time and Predefined-Time Synchronization of Multi-Link Directed Hypergraphs
authors: Manman Yuan, Pengkai Wang, Jiapei Li, Haobin Cao, Huijia Li, Huanhuan Chen
publish: IEEE Transactions on Control of Network Systems
abstract: Pinning control provides a practical and cost-saving means to control hypergraph networks. However, most existing studies on hypergraph networks are restricted to static topologies and often overlook dynamic causal interactions, limiting the effective selection of pinned nodes in practical scenarios. Motivated by these, this paper proposes a novel multi-link directed hypergraph (MDH) and a causality-aware pinning control (CAPC) scheme to ensure fixed/predefined-time synchronization. Specifically, a MDH with time-varying higher-order structures is constructed to characterize dynamic, multi-weight, and directed interactions among nodes. Moreover, to adapt to the time-varying and higher-order properties of the MDH, CAPC is introduced to dynamically identify energy-efficient pinned nodes based on causal relationships in higher-order networks. Finally, based on the data-driven selected pinned nodes, sufficient conditions for the design of adaptive fixed/predefined-time pinning controllers are rigorously derived. Numerical simulations and a secure communication application further validate the effectiveness of the proposed approach in resource-constrained environments.

title: Machine Learning-Enabled Soft Finger Grasping With a Kinesthetic Sense of Touch_supp1-3695331.mp4
authors: Stephanie Herrera
abstract: This paper presents a contact-sensitive fingertip pinch grasping method for a pneumatic soft gripper while removing the need for tactile sensors at contact points, which are points of wear and tear for the sensors. We propose a reference tracking feedback control for bending-sensitive measurements and use machine learning to detect contacts in the process of grasping. Machine learning is trained on signals from the feedback control loop, including the reference, the reference tracking error and controller output signals. We evaluate this method with 140 grasping experiments on a physical system using 7 objects of various sizes and positions, and compare it with a baseline strategy for soft gripper grasping. The performance of our grasping approach leads to a 97.9% grasping success rate with the robotic arm being able to lift objects vertically without losing contact with them.

authors: N/A

title: Degradation Mechanisms Due to Body Diode Operation in Planar SiC MOSFETs
authors: Yangtao Long, Yuan Chen, Pengkai Wang, Bo Hou, Hu He
abstract: In power converter applications, the body diode of SiC MOSFETs is commonly employed as a freewheeling diode to reduce cost and save space. However, this practice can induce bipolar degradation in the devices. This study demonstrates that body diode operation, in addition to inducing bipolar degradation, also results in an increase in gate oxide interface state density and a significant rise in switching losses. Furthermore, this study investigates the characteristics of bipolar degradation under various test conditions, including current density, junction temperature, and gate bias stress. Notably, the influence of junction temperature on bipolar degradation is nonmonotonic: degradation accelerates with temperature up to a critical junction temperature (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mathrm{T}_{\text {SSF}}\text {)}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>, beyond which further temperature increase suppresses the degradation rate. Based on the concept of the “quasi-Fermi level,” the driving mechanisms of bipolar degradation are qualitatively analyzed. The outcomes of this research provide valuable theoretical guidance and practical insights for the reliability-oriented design of power conversion circuits.

title: CIPR: Class Imbalance-Aware Pseudo-Label Regeneration for Single-Cell Multi-Omics Clustering
authors: Zixiao Jin, Xiao Zheng, Weiqing Yan, Chang Tang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Knowledge and Data Engineering
abstract: Single-cell multi-omics clustering has emerged as a powerful paradigm for dissecting cellular heterogeneity and advancing precision medicine. Despite recent progress, most existing methods prioritize the strict alignment of heterogeneous modalities. However, this alignment process is often dominated by majority cell populations, which inadvertently suppresses the representation of rare cell types and exacerbates the long-tail effect inherent in biological data. This imbalance distorts the latent feature space, resulting in suboptimal clustering performance. To address this, we propose CIPR, a Class Imbalance-aware Pseudo-label Regeneration framework. First, CIPR constructs a unified embedding by integrating cellular attributes with local and global structural relationships. Crucially, we formulate the multi-omics pseudo-labeling process as an imbalanced Optimal Transport problem. Instead of enforcing rigid equal-sized clusters that distort true biological proportions, we introduce a soft uniform prior to guarantee a baseline representation for all cell groups. This explicitly prevents rare cell populations from being absorbed by dominant cell types, while allowing the model to naturally adapt to the inherent imbalance of single-cell data. Furthermore, CIPR incorporates a curriculum learning strategy that incrementally learns from high-confidence samples, thereby reducing noise propagation during pseudo-label generation. Finally, a cross-omics interaction mechanism mutually guides prediction distributions, minimizing inter-modality conflicts and maximizing information complementarity. Extensive experiments on various multi-omics datasets demonstrate that CIPR significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods, particularly in identifying rare cell populations and handling severe class imbalances.

title: Consensus Adjustment Mechanism Based on Semisupervised Clustering and Cross Allocation for Large-Scale Social Network Group Decision Making
authors: Zhongming Wu, Zhengyang Huang, Yanjing Guo, Feilan Liu, Neng Wan
publish: IEEE Transactions on Computational Social Systems
abstract: Consensus adjustment is essential in group decision-making (GDM) to reduce the judgment bias of decision-makers (DMs). Advances in communication technology enable greater online participation, foster complex social networks, and provide more diverse evaluation information. However, few existing studies on large-scale social network-based GDM explored consensus adjustment from the perspective of DM interactions. To address this gap, this article proposes a novel large-scale social network GDM method that incorporates consensus adjustment considering the interactions among DMs and subgroups. First, a two-stage semisupervised fuzzy C-means clustering method based on information fusion is introduced to handle the complexity of social networks. Then, to deal with the issue of inconsistent minimum adjustment amounts between individuals, subgroups, and the overall group, a two-stage cross-allocation approach is proposed, which considers the favorable allocation preferences of all DMs under the overall minimum adjustments. Finally, the effectiveness of the proposed method is verified through a case study that demonstrates its practical applicability and performance in achieving consensus in complex social network environments.

title: Application of a Quantum-Inspired Classical Hybrid Model in Bearing Fault Diagnosis
authors: Yangkai Ma, Kai Wang, Mingshun Yang, Kefan Luo, Fan Dong, Menghui Han, Yibo Ma, Xincheng Wang, Xueyang Wang
abstract: As a core component of rotating machinery, bearing failures can lead to equipment downtime and even safety incidents. However, existing diagnostic methods still face bottlenecks of low accuracy and poor generalization capability when dealing with early-stage weak faults and under varying operating conditions. This paper proposes a quantum-inspired-classical hybrid deep learning model, featuring an improved Quantum-Inspired Attention Module (QIAM). This module simulates quantum superposition and entanglement through classical networks, thereby enhancing the discriminability of weak fault features. The model first employs Continuous Wavelet Transform to convert vibration signals into time-frequency representations. Subsequently, a hybrid architecture combining CNN and QIAM is utilized for global feature learning. Experimental validation on bearing datasets from Case Western Reserve University (CWRU) and Paderborn University (PU) demonstrates that the proposed method achieves classification accuracies of 98.5% and 99.12%, respectively. It not only surpasses mainstream CNN and Transformer models but also exhibits higher parameter efficiency and superior global classification performance when compared with object detection models like YOLOv9-v11. This study provides a quantum-inspired technical pathway for fault diagnosis and offers an algorithmic foundation for future implementation on quantum hardware.

title: Model-Free Predictive Repetitive Control of Dual Three-Phase PMSMs for Current Harmonics Suppression
authors: Guanghui Yang, Yong Shen, Hongwei Zhang, Xiaojun Zhang, Qiang Chen
publish: IEEE Journal of Emerging and Selected Topics in Power Electronics
abstract: This paper proposes a model-free predictive repetitive control (MFPRC) strategy for dual three-phase permanent magnet synchronous motors (DTP-PMSMs) drive systems, which employs a dual-subspace regulation mechanism to achieve harmonic suppression. By introducing decoupled virtual vectors (DVVs) and the independent disturbance observer, the proposed method realizes explicit closed-loop current control in both the α–β fundamental subspace and the x–y harmonic subspace, thereby overcoming the indirect regulation mechanism inherent in existing model-free predictive control (MFPC) schemes. Furthermore, a repetitive controller-based extended state observer (RCESO) is incorporated in the harmonic subspace to suppress multiple low-order harmonics without complex parallel resonant structures. Meanwhile, a theoretical analysis of system stability characteristics and parameter selection is then provided to clarify the disturbance rejection capability. Finally, experimental results under various operating conditions validate the effectiveness of the proposed MFPRC strategy.

title: Broadband and High-Sensitivity Photodetector Based on CsAgBiBr/&#x03B3;-InSe van der Waals Heterojunction_supp1-3692466.docx
authors: Qianjin Wang
abstract: Broadband photodetection with high sensitivity remains a critical challenge for advanced optoelectronic applications. Herein, we demonstrate a van der Waals heterostructure photodetector based on Cs<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>AgBiBr<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">6</sub>/<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\gamma $</tex-math> </inline-formula>-InSe with type-II band alignment that addresses this fundamental limitation. It confirms that the formation of a type-II band alignment at the heterointerface enables efficient interfacial charge transfer, inducing highly efficient interlayer electron transitions, thereby extending the spectral response range of the device from 390 to 1300 nm. The optimized device exhibits exceptional performance metrics at 520nm illumination, including a responsivity of 36.12 A/W, an external quantum efficiency (EQE) of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$8.6\times 10^{{3}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>%, a specific detectivity of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$1.14\times 10^{{12}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> Jones, and rapid response speeds of 0.39/0.39 ms (rise/fall time). Scanning photocurrent mapping analysis reveals efficient carrier separation, directly evidencing enhanced interfacial coupling effects. This work establishes a novel materials engineering paradigm for high-performance visible-to-near-infrared photodetection through precise manipulation of excitonic interactions in rationally designed heterostructures.
