title: IoT-Based Microalgae Cultivation Control System
authors: Mark Jermaine T. Ang, Trisha Ysabelle B. Tan, Renann G. Baldovino, Jerahmeel K. Coching, Alvin B. Culaba, Ronnie Concepcion, Andres Philip Mayol
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Consumer Technology (ICCT-Pacific)
abstract: Microalgae are a valuable renewable resource characterized by short cultivation times and high nutrient density. However, scaling production is difficult: open cultivation is cost-effective but prone to contamination, while closed systems require complex environmental controls. To solve this, this research created an automated, IoT-integrated management system for closed cultivation. Using Arduino and ESP32 microcontrollers and MATLAB Simulink and System Identification Toolbox, the cultivation system provided real-time monitoring viewable through the ThingSpeak dashboard and autonomous regulation of temperature at <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{3 5}^{\boldsymbol{\circ}} \mathbf{C}$</tex> and light exposure at 1500 lux with minimal error, ensuring optimal growth conditions without manual intervention.

title: Design and Implementation of a Four-Layer IoT Architecture for Edge–Cloud Monitoring of a Microalgae Photobioreactor
authors: Mark Jermaine T. Ang, Trisha Ysabelle B. Tan, Renann G. Baldovino, Ronnie S. Concepcion, Alvin B. Culaba, Andres Philip Mayol
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Consumer Technology (ICCT-Pacific)
abstract: This paper presents the design and implementation of a four-layer Internet of Things (IoT) architecture for real-time monitoring and basic control of a microalgae photobioreactor (PBR). The perception layer comprises a custom six-liter cultivation vessel instrumented with temperature, pH, and turbidity sensors and controlled via ESP32 microcontrollers that drive an air pump and LED lighting. In the network layer, a Raspberry Pi 3 (RPi 3) operates as a Wi-Fi access point and hosts an Eclipse Mosquitto MQTT broker to provide low-latency, local communication between sensing and actuating devices and the gateway. The service layer is implemented using Node-RED and dual InfluxDB instances: an edge InfluxDB instance on the RPi for local buffering and resilience to Wide-Area Network (WAN) outages, and an InfluxDB Cloud instance for scalable, long-term time-series storage, with a queue mechanism to synchronize buffered records when connectivity is restored. At the application layer, a Node-RED dashboard enables live visualization and on/off control of actuators on the local network, while a Grafana dashboard provides cloud-based access to historical trends and the status of actuators. Experimental operation demonstrated continuous data acquisition every 30 seconds, successful command transmission to actuators, and modest storage overhead, indicating that the proposed architecture offers a reusable and modular blueprint for smart PBR deployments.

title: Survey on Methods for Constructing Spheres of Influence
authors: Masumori Chida, Yota Kurokawa, Masaru Fukushi
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Consumer Technology (ICCT-Pacific)
abstract: The concept of Sphere of Influence (SoI) has been widely applied across a variety of fields, including area marketing, transportation planning, disaster management, and urban analysis; however, its analytical foundations remain disjointed across fields. This paper provides a comprehensive and crossfield synthesis of methods for constructing SoIs by classifying representative existing methods into five major approaches: those based on spatial interaction models, demand forecasting models, clustering, network theories, and spatial structural analyses. The primary contribution of this study lies in a comprehensive survey and systematic organization of existing SoI construction methods across multiple disciplines. Rather than proposing a new SoI model, this study aims to clarify methodological assumptions, characteristics, and limitations of representative approaches through a unified classification framework. Through comparative examination, this paper shows that the definition, computation, and spatial form of SoIs differ substantially. The comparative examination further reveals inherent trade-offs between model simplicity and empirical realism.

title: Academic Open-Source EDA Tool for CMOS Full-Custom Chip Layout and Physical Verification
authors: Yue-Fang Kuo, Chia-Hung Hsu, Wen-Shih Tseng, Ting-Wen Chen, Ming-An Chung, Mao-Ling Chiu
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Consumer Technology (ICCT-Pacific)
abstract: This paper presents a free and open-source electronic design automation (EDA) tool based on KLayout for CMOS full-custom chip layout and physical verification in large-scale classroom teaching. The framework incorporates custom mask layers, design rule check (DRC), and layout versus schematic (LVS) functions, enabling students to complete mixed-signal integrated circuit projects. A virtual 180 nm CMOS process is employed in a final project to design a 4-bit full-adder, demonstrating both software functionality and its suitability for educational use. The tool supports Windows, macOS, and Linux platforms, allowing students to readily access it in computer laboratories and on personal devices.

title: Depthwise Separable Convolutional Neural Network Accelerator Using Channel-Wise Parallel Computing
authors: Chun-Han Fan Chiang, Yen-Wei Chung, Jhe-Syuan Kao
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Consumer Technology (ICCT-Pacific)
abstract: Convolutional neural networks (CNNs) deliver high accuracy in vision tasks but their computational complexity makes deployment on resource-constrained edge devices challenging. Depthwise separable convolution (DSC), while reducing MACs, suffers from low PE utilization and increased latency when mapped onto conventional systolic array architectures. To address this, we propose a high-performance accelerator for MobileNet that adopts a weight-stationary (WS) dataflow and a ChannelWise computation strategy to enhance parallelism and data reuse in depthwise convolution (DWConv). At 400 MHz, the design achieves 4.3 TOPS/W energy efficiency and 911.2 TOPS peak throughput, significantly improving DWConv latency and hardware utilization.

title: Analysis and Evaluation of Losses and Efficiency in Two-Phase Isolated Buck-Boost Converter with Current Balancing Feature
authors: Izumi Kadowaki, Dong Xupeng, The-Tien Nguyen, Van-Dai Bui, Nguyen Gia Minh Thao
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Consumer Technology (ICCT-Pacific)
abstract: In this study, a detailed analysis and calculation of losses and efficiency of a two-phase isolated buck-boost DC-DC converter with automatic current balancing feature are presented. Furthermore, to thoroughly compare with the calculation results, numerous measurements of the efficiency of the considered buck-boost converter at various levels of input voltage and output power are performed. Moreover, a selection guide of the clamping diodes utilized in the buck stage of the converter is concisely described. Brief discussion and explanation of the findings obtained are also provided. The analysis technique of losses and selection guide of clamping diodes presented in this paper can also be applied to other kinds of multi-phase isolated buck-boost converters suitably.

title: An Efficient Vision Transformer Accelerator with Stage-Pipeline Overlap Architecture
authors: Yeong-Kang Lai, Tein-Chun Ling
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Consumer Technology (ICCT-Pacific)
abstract: The commercial success of generative artificial intelligence (GenAI) has driven an exponential surge in demand for real-time inference in Vision Transformer (ViT) applications, particularly in latency-sensitive domains such as autonomous driving, medical imaging, and computational photography. In this study, we propose a hardware accelerator synthesized using a 40 nm process. The accelerator operates at a maximum frequency of 500 MHz, delivering a computational throughput of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{5 0 2. 9\ G O P S}$</tex>. The design also demonstrates high resource efficiency, occupying only <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$8.5\ \text{mm}^{2}$</tex> and consuming 3.3 W of power in the 40 nm process. Our architecture exhibits outstanding performance and resource utilization in edge and embedded application scenarios.

title: An AI-Agent-Based Intelligent Water Quality Improvement Decision System for Aquarium Environmental Management
authors: Jyun-Hao Hsueh, Kuan-Chih Lai, Liang-Bi Chen
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Consumer Technology (ICCT-Pacific)
abstract: The rising demand for emotional companionship has led to significant growth in the ornamental fish market, transforming fishkeeping into a therapeutic practice that alleviates stress and anxiety. However, managing aquatic environments requires complex biological knowledge, often resulting in high fish mortality among busy individuals and novices due to poor parameter management. Current smart aquarium solutions lack cognitive decision support for nonexperts. To address this, the study proposes an AI-powered water-quality improvement decision system. It combines high-precision artificial intelligence of things (AIoT) sensing with a hybrid reasoning engine that fuses rule-based triggers and large language model (LLM) contextual inference. Using a multi-agent system (MAS) architecture, specialized agents collaboratively handle monitoring, diagnosis, and actions via a secure MQTT over TLS protocol. Simulations demonstrate that this system can process multimodal data to provide expert-level advice for water quality issues, effectively lowering the entry barrier for smart aquaculture.

title: SOCAI: A Proof-of-Concept for a Conversational AI-Powered Educational Social Media Platform
authors: Mohamed Ibrahim Abdelaziz, Laila Shoukry
publish: 2025 35th International Conference on Computer Theory and Applications (ICCTA)
abstract: Conventional social media platforms suffer from popularity bias that limits content diversity, while educational platforms often lack dynamic engagement. This paper introduces SOCAI, an AI-powered educational platform designed to provide a personalized yet diverse learning experience. SOCAI leverages a Large Language Model (LLM) to generate pedagogically sound content and a conversational interface to dynamically adapt a user's interest profile based on explicit feedback. This work presents the system architecture and an evaluation framework to measure the balance between personalization and content diversity. A proof-of-concept prototype was evaluated against three models from existing research representing distinct recommendation paradigms: a hybrid learning style model, a standard collaborative filtering approach, and a graph-based neural network model. The results demonstrate that SOCAI consistently achieves superior topic coverage and profile diversity compared to all three comparison models, indicating its potential to mitigate educational filter bubbles while remaining responsive to user preferences. This study contributes a practical framework for combining generative AI and conversational control, presenting preliminary evidence that such a hybrid approach can enhance user agency and content diversity in digital learning environments.

title: A Comprehensive Review of Interest Flooding Attack Detection and Mitigation Techniques in NDN based Wireless Networks
authors: Ankit Kumar
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: While Named Data Networking (NDN) operates with a data-centric approach instead of the usual host-centric approach, routers maintain Pending Interest Tables (PITs) within a stateful forwarding plane. Although this architectural shift brings with it improvements in efficiency and security, it also exposes NDN to Interest Flooding Attacks which exploit the PIT state to deteriorate network performance. Over the years, many such IFA mitigation schemes have been developed, covering areas from statistical anomaly detection, PIT-state regulation, ML models, multi-indicator anomaly detection, hybrid proactive-reactive systems, mobility-aware defenses, to multi-attack detection mechanisms. This work is considered the most comprehensive survey on IFA mitigation so far, relying on statistics, ML-based, and hybrid detection frameworks and placing their findings in the perspective of their effectiveness vis-a-vis different approaches including traditional NDN, IoT, VNDN, and FANET environments. Additionally, we propose an updated taxonomy, provide full comparative analyses, identify fundamental challenges, outline open research questions, and highlight future work possibilities. Our review shows that although existing methods provide reasonable resilience, modern adaptive collaborative IFAs still evade many existing defenses, thus necessitating the consideration of new paradigms based on advanced, context-aware, multi-layer solutions.

title: Automated Radiology X-ray Report Generation using AI
authors: Nagalakshmi N, Kavi Priya S, Thejaswini R, Vishnu Priya B
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: X-ray imaging is the most common diagnostic modality in clinical medicine; however, manual generation of radiology reports is labor-intensive, with variability, and susceptible to diagnostic errors. Automating this process by AI can increase efficiency, consistency, and support clinical decision-making. In this paper, we propose a trained framework that integrates three complementary improvements to enhance the task of X-ray report generation. First, the encoder–decoder model combines DenseNet121 as the feature extractor with a GRU-based decoder, which allows powerful image translation into text and presents high accuracy in the diagnosis reporting tasks. Second, we bring up a novel LHR-RFL strategy to encourage the model to yield low variability across both easy and difficult cases with a hybrid reward function which is a combination of BLEU, METEOR, and ROUGE-L while the harder samples will be emphasized adaptively during training, and this has brought significant improvement on benchmark datasets. It is achieved by coupling a LLaMA large language model with an enhanced ALBEF embedding network, EALBEF, augmented by organ-level segmentation and triple contrastive learning. This design strengthens image-text alignment and reduces hallucinations. All these combined bring about improvements in lexical, semantic, and radiologist-specific evaluation metrics, which showcases enhanced fluency, accuracy, and clinical reliability. This integrated approach thus lays a robust foundation for general X-ray report automation and supports the development of scalable, trustworthy, and clinically valuable computer-aided diagnosis systems.

title: An IoT-Enabled Smart Weather Monitoring and Disaster Prediction System
authors: Pankaj Kumar Keshri, Swapnil Srivastava, Nitesh Kashyap, Shobha Chaudhary, Rahul Singh, Ashish Kumar
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: Early warning systems and environmental monitoring are essential for mitigating the effects of extreme weather occurrences, especially in rural and resource-constrained areas. The design and development of an inexpensive, Internet of Things-capable weather monitoring system built on the ESP32 microcontroller is presented in this study. DHT11 for temperature and humidity, LDR for ambient light detection, MQ-135 for gas concentration monitoring, and a rain sensor for precipitation detection are just a few of the sensors that are included in the suggested system. The Blynk IoT platform continuously gathers and transmits real-time environmental data to the cloud, allowing for remote access and monitoring from any location. Based on sensor data analysis, intelligent decision rules are applied to evaluate the possibility of rainfall, heatwave chance, flood risk, storm conditions, and UV exposure levels. Along with LED-based visual alarms in low-light or dangerous situations, the system also has a 20x4 LCD that shows sensor values over several pages for better user comprehension. The proposed system enhances early-warning features and offers a cost-effective way of tracking the environmental situation through combining real-time sensing, cloud connectivity, and rule-based analytics. This will make rural communities more accepted and secure.

title: Detection of Kidney Stone from Ultrasound Images Using CNN with EfficientNetV2-B0
authors: Gouthami Velakanti, Gangula Shashi Vardhini Reddy, Samala Anjali, Komandla Sahithi
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: Kidney stone disease constitutes one of the most prevalent urological disorders, and the early detection of this disorder is very important as it can to prevent the development of complications such as chronic pain, infection, and renal obstruction. Ultrasound imaging is the first-line diagnostic tool because it is non-invasive, cost-effective, and safe; however, the interpretation of images is difficult due to noise, low contrast, and variations in anatomy. In this study, a deep learning–based framework is presented for automatic kidney stone detection using a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) with the EfficientNetV2-B0 architecture as an enhancement. The model makes use of compound scaling, lightweight parameterization, and improved feature extraction capabilities to have a more precise analysis of ultrasound images with lower computational overhead. The model training and evaluation process used an ultrasound scan dataset which contained annotated data that was obtained through a careful selection process. The results show that EfficientNetV2-B0 outperforms standard CNN benchmarks because it achieves higher accuracy, sensitivity, and F1-score results. The proposed method is expected to assist radiologists by offering a fast, and reliable decision-support tool, which will eventually lead to greater diagnostic consistency and better patient outcomes.

title: Broadband High-Performance Photodetector Using MASI Perovskite Quantum Dots in FTO/MASI PQD/Ag vertical structure
authors: Harshit Srivastava, Satyabrata Jit
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: We report the synthesis of environment-friendly MASI3 perovskite quantum dots (PQDs) using a ligand-assisted reprecipitation method and their integration into a broadband photodetector. TEM analyses confirm the formation of PQDs with an average diameter of ~2.58 nm. An MSM device with an FTO/MASI QD/Ag structure was fabricated through spin coating, enabling efficient charge transport. Under 385 nm (600 nm) irradiation, the device achieves responsivities of 2.388 A/W (2.237 A/W), respectively. High External Quantum Efficiency (EQE) of 769.1% (462.3%) were obtained at –1 V at 385 nm (600 nm). These results highlight the strong potential of tin-based, lead-free PQDs for compact, environmentally safe broadband optoelectronic systems.

title: A Proposal for Verilog Based Histogram Equalization Algorithm
authors: Rashmi Raj, Vandana Niranjan, Rishikesh Pandey
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: In this paper, a Verilog-based algorithm for histogram equalization, a critical image processing technique used to adjust brightness and contrast, is presented, by incorporating the previously established notions of histogram equalization. Verilog-based hardware implementation takes advantage of digital hardware's parallel processing capabilities to achieve significant performance improvements over traditional software-based methods, which frequently struggle with processing speed and efficiency, especially in real-time applications.

title: Knowledge-Guided LLM Compression and Distillation Using Retrieval-Augmented Learning for On-Device Deployment
authors: Parikshit Bhardwaj, Abhishek S. Shamkuwar, Surjeet Kumar, Manjeet Singh
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: Large-scale language models have demonstrated remarkable capability in reasoning, code generation, and technical knowledge processing, yet their deployment is restricted by high memory requirements, latency, and energy consumption, particularly in on-device environments. This motivates the need for efficient compression strategies that retain domain-specific knowledge while significantly reducing the size of the model. In this work, a knowledge-guided compression framework is introduced, where a compact student model is distilled from a larger teacher model under the supervision of a retrieval-augmented knowledge reservoir constructed from curated signal processing and acoustic literature. In the distillation process, useful knowledge is brought into the training step, which helps the student model retain important mathematical details and reasoning that are often lost in standard compression methods. The proposed system demonstrates a balance between model size and performance, with fewer parameters and faster inference, while still achieving strong results on domain-based tasks. This makes the approach suitable for real-time use on consumer devices, where low power usage and privacy are important for language reasoning in speech and signal processing.

title: A Quantum Leap in Cybersecurity: Novel Paradigms for Safe Digital Communication
authors: Muhammad Abdullah Qureshi, Muhammad Zulkifl Hasan, Minahil Kamran, Abdul Rehman Bin Waseem, Nadeem Sarwar
publish: 2026 International Conference on Data Science, Machine Learning, and Intelligence (DataSciMI)
abstract: The popularity of quantum computing poses a risk to the broad usage of public key cryptography since Shor algorithm can shrink both RSA and elliptic curve systems once large enough fault-tolerant quantum computers are available. This paper briefly describes the security implication on current infrastructures such as public key protocols and blockchain transactions, why the concept of harvest now, decrypt later poses instant long term confidentiality risk. We also survey post quantum alternatives such as lattice based cryptography (encompassing NTRU) and code based schemes, as well as Quantum Key Distribution (QKD) as a supplementary tool to establish high assurance keys. Deployment constraints are emphasized as including hardware overheads, complexity of migration and the QKD distance/rate constraints. The article offers a security conscious, implementation conscious analysis relating quantum dangers, migrate to post-quantum needs (crypto agility and hybrid deployment) and practical QKD suppliers that assists to incorporate near term transition planning.

title: From Bug Report to Pull Request: An Autonomous Agent Pipeline for Production Issue Resolution
authors: Roberto Milev, Uday Kanagala, Chris Cholette
publish: Proceedings of the ACM Conference on AI and Agentic Systems
abstract: None

title: SwiftFusion: Scalable Sequence Parallelism for Distributed Inference of Diffusion Transformers on GPUs
authors: Jiacheng Yang, Jun Wu, Yaoyao Ding, Zhiying Xu, Yida Wang, Gennady Pekhimenko
publish: Proceedings of the ACM Conference on AI and Agentic Systems
abstract: None

title: DRCY: Agentic Hardware Design Reviews
authors: Kyle Dumont, Nick Herbert, Hayder Tirmazi, Shrikanth Upadhayaya
publish: Proceedings of the ACM Conference on AI and Agentic Systems
abstract: None

title: Federated Aggregation Scheme with Authentication for Securing IoT-Eabled Social Internet of Vehicles: Enhancing Scalability and Loss Optimization
authors: Sivaranjani Reddi, Muralidhara Rao Patruni, Saraswathi Pedada, Ashok Kumar Das, Mpyana Mwamba Merlec, Hoh Peter In
publish: IEEE Open Journal of Vehicular Technology
abstract: In the Social Internet of Vehicles (SIoV) based on Digital Twin (DT), which incorporates the social networking concepts from the Social Internet of Things (SIoT), Federated Learning (FL) enables distributed vehicular clients to collaboratively learn machine learning models while preserving data privacy and minimizing communication overhead. However, susceptible model parameters render FL vulnerable to inference and impersonation attacks, necessitating strong and lightweight authentication protocols. This paper introduces a federated learning-based collaborative authentication protocol that incorporates anonymous mutual authentication and dynamic key agreement between vehicles and infrastructure nodes, enabling privacy-preserving data sharing while eliminating reliance on centralized entities. The protocol is evaluated using three aggregation strategies: FedAvg, FedProx, and FedOpt under varying client participation levels and epoch configurations. Experimental results demonstrate that FedOpt achieves superior convergence and generalization, reducing average loss by approximately 7.0% compared to FedAvg and 6.5% compared to FedProx, particularly in heterogeneous environments. Security analysis under the stochastic predictive machine model further validates robustness against adversarial threats. Overall, the proposed protocol enhances scalability, reduces propagation delay, and ensures efficient, privacy-preserving authentication in dynamic SIoV networks.

title: Non-Invasive Detection of Hydric Stress in Olive Trees by Microstrip Patch Antennas
authors: Juan Miguel Santos Hidalgo, Yolanda Campos-Roca
abstract: Microwave technology has been proposed as a promising technique for moisture sensing in different applications. This article explores the feasibility of using a microstrip patch antenna placed on an olive tree branch for in-situ detection of water deficiency. The proposed approach aims to overcome limitations of current technologies, such as time-domain reflectometry with invasive needle-like probes, which may damage tissue and increase the risk of long-term infection, as well as capacitive sensors, which are highly sensitive to surface build-up and environmental factors. Firstly, ex-vivo measurements were carried out on tree section samples to develop 3D electromagnetic models of olive branch sections under different hydric conditions. Next, CST Microwave Studio Suite was used to design a patch antenna and numerically simulate its performance in conjunction with the computational olive branch models. A method is developed for detecting hydric stress by extracting a characteristic feature from the antenna reflection coefficient over a range of frequencies and applying a predefined threshold. The feature represents the difference in reflection coefficient levels (dB) between two resonance peaks. The resulting value is compared against a threshold to yield a binary classification: hydric stress or adequate hydration. After antenna manufacturing, in-vivo measurements were conducted on an olive tree branch for experimental validation of the detection method. The simulations carried out for different branch sizes, and the in-vivo measurement results on the olive tree, showed the feasibility of this sensing technique for monitoring hydric stress in olive trees. The method offers notable advantages, including real-time operation, low cost and a fully non-invasive approach, with no probes inserted into the tissue.

title: GNLM: A Graph-Native Language Model with Road Name Address-Based Spatial Reasoning for Geographic Question Answering
authors: Chae-Seok Lee, Dae-seung Park, Jae-min Choi, Ho-jong Chang
abstract: Geographic question answering (QA) requires factually accurate responses grounded in structured spatial knowledge. However, existing approaches based on large language models (LLMs) suffer from factual hallucination, while retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) lacks structural reasoning capabilities. We propose GNLM (Graph-Native Language Model), a novel architecture that restructures the traditional LLM pipeline by performing all reasoning through knowledge graph traversal and restricting the role of LLMs to natural language generation. The knowledge graph is constructed exclusively from government public data sources (building registry, road name addresses) and expert-curated domain knowledge, ensuring factual reliability without LLM-generated content. GNLM introduces four key contributions: (1) a Graph-First, LLM-Last architecture with fact validation that structurally prevents fabrication (all observed errors stem from knowledge gaps, not false generation); (2) an extended Resource Description Framework (RDF) triple attribute model that represents entity properties as independent graph nodes connected by semantic edges, enabling property-level querying and meta-reasoning; (3) coordinate-free spatial reasoning that leverages the Korean road numbering standard (20m interval, k = 10m per unit) for proximity ranking without Global Positioning System (GPS) coordinates; and (4) a 14-strategy reasoning router that classifies queries into specialized graph traversal algorithms. Evaluated on a real-world dataset of Daejeon, South Korea (58,882 nodes, 80,088 edges from 58,408 buildings and 711 roads), GNLM achieves 88.4% accuracy on 250 diverse queries with a hallucination rate (HR) of only 5.7% on 35 fact-verification queries, compared to 60.0% for pure LLM, 25.7% for Dense RAG, and 17.1% for GraphRAG—demonstrating a consistent improvement as structural grounding increases. Cross-model validation with llama3.1:8b confirms LLM-agnostic generalizability. Multi-region validation on Sejong City (17,008 buildings) with identical methodology confirms cross-region generalizability (Yuseong-gu avg ρ = 0.976, Sejong avg ρ = 0.973, both over 10 roads). Road-number-based proximity ranking achieves Spearman ρ = 0.9878 and Precision@10 = 96.0% against GPS ground truth, with short-range (< 500 m) Mean Absolute Error (MAE) of only 31.9 m, demonstrating that structured address numbers alone can effectively preserve spatial ordering without any coordinate data.

title: Variable Phase Shift Control for ZVS in Two Parallel Three-level T-type NPC Converters
authors: Peng Zhang, Hao Xiao, Guoju Zhang, Wei Pei, Lei Zhang, Shiqian Ma, Zhishuang Wang, Chao Pang, Tianhao Wang
abstract: Achieving zero-voltage switching (ZVS) for parallel neutral-point-clamped (NPC) converters under high switching frequency and high power conditions is highly desirable for improving efficiency and power density, but remains challenging without auxiliary resonant circuits. In parallel converter systems, the circulating current between parallel legs provides an additional control degree of freedom that can be exploited to establish ZVS conditions. This paper proposes a software-based ZVS strategy based on variable phase-shift control for two-parallel T-type NPC converters. By actively regulating the circulating current between parallel legs, the inductor current is shaped into a quadrilateral waveform, enabling ZVS turn-on operation while maintaining a strictly constant switching frequency. Based on accurate prediction of the circulating current within one switching cycle, the phase-shift angle required to satisfy the ZVS condition over the line cycle is analytically derived. The proposed method effectively decouples the three phases and is applicable to both single-phase and three-phase systems. Experimental results obtained from a laboratory prototype validate the feasibility and effectiveness of the proposed strategy.

title: Group Fault-Tolerant Time-Varying Formation Tracking Control for Multiagent Systems by Asynchronous Communications
authors: Jing He, Dongsheng Yang, Si Li, Jiayue Sun, Juan Zhang
abstract: This paper investigates the adaptive group fault-tolerant time-varying formation tracking (GFTFT) problem for linear multiagent systems (MASs) subject to actuator faults and switching communication networks. To fulfill complex tasks, a novel multi-layer framework is proposed, organizing agents into leaders, informed followers, and uninformed followers across multiple subgroups. To this end, firstly, a fault-tolerant GFTFT protocol is proposed to relax the restrictions on the severity of actuator faults, thereby enhancing tolerance to unknown faults. Secondly, novel multiple asynchronous event-triggered mechanisms (MAETMs) are designed, which enhance coordination and transparency by implementing a layered triggering strategy with distinct functions for inter-layer and inter-group communication. Third, the connectivity condition is relaxed, requiring only joint connectivity of the graphs over time, which accommodates intermittently disconnected topologies. Finally, the simulation experiment is conducted to validate the theoretical results.

title: Behavior of Image Information Metrics under Controlled Blur and Noise Distortions
authors: Nikola Plavac, Seyedeh Kimia Arfaie Oghani, Seyed Ali Amirshahi, Sophie Triantaphilidou, Marius Pedersen
publish: IEEE Open Journal of Signal Processing
abstract: Camera Image Quality Metrics (IQMs) are widely used to characterise imaging systems for human as well as machine vision applications, yet their relationship to the latter remains insufficiently understood. Recently introduced image information metrics have been proposed as alternatives to traditional IQMs, but their behavior under controlled imaging degradations and relevance to vision tasks require further study. This work systematically analyzes the behavior of traditional camera IQMs and image information metrics under controlled blur and noise distortions. We collected a dataset consisting of two parts. The first part consists of laboratory captures of four different objects, created with systematic camera variations in defocus, noise level, exposure value, and camera-to-object distance. The images included a custom test chart to enable direct measurement of imaging metrics from each captured scene. For the second part, a physics-based imaging pipeline simulation was used to generate synthetic images with independently controlled blur and noise levels. Across both laboratory and simulated data, image information metrics exhibited greater sensitivity to the combined effect of optical and noise degradations compared to traditional camera performance metrics. Multiple object detection networks were evaluated and qualitative relationships between traditional camera IQMs, image information metrics, and detection performance were examined. Although detection performance variations were generally modest, certain image information-based metrics, particularly the ideal observer signal-to-noise ratio (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mathit {SNR}_{i}^{2}$</tex-math></inline-formula>), exhibited qualitatively more consistent monotonic trends than traditional IQMs. Overall, the results indicate that image information metrics show greater sensitivity to combined blur and noise degradations than traditional IQMs, while also highlighting the limitations of imaging metrics in predicting task-level performance, which remains dependent on scene content and object characteristics.

title: Urban Hazard Mapping Using Multi-Spatiotemporal and -Directional SAR Interferometric Coherence: A Case Study on Urban Flood in 2015 Joso City Japan
authors: Wei Cao, Yicheng Song, Akira Hirose, Ryo Natsuaki
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) is widely used for urban damage detection. However, complex backscattering and insufficient utilization of spatiotemporal information in urban area limit the accuracy of conventional interferometric-coherence-based detection methods. In this study, we propose a novel approach named multi-spatiotemporal and -directional SAR interferometric coherence (MSTIC). This method exploits multi-temporal acquisitions to reduce the bias in coherence estimation while using a small spatial window, thereby preserving spatial resolution. In addition, it combines multi-directional observations to mitigate geometric distortions caused by side-looking radar imaging, such as layover and shadow. We evaluated the proposed method on the 2015 Joso City flood event in Japan using data observed by the ALOS-2 satellite. In addition, we also compared among all possible tracks and interferometric pairs. The experimental results indicate that the proposed approach achieves superior performance compared to all alternative combinations, thereby demonstrating the potential of MSTIC method for urban disaster monitoring.

title: Computational Investigation of Magnetophoretic Separation of Blood-Cell-Inspired Particles in Serpentine Microchannels
authors: Sumit Kumar Mehta, Prabal Baishya, Khushi Pathak, Jinmay Kalita, Somchai Wongwises, Pranab Kumar Mondal
abstract: We computationally investigate magnetophoretic separation characteristics of blood-cell-inspired particles representing white blood cells (WBCs) and red blood cells (RBCs) in serpentine microchannels under saline-water diluted conditions. The flow field inside the microchannel and the induced magnetic field distribution are numerically simulated using a finite-element-based framework, while particle trajectories are obtained through Newtonian particle tracking. The study systematically examines the influence of magnetic configuration, channel geometry, and flow conditions on magnetic field gradients, particle trajectories, recovery, purity, and separation efficiency. The results demonstrate that serpentine microchannels generate stronger and more non-uniform magnetic field gradients compared to planar configurations, thereby enhancing magnetophoretic manipulation and particle separation. The influence of magnet geometry is also analyzed, revealing that square-shaped magnets provide the most favorable separation characteristics among the investigated configurations due to stronger localized magnetic field gradients. Furthermore, the combined effects of Reynolds number, serpentine channel amplitude, and magnet position are shown to govern the balance between magnetophoretic and hydrodynamic drag forces, leading to distinct operating windows for enhanced separation performance. The present work should be interpreted as a first-order computational design study employing simplified particle surrogates under low-Reynolds-number conditions. The findings provide physical insights and computational design guidelines for the development and optimization of magnetophoretic microfluidic separators for blood-cell-inspired particles in lab-on-chip applications.

title: Descriptor: LYNRED Mobility Dataset Multimodal Detection Subset (LYNRED-MDS)
authors: Loïc Arbez, Jessy Matias, Xavier Brenière, Jocelyn Chanussot, Ronald Phlypo
abstract: Current road safety systems primarily focus on minimizing post-collision damage. However, advances in algorithmic perception are shifting focus toward early collision prediction, especially in low-visibility conditions like nighttime or fog, where thermal infrared sensing outperforms both human vision and RGB imaging. While available RGB-infrared datasets such as FLIR ADAS and LLVIP are good benchmarks, they mostly consist of clear weather and overly simple scenarios. In this paper, we introduce the LYNRED-MDS : Multimodal Detection Subset, a subset of the LYNRED Mobility Dataset, comprised of 4000 RGB-infrared image pairs captured under diverse weather, lighting, and road conditions around Grenoble, France. Our dataset spans varied driving contexts (urban, rural, mountainous, etc.) and a vehicle fleet compliant with Western European standards. Thermal cross-dataset evaluation using a YOLOv8n baseline suggests that our dataset offers strong generalization potential for pedestrian detection in driving scenarios. By covering critical edge cases, our dataset supports the development of more reliable and deployable vision systems for advanced driver-assistance systems.

title: Enhanced Differential-linear Cryptanalysis of Forró with MILP
authors: Zhengting Li, Lin Ding, Xinhai Wang, Honglei Wang, Jiang Wan, Fan Zhang
abstract: ARX-based design is a major building block of modern cryptographic ciphers due to its efficiency in software. Forró is an ARX-based stream cipher proposed by Coutinho et al. at ASIACRYPT 2022, which was designed to provide higher security margin than the ChaCha stream cipher. In this paper, we propose a full automated MILP model called MinForró, to derive linear approximations for the Forró stream cipher. For the differential part, a two-stage strategy to search for single-bit differential trails with high differential correlations is presented, which helps us to find the first-ever 3-round differential trails for Forró. By combining the linear approximations obtained by MinForró and 3-round differential trail for Forró, we propose improved differential-linear distinguishers for 4-, 5-, 5.25-, 5.5-, 5.75-, 6-, 6.25- and 6.5-round Forró with complexities 232.44, 246, 250, 264.32, 287.12, 2117.92, 2174.92 and 2226.88, respectively. The proposed differential-linear distinguishers for 4-, 5-, 5.25- and 5.5- round Forró significantly improve the existing distinguishers by factors of 24.11, 283.68, 2127.64 and 2178.20, respectively. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first differential-linear distinguisher for Forró that reaches 6.5 rounds, which is a significant advancement over the existing record of 5.5 rounds. We have implemented the differential-linear distinguishers for 4- and 5- round Forró on a common PC, and the experimental results confirm the correctness of these distinguishers. Furthermore, when combined with the Probabilistic Neutral Bits (PNB) technique, we obtain key recovery attacks on 5.5-, 6-, 6.5- and 6.75-round Forró with time complexities 2149.20, 2151.84, 2213.49 and 2251.97, respectively. The proposed key recovery attack on 5.5-round Forró significantly improves the time complexity of the existing attack by a factor of 275.84. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first key recovery attack on Forró that reaches 6.75 rounds, which is a significant advancement over the existing record of 5.5 rounds.

title: Adaptive Prescribed-Time Cooperative Guidance With Actuator Efficiency Loss
authors: Rujia Chen, Haibin Duan
publish: IEEE Transactions on Control of Network Systems
abstract: To address the efficiency loss of actuators in cooperative guidance for multiple missiles intercepting a maneuvering target, this paper proposes a Three-Dimensional (3D) adaptive Prescribed-Time (PT) cooperative guidance strategy. A three-channel decoupled model with actuator efficiency loss decomposes the 3D system into longitudinal and normal/lateral subsystems to separately satisfy impact time and impact angle constraints. For the longitudinal subsystem, an adaptive PT cooperative guidance law with consensus ensures all missiles agree on relative distance and speed within a predefined time under a unified communication topology. For the normal and lateral subsystems, an adaptive PT impact-angle guidance law with virtual control inputs relies only on local state information to meet angle constraints. Rigorous stability analysis verifies the PT convergence of both guidance laws. Simulation results validate rapid convergence within the specified impact time, effective satisfaction of impact-angle constraints, resilience to input disturbances, and robust performance across diverse communication topologies and initial conditions.

title: Portable Carotid Artery Flowmeter Based on Time-to-Digital Converter Implemented Using FPGA
authors: Yun-Yu Hsieh, Yi-Huan Chang, Chun-Ting Hsieh, Wei-Cheng Lin
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Consumer Technology (ICCT-Pacific)
abstract: This paper presents an FPGA-based time-to-digital converter (TDC) architecture for ultrasonic time-of-flight (TOF) measurement in carotid blood-flow sensing. Conventional Doppler systems require high-speed ADCs and complex signal processing, while the proposed carry-chain and phase-wrapping TDC achieves a 5ps time resolution using mixed-mode clock management. Post-implementation simulations demonstrate linearity with an R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> of 0. 9 5 6 9, and experimental results confirm physiologically consistent velocity estimation, yielding peak systolic and end-diastolic velocities of 0.8 m/s and 0.2 m/s, respectively. These results highlight the potential of this compact and low-power architecture for portable biomedical diagnostic applications.

title: Design of an Autonomous Feeding Support Mechanism for Aquaculture Facilities Using UAVs
authors: Taichi Yanagisawa, Hideyuki Takahashi, Daiki Togawa, Kazuki Fukae, Kenichi Arai, Tetsuo Imai, Hirofumi Miyajima, Mitsuru Hattori, Toru Kobayashi
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Consumer Technology (ICCT-Pacific)
abstract: This paper proposes an autonomous control method using UAVs to automate feeding tasks in marine aquaculture, replacing conventional boat-based operations, in order to solve problems like labor shortages and soaring operational costs. Unlike static agricultural path planning, we propose a “Demand-Driven and Energy-Aware Distributed Coordination Mechanism” that explicitly addresses the unique constraints of aquaculture: the stochastic nature of biological demand (fish hunger) and critical physical resource limits (battery). We implemented a multi-agent system that enables multiple UAVs to autonomously coordinate feed transport and feeding based on hunger levels detected by smart cages. The system also automatically selects UAVs for takeoff based on battery status and controls actions by sharing states among the swarm. Through experiments in a simulated aquaculture environment, we verified that our approach achieved zero collisions compared to 31 in a random baseline and significantly improved feed distribution uniformity by reducing the standard deviation from 34.96 to 2.35. These results demonstrate that the proposed distributed control ensures both safety and fairness without compromising energy efficiency.

title: Balancing Speed and Robustness: High-Performance Diffusion Purification Strategies for Adversarial Defense
authors: Tzu-Shan Chang, Yu-Chieh Chang, Bo Hsiao, Hong-Yi Chang
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Consumer Technology (ICCT-Pacific)
abstract: Although deep learning models have demonstrated impressive performance across diverse domains, their vulnerability to adversarial examples remains a critical obstacle to their deployment in security-sensitive applications. Although diffusion model (DM)-based purification methods like DiffPure have strong denoising capabilities, they are computationally expensive and slow due to multi-step DDIM/DDPM sampling. To address these limitations, this study proposes three efficient, diffusion-based purification strategies that enhance speed and robustness: (1) the One-Step/Few-Step Consistency Purifier (CP), which reduces multi-step DDIM sampling to one or a few steps for near real-time inference, (2) Feature-Level Guidance, which enforces constraints in the robust feature space to improve semantic preservation, and (3) Frequency Gating, which suppresses high-frequency adversarial perturbations through frequency-domain constraints. Experimental results show that the One-Step CP strategy achieves a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$20 x$</tex> speedup over the 20-step DDIM baseline <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(\mathbf{1. 1 6 ~ m s}$</tex> to <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{0. 0 6 ~ m s})$</tex>, while the Frequency Gating strategy yields the greatest robustness improvement, achieving a purified accuracy of 0.6469 with a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$4.6 x$</tex> speedup. These findings demonstrate the potential of lightweight, semantically stable, diffusion-based purification frameworks for practical, secure deployment.

title: Bioelectricity Generation from 3D Printed Plant Microbial Fuel Cell to Supply Power to Monitoring Sensors in Vertical Garden
authors: Azizul Moqsud, Futa Akamatsu, Mohd Anuaruddin Ahmadon, Jafreezal Jaafar
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Consumer Technology (ICCT-Pacific)
abstract: This study explores the potential of Plant Microbial Fuel Cells (PMFCs) as a sustainable bioenergy source for powering environmental monitoring sensors. PMFCs harness the symbiotic relationship between plant roots and soil microbes to generate electricity through natural metabolic processes. By integrating PMFCs into vegetated soil systems, we demonstrate a low-cost, renewable method for continuous power generation without external energy input. The experimental setup involved native plant species and optimized electrode configurations to enhance electron transfer efficiency. Results show that the system can reliably produce sufficient voltage to operate low-power sensors for real-time data collection. This approach offers a promising solution for decentralized energy supply in remote or ecologically sensitive areas, contributing to the advancement of green infrastructure and autonomous environmental monitoring.

title: Area Reduction of DCT Circuits for JPEG Encoding Using Approximate Multipliers with Recovery Schemes
authors: Hideyuki Ichihara, Haruki Okahara, Tomoo Inoue
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Consumer Technology (ICCT-Pacific)
abstract: For error-tolerant applications such as image processing and artificial intelligence, where a certain degree of computational inaccuracy can be accepted, approximate computing has been explored to reduce circuit area and enhance fault tolerance. In this paper, we propose an approximate discrete cosine transform (DCT) circuit for a JPEG (Joint Photographic Experts Group) encoder, a widely used method for image compression and decompression. The proposed approximate DCT circuit employs an approximate multiplier with an adjustable error recovery scheme, enabling a trade-off between computational accuracy and circuit area. By appropriately designing the recovery capability of the approximate multiplier according to the characteristics of the JPEG algorithm, we demonstrate that the proposed approximate DCT circuit can effectively reduce circuit area while maintaining sufficient image quality after decompression.

title: A Double-Node-Upset Self-Recoverable Latch with Soft Error Tolerance to Soft Errors Around Clock Edges
authors: Song Wang, Kazuteru Namba
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Consumer Technology (ICCT-Pacific)
abstract: The continuous scaling of semiconductor technology, with smaller device dimensions and lower supply voltages, has significantly increased the susceptibility of circuits to soft errors. In particular, soft errors injected at input nodes around clock edges pose serious risks of erroneous sampling, which most existing hardened latch designs do not address. This paper presents a novel latch architecture that achieves complete selfrecovery from double-node upsets (DNUs) while also providing robustness against soft errors occurring at input nodes around clock transitions. The proposed design employs delay elements (DEs) to introduce temporal redundancy, enabling effective masking of transient faults. Simulation results based on a 1 5 nm FinFET process confirm that the latch can fully recover from single-node and double-node upsets and reliably tolerate soft errors around clock edges. Although the proposed DE offers large delay capability, it incurs considerable power overhead. Comparative analysis demonstrates that the latch achieves enhanced fault tolerance compared with state-of-the-art designs, while highlighting power as the primary limitation, suggesting the potential for further optimization.

title: Design and Implementation of a New Secure Platform for CAN FD
authors: Yu Watada, Hiroki Kumano, Takuya Kusaka
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Consumer Technology (ICCT-Pacific)
abstract: Controller Area Network(CAN) is a well-known invehicle network and which is widely used for consumer equipment. Though CAN FD is an extended version of CAN, it does not have secure functions. In this research, a new design and implementation of Secure Platform for CAN FD is shown. The system includes Message Authentication Code and Payload Encryption based on ASCON lightweight cipher. The system also includes ECDH based key management. The majority of the software are newly implemented as open-source software on BSD license. The performance of the system is evaluated on Raspberry Pi.

title: Improving English Text Normalization With Abbreviation Expansion
authors: Naoki Kawata, Yota Kurokawa, Masaru Fukushi
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Consumer Technology (ICCT-Pacific)
abstract: Social media text often contains slang and heavily abbreviated forms that remain difficult for rewriting models to interpret. This study examines a preprocessing step in which abbreviations are expanded prior to normalization. Using 3,000 training pairs derived from MultiLexNorm and ChatGPT, we fine-tuned T5-small and Flan-T5-small and evaluated them on both reconstructed MultiLexNorm sentences and ChatGPTgenerated data. The proposed method improved BERTScore, especially for Flan-T5, which achieved 0.9928 on the ChatGPT test set. These results suggest that preprocessing can enhance the robustness of normalization models in noisy settings.

title: Edge LLM Inference on Big.LITTLE Architectures
authors: Wei-Cheng Huang, Ming-Chieh Chang, Kai-Siang Shih, Chih-Yen Liao, Chingwei Yeh, Po-Han Huang, Chi-Tien Sun, Tay-Jyi Lin
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Consumer Technology (ICCT-Pacific)
abstract: Recent edge devices are equipped with multiple high-performance processor cores, which are capable of edge LLM inference. Heterogeneous multicores with both performance- and efficiency- (i.e., less-performance but very power-efficient) processor cores are common for tasks with varying workloads to save energy, but they create hurdles for parallel LLM computing. This paper presents core streamlining, fined-grained multithreading, and computility-aware multithreading to improve the performance of edge LLM on heterogeneous multicores. In our experiments, the inference of Llama3 8B on an 8core SoC (RK3588) has been improved from 2.12 tokens/s to 3. 9 6, ~4. 1 3 and 4. 8 5 respectively.

title: Optimized Image Filtering and Multi-Task Deep Learning for Fingerprint Recognition
authors: Abdelrahman Nabil Salaheldin, Khaled Ahmed Hussein, Tamer M. Nassef
publish: 2025 35th International Conference on Computer Theory and Applications (ICCTA)
abstract: Fingerprint recognition systems rely heavily on input image quality, which is often degraded by smudges, lighting variations, and skin conditions. To address these challenges, this paper presents an integrated framework that combines classical image preprocessing with a deep multi-task convolutional neural network (CNN). Ten custom image filters, including Histogram Equalization, CLAHE, Contrast Stretching, Gabor filtering, and an optimized ridge enhancement pipeline, were developed and implemented through a GUI-based interface to improve ridge clarity and contrast. The preprocessed images were used to train a CNN with residual connections, designed to perform user identification, gender classification, and finger-type recognition on the SOCOFing dataset. Experimental results demonstrate that preprocessing significantly boosts performance, with the best filter combination achieving accuracies of 83% for user identification, 8 9% for gender classification, and 8 1% for finger type, surpassing the baseline by 1 0 - 1 5%. These findings highlight that preprocessing is not merely auxiliary but a critical step in fingerprint recognition, and future work will explore adaptive filter selection, class rebalancing, and real-time deployment on resource-constrained devices.

title: Statistical Analytical Review of QoS-Aware Resource Allocation and Performance Optimization Strategies in 5G and Emerging 6G Networks
authors: Ashish Kumar Agrawal, Narendra Kumar Rout, Nirjharinee Parida, Ranjeet Kumar Rout
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: Resource allocation and QoS assurance are the primary architectural and operational difficulties in 5G networks, and 6G research increases these constraints. This study analyzes QoS-aware scheduling, allocation, and optimization techniques from V2X, IoT-edge, mmWave, D2D, HetNets, and 5G network slicing domains statistically. This study evaluates and maps throughput, latency, fairness index, spectrum efficiency, energy consumption, interference reduction, and slice-level performance sets, unlike prior surveys that cataloged approaches. New algorithms include proportional fairness schedulers, hybrid heuristic optimizers, DRL, LSTM-driven prediction models, game-theoretic resource allocation, and entropy-weighted evolutionary algorithms. This structured qualitative synthesis and unified numerical benchmarking analysis reveals how the recommended techniques handle varied traffic loads, fluctuating mobility, mmWave interference, IoT density spikes, and dynamic slicing constraints. RL-driven systems adapt well, heuristic-based algorithms optimize multi-objectively, deterministic schedulers schedule fairly, and statistical interference models stabilize mmWave ecosystems. This statistical analytical analysis solves a long-standing gap by finding similar performance trends across studies and assessing relative gains using consistent assessment criteria, allowing researchers to objectively compare tactics. To achieve 5G and 6G QoS standards, hybrid algorithmic ecosystems with adaptive AI models, statistical interference modeling, and scheduling heuristics are needed for different scenarios. Context-aware resource allocation techniques, 6G mobility prediction, AI-native slicing frameworks, interference-resilient edge architectures, and security-aware allocation systems are built on the study process.

title: Hybrid PSO-ADAM Optimized Feedforward Neural Network for Nonlinear System Identification with Adaptive Activation Functions
authors: Shailesh Pratap Singh, Nishant Rajput, Quish Raj, Omit Singh, Prabhat Kumar, Pankaj Kumar Das, Kanhaiya Kumar
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: Many technical applications like signal processing, robotics, and control systems depend on nonlinear system identification. Conventional system identification methods usually struggle with precision and generalization in very nonlinear settings. Feed-forward neural networks offer a strong replacement, but they still require appropriate architecture and successful training methods. Mix heuristic and gradient-based approaches in hybrid learning to enhance the training process. Apply heuristic algorithms and back propagation among other hybrid learning methods. faster and more precise convergence compared with more traditional training methods. better generalizing ability and stability for complex nonlinear systems.

title: Design of an Integrated Model for Prostate Cancer Analysis Using Self-Supervised Learning, Uncertainty-Aware Multimodal Segmentation and Bioinspired Validation In Process
authors: Sarita Pathy, Narendra Kumar Rout, Nirjharinee Parida, Ishrat Nazeer
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: Multiparametric MRI delineation of prostate architecture and clinically important lesions is fragile for prostate cancer detection when quality, acquisition methodology, and scanner properties Vary In Process. Capsule or apex boundary ambiguity, inconsistent lesion presentation across modalities, and distribution shift reliability hinder segmentation-driven diagnosis methods. Many systems use fully supervised learning with static multimodal fusion, which seems unsuitable for clinical imaging heterogeneity and often produces overconfident but unstable predictions. The chained, validation-centric architecture treats segmentation as a gradual anatomical reasoning process. The prostate structure–aware masked self-supervised learning method ProSAM-SSL pretrains an encoder to internalize cross-modal anatomical consistency before labeling. Based on uncertainty-aware multimodal experts, AURORA-FuseNet may prioritize reliable MRI data at the voxel level. BioContour-CA, a tissue evolution-inspired differentiable cellular-automata technique, iteratively stabilizes gland and lesion boundaries to refine probabilistic segmentations in uncertain regions. ZoneGraph-Transformer models lesions, zonal anatomy, and capsule proximity utilizing better geometry to provide organized, clinically interpretable risk descriptors. Finally, TRUST-Prostate forecasts intervals and triage signals when automated judgments need human review using counterfactual shift analysis and conformal calibrations. Experiments reveal that simulated acquisition shifts improve lesion delineation, border fidelity, and calibrations. These extensions may make prostate MRI analysis more accurate, anatomically grounded, uncertainty-aware, and clinically accountable for the process.

title: AI-Augmented Multiple Model TPMBM Filter for Tracking Maneuvering Objects
authors: Ibrahim Salim, Nermeen Okasha, Wagdy Anis
publish: 2025 35th International Conference on Computer Theory and Applications (ICCTA)
abstract: Multi-object tracking (MOT) of maneuvering targets in cluttered environments remains a formidable challenge for autonomous and surveillance systems. While the Multiple Model Trajectory Poisson Multi-Bernoulli Mixture (MM-TPMBM) filter offers a principled Bayesian solution by integrating Jump-Markov System dynamics, its performance is constrained by a reliance on fixed model transition probabilities and significant computational complexity. To overcome these limitations, this paper proposes an AI-augmented MM-TPMBM filter that synergistically integrates deep learning with traditional Bayesian recursion. Specifically, we introduce a Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) network to adaptively predict motion model transitions online, eliminating the need for a priori transition matrices. Additionally, a feedforward neural network is developed for intelligent hypothesis pruning, dramatically reducing computational overhead by preemptively discarding unlikely measurement-to-track associations prior to data association. Extensive Monte Carlo simulations demonstrate that the proposed framework significantly outperforms benchmark filters, including the standard MM-TPMBM, <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\delta$</tex>-GLMB, and TPMBM, reducing the Generalized Optimal Sub-Pattern Assignment (GOSPA) error by <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{1 2} \%$</tex> and the average execution time per time step by 42%. These results validate the efficacy of the proposed AI-augmented approach in enhancing both the accuracy and efficiency of multi-object tracking in complex, cluttered scenarios, where the computational overhead of the AI modules is significantly outweighed by the gains in data association efficiency.

title: Automated Directivity Testing of Transducers with Integrated Control and Signal Processing

title: Hierarchical Optimization Prescribed Performance Framework for Networked Mobile Manipulators: A Novel Reinforcement Learning Approach
authors: Qian Chen, Ming-Feng Ge, Jing Fu, Teng-Fei Ding, Yi-Fan Li, Can Zhou
abstract: This paper investigates distributed optimal teleoperation control for networked mobile manipulators (NMMs) subject to model uncertainties, nonholonomic constraints, and external disturbances. To achieve cost-minimization cooperative control with prescribed transient and steady-state performance, a hierarchical optimization prescribed performance (HOPP) framework is proposed by integrating a reinforcement learning-based optimization estimator (RLOE) with a prescribed performance stability controller (PPSC). In the proposed scheme, the RLOE generates distributed reference trajectories for slave mobile manipulators through local neighbor interactions while minimizing a cooperative performance index. The PPSC is then designed to guarantee bounded tracking errors with prescribed convergence behavior for both the master and slave manipulators. Lyapunov-based analysis is provided to establish the boundedness and convergence properties of the closed-loop system. Rooted in Lyapunov stability theory, the proposed control algorithm is designed to ensure reliability and efficacy. Simulation studies on a teleoperation system of 2-DoF mobile manipulators demonstrate that the proposed method achieves accurate tracking, reduced cooperative cost, and improved transient performance.

title: Research on modeling and control algorithms for magnetorheological fluid vibration dampers
authors: Mingzhong Wu, Zhihong Lin
abstract: Traditional passive dampers, due to their non-adjustable damping characteristics, no longer meet the vibration isolation requirements of modern vehicles. Therefore, this paper presents research on vibration-isolation control using semi-active dampers that leverage the properties of magnetorheological fluids. First, an improved BO-wen model was employed to establish a mathematical model of the magnetorheological fluid damper. Second, a 1/4-scale vehicle model incorporating the magnetorheological fluid damper was constructed. Finally, a sky-hook fuzzy control algorithm was proposed and compared with traditional fuzzy control. The results indicate that, when driving on Class B roads, the sky-hook fuzzy control reduced the RMS values of cab seat acceleration, tire dynamic load, and damper stroke by 75.46%, 62.43%, and 55%,respectively, compared to traditional fuzzy control condition. On Class C roads, the reductions were 75.24%, 62.71%, and 55%, respectively, compared to the traditional fuzzy control condition.
