title: Design and Performance Analysis of a Compact 4-Element Serpentine-Fed MIMO Circular Microstrip Patch Antenna for Dual-Band X/Ku-Band High-Gain Radar and Satellite Applications
authors: Ch. Raghavendra, Krishna Raju Dannana, Palakeerthi Lokesh
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: A four-element serpentine-fed MIMO circular microstrip patch antenna in-tended for dual-band operation in the Ku (12–18 GHz) and X (8–12 GHz) frequency bands is presented in this study along with a thorough design and electromagnetic performance analysis. A FR-4 substrate (ε<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">r</inf> = 4.4, h = 1.6 mm) with a full ground plane and lumped port excitation is used in the suggested antenna. A 12 mm long serpentine microstrip line feeds a single circular patch with a radius of 2.3 mm. With a gain of 2.4 dB, a bandwidth of 1.2 GHz, and a return loss of -38 dB, it resonates at 13 GHz. A 4-element orthogonal MIMO arrangement replicates the single-patch design along four directions, ensuring balanced excitation and reduced mutual coupling. The array exhibits dual resonances at 10 GHz and 13 GHz with isolation < −20 dB, ECC < 0.02, gain ≈ 4.7 dB, and persistent quasi-circular polarization. The serpentine feed generates broadband operation without the requirement for metasurfaces or faulty ground structures by enhancing current distribution and effectively linking the TM<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">11</inf> and TM<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">21</inf> modes. This small structure demonstrates its potential for X-band radar imaging, Ku-band satellite telemetry, and 5G backhaul lines by achieving a 95% gain enhancement, 47% directivity improvement, and 50% bandwidth extension

title: Security Enhancement of Mechanism for Preventing Unauthorized Recipient Transactions in the Ethereum Blockchain System
authors: Masataka Kawasaki, Yoko Kamidoi, Shin'ichi Wakabayashi
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Consumer Technology (ICCT-Pacific)
abstract: This study focuses on preventing unauthorized recipient transactions within the Ethereum blockchain system. Unauthorized recipient transactions occur when a sender transfers cryptocurrency without the recipient's awareness, posing risks such as the recipient being implicated in crimes such as suspected involvement in money laundering. Previous research has designed a transaction restriction function using smart contracts tailored to Ethereum's unique blockchain model. This prevention mechanism was implemented on open-source software and its functionality verified. This study proposes a method to enhance the security of processing conducted to investigate the relationship between senders and receivers. We implement this method in open-source software and demonstrate its effectiveness.

title: Study Protocol and Pilot Evaluation of Child-AI Voice Interaction Using a Dedicated Dialogue Device
authors: Tatsuji Terada, Shingo Mabu, Mamiko Koshiba
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Consumer Technology (ICCT-Pacific)
abstract: Despite the rapid spread of generative AI in education, conversational AI for children in natural settings remains underexplored. We outline the design and a pilot field test of a dedicated voice dialogue device that supports child-AI interaction. In a pilot study at a private after-school care facility, children and staff used shared M5Stack Core2-based devices. Questionnaires indicated high usability for children and low operational burden for staff, although system response delay increased when multiple devices requested text-to-speech simultaneously. The main phase assigns one device per child for about 28 days to collect detailed dialogue logs and interaction metrics, examining longitudinal changes in conversational behavior and deriving design implications for educational and welfare settings.

title: A Goal-Conditioned Graph Framework for Dynamic Priority Scheduling in Flexible Job Shops
authors: Ling Khee Yaw, Mohd Anuaruddin Ahmadon, Mohd Hilmi Hasan, Shingo Yamaguchi
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Consumer Technology (ICCT-Pacific)
abstract: The Flexible Job Shop Scheduling Problem (FJSP) requires balancing competing objectives such as maximizing throughput, minimizing energy use, and reducing machine fatigue. Traditional meta-heuristics like Genetic Algorithms (GA) produce strong solutions but suffer from high computational latency, making them unsuitable for real-time rescheduling. Conversely, standard Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) agents, such as Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) and Double Deep QNetworks (DDQN), typically optimize a single static reward and cannot adapt when production priorities shift. To address these limitations, this paper proposes a Goal-Conditioned Reinforcement Learning (GCRL) framework built on a multi-stage Graph Attention Network. This enables a single agent to dynamically adjust its scheduling policy based on a user-defined goal vector. Evaluated against Shortest Processing Time (SPT), GA, PPO, DDQN, Multi-Agent PPO (MAPPO), and Reptile, the GCRL agent achieves superior average makespan (57.1) compared to the industry-standard SPT (58.0) and PPO (59.8). Furthermore, it operates with negligible latency (0.11 ms vs. 108 ms for GA), making it suitable for adaptive and sustainable Digital Twin manufacturing environments.

title: A Resilient IoT-Based Mountain Rescue System Using LoRa Mesh and Physiological Sensing
authors: Ming-Ying Chung, Szu-Yu Kuo, Liang-Bi Chen
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Consumer Technology (ICCT-Pacific)
abstract: This paper presents a wearable IoT-based rescue system designed to enhance mountain emergency response in offgrid environments. The proposed platform integrates low-power LoRa mesh communication, ESP32-based physiological monitoring, and a GIS-assisted command center dashboard. By combining long-range mesh connectivity with real-time health sensing, the system enables distress alerts and situational awareness even when cellular networks fail. Field experiments demonstrated communication ranges up to 6 km in open terrain and reliable message relays in mountainous regions. The integration of physiological monitoring provides vital information for rescue coordination and personnel safety. This study aims to contribute to Taiwan's disaster-resilience framework by introducing a scalable, decentralized communication solution for mountain rescue operations.

title: Evaluating the Impact of Technology and Infrastructure on the Competitiveness of International Logistics Centers: Evidence From Vietnam International Logistics Centers
authors: Wen-Kai Hsu, Le Thi Dieu Linh
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Consumer Technology (ICCT-Pacific)
abstract: International Logistics Centers (ILCs) play a crucial role in global supply chains, directly influencing national economic growth. In today's digital era, technology and infrastructure (PTI) have been decisive factors in logistics competitiveness. However, research has yet to provide a systematic framework for evaluating their impact. That observation motivated this study. Here, this study used the Best–Worst Method (BWM) to explore how this factor shapes the competitiveness of Vietnam's ILCs. The analysis confirmed that PTI is more than just important; it acts as the backbone that allows other components of competitiveness to perform well. From a practical point of view, this means that decision-makers should look beyond short-term managerial changes and focus instead on aligning infrastructure investment, spatial design, and technological integration to build resilience and long-term performance.

title: A Novel Camera, Lidar, and Weather Station Dataset for Precipitation Classification and Evaluation
authors: Muhammad Haaris Khan, Abdul Haq Azeem Paracha, Hamza Salaar, Georg Arbeiter
publish: 2026 International Conference on Data Science, Machine Learning, and Intelligence (DataSciMI)
abstract: Object detection algorithms enable an autonomous vehicle to understand and navigate the environment, using perception sensors such as camera and lidar. The performance of these algorithms deteriorates when the sensor data is influenced by heavy precipitation, i.e., in the presence of rain, fog, and snow. A classifier algorithm capable of identifying precipitation levels from lidar point cloud data and camera RGB images without external precipitation sensors is proposed. This paper introduces a novel multi-modal dataset, captured under real-world adverse weather conditions. The weather classification enables the autonomous driving system to switch to an appropriate object detection model according to different weather conditions. The data were recorded using camera, lidar and weather station, placed at two different locations in Kronach, Germany. All the annotations of weather classes were generated by following the World Meteorological Organization (WMO) standards to ensure standardization in training the classification models. The precipitation classification task utilizes Deep Neural Networks (DNNs) models VGG16, EfficientNetV2 and 3D CNN. They were trained and evaluated on our dataset. The result of this research achieved a test accuracy of 99.2% for camera-based EfficientNetV2 and 85.0% for lidar-based EfficientNetV2.

title:
Trap-Induced Transport and Electrostatic Effects in Gate-Underlapped MoS
                    <sub>2</sub>
                    Negative Capacitance TFET
authors: Malvika, Prabhat Singh, Bibhas Manna, Jagritee Talukdar
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: The relentless drive toward continued transistor miniaturization has introduced serious challenges in present-day CMOS technology, including excessive power dissipation, heightened thermal concerns, and significant reliability degradation. Tunnel Field-Effect Transistors (TFETs) have emerged as a promising alternative to conventional MOSFETs because they can achieve a subthreshold swing below the traditional 60 mV/decade limit. Furthermore, incorporating transition metal dichalcogenides (TMDCs), particularly Molybdenum Disulfide (MoS<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf>), provides additional advantages due to their superior electronic, thermal, and optical characteristics. In this work, a novel Gate-Underlap Lightly Doped Drain double-gate Negative Capacitance TFET (GU-LDD-DG-NC-TFET) with a raised drain structure employing MoS<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf> as the channel material is presented. The proposed design integrates ferroelectric layers to improve subthreshold swing, utilizes LDD engineering to enhance short-channel control, and incorporates source/drain underlap regions to suppress leakage currents. The device performance is further examined under a range of interface trap charge densities (1×10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">11</sup>-1×10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">12</sup> cm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">−3</sup>) to provide comprehensive insight into its operational behavior and to substantiate its potential for ultra-low-power electronic applications.

title: A Comprehensive Review of Graph-Based Anomaly Detection for Validating CVSS Scoring Consistency Using Vulnerability Knowledge Graphs
authors: Birendra Kumar Verma, Aryan Tyagi, Ayush Raj Singh
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: The Common Vulnerability Scoring System (CVSS) is still widely used as the method of measuring the severity of software vulnerabilities; however, current studies of the National Vulnerability Database (NVD) show that the scores assigned by analysts are often not aligned with the semantic meaning of the descriptions they provide. These inconsistencies may negatively affect the prioritisation of risks, the stability of patch management, and add bugs to automated security processes. The review unites the results of various fields, such as empirical evaluations of scoring inconsistency, creating cybersecurity knowledge graphs, language modelling based on domains, and anomaly detection with graphs, to understand the reasons and possible solutions. We question how Vulnerability Knowledge Graphs (VKGs) that combine CVE, CWE, CPE, vendor, and product data provide the structural background to diagnose scoring behaviour. Recent models, such as SecureBERT and variants, and graph neural network (GNN) detectors, such as DOMINANT, AnomalyDAE, or CoLA are examined to determine how semantic features and graph topology can be used to identify anomalous or inconsistent CVSS assignments. Based on these advances, we suggest a single KG-GNN system that can detect the possible vulnerabilities that are mis-scored and recommend the corrected CVSS vectors.The review argues that the process of synthesising semantic interpretation with graph-level reasoning is a viable way forward to more consistent and transparent vulnerability scoring.

title: Design, Implementation and Performance Optimization of Dual-Gate Forksheet FET for sub 3nm technology
authors: Abhishek Chauhan, Ashish Raman, Navneet Kumar, Hemaunt Kumar, Dheeraj Yadav, Rajshekhar Bharti, Mayank Singh, Abhishek Singh
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: As scaling of transistors approaches physical and technological boundaries; Forksheet Field Effect Transistors represent a viable alternative for maintaining Moore’s Law. The work explores the process and performance enhancement of junctionless Forksheet FETs through Silvaco TCAD simulation software. In comparison to traditional FinFETs, the Forksheet FET structure improves electrostatic control with minimal footprint, rendering it applicable for technology nodes below 3 nm. The device layout includes silicon as the channel, HfO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf> as high-κ gate dielectric and SiO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf> for the isolation, having a crucial 10 nm dielectric wall in between neighboring p-type and n-type devices. A junctionless doping profile (homogeneous 1×10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">19</sup> cm⁻<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sup>) helps to reduce the fabrication complexity. Simulations have been performed utilizing the Silvaco Atlas simulation environment by making the use of correct models for mobility, recombination, and tunneling effects. DC parameters of the device like threshold voltage (V<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">th</inf>), ON current (I<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">on</inf>), and subthreshold swing (SS) were evaluated. The junctionless Forksheet FET has high promise for future CMOS scaling based on its excellent electrostatic performance, reduced doping scheme, and small size which makes it a top contender for future logic devices.

title: AI-Enabled Framework for Stock Market Prediction Using KSE-100 Index
authors: Muhammad Asfar, Muddasir Hassan Khan, Omer Qureshi, Muhammad Farrukh Shahid
publish: 2026 International Conference on Data Science, Machine Learning, and Intelligence (DataSciMI)
abstract: Financial markets serve as platforms where instruments such as equities, foreign exchange, and cryptocurrencies are traded, and accurate price prediction remains a critical challenge for traders and investors. Data were extracted from Yahoo Finance for the period 2017 to 2023; we engineered a total of 21 features to forecast market trends. The models evaluated include ML algorithms such as XGBoost and Random Forest, and DL architectures such as LSTM, GRU, and Transformers. The experimental results demonstrate that the XGBoost model achieved the highest performance, with a R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> score of 0.92, the lowest test mean absolute error (MAE) and test root mean square error (RMSE) of 72.13 and 126.11, respectively, outperforming all other models on these evaluation metrics. These results are consistent with prior studies on time-series forecasting, where tree-based ensemble methods outperformed recurrent neural networks, particularly in settings where there are engineered features and limited sample sizes.

title: A Hybrid Approach for Low Light Image Enhancement using Traditional and Deep Learning
authors: Sparsh Kumar, Mithilesh Kumar Singh Yadav, Nagendra Pratap Singh
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: Image enhancement of low-light image aims to improve images taken under insufficient lighting by increasing visibility, adjusting contrast, and making important details easier to observe. Deep learning-based methods like RetinexDIP have achieved impressive enhancement but suffer from high computational cost due to iterative optimisation and a deep learning approach. Traditional methods, such as Multi-Scale Retinex (MSR), on the other hand, have little colour distortion and a loss of small details. Our research suggests we can combine the RetinexDIP–MSR methodology, where the results of both techniques are used with an averaging mechanism, in order to balance the losses of the MSR and the time complexity taken by RetinexDIP. The proposed framework leverages the reconstruction and fast illumination ability of RetinexDIP and the fast and fine details correction of MSR, producing enhanced images with improved brightness, natural colour consistency, and reduced processing time.

title: An Optimization-Based Approach to Distributed Generation Placement for Loss Minimization and Voltage Stability
authors: Maloth Srinivasa Rao, Kuna Srinivasa Rao, Ajay Kumar Moodadla, Mahesh Mudavath
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: Voltage instability has become a critical challenge for power utilities, primarily due to inadequate maintenance practices and limited financial resources for sustaining existing power system networks (PSNs). Insufficient maintenance arises when aging equipment is not timely replaced or when necessary system upgrades are delayed. Financial constraints further restrict the ability of utilities to perform these essential activities, resulting in increased power losses and a higher risk of voltage collapse. Reducing system losses is therefore essential to enhance network reliability and operational security. One effective approach to extending the operational lifespan of PSNs while improving voltages stabilities is the optimal integration of distributed generation (DG). Among the widely adopted DG’s technologies are solar’s photovoltaic’s (PV) systems and storm energy conversion systems. This review highlights the environmental, economics, and technically advantages of non-conventional DG sources compared with conventional centralized generation. In addition, key aspects of power loss, energy, and multi-objective optimization in DG planning are discussed. The study also offers a complete overview of existing methods for DG sizing and placement aimed at enhancing the voltage profile.

title: A Compact Folded Dipole Antenna with Improved Broadside Gain
authors: Sonam Raj, Anjali Kumari, Gaurav Sharma, Rajat Joshi, Neha Ahuja, Jagannath Malik
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: In this paper, a compact U-shaped folded dipole antenna designed to achieve enhanced broadside gain and improved radiation efficiency compared to a conventional dipole antenna. The antenna employs a dual-layer configuration, with modified L-shaped feed on the front side and a folded U-shaped dipole arms as main radiator realized on the back side of the dielectric substrate. Modified feeding configuration and dipole coupling arrangement enhances fringing electromagnetic fields in broadside direction, and improves impedance matching, resulting in a gain of 6.58dBi with radiation efficiency higher than 60% in entire band. The proposed structure offers higher gain, improved directivity, and reduced back radiation. Owing to its compact size and directional characteristics, the antenna is well-suited for C-band radar array antenna and satellite communication applications.

title: Stochastic Computing-Based Hybrid Convolutional Neural Networks for Eye Disease Detection on OCT Volumes
authors: Wen-Yi Tsai, Cheng-Hung Lin
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Consumer Technology (ICCT-Pacific)
abstract: Convolutional neural network (CNN) models utilizing stochastic computing for the classification of 3D optical coherence tomography (OCT) eye images. By representing numbers within [−1, 1] using bit-streams, and performing operations with XNOR gates and multiplexers, the model achieves significant reductions in power consumption and hardware footprint. The proposed model attained an accuracy of 93.02% in classifying OCT eye images, demonstrating its effectiveness in detecting various eye diseases. This approach provides a scalable and energy-efficient solution for deploying eye disease diagnostic systems in rural and remote areas, enhancing healthcare accessibility.

title: Rapid Detection of Uric Acid Based on μ-Drift Offset Readout Circuits and its Application in Metabolic Arthritis
authors: Wei-Lun Yen, Tzu-Wei Chung, Bing-Hong Chen, Wei-Cheng Lin, Chi-Jen Lo, Mei-Ching Yu
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Consumer Technology (ICCT-Pacific)
abstract: In this work, we develop rapid uric-acid detection readout circuits for metabolic arthritis screening using a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 0} \boldsymbol{\times} \mathbf{1 0}$</tex> capacitance biosensor integrated with a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\boldsymbol{\mu}$</tex>-drift fully differential amplifier (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mu$</tex>-drift FDA)-assisted readout circuits and a microcontroller. Based on the uricase redox mechanism, electrons released by the reaction alter the fringing electrical field and modulate the inter-electrode fringe capacitance, which is acquired through a multiplexer, amplified by the <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mu$</tex>-drift FDA, and digitized by a 10 -bit SAR ADC. The proposed <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mu$</tex>-drift FDA achieves a low input-referred offset of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$330 \mu \mathrm{V}$</tex> and a measured common-mode gain of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$-21.64 \text{dB}(1 \text{Hz}-100 \text{kHz})$</tex> using combined Miller and negative-Miller compensation. With a 10 s reaction time, the readout circuits exhibit changes in ADC counts of 40.5 and 8.8 for 5 mM and 0.5 mM uric acid after uricase catalysis, respectively, clearly separating concentration levels. Compared with a labowned single-ended folded-cascode OPA, the <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mu$</tex>-drift FDA improves sensitivity by <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$6.23 \times(5 \text{mM})$</tex> and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$2.2 \times(0.5 \text{mM})$</tex>.

title: A New Method for Emergency Information Caching in NDN-VANET Based on Travel Tendencies and the Distance
authors: Naru Tokunaga, Tetsuya Shigeyasu
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Consumer Technology (ICCT-Pacific)
abstract: It has been reported that caching the contents only when they are requested from the neighboring area where they were created, works effectively in content delivery in NDNVANET. This caching strategy is well suited for short-distance cruising of vehicles, but not for long-distance cruising. For long-distance cruising, a more suitable strategy is needed for the acquisition of contents generated in the areas serving as waypoints of an itinerary. This paper proposes a new caching algorithm for NDN-VANET designed for delivering emergency traffic information to traveling vehicles before they arrive at each waypoint of the itinerary. Delivering the emergency traffic information by the algorithm enables vehicles to change their itinerary safely by avoiding high-risk roads.

title: Comprehensive Evaluation of Adapter-Based Fine-Tuning and Post-Training Quantization for LLM Deployment
authors: Hirokazu Madokoro, Stephanie Nix, Taiki Kondo
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Consumer Technology (ICCT-Pacific)
abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have achieved remarkable performance across diverse tasks but remain computationally intensive, limiting their deployment on generalpurpose computing environments. This study investigates model compression techniques, specifically adapter-based fine-tuning and post-training quantization, to enable efficient operation of LLMs under resource constraints. Using the Meta Llama 3.18B Instruct model as a baseline, we evaluate two adapter methods, LoRA and QLoRA, and two quantization techniques, GPTQ and AWQ. Benchmarking on MMLU and JMMLU datasets reveals that QLoRA achieves the highest accuracy among compressed configurations, while GPTQ applied after LoRA maintains performance with reduced model size. Our findings indicate that combining adapter-based fine-tuning with posttraining quantization reduces model size and memory requirements while maintaining competitive accuracy. Although direct measurements of inference speed and power consumption are outside the scope of this study, the observed reduction in model complexity suggests potential applicability to environments with limited computational resources such as general-purpose lowpower devices.

title: Volatility-Aware Sizing and Seasonally Adaptive Control of Hybrid Energy Storage Using Multi-Horizon Forecasting in a Nordic Campus Microgrid
authors: Tareq Anwar Shikdar, Hannu Laaksonen
abstract: The electrification of heating, transportation, and buildings is transforming modern campuses into multi-energy environments where photovoltaic (PV) generation, electric-vehicle (EV) fast charging, and heat-pump (HP) loads interact across multiple timescales. These interactions introduce high volatility that conventional battery energy storage systems (BESS) and short-horizon forecasting techniques cannot manage effectively, particularly in Nordic climates with extreme seasonal irradiance and heating-dominated load patterns. This study develops a unified analytical–AI–optimization framework for sizing and controlling a hybrid battery energy storage system (HBSS) that integrates a fast-response supercapacitor/lithium titanate (SC/LTO) layer with a Li-ion energy storage layer. Volatility is quantified using three indicators: the PV Volatility Index (PVI), EV Fast-Charging Impact Factor (EFI), and Thermal Load Flexibility Index (TLFI) derived from a 3.5-year, 15-minute dataset from a Nordic university campus microgrid. A multi-horizon deep-learning model (HERA-4C), combining convolutional blocks, BiLSTM layers, and attention mechanisms, produces 1-, 4-, 24-, and 96-step forecasts and is embedded into a predictive energy-management and multi-objective optimization scheme using NSGA-II. The five-objective formulation minimizes annualized cost, CO₂ emissions, grid import, Li-ion degradation, and unmet load. Results indicate that the optimal HBSS configuration (≈260 kW SC/LTO and 200 kWh Li-ion) mitigates over 90% of PV–EV–HVAC volatility, reduces peak grid import by 72%, eliminates curtailment and unmet load, and achieves 15–22% annual cost reduction alongside a 14–18% decrease in CO₂ emissions. Seasonal evaluation confirms enhanced winter reliability through adaptive state-of-charge management. The proposed framework provides a reproducible methodology for resilient HBSS design in operational campus microgrids and supports scalable smart-building applications under high-volatility conditions.

title: Neuromorphic Control of the Serv-Arm Robot Using Spiking Neural Networks
authors: Alfredo Paulo Oliveira Barros, Reyner Carlos Silva Alegria, Gabriel Moriz Da Silva, Pedro Victor Dos Santos Matias, Carlos Alberto Oliveira De Freitas, Vicente Ferreira De Lucena, Vandermi João Da Silva
abstract: This work introduces a neuromorphic joint-space controller for a four-degree-of-freedom (DoF) servo-arm robotic manipulator, implemented via a spiking neural network (SNN) on low-cost embedded hardware. Unlike traditional methods based on inverse kinematics or Cartesian-space control, the proposed architecture operates directly on servo joint angles, receiving current and target configurations to predict the next joint-space action. A hybrid dataset with over 500,000 samples was generated by expanding joint-angle logs into a larger synthetic set to comprehensively cover the robot’s workspace, including oversampling of mechanically challenging configurations. The SNN was trained via surrogate gradients (snnTorch), and a systematic grid search was used to optimize the hidden-layer size, learning rate, and membrane decay factor. The final model, featuring a 128-neuron leaky integrate-and-fire (LIF) hidden layer, achieved a mean absolute error (MAE) of 11.06° on the validation set and generated smooth, reproducible trajectories during real-robot execution. Additionally, this study includes an empirical energy comparison between the SNN controller and an artificial neural network (ANN) baseline on a Raspberry Pi under identical motion routines. The results indicate that both models consume similar average power, with the SNN exhibiting a modest increase due to temporal simulation overhead in software-based execution on standard central processing units (CPUs).

title: Learning-Assisted Probabilistic Precoding for PAPR Reduction in OTSM With Different Subcarrier Configurations
authors: Arun Kumar, Venkatachalam Revathi, Nishant Gaur, Aziz Nanthaamornphong
abstract: Orthogonal Time Sequence Multiplexing (OTSM) has emerged as a promising waveform candidate for sixth-generation (6G) wireless systems due to its robustness against high mobility, Doppler dispersion, and time-varying channel conditions, while maintaining low receiver complexity. However, similar to other multicarrier-derived waveforms, OTSM suffers from a high peak-to-average power ratio (PAPR), which degrades power amplifier efficiency and system reliability. This letter proposes a novel Learning-Assisted Probabilistic Precoding (LA-PP) framework to address the PAPR issue in OTSM systems. The proposed LA-PP exploits learned OTSM-specific peak-sensitive signal statistics to guide probabilistic precoding, distinguishing it from conventional PAPR reduction techniques such as selective mapping (SLM), partial transmit sequences (PTS), and optimization-based approaches. Consequently, LA-PP avoids exhaustive search procedures and the need for side information. An OTSM-aware peak cost function is formulated to capture the sequency-domain phase alignment effects responsible for peak formation. Simulation results demonstrate that LA-PP achieves significant PAPR reduction across various subcarrier configurations, providing up to an 8 dB reduction compared to baseline OTSM. In addition, LA-PP improves bit error rate (BER) performance over Rayleigh fading channels. The proposed scheme preserves the standard OTSM signal structure and introduces no additional online computational complexity, making it a practical and efficient solution for real-time, high-mobility 6G communication scenarios.

title: A Survey on SAR ship classification using Deep Learning
authors: Ch Muhammad Awais, Marco Reggiannini, Davide Moroni, Emanuele Salerno
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Deep learning (DL) has become a central approach for ship classification using synthetic aperture radar (SAR) imagery. This survey reviews 74 representative studies selected from 187 publications, categorizing them into a taxonomy with four main dimensions: (i) DL architectures, (ii) datasets, (iii) image augmentation, and (iv) learning techniques. We analyze how approaches such as handcrafted feature integration, data augmentation, fine-tuning, and transfer learning influence classification performance, and summarize the use of public benchmarks including OpenSARShip and FUSARShip. This survey highlights key challenges: limited data availability, class imbalance, lack of standardized metrics, and limited interpretability of DL models. Future research directions include the development of SAR-specific DL architectures, advanced augmentation and generative approaches, integration of handcrafted and deep features, interpretable DL, and stronger interdisciplinary collaboration. By addressing these challenges, DL-based SAR ship classification can achieve greater robustness, accuracy, and transparency, ultimately strengthening maritime surveillance and operational monitoring.

title: SDFFNet: A Spatial-Frequency Domain Feature Fusion Network for Road Extraction From Very High-Resolution Satellite Images
authors: Danni Xue, Lin Bai, Wenqing Zhou, Yunqi Zhang, Amanda Gozho, SiWen Quan
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Despite the progress of deep learning in road extraction from VHR imagery, existing approaches often struggle with restricted receptive fields and insufficient global context modeling. These limitations become acute in complex urban environments, where shadows, occlusions, and inter-class similarities lead to road discontinuities and localization errors. These limitations hinder their ability to handle complex urban scenarios characterized by large road-scale variations, high visual similarity between roads and surrounding land-cover classes, and severe occlusions caused by shadows, vegetation, and high-rise buildings, often resulting in fragmented and inaccurately localized road networks. We propose SDFFNet, a feature fusion network that combines a ResNet encoder with a strip convolution decoder to solve these challenges. It integrates a large kernel cross-dimensional attention submodule to reduce shadow occlusion effects and enhance spatial context modeling ability. A fourier transform unit submodule captures global frequency domain information, while a spatial-frequency domain feature fusion module strengthens encoder-decoder interactions to mitigate interclass similarity interference. Additionally, a spatial pixel fusion module aligns deep semantic information to minimize prediction errors caused by pixel positional deviations. Experimental results show that SDFFNet achieves Intersection over Union (IoU) scores of 67.98%, 60.69%, and 64.85% on the Massachusetts, CHN6-CUG, and DeepGlobe datasets, respectively, outperforming comparable methods. The code for SDFFNet will be made available at <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/xuedanni1225/SDFFNet</uri>.

title: Adapting Self-Supervised Vision Foundation Models for Cross-Domain Surface Water Mapping
authors: Xiaoyan Lu, Qihao Weng
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Accurate and scalable mapping of surface water is crucial for understanding environmental dynamics, ensuring sustainable water resource management, and facilitating informed decision-making. However, the extraction of surface water at the global scale remains challenging due to the scarcity of labeled samples, as well as substantial spatial heterogeneity across regions. Traditional deep learning methods frequently fail to generalize effectively under these conditions. With the advent of vision foundation models (VFMs), such as DINOv3, visual representation learning has demonstrated enhanced generalization and transferability across domains. However, their potential for downstream remote sensing applications, particularly in surface water mapping, remains underexplored. In this study, we propose SWM-DINOv3, a framework designed to efficiently adapt the self-supervised DINOv3 model for large-scale, cross-domain surface water mapping (SWM). The framework employs a pre-trained DINOv3 as the encoder and a LinkNet architecture as the decoder. The pre-trained DINOv3 parameters are kept frozen, while parameter-efficient fine-tuning is performed using a general-purpose low-rank adaptation (LoRA) module and two task-specific adapters. Among these, a pyramid pooling adapter (PPA) is integrated to address the large-scale variability of water bodies by extracting multi-scale contextual features, while an atrous depthwise convolution adapter (ADCA) introduces lightweight local inductive biases, improving boundary delineation for water bodies with low texture and fragmented shorelines. Extensive experiments conducted on two global-scale datasets—OpenEarthMap and GLH-Water—demonstrate that SWM-DINOv3 effectively leverages the generalization capabilities of vision foundation models, achieving superior accuracy and robustness in cross-domain surface water mapping compared to existing VFM-based approaches. Notably, SWM-DINOV3 outperforms Segment Anything Model (SAM)-based methods by over 10% in F1-score. This work establishes a pivotal and scalable solution for global water resource monitoring and management.

title: Ultra-Dense and Multidimensional 98-Channel Chip-to-Fiber Optical I/O With Real-Time Control
authors: Yuxiang Yin, Wu Zhou, Yiou Cui, Zichun Lin, Kaihang Lu, Hao Chen, Zengqi Chen, Xianyi Feng, Xun Guan, Yeyu Tong
abstract: The rapid advancement of co-packaged optics (CPO) has highlighted the spatial arrangement of a large number of optical fibers attached to photonic chips as a critical challenge. In this study, we present an ultra-compact, multidimensional silicon photonic chip-fiber coupling interface, occupying a mere footprint of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mathbf {\text{0.2 }mm^{2}}$</tex-math></inline-formula> while accommodating a total of 98 physical channels. Our ultra-dense optical I/O (UDOIO) features an array of 49 compact, polarization-insensitive two-dimensional grating couplers (2DGCs), which achieve coupling efficiency of −3.47 dB and exhibit channel crosstalk of −31.32 dB. To facilitate real-time, on-chip polarization control, we employ field-programmable gate arrays (FPGAs) to adaptively reconfigure integrated Mach-Zehnder interferometers. Experimental results demonstrate that polarization crosstalk can be mitigated to below −18.8 dB within 690 <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mathbf {\mu s}$</tex-math></inline-formula>, and the actual crosstalk can be reduced to less than −32.5  dB using wide dynamic range optical power meters. In the high-speed data communication, we experimentally validate the signal integrity by transmitting 192 Gbps polarization-multiplexed signals using silicon microring modulators. Open high-speed eye diagrams can be successfully recovered with low bit error rate (BER) penalties.

title: Bilevel Multi-Fidelity Search Framework for Urban Cable Routing Optimization_supp1-3695134.pdf
authors: Rui Wang
abstract: Urban underground cable construction is crucial for improving the reliability of power distribution grids. It inherently constitutes a bilevel combinatorial optimization problem that couples upper-level substation topology decisions with lower-level cable routing optimization subject to street network constraints. Due to the rigid upper-lower hierarchical coupling and the huge combinatorial search space, conventional methods struggle to obtain high-quality solutions within limited computational budgets. To address this challenge, this study proposes a bilevel multi-fidelity search (BL-MFS) framework that integrates an adaptive multiple neighborhood search (AMNS) algorithm for the upper level with a collaborative multi-fidelity screening mechanism for the lower level. Facing only the upper-level subproblem, the hybrid genetic search (HGS) algorithm is first used for solution initialization. The AMNS, featuring seven problem-specific operators, is then utilized for upper-level solution exploration. In every exploration iteration, three hierarchical lower-level optimizers with increasing fidelity levels are employed sequentially to evaluate the upper-level candidates. The number of solutions filtered by the low-fidelity lower-level optimizer is dynamically adjusted based on ranking consistency across consecutive fidelity levels, thereby ensuring the effectiveness of the screening mechanism. Computational experiments on twelve benchmark instances at four problem scales and three real-world cases reveal that BL-MFS outperforms six competitive baselines, achieving solution cost reductions ranging from 3.21% to 81.08%. Comprehensive ablation studies, hyperparameter sensitivity analyses, and ranking consistency analyses comprehensively validate the effectiveness and robustness of the proposed framework.

title: Frontal Delta Decreasing and Occipital Ictal Alpha Increasing associated with Migraine in Preictal and Ictal Phases
authors: Li-Wei Ko, Sandeep Vara Sankar Diddi, Wen-Hsin Wu, Congying He, Kuan-Lin Lai, Yen-Feng Wang, Shuu-Jiun Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Neural Systems and Rehabilitation Engineering
abstract: Subtle changes across different migraine phases in resting electroencephalography (EEG) studies are commonly observed in migraine patients and have the potential to aid in clinical diagnosis. However, exploring the dynamic changes of brain activity across the migraine phases are extremely rare and difficult to analyze. This study aimed to longitudinally investigate the EEG spectral characteristics across the migraine phases in a paired EEG study. Ninety-five episodic migraine (EM) patients without aura and 59 control subjects participated in the study, and the resting close-eye EEG signals were recorded from their frontal and occipital regions. The EEG signals, recorded using a wireless EEG device with three dry spring-loaded electrodes, were retrospectively divided based on the migraine phases and paired after being transformed into wavelet spectra, resulting in 53 interictal-preictal pairs and 17 interictal-ictal pairs. The delta band power of the migraine patients in the frontal region showed that the preictal power, which was comparable to the control subjects, was significantly lower than that of the interictal (ρ < 0.01) and ictal powers. Alternatively, the alpha band power in the occipital region exhibited significantly (ρ < 0.01) higher ictal power compared to the interictal phase but exhibited no significance with preictal condition. The results further showed that 32 out of 46 pairs (69.5%) displayed reduction of frontal delta power from interictal to preictal phase and 11 out of 14 pairs (78.5%) produced increased occipital alpha power from interictal to ictal phase. Further, the low-frequency valley (LFV) power ratio from interictal to preictal phase in frontal region showed a negative correlation with the average headache intensity (AHI) and the domain alpha peak (DAP) power ratio from interictal to ictal phase in occipital region showed a positive correlation with the current headache intensity (CHI). In this longitudinal paired study, our findings suggested that the resting EEG activity efficiently extracted the phase specific spectral characteristics across the migraine patients and obtained significant correlation with the pain intensity levels. Overall, the decreased frontal delta power in the preictal phase and the increased occipital alpha power in the ictal phase may be considered as the potential markers to predict the incoming migraine attack and to quantitatively assess the headache intensity.

title: Neural-Network-Augmented Tobit Kalman Filter with Adaptive Covariance for Censored State Estimation
authors: Sarocha Jetawatthana, Thanakorn Khamvilai
abstract: To estimate states from censored or saturated measurements, the Tobit Kalman Filter (TKF) was developed. However, model errors or mismatches can impair the performance of the TKF, resulting in lower estimation accuracy. In order to increase the TKF’s accuracy and robustness, this study proposes three significant improvements. First, the correlation between the states and the expectation of censoring indicator variables, which predict the occurrence of saturation, is explicitly incorporated into the filtering framework. Second, a neural network, trained online, is augmented to the process model, which allows the filter to dynamically adjust to model mismatches. Third, the theoretical convergence analysis and simulations, based on accepted benchmarks in the literature, are used to assess our proposed Neural-Network-Augmented Tobit Kalman Filter (NN-TKF). Simulation results demonstrate that the NN-TKF outperforms the standard TKF and other estimation methods.

title: Anatomy-Guided Spatiotemporal Affinity Learning for Unsupervised Domain Adaptation in Echocardiography Segmentation
authors: Xinyan Fang, Xinghua Ma, Yang Liu, Jiahao Zhang, Xiaodi Wang, Xiangyu Li, Alfred Wei Chieh Kow, Gongning Luo, Guohua Wang
publish: IEEE Journal of Biomedical and Health Informatics
abstract: Unsupervised domain adaptation for left ventricle segmentation in echocardiography is critical to enhance clinical applicability across different devices and institutions. However, it remains highly challenging due to anatomical context shift and inherent noise interference. To comprehensively tackle these challenges, we introduce an anatomy-guided spatio-temporal affinity framework from two complementary perspectives: (1) at the image level, an Anatomical Context Alignment (ACA) module uses LV-dominant cropping and four-chamber-complete (4C-complete) mirroring to adapt the source domain toward the target domain's anatomical context; (2) at the feature level, an Anatomical Affinity Refinement (AAR) module models pixel-pair anatomical affinities in spatial and temporal dimensions to enforce fine-grained anatomical consistency and suppress noise interference. Specifically, the Spatial Affinity Regularization (SAR) module encourages multi-scale feature consistency within anatomical regions, while the Temporal Affinity Refinement (TAR) module refines pseudo-label leveraging inter-frame anatomical affinities, enhancing temporal consistency without explicit motion estimation or cardiac cycle annotations. Experiments on three public datasets (CAMUS, EchoNet-Dynamic, and CardiacUDA) reveal that anatomical context shift is a key factor in domain discrepancy, and demonstrate that our method effectively alleviates this issue while outperforming previous state-of-the-art UDA methods. Code is released at <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://anonymous.4open.science/r/public4jbhi-D4C7/</uri>.

title: Systematic Gain-Tuned Adaptive Backstepping Control for Electric Cycloidal Propellers With Uncertainties and Disturbances
authors: Subhashis Nandy, Manish Patel, Yoonsoo Kim
publish: IEEE Transactions on Control Systems Technology
abstract: This work addresses the emerging challenge of robust control in electric cycloidal propulsion systems, a technology with significant potential for maritime sustainability. Current control strategies often have limited effectiveness in accurately capturing the nonlinear, coupled electromechanical dynamics and in handling disturbances and uncertainties related to independent blade actuation, which also involves multiblade synchronization challenges. To overcome these limitations, a robust adaptive backstepping (AB) controller is developed to regulate both propeller disk speed and six-blade pitch angles synchronously, explicitly accounting for coupled electromechanical dynamics, external disturbances, and parameter uncertainties. The approach computes actuator torques directly for independent electric motors driving the propeller disk and blades, achieving close alignment with experimental power–speed, hydrodynamic, and kinematic phase data. Compared to the existing nonlinear control method, it demonstrates 52%–86% higher tracking accuracy, 49.5%–59.7% reduced control fluctuations, and 37.5%–66.7% faster convergence, validated through extensive simulations. Unlike prior cycloidal propulsion studies that overlook full electromechanical coupling, do not address a systematic methodology for controller gain tuning, and disregard disturbances and uncertainties, this work presents the first unified, torque-level control framework for multiblade electric cycloidal propellers.

title: Decision letter for "Descriptor: LYNRED Mobility Dataset Multimodal Detection Subset (LYNRED-MDS)"
authors: N/A

title: Both Sides Shifted Fountain Code
authors: Yoshihiro Murayama, Takayuki Nozaki
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Consumer Technology (ICCT-Pacific)
abstract: This paper proposes a fountain coding system by improving the zigzag decodable fountain (ZDF) codes. The main idea of the proposed fountain code is applying shift operation to precoding. Simulation results show that the proposed fountain coding system reduces the decoding erasure rates in both the waterfall region and the error floor region, at only a slight cost in decoding time, compared to ZDF codes.

title: Dynamic Routing for Constructing LLMs Using Geographically Distributed User Resources
authors: Daiki Wada, Shinji Sugawara
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Consumer Technology (ICCT-Pacific)
abstract: In recent years, large language models (LLMs) such as GPT-4 and the LLaMA family have achieved near human-level performance, but their rapidly growing computational requirements and the dense deployment of GPUs in centralized data centers (DCs) have led to serious concerns about localized power demand and strain on power infrastructure. To reduce reliance on centralized DCs, we propose a new LLM inference platform that exploits user-owned compute resources distributed over wide-area networks. In this platform, model nodes host subsets of LLM layers, while strategy nodes continuously monitor node performance, latency, and bandwidth to dynamically select and reconfigure end-to-end inference paths in response to real-time WAN conditions and node churn. We present the design principles and routing strategy of this architecture and evaluate its effectiveness using token generation rate as the primary performance metric.

title: Pilot Design and LMMSE Channel Estimation for OTFS: Simulation Study and Experiment
authors: Tianhan Zhong, Hidekazu Murata, Satoshi Suyama, Huiling Jiang
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Consumer Technology (ICCT-Pacific)
abstract: Orthogonal time frequency space (OTFS) modulation provides a robust signal representation for high-Doppler wireless channels, yet practical channel estimation in the delayDoppler (DD) domain remains challenging. This paper investigates a pilot-aided OTFS framework employing a structured pilot pattern in which pilot and data symbols alternate across delay and Doppler. A Zadoff-Chu sequence is applied along the Doppler dimension, and channel estimation is performed using a linear minimum mean-squared error (LMMSE) approach that exploits DD domain correlation. Simulation results using a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$16 \times 16$</tex> OTFS grid are presented. In addition, preliminary in-lab experiments using software defined radio (SDR) are conducted to provide initial insights into the practical behavior of the proposed scheme under hardware constraints.

title: Prior-Guided Dual-Branch Network with $S^{2}$-Attention for Hyperspectral Image Super-Resolution
authors: Tzu-Ying Lin, Pei-Jun Lee
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Consumer Technology (ICCT-Pacific)
abstract: The fusion of low-resolution hyperspectral images and high-resolution multispectral image is a crucial approach to derive high-resolution hyperspectral images. This paper proposes a prior-guided dual-branch network that integrates <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\boldsymbol{S}^{\mathbf{2}}$</tex>-attention Transformer to model both local and global dependencies while maintaining spectral consistency. SpatialSpectral Total Variation (SSTV) loss is introduced to improve reconstruction fidelity. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed model achieves a 1.805 dB gain in PSNR and a 0.295 reduction in SAM, confirming its superior spatial detail reconstruction and spectral consistency.

title: A 0.5V 10-Bit SAR ADC with Adaptive-Reset Switching and VCO-Based Comparator
authors: Ko-Chi Kuo, Ching-Lun Wu
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Consumer Technology (ICCT-Pacific)
abstract: This study employs an adaptive-reset switching scheme to mitigate the power consumption and area issues in the CDAC residing inside the SAR ADC. This switching scheme reduces the average switching energy of the CDAC by 90% compared to the traditional scheme. Furthermore, the switching scheme maintains a constant common-mode voltage during the SAR conversion, thereby relaxing the design requirements of the comparator. Additionally, this design utilizes a time-domain comparator based on a VCO structure to adaptively adjust its noise performance according to the input voltage difference, thereby reducing the power consumption required by the comparator. By combining the advantages above, the overall energy efficiency of the SAR ADC can be improved. This work implements the TSMC 40 nm 1 P9M CMOS process, with a core area of 0.044 mm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>. The supply voltage is 0.5 V, and the sampling rate is 5 MHz. When tested with input signals approaching the Nyquist frequency, the ADC achieves a DNL error of + 0.251/− 0.249 LSB, an INL error of + 0.275/0.157 LSB, SNDR of 60.878 dB, SFDR of 72.9 dB, and an ENOB of 9.82 bits. The average power consumption is <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$19.5 \mu \mathrm{W}$</tex>, resulting in a FoM of 4.31 fJ/conv-step.

title: FAIR - Federated Agreement for Independent Resolution: A Cross-Chain Decision-Making Protocol
authors: Macià Mut-Puigserver, M. Magdalena Payeras-Capellà, Rosa Pericàs-Gornals, Jaume Ramis-Bibiloni
publish: 2025 35th International Conference on Computer Theory and Applications (ICCTA)
abstract: This paper introduces FAIR (Federated Agreement for Independent Resolution), a cross-chain protocol designed to enhance scalability, transparency, and auditability in decentralized decision-making processes. Unlike traditional blockchainbased voting systems that rely on a single global chain, FAIR leverages a federated model in which local decisions are conducted on independent blockchains and subsequently aggregated into a global result through a secure cross-chain mechanism. The protocol employs Soulbound Tokens (SBTs) and Rejectable SBTs (RejSBTs) to ensure verifiability, immutability, and explicit acceptance of results, thereby strengthening both the integrity and security of cross-chain operations. We formalize the protocol, provide smart contract implementations, and illustrate its applicability in diverse contexts such as multinational corporations, district-based elections, and decentralized autonomous organizations. FAIR demonstrates that a federated and tokenized approach to decision-making enables efficient large-scale participation while maintaining strong guarantees of trust, transparency, and robustness.

title: MODWT-Based Wavelet Neuro-Fuzzy MPPT for Photovoltaic Systems with Comparative Analysis to Machine Learning Controllers
authors: Tariq Kamal, Syed Zulqadar Hassan
publish: 2026 International Conference on Data Science, Machine Learning, and Intelligence (DataSciMI)
abstract: Accurate maximum power point tracking (MPPT) is critical for photovoltaic (PV) systems because the optimal operating point shifts continuously with irradiance and cell temperature. This paper studies a predictive MPPT strategy that combines (i) shift-invariant, multi-resolution features extracted from irradiance/temperature time series using the maximal overlap discrete wavelet transform (MODWT) with a Daubechies-4 (db4) mother wavelet and a three-level decomposition and (ii) a first-order Takagi-Sugeno neuro-fuzzy model that maps those features to a reference MPP voltage. The proposed wavelet neuro-fuzzy (WNF) controller is benchmarked against four common regression baselines (decision tree, random forest, support vector regression, and a multilayer perceptron ANN) using 48 h of per-minute weather data for Lahore, Pakistan (22--23 June 2025). In a 100 kW PV boost-converter simulation, the WNF controller achieves 99.988% average tracking efficiency and yields 1533.7 kWh, which is approximately 2% higher energy than the compared learning baselines under the same test conditions. We also discuss computational implications of MODWT-based feature extraction for real-time embedded MPPT.

title: Depression Prediction among Students using Machine Learning and Voting Ensembles: An Explainable AI Approach
authors: Amina Fatima, Muhammad Shoaib, Muhammad Waseem
publish: 2026 International Conference on Data Science, Machine Learning, and Intelligence (DataSciMI)
abstract: In today’s era, depression is a common yet often unnoticed mental health issue that frequently affects students, impacting their personal lives as well as their studies. Its early prediction, along with identifying the main contributing factors through the use of explainable AI (XAI), can help prevent it and reduce its long-term damages. For this purpose, our study employed machine learning techniques. We conducted the study on a Kaggle dataset consisting of 27,901 records. We trained state-of-the-art tree-based bagging and boosting algorithms, including XGBoost (XGB), CatBoost (CB), Light-GBM (LGBM), Histogram-Based Gradient Boosting (HGB), and Random Forest (RF), and also ensembled them using soft voting and hard voting techniques. Among these, CB achieved the highest AUC-ROC score of 0.9210. This study utilizes a large dataset and contributes significantly to both the academic and medical fields. Furthermore, the use of XAI techniques provides practical steps that academic authorities can take to support students more effectively.

title: Design and Insulation Coordination of 1200 kV UHVAC Substations in India for Bulk Power Transfer and Reliable Renewable Integration
authors: Satyam Jaiswal, Anish Sachdeva
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: India’s rapidly growing power demand, integration of multiple renewable generators and the need for efficient bulk power transmission have driven the development of Ultra High Voltage Alternating Current (UHVAC) technology. UHVAC substations enable large-scale power transfer over long distances with reduced losses and improved grid stability, making them essential for integrating regional grids and supporting renewable energy corridors. However, designing and maintaining such substations present unique challenges, particularly in insulation coordination, which must address lightning and switching overvoltage’s, pollution severity, and diverse climatic conditions in India. The complexity of equipment, stringent safety requirements, SF₆ gas handling, and limited outage windows further complicate maintenance. Additionally, optimizing cost versus reliability trade-offs and ensuring compliance with IEC/IS standards remain critical. This paper explores the design principles and insulation coordination strategies for 1200 kV UHVAC substations in India and identifies research gaps in areas such as surge protection optimization, environmental stress modelling, and digital monitoring. The findings aim to support the development of robust, economically viable UHVAC infrastructure for India’s future power system.

title: Modeling and Simulation of a 3×3 Memristor Crossbar Architecture Using the Yakopcic Memristor Model
authors: Suraj Prajapati, Bodile Roshan Mukindrao, Rohit Singh
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: This work presents the design and simulation of a 3×3 memristor crossbar array based on the Yakopcic threshold memristor model for neuromorphic and memory applications. Each memristive device is modeled using a nonlinear, voltage-controlled state equation capable of reproducing nanosecond-scale switching and realistic current-voltage hysteresis, enabling accurate representation of multiple analog resistance states in circuit-level SPICE simulations. The 3×3 crossbar is exercised with write and read pulse schemes to program and sense synaptic weights, and results show stable convergence without the numerical issues typical of earlier HP-type models when multiple devices switch concurrently. These simulations illustrate how a compact Yakopcic-based array can serve as a building block for larger memristor neuromorphic cores, supporting energy and delay estimation for future high-throughput, low-power computing systems.

title: Design and Control of an AI-Enhanced Zero-Fastener Animatronic Eye Mechanism for Humanoid Robotic Faces
authors: Mohd. Umar Farhan, Harsh Kumar, Mohit Kumar, Ragini Sharma
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: This paper describes the development of an animatronic eye system for humanoid robotics using a fully 3D-printed snap-fit mechanism that requires no traditional fasteners. The design integrates mechanical innovation with computer vision capabilities to replicate human eye movements including saccades, smooth pursuit, and periodic blinks. By eliminating screws and adhesives through snap-fit joints, we reduced assembly time from approximately 45 minutes to under 10 minutes while maintaining structural stability. The system uses four micro servo motors and incorporates Haar cascade face detection with Kalman filtering for tracking. Our testing shows response latency below 50 ms and repeatability within ±2°. Results from building and testing ten prototype units demonstrate practical assembly time improvements, reasonable tracking performance in laboratory conditions, and mechanical durability through over 500 movement cycles. This approach enables accessible expressive robotics for educational and demonstration applications at a total component cost of INR 1700.

title: Emotion Recognition System for Emotion Analysis and Decision Making of Students during Class Sessions: Integration of Real Time Process and Fuzzy Sets in Educational Psychology
authors: Ajay Narayan Shukla, Ravinder Singh Kuntal, Umrbek Karimov, Maksudov Ulugbek, Saydaliyev Rustam, Uktam Tursunov
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: Emotions are important for the cognitive engagement, learning efficiency, and decision making of students in the classroom session. Accurate and real-time recognition of the student's emotional states can assist adaptive teaching strategies and improve the learning outcomes. This paper proposes to develop a real-time emotion recognition system that combines deep learning-based facial expression analysis with the fuzzy set theory to model the emotion uncertainty and facilitate the pedagogically driven decision making. Facial features are extracted with the convolutional neural network and fuzzy membership functions and fuzzy rules based on educational psychology are used to model overlapping emotional states and make adaptive choices with respect to the instruction. Experimental evaluation shows that the proposed framework can obtain better accuracy in recognising emotions, better decision-making performance and lower response time compared with traditional machine learning, standalone deep learning, and rule-based methods. The results confirm the usefulness of combining real-time processing and fuzzy logic in educational systems that will become emotion aware and that will allow responsive, interpretable, and student-centered classrooms.

title: Dual-Band Circularly Polarized Novel Fractal Dielectric Resonator Antenna for Microwave Radio Systems
authors: Ch. Raghavendra, Penumetcha Ananya Shivakami, Ayodhya Sumanth, Balamarthi Jahnavi
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: This paper describes the development of a dual-band circularly polarized dielectric resonator antenna (DRA) intended for microwave radio applications. The antenna is based on a Spidron-inspired fractal shape and is excited using a probe feed. To separate the two circularly polarized bands and to introduce a band-notch effect, a T-shaped slot is carved in the ground plane to create a band-notch effect and divide the two circularly polarized bands. The structure supports several resonant modes, including quasi-TM<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">11δ</inf>, TM<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">21δ</inf>, and TE<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">12δ</inf>, which together improve the overall radiation behaviour. A diode connected across the slot provides the ability to shift or reconfigure the circularly polarized bands when required. According to measurements, the antenna has an axial ratio bandwidth of about 24% and an impedance bandwidth of about 57.6%. . Owing to its compact form and reconfigurable operation, the proposed design is a suitable candidate for microwave systems that need flexibility in operating frequency.

title: FPGA Design of an SNR-Based Adaptive Modulation Selector for 5G/6G Communications
authors: Boda Naveen Kumar, Rohit Singh
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: Adaptive modulation in mobile communication systems changes the modulation order in real time based on the current conditions of the channel. This paper describes an FPGA-based implementation of a lightweight, low-latency SNR-driven adaptive modulation selector that works with QPSK, 16-QAM, 64-QAM, and 256-QAM for physical-layer uses in 5G and 6G. A threshold-based choice mechanism, based on MATLAB/Python BER–SNR simulations, makes sure that switching between modulation schemes is both reliable and quick. The suggested controller is described as a deterministic Finite State Machine (FSM) architecture written in Verilog HDL and implemented on the Xilinx Artix-7 (Basys-3) FPGA. The synthesis results show that the area usage is less than 0.01% LUT usage, the timing closure is stable at about 200 MHz, and the overall power usage is less than 2W. Hardware validation closely matches how the simulation works, showing that modulation transitions are exact and don’t have any glitches. The suggested design has better latency, area efficiency, and power consumption than current FPGA/DSP-based adaptive modulation systems. This design works well for real-time link adaption in 5G NR, Wi-Fi 6, SDR systems, and new 6G platforms.

title: Data Balance Clustering Method for Heterogeneous Underwater Acoustic Sensor Networks Based on Improved Artificial Bee Colony Algorithm
authors: Zhongwen Wang, Yougan Chen, Yihao Zhao, Xuchen Wang, Lei Wan, Yi Tao
publish: Proceedings of the 19th International Conference on Underwater Networks and Systems

title: Cassette: Case-to-Case Structural Distillation for Efficient Legal Case Retrieval

title: Observer-based Robust Cluster Space Control for Omnidirectional Robot Formations
authors: Mauro Baquero-Suárez, Ignacio Mas, Juan I. Giribet
abstract: This article describes an effective control approach for Wheeled Omnidirectional Mobile Robot Formations, capable of compensating disturbances generated by unknown forces acting on the vehicles. The multi-robot formations are modeled as uncertain multi-variable dynamical systems subject to undesired traction dynamics, measurement noise, and unknown attraction or repulsion forces between robots, all of which significantly affect their motion. The proposed approach employs an Observer-Based Sliding Mode Control scheme derived from a dynamic model defined within a Cluster Space framework, which enables the decoupling of inter-robot disturbances along state variables conveniently selected. These decoupled disturbances are actively rejected through estimates provided by a reduced set of High-Gain Observers, which are then incorporated into the feedback control law. The performance of the proposed Cluster Space-based controller is evaluated against an Observer-based Active Disturbance Rejection Control formulated in the conventional pose space of robots, which requires a larger number of disturbance observers. Closed-loop behavior is assessed first through high-fidelity simulations of 2-robot and 3-robot multi-body formations, and then through real experiments with a 2-robot prototype. The results confirm that both controllers achieve successful trajectory tracking and disturbance rejection, with the proposed Cluster Space approach offering notable simplification due to the reduced number of observers, while maintaining comparable performance with a lower computational burden in the estimation process.
