title: A Shared-Geometry Neural Radiance Field for Joint Optical and ISAR 3-D Reconstruction of Satellites
authors: Xindi Yu, Ling Wang, Miguel Heredia Conde, Daiyin Zhu
abstract: With the growing complexity of space missions, achieving accurate three-dimensional (3D) reconstruction of space targets has become increasingly important for supporting perception, recognition, and on-orbit servicing. Conventional optical- or radar-based methods are limited by their sensing mechanisms, often producing incomplete geometries that lack cross-modal consistency and cannot generate geometry-consistent two-dimensional (2D) images from novel viewpoints. To address these limitations, a Shared-Geometry Neural Radiance Field (SG NeRF) is proposed in this paper, which is a unified radar–optics joint 3D reconstruction framework that integrates inverse synthetic aperture radar (ISAR) and optical images. The core idea is to represent the target as a continuous volumetric density field parameterized by an implicit neural network, jointly constrained by physics-informed image formation models of both modalities. A dual-branch architecture is designed, where a shared backbone encodes the geometry and two modality-specific rendering heads synthesize optical and radar images. A modality-aware training strategy with non-uniform sampling is further adopted to achieve robust cross-modal supervision and closed-loop training. Simulation experiments demonstrate that SG-NeRF achieves high-fidelity reconstruction and novel-view synthesis under random and limited viewpoints and varying SNRs, which outperforms single-branch networks without fusion by effectively leveraging complementary information from optical and radar sensors.

title: CITRAS: Covariate-Informed Transformer for Time Series Forecasting
authors: Yosuke Yamaguchi, Issei Suemitsu, Wenpeng Wei
abstract: In time series forecasting, covariates represent external factors that influence target variables. Some covariates are observable only in the past (observed covariates, such as recorded weather data), while others are known in advance (known covariates, such as calendar events or discount schedules). Although covariates have the potential to enhance forecasting performance, most deep learning–based forecasting models struggle to address the length discrepancy between variables caused by the future portion of known covariates and fail to leverage them flexibly. Moreover, capturing dependencies between target variables and covariates is non-trivial, as models must accurately reflect the local impact of covariates while simultaneously modeling global cross-variate dependencies. To address these challenges, we propose CITRAS, a decoder-only Transformer that flexibly integrates multiple target variables, observed covariates, and known covariates. While preserving strong autoregressive modeling capabilities, CITRAS introduces two novel mechanisms within patch-wise cross-variate attention: Key–Value (KV) Shift and Attention Score Smoothing. KV Shift seamlessly incorporates the future portion of known covariates into the forecasting process by aligning them with target variables based on their concurrent dependencies. Attention Score Smoothing refines locally accurate patch-wise cross-variate dependencies into global variate-level dependencies by smoothing the historical attention scores. Experimentally, CITRAS demonstrates strong performance across a wide range of real-world datasets in both covariate-informed and multivariate settings, showcasing its versatile ability to leverage cross-variate and cross-time dependencies for improved forecasting accuracy.

title: Data-Driven Digital Twin for Real-Time State Prediction and Stability Assessment in Two-Stage Single-Phase Grid-Connected Photovoltaic Inverters
authors: Arun Kumar, Nishant Kumar
publish: IEEE Journal of Emerging and Selected Topics in Industrial Electronics
abstract: This paper presents a data-driven Digital Twin (DD-DT) framework for dynamic state prediction and stability assessment of two-stage single-phase grid-connected photovoltaic (PV) inverters. The proposed approach integrates an Attention-Guided Real-Time Stabilized Bidirectional Long Short-Term Memory (AG-RTS-BiLSTM) network to predict the unknown states and capture nonlinear inverter dynamics under varying operating scenarios. Multiple system parameters are predicted using sensor-driven datasets generated from normal, irradiance-varying, and grid-disturbance conditions. The framework not only enables accurate multi-parameter forecasting but also provides a stability indicator by leveraging correlation-based statistical analysis. Experimental validation using real-time hardware setup demonstrates that the developed DD-DT achieves high accuracy with mean absolute error (MAE) ≈ 1.62%, mean absolute percentage error (MAPE) ≈ 1.37% and correlation coefficient (ρ) ≈ 0.99, ensuring high fidelity in state tracking. The results confirm the capability of the AG-RTS-BiLSTM-based DD-DT to enhance operational reliability, and facilitate stability-oriented decision-making in PV-integrated industrial power electronic systems.

title: Always On Trust for Remote Cardiac Monitoring: Quantum Safe Authentication With One Time PUFs and Edge Anomaly Screening
authors: Shafiq Ahmed, Mohammad S. Obaidat, Khalid Mahmood, Mohammad Hossein Anisi, Ayesha Iqbal, Khawaja Qasim Maqbool
abstract: Remote cardiac monitoring is moving from occasional clinic visits to continuous observation at home. In a typical Intelligent Internet of Medical Things workflow a wearable ECG sensor sends readings through an edge gateway to a clinical platform for storage and review. That convenience also widens the attack surface. If a cloned device is admitted or telemetry is altered clinicians may rely on false signals and patient privacy may be exposed. A longer horizon problem is also emerging. Adversaries can store encrypted medical traffic today and target it later with stronger quantum tools. At the same time many lightweight schemes still rely on static Physical Unclonable Functions whose challenge response behavior may be learned from repeated observations. We address both concerns with a compact authentication fabric for real time cardiac monitoring. The design uses a reconfigurable One Time Physical Unclonable Function so each successful session refreshes response behavior and weakens modeling attacks. It also combines ML-KEM-768 for key establishment with ML-DSA-65 for device authentication. An Isolation Forest ensemble at the gateway then screens behavioral drift that may indicate misuse or compromise. Our prototype completes authentication in 14.7 ms with about 4.6 kB per session and reaches 94.5% precision with a 2.3% false positive rate.

title: GC-DETR: Geometry–Color Decoupled Transformer for 3D Indoor Object Detection in Smart Consumer Devices
authors: Yingjian Fang, Qingxiao Wu, Sicong Li, Haitao Xu, Kaice Jiang, Jianxin Cai, Jianyu Wang
abstract: 3D indoor object detection from point clouds is a core enabling technology for perceptive consumer systems such as service robots, smart home devices, and AR/VR terminals. Although geometric (XYZ) and color (RGB) information are both crucial for reliable on-device scene understanding, most existing approaches simply fuse them in a unified feature space, ignoring their heterogeneous characteristics and causing feature interference. We propose GC-DETR, a Geometry–Color Decoupled and Fused DEtection TRansformer designed to address modal interference in complex indoor environments. GC-DETR follows a decouple–then–fuse paradigm with three key components: a Geometry–Color Disentanglement Module (GCDM), a Dual-branch Feature Pyramid Network (DB-FPN), and a Geometry-guided Cross-Attention Module (G-CAM). Extensive experiments on ScanNetV2 show that GC-DETR achieves state-of-the-art performance, with 78.1% AP@0.25 and 65.1% AP@0.5. Cross-dataset evaluation on SUN RGB-D further demonstrates strong generalization and robustness. Additional ablation and edge-device deployment analyses further support the effectiveness and practical relevance of the proposed design. These results indicate that GC-DETR provides a robust and accurate solution for intelligent perception in next-generation consumer applications.

title: Litz-Wire Coil Brings Energy Sustainability in Induction Cap Sealing Process
authors: Arun Kumar Paul, B. Sai Ram
publish: IEEE Journal on Wireless Power Technologies
abstract: In packaging industry, cap sealers using inductive power transfer are dominantly used to create tamper-proof sealing of plastic/glass containers. These moderately rated (≤ 3 kW) continuous-run converters consume large energy cumulatively. Here, energy is handled in several stages – at source, power convertor, coil-head for energy transfer, and its utilization in foil. When non-polluting renewable energy is increasingly playing dominant role as source, optimal energy efficiency in remaining stages will attain energy sustainability of this process. They further need to meet the emerging demands e.g., productivity, widening sealing range, new applications, etc. Scope of improving energy efficiency of a matured soft-switched converter is marginal. This article proposes that, for energy sustainability, flexible litz-wire based coil plays vital roles to achieve high-speed wide range sealing with superior efficiency of utilization of energy in foils. The coil can be made equipped to improve the energy distribution on a moving foil surface electromagnetically, which has been validated by FEM simulations. Further, to resolve problems of sealing continuously fed large-mouth containers, this article further claims that the coil can inherently avoid converter saturation. The same is demonstrated through unconstrained high-speed sealing of containers using wide-range foils. It is also demonstrated that the coil can boost the system efficiency to 92.2% and the efficiency of energy utilization by 3.4-fold, that further boosts productivity. Energy sustainability criteria are also verified in terms of various performance metrics.

title: Adaptive Finite-Blocklength Optimization for the Communication-Sensing Trade-off in Network-Assisted Full-Duplex Cell-Free ISAC Systems with URLLC Users
authors: Xi Lin, Xiaoyu Sun, Jiamin Li, Pengcheng Zhu, Dongming Wang, Feng Shu, Xiaohu You
abstract: Future industrial 6G applications will impose stringent requirements on ultra-reliable low-latency communications (URLLC) and precision sensing enabled by integrated sensing and communication (ISAC) techniques, motivating a comprehensive study of the communication–sensing (C–S) trade-off under finite blocklength transmission. Therefore, this paper investigates the fundamental C–S performance limits in a network-assisted full-duplex (NAFD) cell-free ISAC system with URLLC users. To address the theoretical gap in the finite blocklength regime, closed-form upper-bound expressions are derived for key communication metrics, including transmission delay and decoding error probability (DEP), and a Cramér–Rao lower bound (CRLB) framework is established for multi-static sensing. Furthermore, to explicitly characterize the C–S trade-off, the ISAC network availability is evaluated and the Pareto frontier is obtained using the non-dominated sorting genetic algorithm II (NSGA-II). The results demonstrate that increasing the blocklength improves sensing accuracy at the cost of higher communication latency. To address this inherent conflict, this study proposes a DDQN-based finite blocklength optimization (FBLO) algorithm that performs blocklength selection under URLLC and sensing quality-of-service requirements to achieve a favorable C–S trade-off. Simulation results validate that the proposed algorithm achieves near-optimal performance with reduced computational overhead.

title: Pragmatic Communication in Multi-Agent Collaborative Perception_supp1-3680062.pdf
authors: Siheng Chen
abstract: Collaborative perception allows each agent to enhance its perceptual abilities by exchanging messages with others. It inherently results in a trade-off between perception ability and communication costs. Previous works transmit complete full-frame high-dimensional feature maps among agents, resulting in substantial communication costs. To promote communication efficiency, we propose only transmitting the information needed for the collaborator's downstream task. This pragmatic communication strategy focuses on three key aspects: i) pragmatic message selection, which selects task-critical parts from the complete data, resulting in spatially and temporally sparse feature vectors; ii) pragmatic message representation, which achieves pragmatic approximation of high-dimensional feature vectors with a task-adaptive dictionary, enabling communicating with integer indices; iii) pragmatic collaborator selection, which identifies beneficial collaborators, pruning unnecessary communication links. Following this strategy, we first formulate a mathematical optimization framework for the perception-communication trade-off and then propose <monospace xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">PragComm</monospace>, a multi-agent collaborative perception system with two key components: i) single-agent detection and tracking and ii) pragmatic collaboration. The proposed <monospace xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">PragComm</monospace> promotes pragmatic communication and adapts to a wide range of communication conditions. We evaluate <monospace xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">PragComm</monospace> for both collaborative 3D object detection and tracking tasks in both real-world, V2V4Real, and simulation datasets, OPV2V and V2X-SIM2.0. <monospace xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">PragComm</monospace> consistently outperforms previous methods with more than 32.7K× lower communication volume on OPV2V. Code is available at github.com/PhyllisH/PragComm.

title: Retrieval-Augmented LLMs for Security Incident Analysis
authors: Xavier Cadet, Aditya Vikram Singh, Harsh Mamania, Edward Koh, Alex Fitts, Dirk Van Bruggen, Simona Boboila, Peter Chin, Alina Oprea

title: Computational Intelligence in Knee Osteoarthritis Detection: A Systematic Review of AI and Machine Learning Approaches
authors: Amit Sharma, Vijay Kumar Garg
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: Knee Osteoarthritis (KOA) is a progressive degeneration of the knee that has an important influence on mobility and quality of life, particularly in the elderly. To be able to intervene in good time, it is very important to diagnose KOA as soon as possible. However, the sensitivity of the traditional diagnostic techniques is generally low at the beginning of the disease. Computational Intelligence (CI) and Artificial Intelligence (AI) represent innovative possibilities for enhancing the sensitivity of KOA diagnosis in its initial stages. This article reviews the most recent CI developments, compares them based on methodology and performance, and highlights the gap between research and the future direction of these methodologies for diagnosing KOA. The results demonstrate both the capability and the present limits of using AI-based diagnostics for KOA.

title: Energy-Efficient Edge Scheduling and Resource Allocation for NOMA-Based Hierarchical Federated Learning
authors: Yijing Ren, Changxiang Wu, Daniel K. C. So, Zhiguo Ding
abstract: Hierarchical Federated Learning (HFL) has emerged as a promising approach for scalable and communication-efficient model training in wireless networks. However, achieving energy efficiency while ensuring convergence remains challenging due to limited bandwidth resource and strict latency constraints. This paper addresses energy-efficient HFL under both statistical and system heterogeneity, aiming to minimize long-term energy consumption through adaptive and unbiased edge scheduling and resource allocation in dynamic environments. A convergence analysis is first conducted without relying on a convex assumption, explicitly characterizing the influences of the number of scheduled edges and scheduling probabilities. An iterative algorithm is then proposed to jointly optimize these variables: the scheduling probabilities are solved by using a Barrier Method (BM) with an Infeasible-Start Newton Method (ISNM), while the number of scheduled edges is derived in a closed form. To further enhance communication efficiency, Non-Orthogonal Multiple Access (NOMA) is employed at the user–edge layer. Then, a joint optimization of inter-edge bandwidth allocation and intra-edge local resource allocation is developed to balance computation and communication overhead. Extensive simulations demonstrate that the proposed framework significantly outperforms existing benchmarks in terms of energy consumption under Non-Independent and Identically Distributed (Non-IID) data and dynamic wireless environments.

title: Optimized Deployment of AI Models on Cloud Platforms: Strategies, Challenges, and Future Direction
authors: Misbah Maqbool, Uzma Abdul Rehman, Naeem A. Nawaz, Kashif Ishaq
publish: 2026 International Conference on Data Science, Machine Learning, and Intelligence (DataSciMI)
abstract: Nowadays, big AI networks are expanding exceptionally rapidly, and that implies we need more-scalable methods to enforce them, ones that do not cost millions of dollars to deploy, and which can be scaled up to new levels without problems. Cloud platforms appear to be the source of training and running such large billion parameter models, as it is flexible, handled services, and can be accessed anywhere in the world. Nevertheless, in the case of something such as Falcon 180B, a giant language model, there are all these issues that arise. Hardware may differ between environments, latency is quite variable, prices are not very predictable, there are quotas on what you may do, and you may be stuck with a single supplier. I compared Falcon 180B to three primary clouds, AWS, Azure which is developed by Microsoft and GCP which is developed by Google. The inference workloads were managed and repeatable and we tested such attributes as how many seconds it takes to reply or how many parallel requests or tokens it can handle, cold startup times, and the cost of a million requests or tokens. The findings indicated that there are some apparent disparities in the way each platform is managed with this. AWS is quite easy to deploy and this is good when you are in a hurry to get it going. Azure is one that is bound to enterprise stuff, such as with a large company setup. Then, GCP, it appears to be more cost-efficient and performance-wise, as their TPUs make it faster. I know that is not necessarily always the case but it was so in these tests. We went into the reasons why these differences occur, and examined the architecture beneath each of those differences. As an example, their manner of dealing with acceleration or allocation of resources will have a bearing. Others such as fixing operation headache e.g. working with quotas or latency spikes may involve a switch of providers or workloads. That section is a little sloppy to elaborate on. All in all, this provides you with a pattern to compare AI task clouds with. It assists in making your decision of infrastructure though it may require your individual requirement. There is a list of tradeoffs, which are worthy of mention, but I believe the benchmarking strategy is good to begin with.

title: Pinching-Antenna System Design With LoS Blockage: Does In-Waveguide Attenuation Matter?
authors: Yanqing Xu, Zhiguo Ding, Octavia A. Dobre, Tsung-Hui Chang
abstract: In the literature of pinching-antenna systems, in-waveguide attenuation is often neglected to simplify system design and enable more tractable analysis. However, its effect on overall system performance has received limited attention in the existing literature. While a recent study has shown that, in line-of-sight (LoS)-dominated environments, the data rate loss incurred by omitting in-waveguide attenuation is negligible when the communication area is not excessively large, its effect under more general conditions remains unclear. This work extends the analysis to more realistic scenarios involving arbitrary levels of LoS blockage. We begin by examining a single-user case and derive an explicit expression for the average data rate loss caused by neglecting in-waveguide attenuation. The results demonstrate that, even for large service areas, the rate loss remains negligible under typical LoS blockage conditions. We then consider a more general multi-user scenario, where multiple pinching antennas, each deployed on a separate waveguide, jointly serve multiple users. The objective is to maximize the average sum rate by jointly optimize antenna positions and transmit beamformers to maximize the average sum rate under probabilistic LoS blockage. To solve the resulting stochastic and nonconvex optimization problem, we propose a dynamic sample average approximation (SAA) algorithm. At each iteration, this method replaces the expected objective with an empirical average computed from dynamically regenerated random channel realizations, ensuring that the optimization accurately reflects the current antenna configuration. Extensive simulation results are provided to the proposed algorithm and demonstrate the substantial performance gains of pinching-antenna systems, particularly in environments with significant LoS blockage.

title: MC-SMOTE: A Multi-Cluster Oversampling Technique for Handling Class Imbalance in Software Fault Prediction
authors: Ashu Mehta, Baljeet Singh
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: Software quality assurance critically depends on software fault prediction, which is sometimes prevented by highly skewed data sets in which defective modules constitute a minor fraction of the total. The sampling methods explored in this paper are Random Undersampling (RUS), Random Oversampling (ROS), SMOTE, and a suggested Multi-Cluster SMOTE (MC-SMOTE) on six benchmark NASA PROMISE datasets with six underlying classifiers RF, NB, KNN, SVM, LR, DT. The experimental analysis of various performance indicators proves that MC-SMOTE is always top of the current methods due to the high quality of produced synthetic samples and the minimization of bias in the majority category. The results prove MC-SMOTE as a powerful tool to work with the issue of class imbalance and achieve better accuracy in detecting defects and could be extended to deep learning and real-time.

title: Reinforcement Learning for Autonomous Cyber Defense in Cloud Environments
authors: Noman Mazher, Naser Naser, Nahed Bahman, Zillay Huma, Hadia Azmat, Atika Nishat, Ahmad Raza
publish: 2026 International Conference on Data Science, Machine Learning, and Intelligence (DataSciMI)
abstract: Cloud computing has become the backbone of today’s modern digital infrastructure, but its distributed and dynamic nature makes it more vulnerable to various cyberattacks such as Distributed Denial of Service (DDoS) attacks, insider threats, and advanced persistent attacks. Traditional security approaches, which rely on predefined signatures and then static rules, are proving to be inadequate against rapidly changing and modern adversaries. To address these issues, Reinforcement Learning (RL) has emerged as an effective approach that enables automated cyber defense in cloud environments. RL agents are capable of continuously learning from interactions with the environment and adapting strategies in real time, as well as improving and fine-tuning defense policies against new and unseen threats. This paper studies the applications of RL-based frameworks that support intrusion detection, attack prevention, and proactive defense in cloud systems. It proposes an architecture where RL agents work on a multi-layered defense strategy, balancing accuracy, responsiveness, latency, and resource utilization. Results from creative simulations show that RL agents provide greater coherence and additional flexibility compared to traditional machine learning (ML) models.

title: Conventional, Advanced, and Intelligent VFTO Mitigation Strategies for EHV/ UHV GIS system: EMTP Analysis and Economic Evaluation
authors: Davender Kumar, Anish Sachdeva, Supriya Paul
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: Very fast transient overvoltage (VFTO) poses a serious threat to the insulation reliability and operational safety of Extra High Voltages (EHV) & Ultra-high-voltage (UHV) Gas-Insulated Substations (GIS) due to their steep wavefronts, high-frequency content, and strong sensitivity to system configuration and earthing conditions. This paper highlights a comprehensive analysis of VFTO, which generates in Extra High Voltages (EHV) & Ultra-High Voltage (UHV) Gas Insulated Switchgear (GIS) systems during disconnector switching operations. This paper evaluates various VFTO suppression methodologies (Conventional, advanced and intelligent), with emphasis on their effectiveness and cost-benefit analysis relevant to Indian power systems. The paper also focuses on a comprehensive framework integrating conventional mitigation (shunt resistors, ferrite rings, RF resonators) with intelligent processing via FPGA hardware and AI algorithms for dynamic VFTO suppression. The study provides a structured decision-oriented perspective to support future research prioritisation and the development of integrated, condition-specific and most suited VFTO mitigation strategies.

title: Design of TFET-Based Label-Free Biosensors for Ultra-Low Power and High-Sensitivity Applications
authors: Mohd Saif Khan, Ramesh Kumar Sunkaria, Anil Kumar Yadav
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: The biosensors based on Tunnel Field Effect Transistors (TFETs) are emerging as suitable candidates for next-generation label-free detection with ultra-low power, a steep subthreshold slope, and high sensitivity. In this work, a dielectric-modulated TFET-based label-free biosensor is conceived and analyzed for ultra-low power and high-sensitivity biosensing applications. The sensing principle is based on the variation of gate dielectric permittivity due to the immobilization of the analyte biomolecules inside a nanocavity. Variations of the dielectric constants for each of the biomolecules (Protein(K=1.54),Uricase(K=2.5),Keratin(K=8),Gelatin(K=12)) are investigated through drain current–gate voltage characteristics, subthreshold swing, and I<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">ON</inf>/I<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">OFF</inf>. Furthermore, temperature-dependent performance in the range of 275 K–350 K, variation of cavity fill factor, energy band profiles, and electric field distribution are evaluated for robustness and sensitivity. The results show a clear enhancement in drain current, enhanced I<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">ON</inf>/I<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">OFF</inf> ratio, as well as stable subthreshold behavior, confirming the viability of TFET-based biosensors for ultra-low power and high-sensitivity biomedical sensing applications.

title: IoT Based Realtime Soil Monitoring,Smart Irrigation System and Yield Prediction
authors: Kota Nageswara Rao, Rajupuri Parameswar Reddy, Katta Akhilesh, Ramavath Rohan Kumar
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: This paper describes an IoT-based smart irrigation system to help farmers in field monitoring and water management. It is primarily based on low-cost sensors for measuring the moisture content of the soil, temperature, humidity, and nutrients of the soil, and all these sensors are connected to the ESP32 microcontroller. All the data collected is sent wirelessly to a cloud-based dashboard from where farmers can access and monitor their fields in real-time with the help of their smartphones or PCs.Irrigation is done without human intervention: if the soil becomes excessively dry, a DC motor is turned on to supply water and turned off as soon as the soil reaches the required moisture level. Farmers should be given the option of manual irrigation too by web interface control so that they can irrigate their fields whenever and wherever they want. Integrating on-time monitoring with the feature of automatic irrigation along with manual control, the irrigation system not only conserves water but also keeps the crops healthy and increases the overall agricultural output.Besides, by recording the optimum temperature and moisture levels, the project is instrumental in the most basic form of crop yield forecasting, thus enabling the farmers to estimate the output and the profit. In short, this system is a step towards implementing sustainable agriculture practices through data which is highly beneficial for Indian agriculture.

title: Bridging Traditional Econometrics and Neural Networks: Advances in Financial Forecasting via Machine Learning and Deep Learning
authors: Richa Suneja, Bhavya Alankar, Parul Agarwal
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: Financial forecasting focuses on interpreting market data to support decision-making under uncertain conditions. With increasing market complexity and volatility, advanced computational methods have become essential for improving predictive performance. This study reviews forecasting approaches spanning traditional econometric models and modern machine learning techniques, including deep learning architectures such as CNN and LSTM, along with generative models like GAN. These models are examined in relation to key financial characteristics such as nonlinearity, volatility clustering, and structural breaks. In addition, hybrid approaches that integrate multiple modeling paradigms are analyzed for their effectiveness in enhancing predictive capability. The results indicate that data-driven and hybrid deep learning models generally outperform conventional methods, particularly in dynamic and volatile market environments. The study also outlines current limitations and highlights potential directions for improving robustness and generalization in financial forecasting systems.

title: IoT-Based Smart Wearable for Real-Time Gait Stability Correction in Post-ACL Reconstruction Patients Using Machine Learning
authors: Vyshnavi Nemala, Neha Ahuja, Sunil Sharma
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: This paper presents the design and development of a smart wearable system for real-time gait stability correction in individuals recovering from anterior cruciate ligament reconstruction. Conventional knee braces provide mechanical support but lack sensing and feedback capabilities required for neuromuscular retraining. The proposed system integrates an inertial measurement unit to monitor knee and lower limb kinematics, coupled with a lightweight machine learning model to classify gait patterns as stable or abnormal. When instability or compensatory movement is detected, a vibration motor provides immediate haptic feedback to guide corrective action. The device enables patients to improve walking patterns outside clinical supervision, supporting continuous home-based rehabilitation. A novel Gait Stability Index is introduced to quantify recovery progress over time. Preliminary experimental observations indicate the feasibility of the system for personalized and adaptive gait training. This work demonstrates the potential of embedded sensing and machine learning to enhance lower-limb rehabilitation outcomes through accessible, real-time feedback.

title: EEG-Based Health Monitoring Using Machine Learning: A Comprehensive Multi-Stage Analysis Framework
authors: Shashi K. Sharma, Anant Deogaonkar, Sfoorti Thapliyal
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: EEG has found extensive applications in measurement of neural activity because of its high time sensitivity and its ability to be used both in clinical and computational domains. In the recent past, machine learning and deep learning have shown strong progress in the field of interpreting the EEG, since they allow automated feature identification and strong classification. The analysis framework described in this paper is a complete and entirely reproducible EEG analysis framework with filtering, wavelet-based decomposition, spectral analysis, band-power calculation, statistical correlation, and machine-learning-oriented evaluation. To validate every step of the pipeline in a controlled setting to achieve its transparency and replicability, a synthetic EEG dataset is used. The framework can be used as a generalizable template, which can be literally applied to real EEG repositories to be applied to healthcare and neurotechnology.

title: A Deep Learning-Based Framework for Multiclass Skin Cancer Classification Using ConvNeXt-Tiny
authors: Kamal Gulati, Aruna Malik
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: Skin cancer is a common and deadly form of cancer worldwide, but early diagnosis is the major means of improving the rates of survival. In recent years, deep learning (DL) has been demonstrated as a highly effective way of automating the classification of skin lesions and is able to achieve overall dermatologist level performance on standard benchmarks. This study focuses on the integration of current advances in DL-based skin cancer classifications through the implementation of Explainable Artificial Intelligence (XAI) methods and the changing nature of datasets used in this field. Three key areas considered: contribution of cutting-edge DL-based models towards increasing accuracy and robustness of classifications, role of XAI methods in achieving transparency and clinical validity, and issues of datasets. Additionally, we present a new multi-class skin cancer classification approach using the ConvNeXt-Tiny model and also provide an overview of the current state of trends in methodological approaches, outline fundamental challenges, and provide pathways for future studies to reduce the gap between development of algorithms and their practical applications.

title: AI Based Optimization for Microstrip Patch Array Design
authors: K. Raghavendra, Gayatri Murali, Dabbeta Rishitha, Chepyala Madhusri, Amrit Raj, Gagandeep Singh
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: Microstrip patch antennas are common in broadcasting. Conventional approaches to design microstrip patch antennas typically require lengthy simulation cycles, making them cumbersome for design and prototyping. A 2x2 2.45GHz(S-Band) microstrip patch antenna array has been designed with an ANN for parameter fine-tuning. The approach offered eliminates many simulation runs typically required in the optimization of the design. The S11, VSWR, and gain of the optimized array with radiation patterns are acceptable, with the radiation efficiency realized to be 5 dB and steady radiation to validate the approach of ANN design predictive methodology. Using this approach, the whole process was completed in 10% of the average time for similar designs. The design has stable radiation properties, making it usable in medical applications, also the design is compact and efficient whereby designs for medical wireless communication devices, especially patient monitoring systems and non-invasive microwave imaging systems for diagnosing the brain and breast, can be applied. This work highlights the effectiveness of artificial intelligence-assisted antenna design in achieving rapid optimization, along with delivering results and performance.

title: Design of 5-Stage Hazard-Free Pipelined Architecture for RISC-V-Based Processor
authors: Dakshika Sharma, Ankur Kumar, Naman Garg
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: Modern processors need to handle a large number of instructions efficiently. Executing them one after another often leads to longer delays and reduced system performance. Pipelining helps overcome this limitation by allowing different stages of multiple instructions to run at the same time. As one instruction is being executed, the next can already be fetched or decoded, which improves overall speed and utilization. Among various architectures, RISC-based processors are generally preferred for pipelined designs because of their simpler instruction sets and reduced dependency between instructions. Nevertheless, pipelined systems also have to deal with issues like data and control hazards, which can disrupt the flow of instructions and lower efficiency. This work uses Verilog HDL to design and develop a 32-bit five-stage pipelined RISC-V processor. To reduce delays brought on by pipeline hazards, the design incorporates strategies like branch prediction, stall control, and data forwarding. In addition to the M and F extensions for multiplication, division, and floating-point operations, it supports the RV32I base instruction set. To increase throughput, the processor has a Harvard design with distinct instruction and data memory. The single-cycle implementation of the processor operates at a maximum frequency of 31.6 MHz, while the pipelined version achieves 87.86 MHz—about 2.8 times faster. The frequency increases to 90.2 MHz with optimized hazard-handling and a 4% decrease in power consumption. Additionally, its modular design makes it simple to add custom instructions and scale. Because of these characteristics, the suggested processor is ideal for embedded and Internet of Things applications where both high performance and low power consumption are crucial.

title: Leveraging AI for Real-Time Intrusion Detection in IOT-Enabled Healthcare Systems
authors: Ahmed Butt, Malik Muhammad Huzaifa, Urooba Sumbal, Muhammad Zunnurain Hussain
publish: 2026 International Conference on Data Science, Machine Learning, and Intelligence (DataSciMI)
abstract: The fast growth of the Internet of Medical Things (IoMT) enables remote patient care but simultaneously creates serious cybersecurity risks. Traditional Intrusion Detection Systems (IDS) face challenges in protecting resource-constrained IoMT environments against new threats while maintaining data confidentiality and low network latency. To address these issues, this research proposes a novel, real-time intrusion detection framework tailored to the requirements of healthcare networks. The presented system integrates a Federated Learning (FL) framework with lightweight deep learning models, specifically Convolutional Neural Network-Bidirectional Long Short-Term Memory (CNN-BiLSTM) and Gated Recurrent Units (GRU). In this decentralized approach, models are trained directly on edge devices, preserving patient data privacy while collectively detecting spatial and temporal attack patterns. Evaluated on simulated IoMT network traffic, the federated model achieved an accuracy exceeding 99.8% with a near-zero false-positive rate. Furthermore, the GRU implementation demonstrated high feasibility for ultra-low-power edge deployment, requiring a memory footprint of only 0.04 MB and achieving an inference latency of 26 milliseconds per packet. These results validate the framework's capability to provide robust, privacy-aware, and scalable security solutions for modern smart healthcare systems.

title: Digital Design of WuR based IoT for Low Power Applications
authors: Rangana Bhanu Meher Srinivas, Rohit Singh
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Processing, Hardware, Electronics, and Radio Systems (CIPHER)
abstract: Wake-Up Radio (WuR)-based communication has been recognized as an efficient method to mitigate idle energy consumption in the cases of IoT nodes which have often long sleep period. This paper introduces an fully digital, synthesizable WuR-based IoT node architecture that is specifically designed for low-power operation and ASIC compatibility. The system consists of a digital wake-up detection unit with the use of synchronization, edge detection, and pulse-width filtering, and a lightweight MAC controller that allows event-driven activation of the communication subsystem. The clock gating is performed at the architectural level and implemented through FSM-controlled logic to prevent unnecessary switching during idle periods. The functional correctness and wake-up responsiveness are confirmed by performing RTL simulation, while power efficiency is estimated using Vivado Power Analyzer that is dependent on switching activity. The comparative results against a traditional always-on IoT node show a reduction in dynamic power consumption, thus affirming the applicability of WuR-assisted, event-driven operation in the case of energy-constrained IoT applications.

title: Efficient Edge-Compatible CNN for Speckle-Based Material Recognition in Laser Cutting Systems
authors: Mohamed Abdallah Salem, Nourhan Zein Diab
publish: 2025 35th International Conference on Computer Theory and Applications (ICCTA)
abstract: Accurate material recognition is critical for safe and effective laser cutting, as misidentification can lead to poor cut quality, machine damage, or the release of hazardous fumes. Laser speckle sensing has recently emerged as a low-cost and nondestructive modality for material classification; however, prior work has either relied on computationally expensive backbone networks or addressed only limited subsets of materials. In this study, A lightweight convolutional neural network (CNN) tailored for speckle patterns is proposed, designed to minimize parameters while maintaining high discriminative power. Using the complete SensiCut dataset of 59 material classes spanning woods, acrylics, composites, textiles, metals, and paper-based products, the proposed model achieves 95.05% test accuracy, with macro and weighted F1-scores of 0.951. The network contains only 341k trainable parameters (∼ 1.3 MB)-over 70 × fewer than ResNet-50-and achieves an inference speed of 295 images per second, enabling deployment on Raspberry Pi and Jetson-class devices. Furthermore, when materials are regrouped into nine and five practical families, recall exceeds 98% and approaches 100%, directly supporting power and speed preset selection in laser cutters. These results demonstrate that compact, domainspecific CNNs can outperform large backbones for speckle-based material classification, advancing the feasibility of materialaware, edge-deployable laser cutting systems.

title: Reducing Garbage Collection Overhead in ZNS SSD via ZNS-Aware Data Allocator
authors: Zhen-Yang Guo, Yen-Xin Wang, Yi-Ju Hsiung, Wei Kuan Shih, Chuan-Ming Liu, Shuo-Han Chen
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Consumer Technology (ICCT-Pacific)
abstract: As consumer electronics, which range from smartphones and laptops to high-performance portable drives, evolve to handle increasingly data-intensive workloads, storage efficiency becomes a critical factor in device longevity and user experience. Zoned Namespace (ZNS) SSDs have emerged as a superior architecture for these consumer applications by sequentially organizing logical blocks to minimize write amplification and extend the endurance of flash memory. However, the adoption of ZNS SSDs in consumer devices is hindered by complex data management requirements, specifically the high overhead of garbage collection (GC), which can degrade system responsiveness and increase latency during daily usage. This observation motivates the proposal of a novel ZNS-aware data allocator, designed to optimize storage performance in personal computing and mobile environments. Leveraging the copy-on-write and snapshot capabilities of the B-tree File System (Btrfs), our approach integrates an inode-based data transmission mechanism to improve data locality. By grouping data with similar update patterns, which are common in consumer application workloads, into dedicated zones, the proposed allocator minimizes redundant data migration and inter-zone movements. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed allocator reduces garbage collection overhead and migration latency by up to 24% under high-update scenarios. This optimization presents a viable path for integrating ZNS SSDs into next-generation consumer electronics, offering enhanced durability and sustained performance for end-users.

title: The AI Race Is Not a Race: It Is a Game and We Are Playing It Wrong
authors: Shawn Ogunseye
abstract: None

title: Reinforcement Learning-Based Focality Optimization for Multi-Electrode Temporal Interference Stimulation_supp1-3624869.pdf
authors: Yanming Wang
abstract: Objective: Multi-electrode Temporal Interference Stimulation (TIS) is a promising noninvasive technology for deep brain stimulation, but its clinical translation is hindered by the challenge of optimizing its high-dimensional and hybrid parameter space. Existing algorithms are often limited by high computational cost or a lack of practical flexibility. This paper introduces and validates a novel framework to overcome these barriers. Methods: We developed a novel reinforcement learning (RL) framework that simultaneously optimizes the discrete positions and continuous intensities of stimulation electrodes. We systematically evaluated the framework's performance using six realistic finite element head models and benchmarked it against genetic algorithms (GA) and unsupervised neural networks (USNN). Results: Our RL-based approach significantly enhanced stimulation focality, outperforming GA-based optimization. While achieving performance comparable to USNN, our framework offered the critical advantage of explicit control over the number of active electrodes. Furthermore, our findings reveal that focality improves with an increasing number of electrodes up to 16, with diminishing returns beyond this point. Conclusion: This work establishes a powerful and flexible computational paradigm for TIS optimization. Significance: Our framework provides a crucial guideline for optimal system design and overcomes key limitations of previous methods. This paves the way for more precise and clinically robust noninvasive neuromodulation, accelerating the clinical translation of TIS.

title: IAEAgent: LLM-Based Educational Agent for Instructional Alignment Evaluation in University Courses
authors: Junqi Liao, Minjuan Wang, Xiaofei Wu, Yizhi Wang, Xin Wei
abstract: In university courses, instructional alignment evaluation reflects the degree of alignment between the intended teaching requirements and the actual content delivered by teachers. It can not only provide university administrative departments with evidence about actual teaching performance, but also facilitate teachers' targeted reflection and continuous instructional improvement. However, there is still a lack of a theory-grounded, course-aware, and quantitatively interpretable instructional alignment evaluator. To address this issue, in this article, we propose a new evaluation paradigm—an LLM-based educational agent (IAEAgent)—for instructional alignment evaluation in university courses. Firstly, by injecting teaching materials such as textbooks using parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT), a course-specific LLM with in-depth disciplinary comprehension is constructed. Then, based on this LLM and employing prompt engineering, the intended and actual instructional knowledge graphs (KGs) can be respectively extracted from teaching requirement documents and teachers' actual audio transcripts of a lesson. Subsequently, guided by teaching quality evaluation theories, a quantitative analysis of instructional alignment is conducted for these two KGs across three crucial dimensions: knowledge coverage completeness, knowledge depth consistency, and knowledge structure coherence. Finally, based on the obtained analytical results, a teaching quality evaluation report containing teaching diagnostics and improvement suggestions is generated. Extensive experiments demonstrate that the IAEAgent excels in comprehensive and fine-grained instructional alignment evaluation. Furthermore, ablation studies validate the effectiveness of each step within the IAEAgent.

title: Unsupervised Large-Scale Point Cloud Registration via Spherical Projection Consistency
authors: Yejun Shou, Shuai Li, Haocheng Wang, Zhijie Xu, Yanlong Cao
abstract: In real-world applications, large-scale outdoor Li- DAR point cloud registration faces significant challenges, including data sparsity, occlusions, and reliance on expensive ground-truth pose labels. To address these issues, this paper proposes an unsupervised registration framework based on spherical projection consistency. Specifically, both the input point cloud and its spatially transformed counterpart are projected into range images, and their spatial consistency is exploited as a supervision signal. Furthermore, a multi-scale patch-topatch feature fusion module is introduced to effectively integrate features from point clouds and range images, thereby enhancing feature discriminability. In addition, a dynamic masking strategy is applied in the range image domain to mitigate the impact of sparsity variations and further strengthen the spatial consistency of the supervision signal. Extensive experiments on the KITTI and NuScenes datasets demonstrate that the proposed method outperforms state-of-the-art unsupervised approaches, validating its effectiveness and robustness in large-scale outdoor scenarios.

title: A Perturbation-Aware Local-to-Global Supervised Contrastive Framework for Robust Gastrointestinal Endoscopic Disease Classification
authors: Ying Zhang, Yu-Meng Diao, Hang Yang, Zhong-Hao Huang
abstract: Gastrointestinal (GI) diseases remain a major global health burden, and accurate endoscopic image analysis is essential for early diagnosis and timely intervention. However, endoscopic images often exhibit substantial appearance variability caused by illumination fluctuation, motion blur, specular reflection, viewpoint instability, and device-related factors, which greatly challenge robust automated classification. To address these issues, we propose a perturbation-aware local-to-global supervised contrastive framework for gastrointestinal endoscopic disease classification. The proposed method integrates four key components within a unified representation learning pipeline. First, a dual-layer augmentation strategy generates semantic-preserving and hard-perturbation views to explicitly model clinically relevant appearance variability. Second, a cross-viewsaliency recalibration module enhances lesion-sensitive spatial responses by emphasizing regions that are both discriminative and stable across heterogeneous views. Third, a direction-selective structural mixer captures anisotropic structural dependencies through adaptive row-wise, column-wise, and channel-wise feature interaction, enabling more effective modeling of mucosal folds, vascular patterns, and irregular lesion boundaries. Fourth, progressive cross-layer fusion establishes hierarchical interaction between local texture cues and broader structural context. These components are jointly optimized using classification and supervised contrastive objectives to improve intra-class compactness, inter-class separability, and robustness under appearance degradation. Extensive experiments on two public benchmarks, Kvasir-v2 and Kvasir-Capsule, demonstrate that the proposed framework consistently outperforms conventional convolutional models, transformer-based architectures, and recent advanced methods. The model achieves 97.0 percent accuracy on Kvasir-v2 and 98.2 percent accuracy on Kvasir-Capsule, with superior macro-level performance, strong class-wise discrimination, and improved robustness under controlled perturbations. Ablation and sensitivity analyses further confirm that the performance gains arise from the complementary interaction of perturbation-aware view construction, cross-view stability enhancement, structure-sensitive modeling, and hierarchical local-to-global fusion. These results indicate that perturbation-aware structural representation learning provides an effective and well-grounded solution for robust gastrointestinal endoscopic disease classification.

title: Creating a Sustainable Ecosystem to Support Cyber Workforce Development: Reflections and Lessons
authors: Waleed Farag
publish: 2025 35th International Conference on Computer Theory and Applications (ICCTA)
abstract: This presentation will discuss multi-year novel initiatives resulting in the creation of an impactful ecosystem in western Pennsylvania that positively contributes to the development of skillful cybersecurity workforce. In few years, these initiatives have secured more the $13M in federal funds to support their numerous efforts and services. Representatives of these projects will be introduced along with their impacts and how others can benefit from them. Our efforts did not only focus on increasing awareness, in K-20 environments, with cybersecurity and the amazing opportunities the field offers. But also, we implemented a multitude of successful services that led to the cultivation of a dynamic eco-system providing a sustainable pipeline of students from elementary to postgraduate pursuing cybersecurity degrees. Promoting cybersecurity to K-12 has been a focus. Therefore, we introduced the IUP GenCyber to reach out to middle and high school students and teachers and foster a serious interest in the field throughout the entire region. IUP is the first university in PA to offer GenCyber and the only university in PA that held GenCyber programs every year between 2016-2024. So far, we have held ten different programs and have trained more than 550 middle and high-school teachers and students. Some of our offerings were described by the visiting team as a model that needs to be replicated elsewhere. Scores of students who attended our programs have majored in Cybersecurity at IUP and other universities and several of them have already graduated and joined the cyber workforce in government and private sector. Establishing strong relationships with surrounding Community Colleges (CCs) has also been a priority to ensure reaching out to untapped sector and sustaining a pipeline of students entering the field from all backgrounds. Hence, we introduced a Cybersecurity Professional Development Series funded by the DoD. In three different academic years, we held eighteen daylong workshops for various CCs throughout PA and trained about 180 faculty and students. Moreover, we have built a very successful program that recruits high- achiever undergraduate students at IUP and provides them with opportunities to specialize in cybersecurity via the IUP Cyber Service Academy (CSA). Since 2018, we secured seventy-one full-ride scholarships, and many recipients had joined various governmental agencies upon graduation. Also, we extended such opportunities to our PhD students and secured one out of only five awards in the NCAE-C PhD Scholarship pilot program. The later scholarship recipient is now a faculty member at a known CAE. These successful initiatives have built the foundation needed to get involved in larger impactful projects. One example is PC4A, a multi-million-dollar initiative and one of only five national cooperative agreements funded by NDEP/DoD STEM. PC4A founded a vibrant consortium of six CCs led by IUP working together to benefit about 3,000 students and enhance cybersecurity education across PA. In conclusion, the session will summarize our experience in these major projects and discuss specific lessons learnt and documented impact. We plan to use various active learning approaches to engage participants and provide them with a beneficial, and entertaining learning experience.

title: 2026 International Conference on Data Science, Machine Learning, and Intelligence (DataSciMI)
authors: N/A

title: Numerical and Experimental Validation of a Source-to-Harness Near-Field Coupling Model in an Automotive Context
authors: Martin Humeau, Mohsen Koohestani, Mohamed Ramdani, Richard Perdriau, David Boudikian, Frederic Lafon
abstract: This paper advances the predictive modeling of near-field electromagnetic coupling in automotive wire harnesses by validating the ‘single-wire equivalence’ hypothesis for industrial-scale systems. While previous work established a foundational framework using transmission line theory and the Biot-Savart law on "hand-made" prototypes, this study addresses the transition to produced, stochastic non-uniform harnesses. The methodology employs a two-fold validation: first, a high-fidelity numerical analysis using CST Studio Suite (Cable Studio) to evaluate the impact of random twisting and frequency-dependent losses on model robustness, and second, an empirical assessment using a dedicated experimental testbed with custom PCB terminations for wide parametric impedance variation. The results demonstrate a good correlation between analytical predictions, numerical simulations and measurements from industrial-grade samples. By confirming that the total common-mode response of complex multi-wire harnesses can be accurately represented by a simplified single-conductor model, this work provides a reliable and computationally efficient tool for CISPR 25 compliance, reducing simulation runtimes from hours to seconds without compromising engineering accuracy in real-world automotive environments.

title: Log-Reparameterized Diffusion Priors for Blind Radial Defocus Deblurring
authors: Chang Wang, Guangxu Li, Yaqiang Hu, Tohru Kamiya
abstract: Radial defocus blur—a pervasive degradation in optical systems—exhibits spatially-variant patterns where blur intensity increases monotonically from center to periphery due to spherical aberration and field curvature. While common in systems, existing general-purpose deblurring methods often overlook this physics-informed structure, limiting their real-world performance. Although diffusion models show promise in image restoration, their application to radial defocus remains challenged by a lack of underlying physical modeling and optimization instabilities. To address these challenges, we propose Log-Diff, a physics-informed blind deblurring framework. We first construct a comprehensive dataset of defocus kernels derived from parametric physical modeling to accurately capture spatially-variant and distance-dependent blur characteristics. Crucially, we introduce a log-space optimization strategy (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\boldsymbol{\sigma }^{\prime } = \log \boldsymbol{\sigma }$</tex-math></inline-formula>) within the diffusion process. This mechanism effectively resolves instability by enforcing non-negativity constraints and preventing gradient vanishing through adaptive scaling. By integrating this reparameterized physical model into the reverse sampling process, our architecture enables the joint estimation of the sharp image and the blur field with strict physical consistency. Extensive experiments on FFHQ demonstrate that Log-Diff outperforms baselines by over 39% in perceptual metrics (LPIPS 0.12) while maintaining high fidelity (PSNR 27.41 dB). Unlike normalized approximations, our approach generalizes to real-world projector scenarios, establishing a new paradigm for integrating physical constraints into generative priors.

title: Introducing IEEE Collabratec
authors: N/A

title: Collaborative Meta-Computing for Lightweight and Energy-Efficient Traffic Classification in Industrial Cyber Physical Social Systems
authors: ZiXuan Wang, HongLei Fu, TianShui Chang, Pan Wang, XueJiao Chen
publish: IEEE Transactions on Computational Social Systems
abstract: As a core implementation of cyber physical social systems (CPSS) in smart manufacturing, the Industrial Internet of Things (IIoT) is accelerating the shift of industrial intelligence toward collaborative learning and distributed-intelligence paradigms. However, the explosive growth of industrial data traffic and the heterogeneity of edge devices make it difficult for conventional traffic management schemes to provide high-accuracy, low-latency classification under strict resource and energy constraints. To address these challenges, this article proposes a collaborative meta-computing-based traffic classification framework for IIoT-enabled CPSS, which jointly considers lightweight model design and energy-efficient distributed collaboration. First, a depthwise separable convolutional neural network (DSCNN) is adopted as the local classifier on industrial edge gateways, significantly reducing computational complexity while preserving fine-grained spatiotemporal patterns in network flows. Second, a model-quality-driven node collaboration mechanism is developed to adaptively select high-quality edge nodes for participation in each global round, thereby improving collaborative learning efficiency and reducing redundant communication. Third, an energy–delay optimal CPU frequency scheduling scheme is introduced to dynamically adjust the computing frequency of each participating node, balancing training delay and energy consumption across heterogeneous gateways. Experiments conducted on the CICIoT2022 and UNSW-IoTBotnet2018 datasets demonstrate that the proposed method effectively supports collaborative, energy-aware traffic classification in distributed IIoT scenarios. The DSCNN-based classifier achieves over 99% accuracy with low computational overhead; the node selection mechanism accelerates model convergence by reducing training epochs by approximately 15%; and the joint optimization of node collaboration and computing frequency yields an overall energy reduction of about 24% compared with the baseline meta-computing setting.

title: HSST-EEG: A Hybrid State-Space and Transformer Architecture for EEG Decoding
authors: Emil Kim, Jin Kyu Gahm
abstract: Accurate interpretation of electroencephalography (EEG) signals requires effective modeling of high-dimensional, non-stationary, and long-range spatio-temporal dynamics, which remains a central challenge in both clinical and BCI-oriented decoding tasks. Recent approaches that combine convolutional neural networks (CNNs) or explicit spectral feature extractors with downstream sequence models have improved EEG decoding performance. However, such pipelines remain limited in their ability to jointly capture long-range temporal dependencies and dynamic cross-channel interactions while maintaining computational efficiency. Transformer-based architectures capture pairwise temporal and spatial features via self-attention, enabling flexible long-range dependency modeling but incurring quadratic computational complexity. In contrast, Mamba-based state-space architectures achieve favorable linear-time complexity by modeling temporal dependencies implicitly through shared state evolution, which may limit their capacity to represent complex spatio-temporal EEG structures compared to similarly sized Transformer models. To address these limitations, we propose HSST-EEG (Hybrid State-Space Transformer for EEG Decoding), a hybrid architecture that integrates Mamba-based state-space modeling with Transformer-based attention to balance computational efficiency and representational capacity. Experiments conducted on two widely used clinical EEG benchmarks, the CHB-MIT Scalp EEG Database and the TUH EEG Events Corpus, demonstrate that HSST-EEG achieves up to a 5% improvement in classification accuracy compared to recent EEG decoding baselines.

title: Programmable Optical Spectrum Shapers as Computing Primitives for Accelerating Convolutional Neural Networks
authors: Georgios Moustakas, Adonis Bogris, Charis Mesaritakis
abstract: Photonic convolutional accelerators have emerged as low-energy alternatives to power-demanding digital convolutional neural networks, though they often face limitations in scalability. In this work, we introduce a convolutional photonic accelerator that employs programmable kernels manifesting as trainable waveforms in the frequency domain to enable low-energy, high-throughput scalable image classification. The proposed scheme inherently provides dimensionality reduction and feature extraction directly in the optical domain. Numerical results targeting the Fashion-MNIST show that by using only 16 optical nodes, the system's classification accuracy tops at 90.1%, when typical backpropagation is used. Moreover, by adapting the training technique to forward-forward approach a marginal drop by 1% is recorded compared to the back-propagation scenario, thus showcasing the compatibility of the overall architecture with a hardware friendly training approach. Finally, we experimentally implement the trained kernels using a programmable waveshaper. Despite the difference between the simulated and experimentally generated transfer functions of the programmable Kernels, the classification accuracy based on the experimentally obtained kernels exhibits a marginal 0.2% reduction proving the validity of the idea and its high robustness to variations of the frequency applied complex weights.

title: DMMCNet: Dynamic Multiscale and Multilevel Contrastive Point Cloud Feature Extraction Network for Industrial Defect Detection_supp1-3689660.docx
authors: Zhenyan Ji
abstract: Industrial defect detection is crucial for ensuring product quality and safety. Point-cloud-based methods have attracted increasing attention due to their accurate geometric feature extraction capability. However, most of them first, lack the ability to dynamically adjust multiscale receptive fields, limiting their capacity to perceive defects with diverse sizes, second, ignore semantic inconsistencies among multiscale features during fusion, weakening the ability to distinguish defect regions, and third, pay insufficient attention to key points, lacking sensitivity to fine-grained defects. To address these issues, we propose a dynamic multiscale and multilevel contrastive point cloud feature extraction network (DMMCNet) for industrial defect detection, which consists of three key modules: dynamic multiscale feature encoding module, multilevel contrastive fusion module, and key-point enhancement module (KPE). Specifically, in DMFE, we propose a learnable scale embedding mechanism that dynamically adjusts receptive field sizes according to the geometric context of the point cloud, improving the perception of defects with diverse sizes. In MCF, we develop a multilevel fusion scheme that progressively aggregates multiscale features, allowing fine-scale defect cues to be preserved before incorporating background information. Within this fusion process, we propose a cross-scale contrastive constraint mechanism to align features from different receptive-field scales and alleviate semantic inconsistencies. In KPE, we model spatial correlations among points to guide the network’s attention toward key points with distinct geometric variations, strengthening the response to critical defect areas. Finally, experimental results on three industrial point cloud datasets demonstrate that DMMCNet outperforms thirteen state-of-the-art methods.
